title: Modern Techniques for Ancient Art: Deep Learning in Rock Art Motif Classiﬁcation
abstract: Rock art motifs are painted or carved on the stones. They represent a wide range of subjects,animals, humans,symbols, and abstract designs. They usually provide clarity into the beliefs and daily lives of the ancient human ancestors. These rock art motifs are present around the globe, representing their own unique culture. Traditional methods of analyzing rock art involves heavy ﬁeld work, photographic documentation, manual classiﬁcation by experts, and are also resourceintensive. This research utilizes deep learning techniques such as Convolutional neural network, Efﬁcient-Net, and Res-Net for the automatic classiﬁcation of rock art motifs. Deep learning is the subset of the ML that uses a neural network with many layers to automatically learn and extract complex patterns from large datasets. CNN,along with efﬁcient-net and Res-Net(Residual net),have had a remarkable success in the image classiﬁcation domain. CNN is the type of deep learning technique designed for analyzing image dataset. They have a number of Convolutional layers and pooling layers to help with data abstraction. Efﬁcient-Net is a deep learning technique for image classiﬁcation which uses the compound scaling techniques. Res-Net is also a deep learning technique used for image classiﬁcation, which has residual blocks and identity mapping. Advancements in technology offer promising solutions to these challenges. This intends to enhance the efﬁciency and accuracy of classifying rock art images, providing archaeologists with a powerful tool to preserve and understand our culture. The result of this research extends to archaeology, art history and ancient culture which helps other researchers for future study.

These rock art motifs are present around the globe, representing their own unique culture. Traditional methods of analyzing rock art involves heavy ﬁeld work, photographic documentation, manual classiﬁcation by experts, and are also resourceintensive. This research utilizes deep learning techniques such as Convolutional neural network, Efﬁcient-Net, and Res-Net for the automatic classiﬁcation of rock art motifs. This intends to enhance the efﬁciency and accuracy of classifying rock art images, providing archaeologists with a powerful tool to preserve and understand our culture. The result of this research extends to archaeology, art history and ancient culture which helps other researchers for future study. Index Terms—Convolutional neural networks(CNN),Res-Net, Efﬁcient-Net,Image classiﬁcation,Archaeology,rock art motifs, I. INTRODUCTION Archaeology is the study of human history and prehistory by excavation of artifacts, structures and remains. Among the many discoveries made by archaeologists,rock art motifs are fascinating evidence of early human. Rock art motifs are usually found in caves and cliff’s of various ancient structure.They have various symbols such as animal ﬁgures and patterns providing valuable information about ancient cultures. They serve as record which captures environmental and social development of early human beings [9] . These art work are crucial for connecting modern day people to ancient heritage and appreciation for ancient lifestyle.The early methods involves gathering documents and on-ﬁeld working.This challenge can be overcome by using deep learning techniques such as CNN(convolutional neural network), efﬁcient net and residual net.Deep learning is a sub-ﬁeld of machine learning that focuses on neural networks consisting of number of layers which mimic the human brain. For image classiﬁcation CNN, efﬁcient net and residual net can be used because of their capturing ability of grid like data. CNN is a deep learning technique used for analyzing visual data(images) which is inspired by human cortex which can be seen in [7]. CNN contains convolutional layers used for feature extraction and pooling layer to reduce the spatial dimension that is width and height of the images and in the end they contain fully connected layer for image classiﬁcation. Efﬁcient net is a again a deep learning technique containing number of blocks where each block contains mobile net and a activation function and in the end they have fully connected layer for image classiﬁcation. They use compound scaling technique which scales the complexity, breath, and granularity of the image [16]. Residual net is also a deep learning technique which uses residual connections meaning the network to bypass one or many layers. These connection help to learn residual functions rather than un-referenced one. In general terms these deep learning techniques are used because of it’s capability of capturing features from the image dataset which other deep learning techniques making them invaluable tool for modern archaeological research and 2024 Asian Conference on Intelligent Technologies (ACOIT) Karnataka, India. LITERATURE WORK Recent research highlights the effectiveness of deep learning in several picture categorization tasks. CNN-based methods have demonstrated exceptional performance in interpreting Thai text [13], categorizing old Indonesian monuments [2], detecting Roman coinage [11], and recognizing traditional Romanian themes [9]. Additionally, ResNet models tailored for medical imaging have demonstrated enhanced efﬁcacy in pneumonia identiﬁcation [12], while ResNets have demonstrated efﬁcacy in satellite image categorization [1]. These developments demonstrate how deep learning may be used to precisely analyze historical and cultural artifacts by categorizing themes found in ancient rock art. Recent work on deep learning has produced a number of papers that demonstrate robust methodologies for a variety of classiﬁcation tasks. This study introduces a customized ResNet-18 model architecture explicitly designed for classifying eye illness images into four categories with significant medicinal properties [10]. These include studies on the application of VGG16 and EfﬁcientNet for plant disease classiﬁcation [14], enhanced EfﬁcientNet for blueberry variety recognition [16], different models for the classiﬁcation of ancient Tibetan books [18], and experimental comparisons between CNN and RNN models in image classiﬁcation [8] . The variety of deep learning applications is further demonstrated by breakthroughs like multi-scale dense residual networks for ﬂoral image classiﬁcation [6] and thorough reviews on modulation classiﬁcation [3]. All of these studies show that deep learning can improve the way complex patterns in prehistoric rock art are classiﬁed, which could lead to improvements in the ﬁeld of archaeological study and cultural asset conservation. Several recent papers serve as examples of deep learning advancements: The automated modulation classiﬁcation that is essential for military and surveillance applications is the subject of [3] . A CNN recommendation system for automating the selection of image task models is presented in [15]. CNN models are investigated in [7] for accurate music genre classiﬁcation. A sequential CNN model for efﬁcient mushroom categorization in biodiversity studies is presented in [4]. To improve diagnosis accuracy, a CNN-based technique for automatic thyroid ultrasound picture classiﬁcation is proposed in [5]. In conclusion, [17] presents an enhanced ResNet method for accurate Chinese native dog breed classiﬁcation, demonstrating the revolutionary inﬂuence of deep learning in the ﬁelds of healthcare, biodiversity, and cultural heritage. Recent studies demonstrate the efﬁcacy of deep learning in a range of picture classiﬁcation tasks, employing CNNs to identify cultural themes such as Roman items and Romanian customs, as well as to translate historical texts and classify monuments [2, 9, 11, 13]. In medical imaging, ResNet models are also used for satellite image processing and pneumonia diagnosis [1, 12]. These uses highlight deep learning’s capacity to analyze historical objects, including prehistoric rock art. In addition to comparisons between CNNs and RNNs in image analysis [8], notable recent contributions include a ResNet-18 model for identifying eye diseases [10], VGG16 and EfﬁcientNet for plant disease recognition [14, 16], and models for classifying Tibetan texts [18]. Deep learning is further extended by innovations such as multi-scale dense residual networks for ﬂoral imagery [6] and thorough reviews on modulation categorization [3]. III. This study’s primary goal is to improve accuracy and efﬁciency through iterative processes and hyperparameter adjustment. A. CNN can be trained on a big dataset capturing different types of shapes, lines and textures. CNN uses number of neural layers or also called as convolutional ﬁlters used to capture important features such as shape of the motif, the edges of the motif and the colour included in them, this method is called as feature extraction, pooling layer helps in reducing dimensions of each output, here each feature captured by the convolutional layer are reducued in dimension to increase the computing efﬁciency and in the end they have fully connected layer which used for ﬁnal classiﬁcation. Resnet Res-net or Residual network is effective for classiﬁcation of rock art motifs because of it’s ability to reduce issues with deep networks. Vanishing gradient is a such a technique used in Res-net. Rock art motifs require capturing some delicate and detailed features that require some deep networks to accurately capture them. The residual blocks are connected which allows networks to maintain high accuracy even after after increasing number of layers. Each of these blocks contain two or more convolutional layers which uses kernels to capture features, activation function introduces non-linearity in the network which allows network to record much more more structures and a normalization technique to stabilize training and improve gradient . This makes it a deep networks which improves accuracy and performances. These blocks helps the model to learn residual functions. This capability is very important for identifying patterns associated with motifs leading to more accurate classiﬁcation. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION Our tests examined how alternative convolutional neural network(CNN) architectures and activation function performed in the separation of the motifs art from the Mayan,Egyptian, and Indus civilizations. The examine is based on the exactness, precision, recall, and F1-score as performance indicators. TABLE I: Comparison of different activation functions in CNN CNN Activation function ReLU Tanh Sigmoid Softmax Number of classes 3 3 3 3 Batch of size 35 35 35 35 Number of Iteration 10 10 10 10 Accuracy 0.99 0.95 0.83 0.73 With the three classes(Mayan,Indus,and Egypt), 35 as batch sizes, and 10 iterations as shown in TABLE 1. The CNN with ReLU activation function was the most effective model, achieving the highest accuracy of 0.99 across all classes. The CNN with Softmax activation function had moderate performance, with signiﬁcant variation in accuracy, recall, and F1scores. The CNN with Sigmoid activation function performed better, particularly for the Mayan and Indus civilizations, with an accuracy of 0.83. The CNN with Tanh activation function also performed better, achieving the highest accuracy, recall, and F1-scores in all classes. Fig. 5: Confusion Matrix for all Activation The examination of the confusion matrix shows how different activation functions employed in a classiﬁcation model exhibit differing in accuracy. Firstly, best performance across all classes was demonstrated by the ReLU activation, which consistently obtained excellent accuracy with 35 accurate classiﬁcations for Egypt, 34 for the Indus, and 35 for the Mayan. However, compared to ReLU, the softmax activation function had a lower accuracy, properly categorizing 29 images (20 of which were classiﬁed as Indus and 28 as Mayan). This suggests that there may have been some misclassiﬁcations. In the Training and Validation Graph With 34 Indus and 28 Mayan cases properly identiﬁed, the sigmoid activation function showed moderate accuracy; however, it was less accurate with other classes. Tanh activation function, on the other hand, better than the others in several aspects: it accurately predicted 33 images of Egypt, 32 of Indus, and all 35 of Mayan, demonstrating its greater ability to capture differences in class. Tanh’s near-perfect depiction for Mayan in particular demonstrated its best in identifying this category but, ReLU demonstrated the great accuracy overall across all the classes in confusion matrix. The individual strengths and disadvantages of each activation function underscored the signiﬁcance of choosing the best suitable activation function in accordance with the particular classiﬁcation job. 5 Authorized licensed use limited to: b-on: UNIVERSIDADE DA BEIRA INTERIOR. CONCLUSION The research results by using a machine learning algorithm (CNN,Efﬁcient and ResNet) to accurately classify rock art motifs, achieving an 85 percent of accuracy. This high accuracy demonstrates potential of automated classiﬁcation methods in archaeology, providing a more efﬁcient and objective analysis of rock art. This accuracy has signiﬁcant implications for studying prehistoric cultures, allowing researchers to analyze larger datasets more quickly and gain a deeper understanding of cultural and artistic patterns. VI. FUTUREWORK These analysis future prospects would be to evaluate the capability of more computational techniques in further study of rock art motifs.Investing the multiple rock arts,dynamic settings, and uncertain variables are some of the suitable types of it.We will apply our methodology to the real life context including cultural heritage tourism, archaeological site management, and heritage protection.We will measure it’s corrections, performance in motifs classiﬁcation, and ﬂexibility with excessive data values. title: Fernández-Lozano  a , ∗ ,  Gabriel  Gutiérrez-Alonso  a , b ,  Miguel  Ruiz-Tejada  c ,
abstract: The Castrocontrigo rock art, located in the southwestern area of the León province (Spain), is one of the Neolithic rock art occurrences in northwest Iberia. The showings comprise three schematic panels within two rock shelters characterised by the presence of anthropomorphs and sun-forms. This paper deals with the digital description and documentation of the two shelters using non-invasive procedures based on portable white-light scanner, photogrammetry and digital image enhancement. The acquisition of 3D digital information in combination with the application of image enhancement tools aimed to improve visualization and analysis of motif’s degradation by delamination and water runoff. Additionally, enhancement was used to reveal the presence of new motifs in poorly preserved sectors of the studied panels aiming at their conservation. The generation of photorealistic models from the outcrops aims also to better visualization of the motifs from different angles and perspectives, providing valuable qualitative and quantitative information for archaeologist, historians and the general public. © 2017 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.

Facultad de Ciencias Geológicas. University of Salamanca, 37008 Salamanca, Spain b Geology and Geography Department, Tomsk State University, Lenin Street 36, 634050 Tomsk, Russian Federation c EGeomapping, C/Arrastraria, 21, 28002 Madrid, Spain a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received 4 October 2016 Accepted 13 January 2017 Available online 15 February 2017 Keywords: 3D documentation Schematic rock art Neolithic Castrocontrigo Spain a b s t r a c t The Castrocontrigo rock art, located in the southwestern area of the León province (Spain), is one of the Neolithic rock art occurrences in northwest Iberia. The showings comprise three schematic panels within two rock shelters characterised by the presence of anthropomorphs and sun-forms. This paper deals with the digital description and documentation of the two shelters using non-invasive procedures based on portable white-light scanner, photogrammetry and digital image enhancement. The acquisition of 3D digital information in combination with the application of image enhancement tools aimed to improve visualization and analysis of motif’s degradation by delamination and water runoff. Additionally, enhancement was used to reveal the presence of new motifs in poorly preserved sectors of the studied panels aiming at their conservation. The generation of photorealistic models from the outcrops aims also to better visualization of the motifs from different angles and perspectives, providing valuable qualitative and quantitative information for archaeologist, historians and the general public. © 2017 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction and research aims The access to geomatic technologies has provided new and improved techniques for digital documentation and rock art conservation directives. The preservation of cultural heritage archaeological motifs and panels is always problematic due to the occurrence of natural processes (i.e. geological factors, vegetation, lichens, humidity, bacterial activity, thermal gradient, etc.) or anthropic deterioration such as grafﬁti [1–4]. Raised concern about the need for preservation has led to a large number of studies, focused on new technologies, providing 3D reliable documentation of different archeological remains. These methods are based on the use of image and range sensors to record surface data from regional scale archaeological landscapes, to local scale architecture and excavations or even to small scale object surveying [5–12]. These technologies allow the acquisition of high-resolution digital data such as point clouds and orthomosaics that may help for the ∗Corresponding author. Tel.: +34 91 415 96 91. E-mail address: jﬂ@usal.es (J. Fernández-Lozano). identiﬁcation and description of the panels aiming at their digital documentation and preservation [13–16]. In this work, we have studied two rock shelters comprising three panels characterised mainly by the presence of anthropomorphs and sun-forms together with other less recognizable abstract representations (i.e. ﬁngerprints, circles, etc.). These motifs represent a good example of the connection between the Atlantic fac¸ ade domain and the central Spain (Mediterranean) post-paleolithic rock art (allegedly between the Chalcolithic and the Iron Age). The location of the shelters is conditioned by geological factors, lithology, bedding attitude and joints (fractures) distribution. Recent wildﬁres, the presence of lichens and humidity, and grafﬁti have contributed to damage the outcrops, causing the partial destruction of the paints. This paper presents comprehensive and detailed 3D documentation of the panels using a combination of robust and non-invasive geomatic approaches (based on portable white-light 3D scanning and photogrammetry) and image enhancement tools for the identiﬁcation, description and preservation of the rock panels. The latter also aimed at the location of new motifs, improving the comprehensiveness of the panels. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.culher.2017.01.008 1296-2074/© 2017 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved. J. Fernández-Lozano et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 26 (2017) 160–166 161 2. Schematic Rock Art in Northwest Spain The Iberian schematic rock art gathers different types of prehistoric drawings and engravings broadly dated between the early Neolithic and the Iron Age [17–21]. Drawings are usually made using different techniques, resources and colours. The presence of schematic rock art in the province of León is scarce and the style (i.e. drawing or engravings) is deﬁned by the different types of rock in which they occur, mostly slates that were quite easy to engrave (engravings) and the much harder quartzites and scarce limestones (paintings). In general, panels have been interpreted as sanctuaries or areas dedicated to cultural activities, although their signiﬁcance is still a matter of debate [22,23]. There are at least six studied Neolithic archaeological sites in the León province, Librán, Sésamo, Boudela, Filiel, Andi˜nuela and Castrocontrigo [22,24–31]. Pictorial representations comprise a wide variety of zoomorphs, hands, ﬁngerprints, idols and anthropomorphs. The latter are represented by T-types, cruciforms, Pi and Phy, Y-type, etc. [27]. They can appear isolated or in small groups together with zoomorphs or other geometric shapes. The paintings were made using different instruments (i.e. hands, natural brushes, etc.) and colours (red and black principally) [24]. 3. Materials and methods Traditional analysis of prehistoric rock art has been widely focused on the direct observation of drawings and/or photographic images. However, the advent and combination of geomatic techniques and digital image treatment can improve the results by providing non-invasive qualitative and quantitative digital information, enhancing visualization and identifying possible superposition of different paintings or the presence of previously unseen motifs. The integrated analysis of prehistoric rock art using 2D images and 3D information reduces the possible distortion problems caused by the recording of drawings and/or photographic images. In addition, the 3D resolution reduction —i.e. due to Fig. 1. A. Panoramic view of Panel I and different enhancement settings emphasizing red colours (B–D) according to processing by image enhancement tools following the procedure suggested by Haman [52]. B and C show six human ﬁgures, one of them carrying what we interpret as a shield. There are other motifs characterised by ﬁngerprints and circles C. The presence of water runoff and erosion destroying the panel can be seen in D. E. Sketch with the interpretation of the motifs based on observations carried out on B, C and D (red motifs represent newly described motifs, see text for further explanation). 162 J. Fernández-Lozano et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 26 (2017) 160–166 information loss—, during the acquisition of digital information is very minor and nowadays can be dismissed [9,12,32–35]. The generation of 3D-photorealistic models also retrieves the volumetric sensation and the colours preserved by the jagged relief of the outcrop [12,36]. The possibility to observe the panels through different angles and perspectives and the application of colour enhancement by comparing the original and enhanced models contributes to a better analysis, interpretation and description of the pictorial motifs. 3.1. Documentation The study area is a mountainous region characterized by a complex topographic relief, where GPS signal may be strongly shadowed and receivers were proven not valuable due to the limited space for data acquisition. Moreover, the use of traditional topographic techniques is also a time-consuming task —because it is necessary to locate a GPS RTK receptor combined with the use of a Total Station (TS)—, which requires complex post-processing tasks for coordinate transformations, and it is not free of errors. Because of these reasons, we have used a relative coordinates system that, in this case, provides better accuracy. The studied shelters are located at high topographic elevation (ca. 1260 m.a.s.l.), in steeply inclined, even overhanging, rock surfaces, difﬁcult to access, which makes difﬁcult the use of traditional terrestrial laser scanners and/or any other traditional topographic surveying method (TS). Because of this withdrawal, 3D documentation was acquired using a highprecision portable Faro Freestyle3D handheld structured-light 3D scanner (SLS), commonly used for industrial purposes. The small dimensions and weight (< 2 kg) of the equipment enabled the acquisition of high-resolution point clouds in rock shelters and rugged rock walls characterized by corners with limited visibility. The acquired structured-light point cloud served as a reference to constrain the accuracy of the photogrammetric work. A calibration prior to documentation was carried out to ensure the best results, taking into account parameters such as illumination, color textures, temperature and humidity of the scanned area as well as the variations in reﬂectance provided by different rock types. In addition to portability, SLS scanning shows several advantages to laser scanners: (i) increase accuracy due to the multiple surface readings; (ii) higher scanning speed (about 1scan/second); and (iii) safety. On the other hand, SLS are sensible to lighting conditions, which eventually can make difﬁcult the scanning under non-appropriate conditions (strong and direct sunlight or shiny surfaces, for example). We performed 6 individual handheld scans for each studied surface resulting in point clouds formed by approx. 109 points when merged together. The information includes the relative position and the surface colour at each point. The 3D point cloud accuracy obtained with the SLS was a millimetre when scanned at a distance less of 1 m; the maximum area covered by a single scan was 8 m2. The scans were performed within a distance from the object of ca. 0.30 to 0.60 m. The integrated memory-scan technology present in the used scanning device allowed us to perform repeated scans, and to use the location of initial low-resolution data for further rescanning and point cloud merging. Processing of point clouds was carried out using Faro ® software SCENE ®, point interpolation was performed during processing, enhancing the model resolution up to c. 0.2 mm. The portable scan data was further used to provide the local scaling of the 2D-3D photogrammetric restoration, adding a valuable high-resolution locally georeferenced point cloud for the subsequent digital models generation. 3.2. Photogrammetry The new Structure from Motion photogrammetry (SfM) range image approach [37], based on the acquisition of 3D information from correlation of 2D images, provides high-resolution photorealistic models by bundle adjustment of photographic images through the recognition of objects or pixel elements, by comparison of several images obtained at different angles. Therefore, SfM refers to the phenomenon by which observers can build 3D structures from the projected 2D motion ﬁeld of a moving object. This is a low cost, reliable and precise method for generation of dense point clouds and high-quality orthomosaics. During the last few years, photogrammetry works have been used for the documentation and preservation of rock art imagery, often combined with 3D data [16,38–40]. The accuracy and resolution of the photogrammetric works strongly depend on the geometry of the images and the physical characteristics of the sensor [41–43]. We used a digital SLR camera CanonEOS5DMarkIII with 20 megapixels (5760 × 3840 pixels) and CMOS sensor (50 mm). Several bull’s eyes were used to mark the position of reference points (control points) that aimed to constrain the point clouds accuracy by using the same reference points acquired with the portable white-light scanner. The photogrammetric point cloud accuracy was within millimetres (see Supplementary Material Quality Report). The camera was calibrated using a calibration module and a minimum image overlap of 80% from different perspectives. We used ImageMaster Calib. software by Topcom ® using 5 different focusing positions and the following control parameters: focal lens, radial distortion, tangential distortion and location of the principal components of the camera sensor. The criteria followed for a correct calibration Fig. 2. A. Standard photographic image of the lowermost part of Panel II and image treated for emphasizing red colours (B) according to processing by image enhancement tools following the procedure suggested by Haman [52]. C. Interpretation based on observations carried out on B. Five anthropomorphs and two sun-forms can be distinguished despite the presence of soot covering part of the panel. Another sun-form is observed in the upper left side of the panel (red motifs represent newly described motifs, see text for further explanation). J. Fernández-Lozano et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 26 (2017) 160–166 163 of the camera is based upon the assumption, that during image processing, point residuals are below 0.25 pixel. Afterwards, the camera calibration bundle adjustment of the images taken in the studied panels was performed using Agisoft Photoscan ®, obtaining point clouds, orthomosaics and digital models. 3.3. Image enhancement tools Digital imaging and enhancement of the studied rock art panels was performed using the RAW images obtained with the digital camera aforementioned. Thus, we ensured the high-quality and high-resolution of the enhanced images for the analysis and recognition of new motifs. Several images were merged into a single mosaic and the lens correction was carried out to dismiss the distortion effect. Final images were exported into a high-resolution .jpg ﬁle format for further treatment of the image. In the last 20 years, the analysis of principal components, K-means, Decorrelation Stretch in RGBN images, based on photometric stereo methods have provided reliable results in enhancing the visualization of low contrast or extremely faded characteristics in rock surfaces with archeological paintings [44–50]. The study of balance, curves, brightness and hue for the identiﬁcation of faded or deteriorated colours has become a widespread and accurate method for image enhancement [51] especially applicable to archaeological sciences. We used the image enhancement tools based on the decorrelation stretch algorithms implemented by Harman [52]. This method is based on principal component (PC) analysis and transformation of spectral information contained in the picture. The method provides contrast exaggeration by correlating the image channels displayed as R, G and B in order to expand the dark-light range of intensities by augmenting the colour saturation, independently of the light conditions [53]. Furthermore, in order to perform the decorrelation stretch procedure, we have used the shareware DStretch plug-in [52] implemented in JavaTM for ImageJTM, a freeware tool for processing and analysis ﬁrstly developed by the US Department of Health and Human Services in 1997 [54]. Using this method, the image enhancement artiﬁcially highlights different colours by selecting a colour space and using different colour ﬁeld separation increase or reduction —i.e. ﬁltering different RGB bands—. The software computes intensity variations, adjusting hue and saturation by using different provided command matrices (YDS, YBR, YBK, CRGB, LDS, LAB, LRE and RGB for red and black colours used in paints). The image improvement resulted into sharpening the recognised motifs and brining out different elements Fig. 3. Upper left part of Panel II. A. Standard photographic image of the complete panel. B. Colour enhanced image showing the location of motifs, and recent damages such as grafﬁti, bird’s droppings or lichens. The area is strongly affected by recent ﬁres, almost destroying some ﬁgures that can still be barely noticed. C. Interpretation of the panel showing the presence of four anthropomorphs, a sun-form, and a circle (see text for further explanation). 164 J. Fernández-Lozano et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 26 (2017) 160–166 not observable upon direct eye observation of the studied panels. 4. Results The Castrocontrigo rock art comprises three panels characterised by the presence of anthropomorphs and, in two of them, sun-forms. Their characteristics, style, and state of conservation are variable and the nature and size of the motifs seem to be inﬂuenced by the location of the outcrops. We describe the results of three different panels; the ﬁrst one is located at the Pe˜na del Pozo de Rocebros, and the two others, in the same nearby shelter at the Cerro de Llamaluenga, 350 m to the NE of the ﬁrst one. Ongoing research on the Castrocontrigo rock art showings is also described in CadiernoGuerra [31]. All the studied panels take advantage of the occurrence of fractures, delamination and rock roughness, which also constrain the distribution of the compositional elements, providing volumetric feeling. The new observations in the three panels provide new insights into the quantity and quality of the drawings and aims at the enhancement of previous descriptions and their future preservation. Digital 3D models of the studied shelters depicting the geometry of the surfaces with merged colour enhanced images can be found in the Supplementary Material Panels. 4.1. Panel I According to the information deposited at the “Catálogo de bienes protegidos” (Junta de Castilla y León), these drawings were ﬁrst discovered by Luis Crespo Cenador in 2000 in the core of a small anticline. The site was declared Site of Cultural Interest under the Spanish regulations (B.O.E., 155, 29/06/1985) and is described as including ﬁve male anthropomorphic representations together with one circular-shaped form, all of them drawn in red colour [55]. Two of the ﬁgures are attached to each other while the others appeared separated (Fig. 1A and B). Also, it is worth to notice that the central ﬁgure carries what we interpreted as a shield on the right hand, previously described as a weapon [31] or just another human ﬁgure by Ollero-Cuesta [55] (Fig. 1). The observation and analysis through image enhancement has allowed the identiﬁcation of new motifs in this panel. On the left side, three more partially erased ﬁgures can be recognised (Fig. 1A, B and E). The presence of oxides and calcite coatings has obscured the paints, and the delamination processes, likely due to thermal gradients (e.g. during wildﬁres, day-night temperature variations, etc.), has affected the drawings, favoring the destruction of portions of the panel (Fig. 1C–E). The presence of water runoff has also bleached part of the right side portion of the panel possibly destroying other existing ﬁgures (Fig. 1C–E). On the right side of the panel, at least, two more new ﬁgures can be identiﬁed. They also correspond to male human ﬁgures with the same size (10 and 15 cm). The thickness and roundness of the paint stroke suggest that the motifs were made by hand. A 3D representation of the panel can be found in the Supplementary Material Panels/Panel I. 4.2. Panels II and III Both panels II and III are located within the same shelter, at the so-called Cerro de la Llamaluenga and share the same discovery history than the nearby Panel I paintings [31,55]. The Fig. 4. A. Outcrop view showing the location of the polished fault plane comprising Panel III. B. Enhancement of red colours using image enhancement tools. C. Interpretation of the panel showing the presence of a single anthropomorph (see text for further explanation). J. Fernández-Lozano et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 26 (2017) 160–166 165 description of both panels will be done separately in the following paragraphs. In Panel II, drawings appear on a polished fault plane within the northern limb of a minor anticline with its core eroded and used as a tepee-shaped shelter. In the central part of the panel representations consist of three human ﬁgures (two of them identiﬁed as males, phy-type) in the upper part and a sun-form as described by Cadierno-Guerra [31]. The digital treatment by image enhancement carried out in this study has revealed new paints. In particular, two faded anthropomorphs and another sun-form (Fig. 2). In the upper left side of the panel, the colour digital enhancement has revealed new, non-previously documented four ﬁgures and a sun-form (Fig. 3 and Supplementary Material Panels/Panel II). They are poorly preserved, but human shapes and the sun-form are still recognizable. A red circular motif is also observed, but it is difﬁcult to determine whether it is natural or rock art. The motifs’ stroke in Panel II is thinner when compared with Panel I, indicating that they were likely performed with an object used as a paintbrush. The size of the antrophomorphs and the sunforms shows more variability (10–20 cm) than in Panel I. They were painted in red-brownish colours. The outcrop has been strongly damaged by scaling and delamination caused by thermal gradient, strongly increased during recent wildﬁres, biological destruction by lichens and bird droppings, human activity and water runoff that has covered large portions of the panels with Fe-oxides (Fig. 3). Panel III is located in another surface in the same shelter than the previous Panel II, oriented aproximately normal to it. It also takes advantage of a polished fault surface affected by the same damage that Panel II. In this panel, only one motif has been identiﬁed, which represents a phy-shaped anthropomorph (∼10 cm size male) painted in red (Fig. 4). The thickness of the stroke is similar to Panel I ﬁgures, suggesting that was carried out using the hands. A 3D representation is shown in the Supplementary Material Panels/Panel III. 5. Conclusions The Castrocontrigo schematic rock art in northwest Spain comprises drawings located in small shelters. The geomatic and digital methods used in this work has allowed the recognition of new schematic motifs located in small shelters in the area of Castrocontrigo (NW Spain). The SLS technology has been proven as an extremely useful tool for documentation of rock art located in inaccessible sites, providing point clouds that compete in resolution with less portable laser scans. Within the studied panels, the 3D documentation and image enhancement analysis has improved the characterization and study of the motifs, providing useful information about the existing conservation problems. Moreover, the acquisition of photorealistic 3D digital models ensures a better visualization and preserves useful information from possible destruction of the panels. title: A simple methodology for recording petroglyphs using low-cost digital image correlation photogrammetry and consumer-grade digital cameras
abstract: In the present study a simple method is described for obtaining 3D digital models of petroglyphs of different sizes and characteristics and its usefulness, in the study of this cultural heritage, is evaluated. For this research we used low-cost photogrammetric software and consumer-grade digital cameras, without any other surveying techniques. In order to test this methodology, three ﬁeld research studies were carried out in Galicia (Spain). By means of the proposed methodology, 3D digital models are obtained using basic equipment and its accuracy can be evaluated easily with the help of a calliper. Given the possibilities of analysis that they offer, 3D digital models can substantially assist in the study, comprehension and interpretation of petroglyphs by archaeologists, conservationists and rock-site managers. Low-cost digital image correlation photogrammetry is a simple and very versatile technique in its application to the documentation, analysis and diffusion of this cultural heritage. As has occurred with other 3D modelling techniques, it not only complements traditional techniques but can indeed substitute them, owing fundamentally to its simplicity and the interactive character of the 3D digital models, that can easily be inserted in the web.  2010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

full_text: A simple methodology for recording petroglyphs using low-cost digital image correlation photogrammetry and consumer-grade digital cameras Juan Ortiz Sanz a,*, Maria de la Luz Gil Docampo a, Santiago Martínez Rodríguez a, María Teresa Rego Sanmartín a, Gonzalo Meijide Cameselle b a Agricultural and Forestry Engineering Department, University of Santiago de Compostela, Benigno Ledo, Campus Universitario, 27002 Lugo, Spain b Department of Culture and Tourism, Autonomous Government of Galicia, Ronda da muralla 70, 27003 Lugo, Spain a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received 5 May 2010 Received in revised form 16 July 2010 Accepted 16 July 2010 Keywords: Close-range photogrammetry Digital image correlation Petroglyph Consumer-grade digital cameras 3D modelling a b s t r a c t In the present study a simple method is described for obtaining 3D digital models of petroglyphs of different sizes and characteristics and its usefulness, in the study of this cultural heritage, is evaluated. For this research we used low-cost photogrammetric software and consumer-grade digital cameras, without any other surveying techniques. In order to test this methodology, three ﬁeld research studies were carried out in Galicia (Spain). By means of the proposed methodology, 3D digital models are obtained using basic equipment and its accuracy can be evaluated easily with the help of a calliper. Given the possibilities of analysis that they offer, 3D digital models can substantially assist in the study, comprehension and interpretation of petroglyphs by archaeologists, conservationists and rock-site managers. Low-cost digital image correlation photogrammetry is a simple and very versatile technique in its application to the documentation, analysis and diffusion of this cultural heritage. As has occurred with other 3D modelling techniques, it not only complements traditional techniques but can indeed substitute them, owing fundamentally to its simplicity and the interactive character of the 3D digital models, that can easily be inserted in the web.  2010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Petroglyphs are engraved features where rock has been scraped or pecked away in a subtractive process (Rosenfeld, 1988) and are undeniable documents of world history. They have been found world-wide and are associated with ancient civilizations representing one of the few physical remaining fragments that are directly accessible. Archaeology has traditionally used three methods to document rock art graphically: freehand drawing, rock rubbing (Fig. 1) and photography (Stanbury and Clegg, 1990). The documentation of cultural heritage employing digital techniques aimed at obtaining 3D models of high photo-realistic quality is now considered a necessary and vital practise in order to provide a complete monitoring, protection and maintenance plan (Al-kheder et al., 2009). These techniques do not have any of the limitations associated with previous ones, which were limited in accuracy or were too time consuming. They present great potential in recording rock art (Díaz-Andreu et al., 2006; Chandler et al., 2007). Nowadays, the most common techniques used for threedimensional (3D) modelling are based on images (including photogrammetry) and range data (structured light, coded light or laser light). Some authors combine both techniques together to enhance qualities of each one, achieving interesting results when applied to cultural heritage. Examples of this are Dontelo’s Madalena (Guidi et al., 2004), the desert palaces in Jordan (Al-kheder et al., 2009) and the Dolmabahce Palace in Istanbul (Yastikli, 2007). Both approaches produce high quality images, although range-based methods (Beraldin et al., 2000; Blais, 2004), based on active sensors that directly record geometric 3D information of an object, remain bulky and expensive and are not easy to operate (El-Hakim et al., 2005). The paper presents the following structure. Following this introduction, some Digital Image Correlation techniques for 3D modelling are brieﬂy reported and discussed in Section 2. Section 3 describes in detail the low-cost photo-scanner methodology employed for the recording, modelling and virtual visualization of objects. Section 4 describes the petroglyphs used and the experimental application carried out in each particular case, while * Corresponding author. E-mail address: juan.ortiz@usc.es (J. Ortiz Sanz). Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Journal of Archaeological Science journal homepage: http://www.elsevier.com/locate/jas 0305-4403/$ e see front matter  2010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. doi:10.1016/j.jas.2010.07.017 Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 different considerations of the proposed process are discussed in Section 5. The ﬁnal section outlines some conclusions. 2. Close-range digital image correlation photogrammetry for 3D modelling 3D image-based methods (Remondino and El-Hakim, 2006) are widely used to record cultural heritage (Patias, 2007), archaeological surveying (McPherron et al., 2009), terrain and cities (Gruen, 2000), monuments and statues (Visnovcova et al., 2001) and rock art (Atkinson, 1968; Rivett, 1979; Ogleby and Rivett, 1985; Ogleby, 1999). For some years now, 3D image-based techniques that combine close-range photogrammetry and digital image correlation (DIC) have attracted a great deal of interest. This technique has been employed in the study of river beds (Chandler et al., 2003), rock art (Fryer et al., 2005; Chandler et al., 2007); buildings (Desmond and Bryan, 2003; Remondino and Campana, 2007), geosciencies (Smith et al., 2009) and microtography (Abd Elbasit et al., 2009). DIC is a technique for recovering the 3D structure of a scene from two different viewpoints (Fig. 2) where P(X, Y, Z) is the 3D point to be measured, p(u1, v1) and p(u2, v2) are its stereo projections in the images and C1 and C2 are the optical centres of the two cameras (Orteu, 2008). From a pair of oriented images, it is possible to compute the 3D coordinates of a physical 3D point by triangulation assuming that the two image points p1 and p2 are matched, i.e. identiﬁed as corresponding to the same physical point P. This is called the stereo-matching problem (Orteu, 2008). The measurement can be performed with manual or automated procedures (Remondino and El-Hakim, 2006). The main difﬁculty in stereovision is to establish correspondences between pairs of images. Over the years, numerous algorithms for image matching have been proposed. A description of these algorithms can be found in Orteu (2008) and in Remondino and El-Hakim (2006). These methods can automatically produce very dense 3D coloured point clouds on the surface of the studied object i.e. the 3D coordinates of up to several points per mm2, and each one of them with chromatic information (RGB). In the literature, comparisons between image-based methods and range-based methods are often reported. Some authors predict a very promising future for image-based methods, fundamentally because of their cost, ease of use, portability, accuracy and resolution. It is possible to generate digital elevation models (DEMs) to sub-millimetre accuracies at a camera-to-object distance of 1 m. Resolution remains important and it is perhaps prudent to suggest that a 5 megapixel camera currently provides a realistic resolution entry point for routine surface measurement (Chandler et al., 2005). Data acquisition using cheap digital camera technology can be taught comparatively easily and modern digital photogrammetric software is easier to use than many people may imagine, particularly for routine tasks (Chandler et al., 2007). DIC is considered by some authors as the only modern surveying technique which can approach the speed of data capture of the laser scanner. Rates of approximately 1000 points per minute are possible and the accuracy is similar (Fryer et al., 2005). Range-based methods often present serious problems of practicality in some archaeological sites, owing to their high cost, transport difﬁculty, time-consumption and the complexity of the related data management (Remondino and Campana, 2007). As well as the advantages previously discussed, this photogrammetric procedure allows more ﬂexibility during ﬁeldwork and does not require the physical stability of other equipment. An added beneﬁt is that the photographic images required for orthophoto generation are the same as those used to create the DEM. In contrast, problems may arise when trying to match digital camera images with a laser-scanned DEM to produce the textured surface. It can be a very complicated procedure to combine data from different sources and lead to inaccuracies (Fryer et al., 2005). There is no doubt that the 3D point clouds obtained by means of other techniques such as laser scanner are denser, more precise and more rapidly recovered in the ﬁeld than is possible with photogrammetric methods. Nevertheless, this does not mean that this technique is the most suitable in all situations if we consider certain aspects referred to by El-Hakim et al. (2005) and Fryer et al. (2005) such as cost, ease of use, portability, reﬂectance and power. Fig. 2. Binocular stereovision. Fig. 1. Rubbing of engravings of “Pena de Chaos” petroglyph, Galicia (Spain); published in Ref. A Ulloa en fotos. J. Ortiz Sanz / Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 3159 Disadvantages arise when the surface of a scene is of a uniform nature and does not possess a surface texture suited to this process because of the difﬁculty or even impossibility, of ﬁnding suitable points for DIC (e. g. a surface with uniform patches of colour will not permit image correlation) (Fryer et al., 2005). Nevertheless a speckle pattern can be applied by means of adhesives or painting which allows modelling of any type of surface (Lecompte et al., 2006). It may also be possible to increase the contrast of the imagery, through a combination of closer shots, imaging with lower illumination angles to introduce shadow and therefore contrast, or the use of a speciﬁc channel of the image (Smith et al., 2009). This limitation does not, initially, raise any problems for rock art, as the rock composition presents, in general, a suitable texture. The disadvantages of using image correlation routines include the need for some control points in order to determine the orientation of the camera stations (these typically require a total station to record the XYZ coordinates of some clearly deﬁnable spectral features, artiﬁcial or natural). Such control points are also required if multiple laser scans have to be overlapped (Fryer et al., 2005). Different digital photogrammetric workstations exist that are capable of generating dense surface point clouds from overlapping stereoscopic pairs of photographs but, as the image correlation software was designed and developed for large aerial survey mapping projects, it is quite specialized for a small market and consequently expensive (Fryer et al., 2005). However, nowadays, there are close-range and low-cost DIC based commercial software packages such as Photomodeler Scanner (2009) (PMS) (Eos Systems Inc., Vancouver, Canada) or Image Master (Topcon Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) which price, less than 3000 V, is about 90% less than the price of a digital photogrammetric workstation. This software combined with consumer-grade digital cameras, constitutes a technique that we will call a low-cost photo-scanner (LCPS) and that is already applied successfully in different ﬁelds such as biology (Ylenia et al., 2008) or cultural heritage (Alby et al., 2009). This process has already been applied in the UK on a large-scale rock-art recording project (2004) e the Northumberland & Durham Rock-Art Project (The NADRAP). This 3 year project resulted in the recording of the “England Rock-Art” (ERA) database and website creation. It provides on-line access to a variety of data for over 600 panels which was photogrammetrically recorded across both counties involved, using 70 non-specialist volunteers. This paper outlines an experiment for the photogrammetric generation of DEMs as input to petroglyph studies and for the recording and diffusion of such cultural heritage information. For this research, we used low-cost photogrammetric software and consumer-grade digital cameras, without any need for other complementary surveying techniques. Three test ﬁeldworks were undertaken in Galicia (Spain), in order to test the effectiveness of this technique for providing low-cost, high resolution data, with this new commercial photogrammetric software. 3. Methodology The equipment used in the present study comprises PMS software, version 6.3.3, a reﬂex Canon EOS 400D digital camera with a resolution of 10.1 Megapixels (3888  2592 pixels), a focal length of 20 mm, a sensor size of 22.2 14.8 (mm) and a Canon EF20 mm 1:2.8 objective. The pixel size is 5.71 5.71 microns and its focal length and 35 mm equivalent (mm) are 20/32.5 respectively. As additional metric instruments, a measure tape Class II (II DIRECTIVE 2004/22/EC), a millimetre ruler and a calliper (Accuracy: 0.05 mm; Graduation: 0.05 mm) are used. A printer, a folding ladder, a tripod, a computer, a small board and the software of the camera (Digital Photo Professional. Version 3.1.0.0. CANON Inc.) complete the necessary equipment to develop the simple methodology proposed. The overall image-based 3D modelling process involves established procedures: design, 3D measurements, structuring and modelling, texturing and visualization (Remondino and El-Hakim, 2006). A ﬁfth step is added: assessing the accuracy of the photogrammetrically acquired 3D model (Fig. 3). The proposed methodology to obtain the 3D digital models of petroglyphs is described as following. 3.1. Design The design stage involves the following procedures:  Camera calibration: The objective of camera calibration is to resolve the interior parameters of the camera, a process that is also known as internal orientation. To be able to carry it out, a total of 12 photos were taken, following some simple criteria deﬁned by the software manufacturer, on a calibration sheet printed on a simple consumer-grade printer (Fig. 4). These 12 photos are imported by the software and the internal parameters of the camera are automatically determined. It is necessary to take the 12 photos of the calibration with the focus ring adjusted to the same point where the photos of the petroglyphs will be taken. The parameters of calibration are ﬁled and the software will use them, with the camera so adjusted, in all future projects. The Camera calibration is the most important step in the entire 3D modelling process, because it underpins the accuracy achievable by the photogrammetric process and performance. This step also determines how a potential user might apply photogrammetry within their own recording projects.  Positioning of targets: The targets are elements that make the position of the point stand out and that provide easily recognizable information for the photogrammetric software. They also considerably increase the precision of the project which means it is more effective when modelling petroglyphs. PMS is capable of identifying and automatically recognizing these coded targets which were previously introduced to the photo shot at the scene. The targets are generated by the same program depending on the type of camera and the distance from the object, and it is only necessary to print and secure them around the element that Fig. 3. Low-cost methodology for rock-art recording. J. Ortiz Sanz / Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 3160 is to be modelled. Removable putty can help secure the targets directly on the petroglyphs or on objects introduced at the scene.  Image acquisition: To achieve the digital model of each petrolgyph it is only necessary to obtain a stereo pair with parallel (<30) optical axes, with image plane parallel to the approximate plane of the object. The base-to-height ratio (distance between camera and object divided by separation between photo pair) must be between 0.1 and 0.5. This photogrammetric software also allows the use of additional oblique images to better determine the relative orientation of the stereo pair. This last aspect is crucial to achieving a good dense point cloud by DIC means, given that this is only generated from one stereo pair.  Image pre-processing: The photographs are taken preferably in RAW format to avoid alterations by compression that may affect the ﬁnal model. This extension of image is not recognized by PMS and it is necessary to convert the images to another format. Although the software is compatible with more common formats (TGA, TIF, GIF, PCX, CAL, DCX, BMP, DIB, JPG, WMF, WPG, MAC, PCT, PNG, PPM, PSD and IFF), the most recommendable for quality is the use of TIF format. Once modiﬁed, the photos which are selected, are those which will be used to generate the DEM.  Calibration ﬁle assignment: Prior to the target marking and referencing, it is necessary to indicate the program the photographic camera used and how it was adjusted.  Targets marking and referencing: To achieve the external orientation of all the images in the project, it is necessary to inform the program about which points of each photograph correspond to those of the other photograph (homologues or common). Software allows the automatic detection of homologous points in all images by including a redundant set of coded targets in the scene.  Exterior orientation: This procedure, also automatic, can determine the position and angle of the camera with respect to the object when the image was taken. These are the coordinates of the camera projection centre (Xc, Yc, Zc) and three rotation angles around the X (pitch or x), Y (roll or y) and Z (yaw or j) axes. It is not necessary to determine the coordinates of the said points to obtain the exterior orientation of each image so no other technique is required to obtain the 3D model.  Scale, translate and rotate: It is possible to translate and rotate the 3D digital model employing a tool in the same program. In other words, it is possible to situate the model within the system of coordinates and the place where required, without changing the scale or the orientation relative to images and the object. The scale of the model can be adjusted independently from its rotation and translation, assigning a distance measured between two known points of the model (generally two targets). It also possible to assign the scale of, translate and rotate the model simultaneously, positioning manually the coordinates of three or more control points that have been determined previously by means of another suitable technique. Fig. 4. Camera calibration: images used and PMS’s calibration process. Fig. 5. Details of dense point cloud (a), triangulation (b), shaded (c) and textured (d) model of a petroglyph. J. Ortiz Sanz / Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 3161 3.2. 3D measurements Once the images are oriented, the surface 3D measurement step is performed with an automated procedure, the “Create Dense Surface” tool, which matches areas of textural detail on overlapping stereoscopic pairs, to extract a dense matrix of point elevations to represent the morphology of the rock surface (Fig. 5a). This process deﬁnes the size of the mesh and depends on the level of realism that we want to achieve, affecting the ﬁnal number of points. 3.3. Structuring and modelling Once the point clouds are obtained, these can be transformed into a surface. Polygons are usually the most ﬂexible way to accurately represent the results of 3D measurements, providing an optimal surface description (Remondino and El-Hakim, 2006). This step involves data triangulation to derive a 3D triangular mesh (Fig. 5b). Fig. 6. Manual measuring of Z coordinate (Z ¼ R  D) in a petroglyph; where R represents the calliper measurement and D, the ruler thickness. Fig. 7. Location of three petroglyphs in northwestern Spain. J. Ortiz Sanz / Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 3162 3.4. Texturing and visualization After the creation of a polygonal mesh, the results are generally visualised in wire-frame form, or in shaded (Fig 5c) and/or textured mode (Fig. 5d). In texture mapping, colour images are mapped onto a 3D geometric surface. Knowing the parameters of the interior and exterior orientation of the images, the corresponding image coordinates are calculated for each vertex of a triangle on the 3D surface. Colour RGB values within the projected triangle are then attached to the surface (Remondino and El-Hakim, 2006). PMS automatically assigns the texture in function of certain parameters such as the perpendicularity or the closeness of each one of the triangles that form the mesh, although it is also possible to choose them manually considering other viewpoints that the program is not able to recognize, such as luminous quality or focus, that has an effect on the visual aspect of the ﬁnal model. 3.5. Accuracy assessment It is important to know to what extent the dimensions of the model correspond to reality and how accuracy assessment should be carried out. The quality of the measurements done with PMS can be determined with a simple test (Fig. 6). A ruler is placed between two points marked with targets, so that the origin of the numeration of the ruler corresponds with one of the targets. Following this, the depth of the surface of the petrolgyph is measured with the calliper with respect to the line that joins the two points. Measurements can be made, at most, each millimetre of the ruler, depending on the number of checkpoints that are required for the evaluation. In the 3D model we can measure the same distances for each check point. Standard deviation, minimum and maximum values of errors and relative errors, with regard to the diagonal of the scene, can be then reported. 4. Experimental applications The petroglyphs tested are located in Galicia (Spain) (Fig. 7). The ﬁrst two (the “Pena de Chao” and Gurgulla petroglyphs), form part of a group of rock art situated in “Sierra de Farelo” and the third (the Formigueiros petroglyph) has the same name as the settlement in which it was found. There is a high concentration of petroglyphs on the west slope of the Sierra del Farelo, and thirty stations have now been discovered. It is by far, the richest area for petroglyphs in the province of Lugo, and it is the most important group in central Galicia, far away from the traditional areas of rock art in the proximities of the west coast. The most characteristic group belongs to the Bronze Age, which converts the area into an important cultural heritage landmark for Galicia and Europe. Many of the engravings of the Sierra del Farelo belong to this period and include a wide range of concentric circles, Fig. 8. Petroglyphs of “Pena de Chaos”(a), Gurgulla (b) and Formigueiros (c). Fig. 9. Photographs used for modelling “Pena de Chaos” (a), Gurgulla (b) and Formigueiros (c) petroglyphs. J. Ortiz Sanz / Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 3163 complex circular combinations, labyrinths and cup marks. Nearly all the petroglyphs are found in the low area of the mountain not far from the valley and the fertile lands. The “Pena de Chaos” Petroglyph: The “Pena de Chaos” petroglyph (Fig. 8a) (UTM: 588006, 4735561) is one of the ﬁrst to be identiﬁed in this territory (1980s). It is decorated with a profusion of circular combinations, with quite a complex central group. It is possibly the best preserved petrolgyph in the area, with deep and wide grooves. To get the petroglyph to ﬁll the biggest possible part of the photogram and so as to maximize the resolution of the equipment, it was necessary to take the photo with the camera focus ring adjusted to inﬁnite (Fig. 9a). Targets were temporarily attached to non-engraved sections of the rock surface using a reusable puttylike pressure sensitive adhesive, which can be easily removed after the photo shots without damaging the petroglyph. The photos were taken with a handheld camera and a folding ladder was employed. Table 1 shows the data of the images of the stereo pair in this experiment. The model of this petrolgyph is elaborated with a stereo pair and 44 oblique photos. Calibration ﬁle assignment, image processing and scale of the model were done by means of a distance obtained between two targets with measure tape. After this, the 3D digital model was rotated so that the surface of the petroglyph was approximately parallel to the XY plane. The 3D measurement, structuring and modelling steps resulted in a cloud of 800,000 points and a surface which consisted of 1,100,000 triangles. After texturing the ﬁnal model could be obtained (Fig. 10a). In this petroglyph, only the ﬁrst four steps of the methodology were followed. The Gurgulla Petroglyph: The Gurgulla petroglyph (Figs.1 and 8a) (UTM: 588310, 4733035) engravings were quite eroded. The petroglyph studied extends over a granite surface of approximately 1m2 and its longest diagonal is approximately 1000 mm. The average depth of the grooves is 5 mm, that is 1:200 referring to the diagonal of the petroglyph The advanced state of erosion and the small depth of the groove in this petroglyph, made its modelling more difﬁcult than the example in the previous study. Given that the dimensions of this petroglyph are similar to those in the previous study, it was also photographed with the camera focused to inﬁnity (Fig. 9b), so it was not necessary to adjust the camera calibration procedure. The targets were located in the same way as the previous petroglyph. The photos, in this case, were also taken with a hand camera, placed on top of a solid transport equipment case, 40 cm high. The model of this petroglyph was elaborated with a stereo pair (Table 1) and 18 oblique photos. After completing the design and carrying out the 3D measurement step, a cloud of 600,000 points was obtained. This comprised of 750,000 triangles following the structuring and modelling step. Applying the texture completed the ﬁnal model (Fig. 10b). The accuracy assessment of the 3D model was evaluated by 14 checkpoints on the surface of the petroglyph. Table 2 and Fig. 11 show the results obtained at this stage. The Formigueiros petroglyph: The settlement of Formigueiros is a fortiﬁed village, belonging to the castreña culture and characteristic of the iron Age in the northwest of the Iberian Peninsula. It is situated between two important megalithic necropolises and belongs to the populous of the Seurri. Several excavation digs were carried out here, in which voluntary workers from different countries took part. Its most distinctive trait is the powerful and complex defence system with a stone wall that reaches a height of six meters and a variable width of around 4 meters, complemented by a labyrinth framed by cavities and parapets. Its origins belong to the III century BC and it was abandoned in the Ist century AD, decades after the ﬁnal conquest of the territory by Augusto. Some Tiberian coins and several fragments of terra sigillata were uncovered and, among other indications, contributed to the dating. Centuries after, the village was inhabited again although we have hardly any information about this time. Another characteristic described is the presence of interesting Table 1 Acquired image and pixel geometry achieved during main tests. Petroglyph Camera height (mm) Approx. image scale Pixel size on petroglyph plane (mm) “Pena de Chaos” 2000 1:100 0.57 Gurgulla 1200 1:60 0.34 Formigueiros 400 1:20 0.11 Fig. 10. Textured model of the “Pena de Chaos” (a), Gurgulla (b) and Formigueiros (c) petroglyphs. Table 2 Descriptive statistical terms to test errors in coordinate Z of 3D model of the Gurgulla petroglyph. 14 checkpoints. Error (mm) Relative error Standard deviation (mm) 1.08 1:925 Minimum value (mm) 2.08 1:480 Maximum value (mm) 1.77 1:564 J. Ortiz Sanz / Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 3164 domestic architecture. The material used for the construction of walls was slate, which is also used for the rooves, tiles of the patios and the streets. The most unexpected thing was the discovery of, an up to now, inedited artistic manifestation in the castreña culture: a wide range of engravings on slate in diverse contexts that represent horses, ﬁsh, board games, circles, triangles and labyrinths (classical seven and eleven circuit labyrinth). Four cases of the last type are documented, a surprising fact as it is the ﬁrst time it has been found in a village belonging to the iron age period. The most surprising of the four is the little engraved object in this study. It is certainly an example of the oldest labyrinth of eleven circuits registered, up to now, in Europe (Fig. 8c). It occupies a surface of 0.2 m2, its longest diagonal is 500 mm and the average depth of the groove is 0.5 mm, i.e., 1:1000 with respect to the diagonal of the petroglyph. In this case it was necessary to take the photos closer up compared to the previous petroglyph (Fig. 9c), so it was carried out with another camera calibration procedure with the focus ring set to 50 cm. Its size also allows the preparation of a mesh of 404 automated coded targets (printed on self adhesive paper) which was placed on a thick ﬂat wooden board. The model of this petroglyph was elaborated with a stereo pair and thirty oblique photos, all of which were taken with the help of a tripod. So in this case, the calibration ﬁle used was that of the camera focused at 50 cm. The Scaling, translation and rotation of this petroglyph were done simultaneously, providing the program with the coordinates X,Y,Z of 50 control points, randomly selected among all the coded targets of the mesh. The coordinate Z ¼ 0 was assigned to all the control points, that were put on the board of 3 cm of thickness. The coordinates X and Y were determined by means of a calliper. In this way, the origin of coordinates were moved to the board and rotated so that the board coincided with the XY plane. As the plane of the petrolgyph is parallel to the board, it was possible to get the Z-axis to be perpendicular to the plane of the petrolgyph. The cloud obtained in this case at the 3D measurement step was of 550,000 points (5a) and the corresponding surface, once structured and modelled, was formed by nearly 700,000 triangles (5b). The ﬁnal model was obtained after the texturing stage (Fig. 10c). Accuracy assessment of the Formigueiros petroglyph involved the use of 35 checkpoints. Table 3 and Fig. 12 show the results obtained at this stage. 5. Technical discussion In this section different considerations of the proposed process are discussed. In order to evaluate the success of the project output, it is necessary to review simplicity/cost, accuracy of the photogrammetry and validity/utility of the 3D model output for analysis of petroglyphs. These three attributes are required so that methodology can be used in a general way for quantitative and qualitative petroglyph studies. The methodology proposed is presented as a suitable for use by staff with a basic knowledge of computer technology and photography. In relation to ﬁeldwork, once the targets are positioned, taking around 10e15 minutes per petroglyph, the operations of downloading images from the computer and changing batteries require less than 10 min. This simple methodology, considering maximum mobility and portability, permits only one operator to manage the whole process and to achieve performances of 6e8 m2/h approximately (the greater the surface the more this increases). The cost of the software is signiﬁcant: less than 2700 $ in 2010. The technique allows point clouds of a wide range of sizes of objects to be obtained with only one piece of equipment; from an object the size of a coin to an extensive area of land of several hectares. This allows for a minimal investment in equipment. In relation to the study, counting on automatic coded targets means an enormous saving on time and allows us to work with a large number of photos. One hour of work is sufﬁcient to obtain each one of the models here presented, not including the time required for computer processing. The accuracy of the photogrammetric data, evaluated in the weaker Z direction, exceeded expectations. It was initially thought that the combination of the consumer-grade digital camera and low-cost software might degrade overall performance, but this was not the case. The accuracies of measured checkpoints were Fig. 11. Gurgulla petroglyph. Contrast between real section and the same model section. The dimension along the Z-axis has been magniﬁed by a factor of 5 for clarity.
