Error (mm) Relative error Standard deviation (mm) 0.26 1:1923 Minimum value (mm) 0.74 1:675 Maximum value (mm) 0.56 1:893 Fig. 12. Formigueiros petroglyph. Contrast between real and photogrammetric Z coordinates. The dimension along the Z-axis has been magniﬁed by a factor of 30 for clarity. J. Ortiz Sanz / Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 3165 excellent. All the statistical values were very low, implying potential for high accuracy data acquisition in all cases. A more detailed analysis of the obtained values reveals a better standard deviation for the Formigueiros petroglyph than for the Gurgulla petroglyph. This could be due to the different techniques and material used for the scale in each case. The utilization of control points, in spite of allowing the generation of a better model, means an increase in ﬁeld and study work, which might not be advisable if this technique is intended to be applied to modelling on a big scale. The quality of the DEM is directly related to the location and density of extracted points. In our case, given the texture of the petroglyph, the point density is as high as it needs to be. The distribution of measured points is particularly good; in regions with rich texture, extracted points are regularly as close as 1:2500 with respect to the diagonal of the petroglyph, whereas in regions with poor texture, this only decreases to 1:1000. The vertical resolution also adequately reﬂected the relief of the petroglyphs that are extremely eroded. Nevertheless, in spite of using control points, areas of the Formigueiros petroglyph exist where it is possible to perceive the grooves whereas in other areas, they are impossible to observe. This could be due to the relative depth of the grooves in this case, which is much less in the Gurgulla petroglyph. Given that the photogrammetric network is completely symmetric, the relative depth of the groove of the Formigueiros petroglyph could be the limit of the vertical resolution of this technique with that camera. It is necessary to indicate, in this sense, that the set resolution is highly dependant on the texture of the object. It is therefore important to recognize that derived DEMs must be generated at a resolution and level of accuracy that is appropriate for the research questions being posed (Chandler et al., 2000). Perfect measurement, and by implication perfect surface representation, does not exist and this should be borne in mind when assessing the quality of surfaces and derived DEMs (Smith et al., 2009).We considered the level of acuracy to be more than sufﬁcient for our purposes. Many tools can be used to contribute to the analysis and diffusion of results.  The primary product to be obtained with this methodology is the high-resolution and photo-realistic 3D model (Fig. 10), which can be visualised through the PMS, but also by means of any 3D design commercial software prior to exportation to the most popular formats: 3DS (3D Studio), 3DM (Rhino), DXF (2D and 3D DXF), FBX (Filmbox), IGS (IGES), KMZ (Google Earth), MA (Maya script), MS (3D Studio Max script), OBJ (Wavefront), RAW (RAW XYZ coords.), STL (Stereo-lithography) and WRL (VRML 1 and 2). Most of these extensions correspond to programs with licences, although open source software also exists that allows the visualization of the models directly: 3DS or Fig. 13. 3D drawing of the bottom of the source (a) and high resolution textured orthophoto (b) of the Formigueiros petroglyph. Fig. 14. Shaded orthophotos of the “Pena de Chaos” petroglyph where Z coordinates has not been magniﬁed (a) and Z coordinates has been magniﬁed by a factor of 2 (b). J. Ortiz Sanz / Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 3166 Ani8tor; plug-ins such as VRML (VRML97 ISO/IEC 147721:1997)/X3D client (Web3D Consortium) that allows its visualization through the web. More information can be obtained on this topic in Koutsoudis et al. (2007). This can be particularly useful when sharing 3D information of petroglyphs with researchers and technicians or for the diffusion of this cultural heritage on the net. Line drawings similar to traditional rock-art representations can be provided by PMS. By manually editing the groove lines (erasing or changing colour).  The photogrammetric techniques allow 3D models to be obtained with high metric quality, so that, archaeologists can extract any metric measurements interactively directly from the model (Fig 13a), or from the petroglyph ortophoto (Fig. 13b). Both allow photogrammetric restitution.  The possibility of observing the shaded model, without texture, can be very useful as the model visualised as such displays the relief of the stone without the interference of colour in the analysis (Fig. 14a). Speciﬁc cross sections can assist in the study of the morphology of the engravings, without distortions originating from projecting non-planar 3D information in 2D views.  Another interesting tool relates to the magniﬁcation of the dimensions of the model in the perpendicular axes of the object (in this case Z axes),where areas of depression of the rock and the elevations separate themselves allowing the designed engravings to be more clearly distinguished (Fig. 14b).  Traditional documentation based on photographs and artiﬁcial in situ illumination, can prove to be a difﬁcult and expensive application in some cases, either because of the remoteness of the location, adverse weather conditions, the variation of illumination during the day or simply because the vegetation or the buildings shade the object. In these cases, the possibility of virtually simulating different types of illumination of the petroglyph would be very useful (Fig. 15). PMS allows the application of a virtual lateral light, and includes variations of colour and intensity. Most of the design programs possess many tools and functions that enhance this aspect (point light, in line light or several lights). The application of lateral light over the model allows for the generation of shades that highlight the drawing and this effect can be increased with the use of a model without textures i.e. shaded. It is possible, through georeferencing the model, to simulate the illumination that the Fig. 16. Contours (a) and coloured contours (b) of the “Pena de Chaos” petroglyph. Fig. 15. 3D model of “Pena de Chaos” petrolgyph: lighted from the right (a), from above (b), from the left (c) and lighted from below (d). J. Ortiz Sanz / Journal of Archaeological Science 37 (2010) 3158e3169 3167 object would receive from the sun or the moon at any time of the year. Light effects can also be applied that simulate bonﬁres were the effect of ﬁre on the engravings can be observed.  PMS offers the possibility of generating contour maps with respect to any axis and contour interval, allowing a different perspective that in this type of work that may be quite revealing (Fig. 16a). These contours can be, subsequently, exported to any CAD program and different actions such as colouring by height (Fig. 16b), by orientation or by sloping can be carried out. Traditional rock-art drawings do incorporate interpretational information in a very visual and graphical form, however they remain subjective (Fig. 1) and so can be challenged. If the rock is very ﬂat, line drawings can also be objectively provided by PMS, through editing the points placed under or above a ﬁxed contour.  Another option with a similar result to the former section consists of using inspection programs such as Rapidform (INUS Technology Inc., Seoul, South Korea) which is used for inverse engineering works. The dense point cloud is, ﬁrstly, exported to any of the formats compatible with the former one. This ﬁle is used by the aforementioned inspection program and through its multiple tools results can be obtained. Maps with each point of the petroglyph can be obtained corresponding to its depth and curved surface (Fig. 17). This technique may be interesting for the analysis of petroglyphs that are found on irregular or curved surfaces. As can be observed in the previous ﬁgure, line drawings can also be provided with the combined use of PMS and inspection software. This method is more objective, irrespective of whether the rock is curved or ﬂat, than traditional methods (Fig. 1) and the manual edition of the point clouds with PMS. 6. Conclusions This paper has been motivated by the need to design low-cost simple photogrammetric techniques to obtain 3D models of petroglyphs and is designed to demonstrate the potential for commercial photogrammetric software and consumer-grade digital cameras to elaborate such 3D models. A simple methodology was used to model petroglyphs at three study sites, testing whether such data was suitable for photogrammetric recording, analysis and diffusion. The methodology proved time-efﬁcient and very cost effective, yielding a successful 3D model with an elevation standard deviation error of 1 mm, which conﬁrms that high accuracy photogrammetric surveying is achievable. Indeed, separate point clouds can be easily obtained at 1:2000 referring to the diagonal of the object, which allows the appreciation of deep grooves of up to 1:1000 with respect to the diagonal of the object. By means of a simple procedure and a calliper, it is possible to acquire check data, which allows the easy estimation of metric quality of the ﬁnal model. The checked software is very well optimized for professional work and can be used on computers equipped with basic hardware, and although it tries to obtain high detail models, aspects such as the processor (CPU) and RAM memory must be powered. 3D digitalization methods for the documentation of rock engravings allow the registering of much more detailed and varied information than 2D traditional methodologies. It provides archaeologists, conservationists and rock-site managers with a wide range of virtual techniques, which if applied independently or in conjunction, can create a valuable tool for the analysis and the diffusion of this type of cultural heritage. The present work focuses on petroglyphs etched on almost ﬂat rocks, so that only one stereo pair is sufﬁcient to generate the point cloud. When the object shape is more complex, it might be necessary to obtain the 3D model using a higher number of stereo pairs and, therefore, further experimentation is needed. The technique could present limitations especially in dark places, such as caves, so that another challenge would be the application of the method in conditions of little light, requiring ﬂash and longer exposure times or. It would be interesting to experiment how the method could be developed with other equipment (software and/ or camera) and if it could be applied by staff without special photogrammetric knowledge. Finally, a comparative study of the other currently available low-cost photogrammetric software would be beneﬁcial. title: A step further in rock art digital enhancements. DStretch on Gigapixel imaging
abstract: Methodology for rock art documentation has signiﬁcantly improved in the last ten years, during which cutting edge technologies have contributed to achieving more accurate representation of pictographs. The plug-in DStretch is an important tool in that process, allowing the identiﬁcation of very poorly conserved pigments and an easier distinction of overlap in motifs. Likewise, the generation of high-resolution gigapixel images of the panels through stitching processes produces very high quality images, but also very large ﬁles, which can be unsuitable for work with DStretch. This article presents two alternative methods (A and B) for the enhancement with false colors of large gigapixel panorama ﬁles, based on the experience of the 4D⋅arte rupestre research team. Some improvements made in the latest versions of DStretch allow the easy creation of high-resolution enhanced panoramas conveniently for research purposes.

DStretch on Gigapixel imaging Elia Quesada a,*, Jonathan Harman b a Department of Geography and Territorial Sciences, University of Cordoba (UCO), Plaza Cardenal Salazar 3, 14071, Cordoba, Spain b DStretch.com, Paciﬁca, CA 94044 United States A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: DStretch Rock art Pictographs False color enhancements Gigapixel images Good practices A B S T R A C T Methodology for rock art documentation has signiﬁcantly improved in the last ten years, during which cutting edge technologies have contributed to achieving more accurate representation of pictographs. The plug-in DStretch is an important tool in that process, allowing the identiﬁcation of very poorly conserved pigments and an easier distinction of overlap in motifs. Likewise, the generation of high-resolution gigapixel images of the panels through stitching processes produces very high quality images, but also very large ﬁles, which can be unsuitable for work with DStretch. Some improvements made in the latest versions of DStretch allow the easy creation of high-resolution enhanced panoramas conveniently for research purposes. 1. Introduction Digital imaging has revolutionized rock art documentation. Undoubtedly a great step forward is that formerly invasive techniques for rock art recording have been replaced by non-invasive methods, such as the 3D modeling procedures or the high-resolution panoramas, which have solved previous issues with projections and distortions and greatly improved the quality and detail of the visualization (De Reu et al., 2013; Kersten and Lindstaedt, 2012; Lopez-Montalvo, 2010). Regarding the latter, the current implementation of gigapixel imaging produces large size panoramas of the painted panels, which are suitable for a precise analysis and study of iconography, superimpositions, and monitoring of the site degradation over time (Mark and Billo, 2011, 2012; Ruiz et al., 2016b). In this scenario, digital enhancements for rock art images have also been widely implemented (Cerrillo et al. 2013, 2015; Domingo et al., 2015; Fredlund et al. .2007; Gillespie, 1992; Hollmann and Crause, 2011; Rogerio-Candelera, 2015; Salerno et al., 2014). The application of digital algorithms now allows faded motifs to be distinguished and paint superimposition determined, as well as to monitor degradation suffered over time in a more or less automated way. Today there are many analysis tools for digital images, most of them based on principles from remote sensing, among which the DStretch plug-in of ImageJ has proven to be one of the most successful. This is due to its combination of efﬁciency, functionality and affordability. It provides advantages over other alternatives including utility during ﬁeldwork in areas with limited access (Evans and Mourad, 2018; Harman, 2005; Le Quellec et al., 2013, 2015; Lopez et al., 2016; Martínez Collado et al., 2013; Oktaviana, 2015; Quesada, 2010; Robert et al., 2016; Tomaskova, 2015). The Spanish research team 4D⋅arte rupestre has been implementing gigapixel panoramas using DStretch since 2013. This procedure takes part in a comprehensive protocol developed to record and monitor rock paintings over time with new technologies. Especially it has been applied in some rock art sites of the Mediterranean Basin on the Iberian Peninsula on the World HeritageList (Ruiz et al., 2016a,b). This paper presents the experience gained by this research group in the implementation of gigapixel images with DStretch. The challenge is to preserve the highest possible quality in the enhanced output panorama. Two workﬂows are compared (A and B) which, despite appearing very similar, result in nuanced color changes. Depending on the desired result each method allows more or less control for the researcher. * Corresponding author. E-mail address: z42qumae@uco.es (E. Quesada). DStretch on Gigapixel imaging, Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.daach.2019.e00098 2. Gigapixel image & DStretch 2.1. What is a Gigapixel image? Digitally stitching images together to form a larger image began in the late twentieth century, and its use spread quickly to the rock art documentation (Mark and Billo, 1999, 2011). Dr. Helmut Dersh released PanoTools software in 1998, a package with pre-compiled library, which provided a range of algorithms for stitching (enblend, enfuse), blending and re-projecting many types of panoramas. This was the framework for subsequent implementation of both commercial and open source stitching software, such as Hugin, Autostitch or Image Composite Editor regarding the former, or PTGui, Kolor Autopano and GigaPan Stitch for the later (Mark and Billo, 1999, 2012). By deﬁnition, a gigapixel image is compounded by a bit map of greater than 1,000,000,000 pixels. A gigapixel image in other words, is 1000 times larger than a 1 GB image (Ruiz et al., 2016b). These huge panoramas are the result of the pairing and overlapping of a large amount of lower resolution images. Current robotics heads replace the former nodal panoramic swivels and provide an easier method to obtain panoramas with arrays of hundred images, avoiding complex calculations and ensuring optimal overlap. A digital camera mounted to a robotic head makes a shooting displacement according to previously programmed parameters. The camera is rotated about the lens null point without parallax errors (Mark and Billo, 1999, 2006; Kopf et al., 2007) (Fig. 1a). Finally, the images are processed by speciﬁc computer vision software, which carries out the reconstruction to a continuous image in gigapixel resolution (Ruiz et al., 2016b). The assembling of the images is made via algorithms such as SIFT (Scale-invariant feature transform), which identify common local features between images. The result is an Ultra-high resolution image, which allows large zoom factors (Fig. 2). To perform gigapixel panoramas in 4D⋅arte rupestre projects, a robotic head GigaPan Epic Pro with a Canon EOS 7D camera have been used (Fig. 1b), as well as a variety of lens depending on the scene features (Canon EF 70–200 mm f/4 L IS USM, Canon EF 70–300 mm f/4–5.6 IS USM, Canon EF 100 mm f/2.8 macro USM and Canon EF 35 mm f/1.4 L USM). This is a versatile robotic head which allows to set a wide range of parameters such as time of focusing, multiples camera expositions to obtain HDR images, to program a shooting array or a 360 panorama, and so on. Every panorama requires setting the area to be covered with a starting point (left corner up) and with an ending point (right corner down), as well as to calibrate the ﬁeld of view of the scene depending on the lens and focal length used in each case. Also the overlapping must be previously conﬁgured, which is usually established between a 40–60%. It is recommendable not operate lower than 30% to ensure optimal results. Other recommendations are the camera must be set in a good depth of ﬁeld, focus should be consistent and natural lighting should be as stable as possible (if not using controlled lighting methods). Undoubtedly a solid tripod is mandatory to provide stability to the whole set (Mark and Billo, 2012; Ruiz et al., 2016b). Finally, as image color management is a constant procedure in these research projects a previous picture of the scene with a color chart Color Checker Passport is taken before the GigaPan shooting starts (Pereira Uzal, 2012; 2013; Ruiz et al., 2016b; Ruiz and Pereira, 2014). Fig. 1. (a) Displacement of a gigapixel robotic head (in: Mark and Billo, 1999). (b) GigaPan Epic Pro swivel used in the 4D⋅arte rupestre team ﬁeldwork. Shooting processed with ﬂoodlights this time. E. Quesada, J. Harman Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage xxx (xxxx) xxx 2 Different software options have been employed for processing. However Kolor Autopano Giga is the most commonly used by this team, since it provides an easy workﬂow and a high quality preview of the panoramas. A functional interface allows the user to select between several projection options (up to 10), edit other features such as the RGB levels or the calculation of an evaluating index of the stitching quality (RMS), which can be optimized (Fig. 3). A result is optimal when lower than 5 RMS. A bad assemblage can be caused, for instance, by parallax errors during shooting or the wrong selection of the nodal point of robotic head and lens. Rendering can take several hours depending on the panorama size. Resulting image can be saved in different format ﬁles such as.jpg, tiff,.psd or.psb among others, and color depth can vary between 8 and 32 bits. Looking for the best resolution of images, less compressed format as possible should be selected, however this must be evaluated depending on the ﬁnal use of panorama (Ruiz et al., 2016b). 2.2. Why Gigapixel? Given the broad range of new technologies available for rock art documentation, researchers must deﬁne in advance their strategies and make decisions depending on speciﬁc results desired (Brady et al., 2017). Gigapixel imagery is very useful tool to record large panels of rock art. This technique provides high detail appropriate for the study of conservation issues in paintings and rocky surfaces, for the identiﬁcation of motifs and compositions spread all over the wall as well as for mapping the position of physic-chemical samples, among other purposes (Mark and Billo, 2012; Le Quellec et al., 2015). However, stitching images are characterized by geometrical distortions and therefore should not be considered as a substitute for a more accurate 3D geometric documentation of the sites. In 4D⋅arte rupestre projects, gigapixel panoramas are a complementary tool for the study of iconography, superimpositions and preservation Fig. 2. Zoom detail of a gigapixel panorama. The priority here is obtaining high-resolution images that properly frame the paintings in relation to the panel and to the overall site, instead making large general compositions with lower detail. The topography of the shelter or cavity would determine the shooting distance between the camera and the panel as well as the appropriate lens to be use in each case. 2.3. DStretch challenges using Gigapixel images DStretch has become well known in the rock art research, where it has become an essential tool. It is a plugin to the imaging program ImageJ (Rasband, 1997–2018). It is based on the application of the decorrelation stretch algorithm to a color digital image, resulting in a false color output image (Alley Ronald, 1996; Soha and Schwartz, 1979). The fundamentals and instructions for its use have been provided by its developer (Harman, 2005, 2014) and other researchers, or by tutorials available online (Brady and Gunn, 2012; Gutierrez et al., 2009; Hein, 2015; Le Quellec et al., 2013; Mark and Billo, 2006 ; Martínez Collado et al., 2013 ; Quesada, 2010; Sefcik, 2012). Nevertheless, there is still some confusion about DStretch, which is often simply described as the application of the decorrelation stretch algorithm. This view misses the key fact that DStretch applies this algorithm on many different custom color spaces getting results that are optimized for the pigments found at typical rock art sites. There are over 20 color spaces built into the DStretch and the decorrelation stretch algorithm produces different results in each of them (Fig. 4). Moreover enhanced images can be further edited by a range of color adjustment tools, which allows modifying the vibrant colors of the enhancements or improving contrast, among other possibilities. Gigapixel images are panoramas that can be generated from more than 250 pictures each around 18-20 MB of resolution, which may result in ﬁles of approximately 3–10 GB (Ruiz et al., 2016b). ImageJ (and so also DStretch) has limitations to the size of the pictures that can be read, due to the use of 32bit signed integers in Java to index the points in an array. This translates to a limit of 2 Giga pixels or less per image. Generally, pictures about 1,6 GB can be more or less easily handled with DStretch if working with x64 version of ImageJ. This is, therefore, a serious disadvantage for enhancing a complete gigapixel panorama with the plugin, since an image small enough to get used in ImageJ would be greatly compressed and resolution would be lost from the original image. Furthermore, a signiﬁcant size reduction of the image involves degradation of the color information and may negatively affect the enhancements (Mark and Billo, 2002). However, it is desirable to keep the quality as high as possible for research purposes, even if it may be reduced for other targets, such as presentations or publications. To accomplish this DStretch Batch Mode can be used. The two methods (A and B) discussed in this paper are based in this routine as detailed below. 3. Operating with DStretch in Gigapixel imaging. Methods A and B There is no unique methodology to enhance a gigapixel image in DStretch and every researcher may develop a different one. The challenge for 4D⋅arte rupestre projects is to conserve as much as possible resolution in the output enhanced panorama that is similar to the original non-altered gigapixel image. Therefore, the option of reducing the size of the gigapixel image before processing with DStretch, is not addressed in this paper. Two workﬂows are compared (A and B), which despite appearing very similar, provide different results and can be adopted depending on the goal and the control the researcher desires over the ﬁnal results. The examples here are executed with graphical material generated by this research team in the Shelter of Ermites I (Ulldecona-Tarragona. Spain), during the ﬁeldwork carried out in January 2016 (see references for RdAR 4D VULL Repository link) (Ruiz et al., 2017) (Fig. 5). The same DStretch enhancements have been applied in all the following gigapixel images: YDT color space, with an intensity scale of 15 and a Hue Shift of 36. 3.1. Method A. DStretching every single picture This ﬁrst method A consists in enhancing each input image in the panorama prior to loading into the gigapixel software. This seems a priori more intuitive and faster. Harman Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage xxx (xxxx) xxx 5 DStretch contains a Batch Mode routine, which can apply an enhancement or series of enhancements to all the images in a folder (ImageJ->plugins->DStretch menu) (Fig. 6a). This feature of the plugin has been improved from its previous version by increasing the number of enhancements that can be applied in every batch of images, which facilitates the work with gigapixel images. An Input and an output folder must be selected before a user dialog displays for setting the batch enhancements (Fig. 6b). In the initial dialog up to four different enhancements can be chosen, though four more can be speciﬁed by checking the “More Enhancements?” checkbox (Fig. 6c). The enhancements will be performed in sequence and, ﬁnally the resulting images will be saved in the speciﬁed format (Fig. 6d). Thereby in method A, after applying Batch Mode in every single picture from a panorama, an output folder with a false color version of these images is generated. Then those enhanced images have to be compiled in the stitching software to create the ﬁnal enhanced gigapixel image (Fig. 7). In this method each image receives its own enhancement based on the image histogram. Although the DStretch algorithm creates a different result for each image, the same enhancement color space generally gives similar results for photos taken at the same site. However, sometimes a variation in background (especially the presence of green vegetation) can cause enhancements to differ. Thus the user cannot control the changes occurring in the whole gigapixel scene. To ensure similar results for all images of the panorama, a matrix ﬁle (.txt) can be saved from an enhancement made on a representative image (Fig. 8a). This ﬁle can be chosen as one of the enhancements in batch mode, which will then apply the matrix to all of the images in the input folder (Fig. 8b). However, the matrix ﬁle only saves color space enhancement information, such as the color space itself, the mean color value of the image selection, and the 3x3 matrix of numbers determining the enhancement. Any other steps performed before or after the color space enhancement, such as hue shift, brightness, ﬂattening or contrast enhancement must be duplicated in the batch mode settings. When a matrix is used in batch mode, the user has the option of using the mean calculated from the image or the mean stored at the time the matrix ﬁle is created (Fig. 8c). The most consistent results are obtained by using the mean stored in the matrix ﬁle. If this option is not checked, the mean for the current image will be used. Note that in some DStretch versions prior to 8.21 there was a bug in this feature. Moreover if selection tools were used to create the initial enhancement matrix, then the mean of the previously saved matrix should be used. Fig. 7. Autopano Giga Interface and method A routine. The previously enhanced images are uploaded (1) to the stitching process (2,3). Notice that no EXIF data is appearing. E. Quesada, J. Harman Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage xxx (xxxx) xxx 6 Generally, Method A can be used during ﬁeldwork to implement documentation procedures such as creating a sketch of the position of the pictographs, Identifying faded motifs, or to place physical-chemical samples (Le Quellec et al., 2013, 2015; Mark and Billo, 2012). Also this option provides more contrasted and deﬁned backgrounds in case other dynamic elements in the rocky surface are being studied, as presence of bioturbations or calcite washes (Rogerio-Candelera, 2016; Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2011) (Fig. 9a). Nevertheless, there are some disadvantages in assembling enhanced individual pictures in the stitching software. For example, DStretch does not save the metadata from the original image (Fig. 7). Although this still need to be tested in depth, it might have some consequences for the stitching results since these programs use EXIF data from the images to achieve optimal geometric transformations (Fig. 3). There are external tools helping in those cases, such as EXIFTool by Phil Harvey, although this could be a time-consuming process (Ruiz et al., 2016b). 3.2. Method B. Harman Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage xxx (xxxx) xxx 7 The second method (Method B) involves cropping large sections from the processed PSB ﬁle, enhancing those using DStretch, and then combining them back together using Photoshop. Method B is more timeconsuming, but it has provided better results for research targets in 4D⋅arte rupestre projects. The aim here is to achieve a better visualization of the pictographs in a gigapixel panel conceived as a whole scene, as well as to monitor the response of the different elements to the enhancements applied and the relation between them. This is more creative and interactive method where the researcher has more control over the ﬁnal results. 3.2.1. Selecting enhancements For this purpose, the enhancements should be previously determined from a low resolution version of the complete gigapixel image, and then they will be applied to sections of the original panorama in Batch Mode: a) Create the gigapixel panorama by conventional processes, without enhanced images and save it as.psd ﬁle (Photoshop). b) A reduced resolution copy of the panorama in.JPG or TIFF format is made. To be easily run in DStretch it must be less than 1.6 GB (ImageJ x64). c) This image is then opened in the plugin and the most suitable adjustments are determined by pursuing a better visualization of the pictographs and the background ﬁltering. d) The matrix can be saved (.txt) however other added adjustments must be noted to manually in order to use them latter in batch mode. 3.2.2. Enhancements by sections In the following, the gigapixel panorama must be divided in proportional and appropriate sections to be processed in DStretch: a) The original gigapixel image in.psd format is opened in Photoshop. Sections must be overlapped at least a 20–30% between them. Again, every section of the picture should not be larger than 1/1.5 GB (depending on the ImageJ version x86/x64) in order to be processed in DStretch (Fig. 10). Fig. 10. In method B, the full resolution panorama is split in Photoshop in proportional parts. Fig. 11. Method B. Once the sections has been enhanced (a), they are merged again with Photomerge tool of Photoshop, or similar (b). E. Quesada, J. Harman Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage xxx (xxxx) xxx 8 b) The Batch Mode process is the same as mentioned above. Only in Method B, the enhancements must be applied to the Input folder containing the panorama sections. This process is quite efﬁcient since up to 8 enhancements are allowed in this routine. Then, each enhanced section must be saved in TIFF format. c) Finally, enhanced parts are now stitched with photomerge tool of Photoshop (or similar) (Fig. 11 a,b). The result is a new false color gigapixel image with good quality, where enhancements effects are nearly identical to those obtained in the reduced panorama. Therefore, previous enhancements in a downsized version of the gigapixel image give a clear idea of the ﬁnal results of the method B, and a user more control in the process. 4. Further considerations about DStretch in a scientiﬁc context At this point, it is considered appropriate to discuss some good practices in the use of DStretch in Gigapixel documentation. The application of DStretch in gigapixel images in a scientiﬁc context aims to provide new graphical information, sometimes not originally visible, to the rock art record. This plugin uses automatic processes and makes it possible to achieve more objective results with minor user intervention, though some user ability to control the results is desirable for better outcomes. Therefore, the DStretch enhancements should not be greatly altered, since they offer qualitative and quantitative data about the features of a panel (Brady and Gunn, 2012, 2017; Defrasne, 2014; Evans and Mourad, 2018; Gunn et al., 2010, 2014; Le Quellec et al., 2013, 2015). While the ﬁnal enhanced gigapixel image can be displayed in other graphics editing programs for further adjustments in the contrast or exposition, excessive alterations in DStretch outcomes may lead to misinterpretations and to less objective documentation. Moreover, it is recommended to enhance the panoramas with a range of color spaces to ensure the accurate identiﬁcation of the pictographs, mostly for panels with several color pigments or very poorly preserved. Especially in those cases, comparing different enhancements of the same image is advisable to not draw hasty conclusions (Quesada , 2010). 5. Discussion In summary, there are strengths and weaknesses in the two workﬂows presented (A and B). Although method B is better suited for the 4D⋅arte rupestre targets, the ultimate goal of the researcher should determine the use of one of them speciﬁcally. Comparing the results between method A and B (Fig. 12):  Backgrounds are less contrasted and paintings are more isolated from the rocky surface with method B. On the contrary, method A provides more contrasted backgrounds and more ﬂashy colors.  Method A yields less predictable effects in the enhancements, even applying the same sequence as in B mode. Final results with workﬂow A cannot be evaluated until the panorama is fully assembled, while Fig. 12. Details of the panorama enhanced with Method A (up) and B (down). Notice the nuance of the more contrasted background in method A and cleaner pictographs in method B. E. Quesada, J. Harman Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage xxx (xxxx) xxx 9 the output image in Method B is quite similar (but with higher resolution) to one resulting from the enhancement of a reduced version of the original panorama.  Although not a key matter, the assembling process of a gigapixel image is more successful when Exif metadata of the individual pictures exist. With method A this information is not available. More control over the contribution of every element within a gigapixel image (pigments, calcite washes, and so on) to an enhancement, can be very useful for research purposes. Method B provides this advantage and also obtains painted motifs slightly more separated from the background. 6. Conclusion Digital enhancement for rock art images has become widespread. The DStretch plugin to ImageJ is one of most versatile and feasible tools for that purpose. It has been combined with other cutting-edge technologies in the rock art documentation process. This paper is intended to give details of methodology for the combination of DStretch with gigapixel images. The pros and cons of two different workﬂows (A and B) have been presented from the experience accumulated by the Spanish research team 4D⋅arte rupestre. Both methods are based in the use of Batch Mode routine of DStretch and provide similar output gigapixel panoramas. However differences between them make method B more suitable in many cases. It gives more control over the ﬁnal results of the enhancements and a visibly less contrasty background. However, Method A can be more advantageous for enhancements made during ﬁeldwork, since it is a more automatized and simple process. Finally, some recommendations for best practices in the use of DStretch with gigapixel panoramas within a scientiﬁc context have been highlighted. This will avoid the loss of valuable information contained in the enhanced images. It is hoped this article will contribute to the development of methodologies for gigapixel image enhancement with DStretch, as well as foster a constructive discussion between researchers. ‘Declarations of interest None'. title: Accurate 3D models in both geometry and texture: An archaeological application
abstract: There are two main ways of building 3D models of archaeological objects. The first way is to use photogram­ metry by means of image-based modelling systems. The second way is the use of 3D scanners. Both methods have advantages and problems. In this work, we propose a simple way to merge both methods to preserve the best of each method and obtain 3D models with high accuracy and excellent resolution and texture fidelity. Building on the work of other proposals offering an alternative solution, we demonstrate a model with absolute units, where the colour of the texture is calibrated to assure a very good photo finish. Fidelity results produced by RMS estimation of the deviations of the colour patches offer better results for the photo camera than for the cameras in the scanners used. The final model maintains the metric properties of the scanned model, which was our metric reference.

full_text: Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 27 (2022) e00248 Available online 29 October 2022 2212-0548/© 2022 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Accurate 3D models in both geometry and texture: An archaeological application María-Eugenia Polo *, ´Angel M. Felicísimo, Guadalupe Dur´an-Domínguez University Center of M´erida, Sta. Teresa de Jornet 38 06800, University of Extremadura, M´erida, Spain A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: 3D scanning 3D model Archaeology Colour calibration Metric accuracy Photogrammetry A B S T R A C T There are two main ways of building 3D models of archaeological objects. The first way is to use photogram­ metry by means of image-based modelling systems. The second way is the use of 3D scanners. Both methods have advantages and problems. In this work, we propose a simple way to merge both methods to preserve the best of each method and obtain 3D models with high accuracy and excellent resolution and texture fidelity. Building on the work of other proposals offering an alternative solution, we demonstrate a model with absolute units, where the colour of the texture is calibrated to assure a very good photo finish. Fidelity results produced by RMS estimation of the deviations of the colour patches offer better results for the photo camera than for the cameras in the scanners used. The final model maintains the metric properties of the scanned model, which was our metric reference. 1. Introduction The notion of cultural heritage has evolved in recent decades. The development and diversity of digital techniques for improving the study, representation, conservation, documentation and dissemination of cul­ tural heritage offer new perspectives and lead to adopting new schemes for correctly managing historical places and objects. Digital transition opens up new scenarios. In this context, we are referring to heritage information systems (HIS) or the progressive relevance of heritage building information modelling (HBIM), which leads to strengthening the relationship between the digital model and the real world to improve the conservation of historic sites (Jouan and Hallot, 2020). This concept is supported by the environment of the Digital Twin, which was defined by Michael Grieves as a “virtual representation of what has been pro­ duced”. This term, originally applied in engineering (Grieves, 2015) is referred to here as the Historic Digital Twin (HDT) and is understood as a digital replica (digital twin) of the main characteristics (geometric, structural, mechanical, historical, etc.) of an object with the potential to change the processes of conserving, managing and maintaining cultural and historical heritage (Marra et al., 2021). The digital twin paradigm plays an important role in the dilemma between protecting the identity of a cultural heritage element and adapting the element to a possible functional use (Massafra et al., 2022). In the scope of the 3D modelling of archaeological objects, 3D digital models are used to recreate reality as digital reproduction, thereby allowing the study and documentation of objects and scenes (Grilli and Remondino, 2019). In cultural heritage, 3D models must be as identical as possible to the reality that they represent (i.e. they must be digital twin), not only from a metric point of view (dimensions and scale) but also in appearance (colour and texture) (Molada-Tebar et al., 2018). One way to create a 3D digital model is by means of a 3D scanner. There are many types of 3D scanners with different characteristics, such as range, technology, and accuracy, that allow users to choose the best scanner for their purposes (Di Angelo et al., 2018). These devices are known for their accurate three-dimensional measurements (Georgo­ poulos et al., 2016) and high speed of data acquisition. Different ap­ plications use 3D scanners, such as monitoring archaeological excavations (Martínez-Fern´andez et al., 2020) or detecting moisture in heritage buildings (Lerones et al., 2016). In the first step, a 3D scanner generates a point cloud that represents the scanned object. The point cloud can be converted into a mesh to provide topological consistency with the virtual representation. The operations carried out on the point cloud to obtain a final 3D model will depend on the software that manages the scanner, and it is necessary to use other software to continue with the modelling. Some operations, such as filtering or rendering, can be carried out on both the point cloud and the mesh. The last step involves including texture information to offer a more realistic solution. At this point, photographic information is usually used to * Corresponding author. E-mail addresses: mepolo@unex.es (M.-E. Polo), amfeli@unex.es (´A.M. Felicísimo), gldd@unex.es (G. Dur´an-Domínguez). Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/daach https://doi.org/10.1016/j.daach.2022.e00248 Received 11 August 2021; Received in revised form 18 October 2022; Accepted 25 October 2022 Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 27 (2022) e00248 2 provide texture in 3D models, but there are other options, such as the obtained reflectance function per vertex over the sparse data. These solutions have some limitations that depend on the geometric repre­ sentation of the model or the materials (Coutinho et al., 2017). How­ ever, some scanners are not provided with photographic information, and sometimes this option is either acquired independently or unavai­ lable. In some cases, the photographic fidelity is not as good as the fi­ delity that is needed. Other options for creating 3D digital models have been developed over the last decade by using digital photography. Image-based modelling (IBM) techniques, also known as digital photogrammetry, enable the generation of 3D models of an object or scene from a set of overlapping photographs of the object from different points of view (Remondino et al., 2012) by using several algorithms, such as structure-from-motion (SfM) and multi-stereo view (MSV). Currently, there are several IBM software solutions for the digitalization of various scales for both small objects (Kingsland, 2020) and large datasets (Remondino et al., 2017). IBM systems can offer a very good quality texture but provide non- scaled results due to a lack of metric information. Some factors affect the final result. For example, the same quality cannot be expected for an image taken with a 2-megapixel non-static camera and an image taken with a medium format camera, a high-quality prime lens and a 50 megapixel CMOS sensor. Other factors affecting the final result can be the percentage of overlap between consecutive photographs; when this value is below 50%, the alignment process sometimes fails, while values above 60% make the features of the model sharper and more defined (Marshall et al., 2019). The object to be modelled also determines the quality of the results. In the case of small objects, using a reduced depth of field produces a more complicated point matching, thus resulting in a mesh with a high noise level and a final texture with some out-of-focus areas (Nicolae et al., 2014). Related to the quality of the texture, there are techniques to optimize the texturing process in the image-based modelling pipeline to achieve a higher geometric definition (Bolognesi and Fiorillo, 2018). There are several studies that compare both techniques, 3D scanning (range-based modelling) and image-based modelling (Bianconi et al., 2017; Remondino, 2011; Remondino et al., 2014). There are also studies that use both techniques (Alshawabkeh et al., 2020; Guidi et al., 2017). At this time, neither range-based modelling nor image-based modelling is superior. One method alone may not always be sufficient in some works, and both methods have limitations and strengths, depending on the case (Lachat et al., 2017). Merging both sources of 3D data is not a new issue. Initial studies (Alshawabkeh and Haala, 2006) combined scanned data with image-based data to bridge gaps to accurately determine the shape of some 3D features in the modelling of the Al-Khasneh monument in Petra. The same idea is applied by Becker and Haala (2008) when using scanned (LiDAR) data for modelling large structures and then refining some elements of the structures using photogrammetric modelling. Rodríguez-Gonz´alvez et al. (2012) describe the integration of laser scanning and close-range photogrammetry to create a visualization system to manage archaeological information. Moussa et al. (2012) perform merging based on adjustments for the orientation of estimation of images from scanner and camera images using an optimized structure and motion reconstruction method. Meschini et al. (2014) modelled an Italian convent with a terrestrial laser scanner (TLS) and integrated the photogrammetric data to survey some parts (roofs) that are difficult to reach with TLS. On the other hand, G´amez Serna et al. (2015) proposed aligning in 3D from the two sources through the scale iterative closet point (ICP) algorithm. Dostal and Yamafune (2018) utilize a technique referred to as photogrammetric texture mapping (PTM) to map the texture generated from the IBM system onto the geometry of the scanned data. Recently, Jaber and Abed (2020) use the commercial software RealityCapture to import laser scans and then convert them to multiple images by re-projecting onto virtual planes. These multiple images, with camera orientation parameters in the scanner coordinate system, are used to create a 3D model. 2. Case study 2.1. A replica of a Greek vase We scanned and photographed two objects. The first object is a replica of a Greek vase from the classical period (5th-4th centuries BC) with a height of 22 cm and a diameter of 11.5 cm (Fig. 1). This vase is finely decorated with drawings whose sharpness is a critical issue during the process of making a model. 2.2. A replica of a jaguar head The second object is a replica of a jaguar head from the pre- Columbian Mexico period with a length, width and height of 17 cm, 22 cm and 11 cm, respectively. The jaguar head is constructed of limestone with brown veined effects (Fig. 2). This object was selected because of its highly reflective surface. These kinds of surfaces usually present problems in generating 3D models from both scanners and digital photography. Fig. 1. Replica of the Greek vase on the turntable with a structured- light scanner. Fig. 2. Replica of pre-Columbian jaguar head constructed of limestone on the turntable inside a lightbox and illuminated with LED panels. M.-E. Polo et al. Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 27 (2022) e00248 3 3. Aims Considering the importance of both methods (range-based modelling and image-based modelling) for modelling archaeological objects, the purpose of this paper is to combine the metric accuracy of a scanner and high-resolution images to generate an accurate model both from a metric point of view and from texture’s photorealistic quality. A work­ flow is proposed to create faithful 3D models of archaeological objects to be used in cultural heritage applications such as preservation, recon­ struction projects, storage of data in virtual databases or conducting non-destructive test and simulations (Morena et al., 2019). Our proposal, based on the work of Dostal and Yamafune, is per­ formed, in part, with open source software, as the free Cloud Compare software. The models generated with our procedure are metrically referenced with the final model and expressed in real units (mm), which enables the collection of measurements and sections and volume cal­ culations in real units. Additionally, we propose including a colour calibration stage of the images during the process so that the final models have real units for both the metric and well-calibrated RGB colour values. Another advantage of the proposed procedure is that a scanner with the ability to capture colour or textures is not necessary because our goal is to retexture the 3D model obtained from the scanner with the model coming from the photogrammetric model. 4. Data acquisition and processing 4.1. Photogrammetric acquisition and processing Agisoft Metashape software was selected to build the photogram­ metric model running on an HP laptop with Windows 10 OS 64 bit, 32 Gb RAM, Intel Core i7 CPU, and NVIDIA Quadro K1100 M GPU. Pho­ tographs were taken with a Nikon D7000 camera (16.2 megapixels) with a prime 100 mm lens (equivalent to 150 mm due to the crop factor). The working distance, in these example cases, was approximately 150 cm. At this distance, with the 100 mm lens and considering a crop factor of 1.5, the field of view is 331 × 218 mm. Since the sensor has 4928 × 3264 pixels, the theoretical resolution on the object is 0.055 mm. Lighting was performed with two bi-colour LED panels with a colour rendering index of CRI = 95. An X-Rite ColorChecker Passport colour chart, which includes ColorChecker Camera Calibration software for colour profiling, was utilized for calibrated colour references. The im­ ages were processed with Adobe Photoshop CC. The colour chart has 24 patches, six of them are grey of different densities, of which the values are known in several colour spaces (RGB, CIE Lab …). Auxiliary materials such as tripods, backgrounds, light diffusers, and photometers were also employed. RAW image processing, including colour calibration, was performed with Adobe Photoshop CC, and CloudCompare open source software was applied for scaling and regis­ tration of the meshes. Digital photographs are the source for this 3D modelling, so the quality of the camera and the environment should be controlled. For this purpose, the object is placed on a turntable inside a lightbox and illu­ minated with LED panels with a colour rendering index of CRI = 95 (Fig. 2). The light should be diffuse, ideally hemispherical, to avoid deep shadows and to conserve coherent illumination when the object turns, especially in the case of highly reflective objects. Studies carried out with highly reflective surfaces, such as gold, offer some solution, such as the development of a lighting technique called Ring Reaction Lighting (RRL), to provide diffuse lighting (Antinozzi et al., 2020). Maximum acutance and sharpness are achieved with the following camera configuration: tripod, prime lens, diaphragm for an optimal f- value (f:8 in our case), mirror up, and remote shooting control. Colour calibration is performed using a standard colour chart to create session- specific profiles. A total of 65 shots are taken in the case of the Greek vase, distributed in three circles covering the entire figure (Fig. 3), and 55 shots are taken in the case of the jaguar head. The photographs are processed to obtain images with corrected colour and the maximum resolution using Adobe Photoshop CC and the colour profile generated for the photographic session. The procedure is described as follows: • Detection of the colour chart by the software; it is usually an auto­ matic process. • Construction of the International Colour Consortium (ICC) profile; carried out by the colour chart software, the profile is available for the image processing application Adobe Photoshop. ICC profiles contain information about colour errors so that the software that reads the image can properly correct these errors. • Reading of the image to be profiled with Adobe Photoshop and assignment of the ICC colour profile. • At this point, the colours in the images are modified according to the specifications of the profile and should be adjusted to the original object with greater fidelity. The set of images is further processed with Agisoft Metashape following the usual workflow: alignment of the photos, dense cloud construction, mesh construction, debugging and texture map genera­ tion, without any special configuration (Kingsland, 2020). The final model is exported in OBJ format, which consists of three files. The first file is the OBJ itself, which contains the geometric information. The second file is a graphic file (BMP, JPG, TIF, TGA, etc.) that contains the texture to superimpose on the geometric model. The last file is a text file (MTL) that defines, among other things, the name of the texture file. 4.2. Structured light scanner acquisition and processing The scanning process for the Greek vase was performed with two scanners: Go! Scan 20 and Go! Scan 50 by Creaform (Fig. 1). The jaguar head was scanned with Go! Scan 50. Both scanners are portable hand­ held structured-light scanners (Rachakonda et al., 2019), with nominal resolutions of 0.1 mm and 0.5 mm, respectively. GO!SCAN 20 has a scanning area of 143 × 108 mm and is thus designed for small objects (approximately 5–50 cm) that require a greater degree of detail. The GO! SCAN 50 has a scanning area of 380 × 380 mm, and can thus support larger objects, as it has a greater depth of field (250 mm vs. 100 mm for GO!SCAN 20). The texture was also captured by the cameras integrated into each scanner with resolutions of 50–250 dpi for GO!SCAN 20 and 50 to 150 dpi for GO!SCAN 50 and a texture size of 4096 × 4096 pixels. The texture acquisition is an automatic process that can be deactivated, thus creating a non-textured 3D model. The scanner was managed by the VXElements software package, which is a platform for running several Fig. 3. Distribution of the 65 photographs taken to build the photogrammetric model in the case of the replica of the Greek vase. M.-E. Polo et al. Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 27 (2022) e00248 4 modules, such as VXscan, for the capture of data, and VXmodel, to manage and process the mesh. These scanners and software are pri­ marily intended for industry and engineering applications with high metric resolution (Polo et al., 2019). The VXElements software package runs on the same desktop computer as the Agisoft Metashape software. The object is placed on a turntable with standard 6-mm target stickers. The targets help the scanner to triangulate its position in 3D space and allow accurate results (Allard and Lavoie, 2014). Prior to the scan, a self-calibration process was performed. The scanning process is simple if the object has good reflectance, ideally Lambertian. Generally, it is not possible to scan transparent objects or highly reflective objects. If it is not possible to completely scan an object in one operation, it must be covered in two or more parts, e.g., one of the obverse sides and one of the reverse sides. All the parts must be overlapped to permit alignment, stitching, and merging. Since the scanning is performed using structured light, the work is performed in a darkened area to reduce noise. Basic processing is per­ formed during the session so that areas without data can be detected and corrected. Later, the sessions are stitched, and the model is converted to a mesh and then corrected. After the scanned data were processed with the VXModel software, the results were exported to OBJ format. In our case, it is possible to export to OBJ format without texture (if texture exists, it will be discarded). 4.3. Data comparison 3D models were generated both with the scanner and from the digital images. The explained procedure has been applied to the Greek vase using both the Go! Scan 20 and 50 scanners and the jaguar head using the Go! Scan 50 scanner. Although the improvements needed are mainly related to texture sharpness, some tests have been performed to evaluate any improve­ ment in the colour fidelity of the Nikon D7000 camera images and the texture captured by the scanners. Fidelity results were produced by RMS estimation of the deviations of the colour patch values from the standard CIE Lab data provided by the company and the measured value of the same patches by means of the “Colour selector” tool of Adobe Photoshop. The results are detailed as follows: the RMS of the Nikon D7000 camera is 11.5 with a maximum error of 23 (blue patch). The RMS of the Go! Scan 20 scanner is 17.7 with a maximum error of 31 for the neutral grey 1.05 D patch and a maximum error of 30 for the purplish blue patch. The RMS of the Go! Scan 50 scanner is 17.5 with a maximum error of 33 for the cyan patch. There­ fore, the texture obtained with the Nikon D7000 camera provides better results than the cameras of the scanners. In relation to the models obtained by scanning and according to our tests (Polo et al., 2019), a metric accuracy of approximately 0.2 mm is expected, but compared with the texture of the Nikon D7000 camera, the texture of the cameras of the scanners provides worse results, as stated before. The composition of the image from the scanner is dy­ namic; the scanner sweeps the object and performs a composition “on the fly” from what is captured in the field of vision at each moment. This process, therefore, differs very much from the one used in the static and well-controlled images of the camera. The result is a blurred texture for the scanned model that contrasts with the quality of the photogram­ metric model as can be observed in Fig. 4. This figure displays the details of the textures and colour for the two Greek vase models, both scanned and photogrammetric, showing the differences in spatial resolution and colour fidelity, which are important aspects in cultural heritage modelling. In addition to the differences in spatial resolution and sharpness, there is also a difference in colour: the photogrammetric model is calibrated, and the scanned model is not calibrated. Use of the photogrammetric model for the texture and colour components is desirable to correctly reflect the true colour of the object. The full size of both images consists of 4096 × 4096 pixels. 4.4. Model texturing from external cameras At this moment, we have two OBJ files: an OBJ file from the scan (scan_obj) and an OBJ file from the photogrammetric process (photo_­ obj). Our goal is to retexture the 3D model obtained from the scanner with the model coming from the photogrammetric model. A problem is that both models have reference systems with origins and axes in arbi­ trary directions, and it will be necessary to carry out a rototranslation to align the scanner model with the one obtained by photogrammetric methods so that they have the same scale and location in a common coordinate system. This process can be done in several ways using different commercial or free applications that allow us to achieve the defined purpose. A proposal can be carried out in the following steps: (1) Import (Open) both OBJ models in CloudCompare software. Due to the difference in scale and reference system, only one of the models is visible. (2) Select both models, and adjust the scale approximately with Tools – Registration – Match Scales. Make sure that photo_obj is selected as the “reference entity.” The matching criterion is not a critical parameter; bounding-box volumes or max bounding-box dimensions are good options. Fig. 4. Details from the texture of the photogrammetric model (left) and the scanned model (right) in the case of the replica of the Greek vase. M.-E. Polo et al. Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 27 (2022) e00248 5 (3) Adjust the centre of the boxes with Tools – Registration – Match bounding box centres. Both models are now superimposed but not oriented (Fig. 5). (4) Align the models in two steps. First, select scan_obj only and adjust the orientation approximately with photo_obj and using Edit – Translate/Rotate; then, select both models and use Tools – Registration – Fine registration to make the exact adjustment. Ensure that the reference model is photo_obj and that the Adjust scale checkbox is activated. (5) After adjustment, a register report is displayed. If the data have reasonable values (scale value nearly 1), the model can be exported in STL format, for example, as no texture is needed at this point of the procedure; the model is called scan_obj_registered. At this point, we adjusted and oriented the scanned model as the photogrammetric model. It is time to replace it in Agisoft Metashape in three steps, as discussed in (Dostal and Yamafune, 2018). (1) Reopen the Agisoft Photoscan project and delete the 3D Model retaining the other components (cameras, tie points, and dense cloud). (2) Import the scan_obj_registered.stl model (File – Import – Import model) and superimpose the texture on the new model with workflow – built texture. (3) The result is the scanned mesh with the photographed texture; the model must be exported as OBJ with the name merge_obj. Dostal and Yamafune (2018) consider the objective achieved at this stage of the process. Although the texture and metric are optimal, the digital model is out of scale as it has been adapted to the photogram­ metric model. The final model must ensure that the correct dimensions have mm as units of measurement and that the texture has high reso­ lution. The procedure to achieve this outcome is to repeat some of the steps of the first phase of the procedure and adapt the scale of the retextured model to the photogrammetric model. (1) Import (Open) the merge_obj and scan_obj models in CloudCompare. (2) Adjust the scale approximately with Tools – Registration – Match Scales but ensure that scan_obj is applied as the “reference entity.” (3) Adjust the centre of the boxes with Tools – Registration – Match bounding box centres. (4) Align the models: select merge_obj, and approximately adjust the orientation with scan_obj using Edit – Translate/Rotate; then, select both models, and use Tools – Registration – Fine registra­ tion to perform the exact adjustment. Ensure that the reference model is scan_obj and that the Adjust scale checkbox is activated. (5) Export the result model in OBJ format with texture. Following this procedure that it is summarised in Fig. 6, we have obtained a dimensioned model with the metric from the scanner and texture in high resolution and calibrated colour. A comparison between the generated models was performed by calculating the differences in surfaces and form (Morena et al., 2019) by Fig. 5. Image of the models scaled and centred but not aligned. The model photo_obj appears in vivid colour as the texture is calibrated, and scan_obj appears in dark tones. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.) Fig. 6. Workflow followed in the proposed procedure. To analyse the differences in surface area, it is necessary to know the number of faces of each model. As shown in Table 1, the final model has the same number of faces as the original scanned model does, as expected, but the photogrammetric model always has a smaller number of faces, which results in a worse visualization of the object as it was seen in Fig. 7. The differences in form are displayed as a colour map distribution Fig. 7. On the left the photogrammetric model, on the right the scanned model. Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 27 (2022) e00248 7 that ranges from red to blue. Fig. 8 shows the distance distribution map between the original scanned model and the final model of the jaguar head and the histogram of these differences in distances. Both models have the same volume, and the differences in distances are almost insignificant (the mean distance is −0.00001 mm, and the standard deviation is 0.0001 mm). The histogram shows symmetrical values, and according to the colour map distribution, there are differences in the ears of the jaguar, with these zones (red colour) having larger distances in positive values in relation to the scanned model (reference). Similar results can be observed for the Greek vase for the Go! Scan 20 scanner in Fig. 9 with a mean value of 0.00008 mm and a standard deviation of 0.001 mm. The results for the Greek vase and the Go! Scan 50 scanner are 0.00007 mm and 0.001 mm, respectively. The higher values of the differences for the Greek vase (blue colour) are concen­ trated in the handle area due to the difficulty of correctly scanning this area. Therefore, the final models maintain the metric properties of the scanned model, which was our metric reference in this work. It is important to highlight that the user has hardly any control over the operation of the camera in the scanning process (Fig. 10, top) only to disconnect it. On the other hand, a digital photography session allows us to choose the lighting, perform a colour calibration and process the images. Fig. 10 (below) shows the final model for the jaguar head. The final 3D models, resulting from merging the scanned meshes and photo textures, which are scaled, can be freely downloaded from the following links: (1) Greek vase from Go! Scan 20, https://skfb.ly/6CMJO (2) Greek vase from Go! Scan 50, https://skfb.ly/6CMJU (3) Jaguar head from Go! Scan 50, https://skfb.ly/6JuOp 5. Conclusions Many disciplines use 3D models for different purposes, but the de­ mands are different. For example, in industrial applications, the accu­ racy of measurements is critical, but colour is not usually important. On the other hand, in art and archaeology, both metrics and texture are essential. Two techniques are able to create 3D models from an object or scene: 3D scanning (range-based modelling) and digital photography (image- based modelling, IBM). Both techniques have advantages and disad­ vantages. Some 3D scanners generate very accurate 3D models, and IBM systems present very good visual results. Conversely, 3D scanners show much more blurred visual texture than IBM, and IBM does not allow models to be dimensionally accurate and shows more artefacts and noise when photographs are taken without special care. The ideal result in 3D modelling would include “the best of both worlds”, that is, the metric of a 3D scanner (when the metric is tenths of millimetres) and the texture of digital photography (with a high- resolution camera and calibrated colour). This paper presents an accessible workflow to obtain an accurate model from both metric and visual perspectives that could be beneficial for the conservation and archaeology communities. This workflow is Fig. 9. Distance distribution map between the original Go! Scan 20 scanned and the merged model of the Greek vase (top) with the histogram of the differ­ ences (below). M.-E. Polo et al. Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 27 (2022) e00248 8 based on previous work from (Dostal and Yamafune, 2018) but expanding the workflow, highlighting the importance of controlling the photo shot environment and calibrating the colour in the images. In this way, the process of replacing a real object in research studies with an accurate 3D model is simplified, preserving the original object and protecting it from damage. Moreover, a realistic texture helps to preserve visual information in situations when archaeological objects (e. g., painted potteries and rock arts) are at risk of losing their pigments. title: An assessment of methods for the digital enhancement of rock paintings: the rock art from the precordillera of Arica (Chile) as a case
abstract: The digital tracing of rock art is becoming a standard for archaeologists working in this ﬁeld of research. The lack of speciﬁc software for this task has resulted in archaeologists either using solutions that are not statistically robust enough or working with overly generic ﬁelds of image analysis. This paper will assess the application of three techniques for digital tracing: Principal Components Analysis, K-means, and Decorrelation Stretch. In addition to these techniques of image analysis, this paper will also explore three selective techniques that classify or enhance pigmentation. These analyses have been implemented in a software package called PyDRA (developed by one of the authors, ECC). This software makes use of several scientiﬁc libraries for the digital analysis of an image. As a case study, we chose three rock art sites located between 3100 and 3500 m above sea level in the precordillera of Arica, the northern region of Chile. All of the paintings are located inside rock shelters that are easily accessible; however, we lack a systematic recording for analysing these sites. Pampa El Muerto 14 and Mullipungo 1 were recorded through direct tracings between 1980 and 1990. The Lupica 1 site was identiﬁed only in 2013 and has not been recorded until now. Due to the advancement of technology in the years since the 1980s, we have been able to compare the proﬁciency of different digital and statistical techniques. Our study uses the most adequate parameters and enables us to trace the paintings digitally without actually handling the surface of the rock. © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
