Reina Mercedes, 10, 41012 Sevilla, Spain a b s t r a c t a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received 29 January 2016 Received in revised form 17 June 2016 Accepted 23 June 2016 Available online 30 June 2016 Approaching rock art as one of many elements of greater, complex natural systems allows for a better understanding of rock art panels as dynamic realities. This stands in contrast to the traditional concept in which rock art is framed as a static set of paintings and/or engravings over a rock surface. The nature of the rock surface, and even other elements related to the supporting rock, such as the network of microﬁssures or the very composition of the rock, have generally been of secondary interest. Information on the environmental conditions, even at the most basic level, has been absent, as has the nature (or even the mere presence) of the biological communities harboured by the supporting rock. This new, dynamic, concept of rock art, needs to be understood by means of empirical data, in which quantitative and non-contact methods are of particular use. This paper deals with the application of digital image analysis (Principal Component Analysis and digital classiﬁcation algorithms) as the basis of several research strategies for identifying these elements, measuring their extent, and monitoring the effects of these elements over time. It also presents the advantage of freely available software, minimising the overall cost of the analyses. Some examples were performed in laboratory conditions, and others in subterranean environments such as Roman hypogean tombs and Altamira Cave. © 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Keywords: Digital image analysis Monitoring Rock art Principal Components Analysis Digital classiﬁcation 1. Introduction: rock art and complex natural systems From the very beginning of scientiﬁc research on prehistoric rock art, signiﬁcant effort has been applied to the development of recording techniques. Of course, these techniques were mostly developed with a nearly exclusive focus on only the manifestations of rock art rather than on other issues, and their goals were merely to reproduce the rock art itself. In the 20th century, the goal of improving the visibility of the motifs in order to understand (or at least to see) what was depicted was added, but the physical context of the rock art in terms of the supporting rock, still received little interest. Thus, many of the classical records of rock art consist of a series of drawings or photographs (sometimes scaled, sometimes not) focused on the motifs without any reference to their environment - as if the rupestral representations existed in a void, ﬂoating in space like some kind of prehistoric astronauts. Interestingly, in some early examples of rock art recording we can ﬁnd some kind of interest in depicting the substrate of the artistic manifestations, as is the case of the ﬁrst recordings of the Portuguese rock art site of Cachão da Rapa, performed as early as 1734 (Contador de Argote, 1734, Santos Júnior, 1933, Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2013). In contrast to the traditional approaches to rock art which, as mentioned above, usually emphasized the study of the rock art itself (often to the exclusion of many other elements of interest for understanding the organization of the panels and their implications for conservation), systems theory allows for the improvement of the description of the complex network of characteristics and relations that compose a rock art panel, thus supporting a more complete and comprehensive vision in which taphonomy plays a more relevant role. It seems necessary to state here that we understand taphonomy not as deﬁned by Efremov (1940): the study of the transition of life forms from the biosphere to the lithosphere, but in the updated deﬁnition of Domínguez-Rodrigo et al. (2011:5), i.e., “the study of the dynamic processes of modiﬁcation of the original properties of all the components (including those that are missing) of any palaeontological, archaeological or forensic assemblage, comprising its constituent materials and its context.” Rock art constitutes, in our framework, one part of a complex natural system. Fig. 1 synthetically illustrates some of the complex networks of relationships and synergies inherent in these kinds of systems, in which the rock art is one of the many abiotic constituent elements. Nevertheless, in order to avoid misunderstandings it is probably necessary to deﬁne rock art here, and for these purposes, our deﬁnition can only be operative: “every graphical act, independent of its possible semantic value inside a concrete belief system, produced by means of the application of paint, scratching, engraving, bas-reliefs or full-relief sculptures, with so varied instruments as ﬁngers, burins, ﬂint points or paintbrushes, Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 10 (2016) 864–870 E-mail address: m.rogerio@csic.es. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jasrep.2016.06.041 2352-409X/© 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jasrep effectuated over rocky surfaces (be them hard or soft), without a previous preparation or with a very slight preparation of the supporting rock” (Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2011:2–3). Complex natural systems are characterised by contingency and also by the irreversibility of the processes, due to their tendency toward increasing entropy as stated by the second law of thermodynamics. Consequently, they are subjected to continuous evolution due to their own elements, or even by the action of other elements that cannot be regarded as parts of the system because of their contingent and limited actions in the systems (impulsive processes, in the words of Brimblecombe, 2005). Vandalism on rock art, or a wildﬁre, for instance, are two good examples of these kinds of contingent actions. One of the main implications of this concept of rock art is that none of the parts of the system can survive independently. On the contrary, the relationships and synergies generated are together responsible for the conservation of the broader system. Thus, paradoxically, the same elements that can destroy the rock art can also contribute to conserving it. Maybe one of the most important ways to inﬂuence positively in the conservation of the system lie in the discovery of its structure, as the negative impacts of the elements can thus be proactively minimised. Biodeterioration was deﬁned by Hueck (1965) as any undesirable change in the properties of a material caused by the vital activities of organisms. The different materials can include not only the supporting material in which the organisms grow, but also the very source of C and N for many microorganisms. Thus, microbe-pigment interaction can be the cause for the disappearance of painted rock art in some cases. Not only the organic matter, but even the inorganic pigments used in some rock paintings can be affected, as they can be reduced or oxidized by the metabolic activity of members of the microbial bioﬁlms developing on them. In this sense, the reduction of haematite by bacteria in a rock shelter harbouring prehistoric rock paintings has been reported (Gonzalez et al., 1999). All these biotic elements have a spatial dimension, which is susceptible to be represented graphically, as well as a temporal dimension that it is important to know (RogerioCandelera et al., 2011). Digital image analysis currently constitutes the simplest tool to achieve the two goals of understanding what is happening and where it is happening in a rock art panel. The elaboration of a detailed record of a rock art site is in fact an exercise in cartography, in which the different elements (including the rock art manifestations) can be considered as different expanses expressed in a thematic map. As not all of the processes happen in the same places and at the same moments in time (Margalef, 1982), it is useful to deﬁne the spatial extent each of the different elements of the system. Moreover, the preparation of a detailed map is a prerequisite for the assessment of the evolution over time of these systems. Digital image analysis techniques constitute an essential set of tools which maximize the accuracy of representations while minimising contact with the rock art and simplifying the task of documenting the extent of distinct elements over time. In the following sections, we provide several examples of detection, quantiﬁcation, and monitoring of biogenic or biologically induced elements that affect the conservation of rock, and subsequently the conservation of painted rock art, using digital image analysis (DIA) as our main tool. The chosen case studies were performed over a period of time ranging from eleven years ago to six years ago, so they can also be seen as examples of the evolution over time of this research strategy. In summary, as we have stated in other places (Rogerio-Candelera, 2008, 2009, 2013, 2015), DIA has demonstrated a wide range of capabilities in the detection, distinction, and classiﬁcation of elements with different features, detection of phases of painting and repainting, and the quantiﬁcation of surface areas of elements, and the monitoring of processes with a temporal dimension, all of which are of special utility in the study and conservation of rock art. Economic factors are an important dimension when dealing with cultural resources and cultural heritage, as substantial funding is rarely available. Consequently, most of the software packages used in this study (the most important sets of tools used following this approach) are freely available on the Web, making the method both non-invasive and cost-effective. 2. Digital image analysis techniques The range of available techniques for digital-image-based analysis is ever expanding. Nevertheless, the approaches presented here are based on two basic techniques: On the one hand, the use of Principal Components Analysis (PCA); on the other hand, the implementation of basic classiﬁcation techniques like the application of thresholding algorithms for the segmentation into binary of the resulting images. In the present case studies, DIA techniques were applied to digital or digitized images obtained with conventional cameras (both analog and digital) using natural light, LED light sources (LitePad DL, 900 Lux, 6000 K colour temperature) or illuminated by means of a ﬂuorescent UV light source (Omni-Lux, Wood's glass, λ = 400 nm) to induce ﬂuorescence in the images. When time is a relevant dimension for the research objectives (i.e., for monitoring purposes), the construction of coherent image series plays an important role, as it constitutes the basis for establishing comparisons. Geometry and radiometry of the different images was homogenised by various methods in order to build comparable series. In terms of geometry, the comparability of the images was achieved using projective methods (by means of the Adobe Photoshop [Mark and Billo, 1999] and HyperCube software packages). The comparability of the images in terms of radiometry was achieved by extending the histogram to the maximum and minimum values in several images considered as a series (i.e., using the histogram to match some images to others, be they greyscale or full colour images). Of course that does not constitute an exact reference to radiometry in a strict sense, although the objectives of comparability (the possibility to follow the development of several microbially produced stains over time) were reached in a relatively easy and inexpensive way thanks to this research approach. 2.1. Principal Components Analysis PCA is a widely known statistical technique for multivariate analysis developed at the beginning of the 20th century after the works of Fig. 1. Some of the elements which compose a complex natural system, including rock art. 865 M.Á. Rogerio-Candelera / Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 10 (2016) 864–870 Pearson (1901). Due to its capability of reducing redundancy in datasets, PCA has been widely used in varied research ﬁelds, such as Chemometrics, Economics, Taxonomy, Ecology, and Medicine (Sanguansat, 2012). In Satellite Remote Sensing, it has been applied to the extraction of information from multiband highly correlated images. This technique allows the representation of the Pixel Values of the different bands in a new, different, reference system of uncorrelated axes, allowing the detection of elements that can be difﬁcult to observe or identify in the original images (Sabins, 1987, Lasaponara and Masini, 2012). Applied to rock art recording, PCA forms the foundation of most of the approaches elaborated by the research group of the author of this paper, with a long list of publications from 2007 onwards (for a noncomprehensive review, see Rogerio-Candelera, 2015), as is recording conducted using the widely available software DStretch (Harman, 2008). Among the advantages of this technique is the ability to detect subtle differences in the images that are frequently difﬁcult to observe (Vicent García et al., 1996) or not visible at all (Mark and Billo, 2006). 2.2. Digital classiﬁcations According to Chuvieco (2002), a digital classiﬁcation is the process of assignation of every pixel of a digital image to a category, or in other words, we could deﬁne it as the process of transformation of the image data into another format of information. In the case studies reported in this paper, the classiﬁcation strategy adopted has been the simplest, i.e., the segmentation of the image into a binary system. With this approach we reach the goal of separating the data into two categories: the interesting and the uninteresting pixels for the particular objectives in each case. The segmentations in these works have been performed by means of an iterative thresholding algorithm, which works using the mean of the foreground and background pixels of the images, establishing new thresholds by iteration. A detailed description of the method and some of the results is available at Rogerio-Candelera (2015) and Sezgin and Sankur (2004). 2.3. Software Most operations have been performed using several versions of the HyperCube software package, developed by Robert Pazak (Army Geospatial Centre, Alexandria, VA, USA) and the Java-based image analysis and processing software ImageJ (Wayne Rasband, National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD, USA). Both software packages are freely downloadable from the Web. HyperCube is a software tool developed for the work with multi- and hyperspectral images originated by satellite sensors; ImageJ, in contrast, was originally developed for biomedical sciences. Notwithstanding, both constitute a set of strong and complementary tools for the study of rock art. 3. Some case studies 3.1. Assessment of the bioreceptivity of limestones under laboratory conditions Bioreceptivity was deﬁned by Guillitte (1995: 216) as “the totality of material properties that contribute to the establishment, anchorage and development of fauna and/or ﬂora” as well as “the aptitude of a material to be colonised by one or several groups of living organisms without necessarily undergoing any biodeterioration”. All these characteristics are in continuous change as a direct result of weathering and, for this reason we cannot consider bioreceptivity as a static Fig. 3. Stone surface areas covered by algal bioﬁlms in laboratory conditions after 0, 45 and 90 days of incubation, quantiﬁed by digital image analysis. Fig. 2. Digital image approach followed in the case study. 866 M.Á. Rogerio-Candelera / Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 10 (2016) 864–870 property. Thus, this researcher also deﬁned three types of bioreceptity: primary, secondary and tertiary, depending if this property was deﬁned for pristine, weathered or even restored materials (Guillitte, 1995). As a way to assess the bioreceptivity of limestones of special interest for the restoration and preservation of cultural assets, a total of ﬁve types of limestone was chosen – two Portuguese: Ançã (CA) and Lioz (CL) limestones; two Spanish: San Cristóbal (SC) and Escúzar (PF) stones; and one Italian limestone: Lecce (PL) stone. Stone probes of these ﬁve lithotypes were inoculated with a consortium of microalgae and cyanobacteria and placed in a microclimatic chamber. All the characteristics of the experiment are described in extenso in Miller et al. (2010). The biomass colonising the lithotypes was measured along time using two non-invasive, complementary approaches: in vivo chlorophyll a ﬂuorescence and digital image analysis. The strategy of digital image analysis adopted consisted of the application of PCA to standard RGB images obtained by a conventional digital camera, previously homogenised radiometrically and geometricly in order to build coherent temporal and scaled series. The most suitable band product of Principal Components was then chosen for the application of a thresholding algorithm allowing the binarisation of the image. The last step of the process was counting the number of pixels classiﬁed as bioﬁlm in order to numerically estimate the extent of the area occupied by the biological colonisation (Fig. 2). The result of this approach consisted of a series of numerical values (Fig. 3) comparable to those obtained by means of in vivo chlorophyll a ﬂuorescence (Miller et al., 2012). 3.2. Change detection by means of Principal Components Analysis inside a Roman subterranean tomb Circular Mausoleum, in the Roman necropolis of Carmona (South Spain) is a subterranean tomb carved in a 30 m-thick Neogene sedimentary sequence mainly composed of decimetre-thick beds of poorlysorted, massive biocalcarenites, separated by discontinuous lutitic and/or marly beds, ranging from millimetres to a few centimetres thick (Cañaveras et al., 2015). The tomb is a subterranean barrel-vault structure, connected with the surface by means of a rounded arch and a vertical shaft, and covered by a circular earthen superstructure delimited by a ring of limestone ashlars. Inside the tomb, ten niches are disposed along the two main walls. The ﬁnal wall supports a bigger, probably the main, niche (Fig. 4). A varied catalogue of alterations is present in this Roman tomb, ranging from fragmentation of the host rock, to loss of material including microbial colonisation (Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2008, Benavente et al. 2013, Cañaveras et al., 2015). The case study that we present here focuses on the monitoring of several violet stains produced by a consortium of microorganisms, including some Streptomyces species (DiazHerraiz et al., 2016). As an increase in the extent of the stains was qualitatively detected by the naked eye, several of these violet stains were selected and measured from different series of photographs ranging from 1997 to 2005, for quantitative assessment. The digital image analysis approach followed in this case-study is derived from a particularly successful one widely used for the quantiﬁcation of burnt areas from wildﬁres based on satellite imagery Fig. 5. Example of the digital image analysis strategy, followed in the case-study. Note that the PC1 band in the results (initial stage) is quite similar to the 1997 greyscale photograph. The PC2 band (ﬁnal stage of the results) shows in black the areas stained in the period 1997–2002. Fig. 4. The Circular Mausoleum (A) sight from the rounded arch near the shaft; (B) from the main niche. 867 M.Á. Rogerio-Candelera / Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 10 (2016) 864–870 (Chuvieco, 2002, Gimeno et al., 2003, Heredia et al., 2003). The approach is not new (Byrne et al., 1980) and is based on the detection of repeated information (totally correlated), and different information in synthetic two-band image cubes composed with images of the same area but from different dates. After applying PCA, the result is another two-band image cube expressing in the ﬁrst Principal Component (PC) band the repeated information (comparable to an initial state), and in the second PC band the non-correlated visual information (which we can consider the ﬁnal state). Fig. 5 shows one of the synthetic image cubes and the results obtained by PCA. The results allow the qualitative assessment as well as the quantiﬁcation of both the initial and the ﬁnal stages, and therefore an assessment of change over time in the extent of the violet stain. 3.3. Monitoring the development of microbial colonisation at Altamira Cave Altamira Cave is widely known for its impressive Polychrome Hall and as the ﬁrst cave with Palaeolithic paintings recognised as authentic, some thirty years after their discovery and publication by Sanz de Sautuola (1880). Altamira Cave was declared as a World Heritage Site by UNESCO in 1985, and closed to the public during 1979–1982, and again from 2002 to 2014 due to problems with the conservation of the paintings. Among the principal threats was the proliferation of microorganisms in several sectors of the cave (Sanchez-Moral, 2014; Sanchez-Moral et al., 2014). The presence, in particular, of white, grey and yellow bacterial colonies was a concern, and it seemed worthwhile to attempt to understand if the colonies had stabilized in the period from 2007 to 2009, or if, on the contrary, had proliferated. If the latter were the case, determining a yearly growth rate was desirable. Again, digital image analysis was the elected methodological tool, using both conventional RGB digital images and UV (λ = 400 nm) induced ﬂuorescence photographic images to build three- and six-band image cubes in order to perform PCA. The goal was to isolate each distinct kind of colonisation and then measure the area of the surface respectively occupied by them. In order to build coherent image series, it was necessary to adapt the geometry of the images one to another, as well as to reach an acceptable degree of radiometric homogeneity. PCA from RGB images (Fig. 6) proved to be an efﬁcient tool for the isolation of the yellow colonies from the white and grey ones, so their extent was easily calculated by deﬁning a threshold and then performing binary segmentation for pixel counting. The artiﬁcial category “white/grey” was also measured and calculated. For the differentiation of the white and the grey colonisations, though, UV induced ﬂuorescence images were necessary. The chosen procedure, the addition of the bands 1 and 2 obtained by PCA of the UV induced ﬂuorescence images allowed the detection of the white colonies (Fig. 7) in a new greyscale-derived image. This allowed us to quantify them by binary segmentation and pixel counting. The value of the area occupied by the grey was calculated by subtracting the value of the white colonies from that established for the combined grey/white colonies. 4. Discussion and conclusion The examples already described show the important role of techniques based on digital image analysis for documenting and representing in a non-invasive way the spatial dimension of the different elements of the complex systems in which rock art is usually embedded, particularly their usefulness for reﬂecting the fourth dimension of these systems: Fig. 7. The addition of the ﬁrst and second PC bands of the UV induced ﬂuorescence images (PC1UV + PC2UV bands) allowed detecting the white colonisations in white colour. Control point number 2 (wall of the Kitchen Hall). Fig. 6. False-colour image elaborated from the PCA bands of a standard RGB image in one of the control points of Altamira Cave (Polychromes Hall) in 2007 (bands 1, 2, and 3 of PCA used as R, G, and B channels). The magenta spots are white/grey colonies. Bar: 10 cm. 868 M.Á. Rogerio-Candelera / Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 10 (2016) 864–870 time. In other words, these methods present a technique for monitoring the processes that are happening in a system over time. As we have seen above, monitoring implies the elaboration of coherent and comparable series of images. In the ﬁrst of the three case studies presented, the geometric and radiometric coherence is practically ensured by the shape of the samples (all of them were circular stone probes with the same diameter and ﬂat surfaces), the position of the camera (situated in a position normal to the samples), as well as the illumination and the homogeneity of the photographic record. This practically obviated the need for geometric or radiometric corrections. Nevertheless, a certain degree of radiometric correction was performed by adjusting the histogram of the different photographs to the same maximum and minimum values. The geometry of the samples was also adjusted by means of the election of several control points that were easily identiﬁable in every sample and thereafter adjusting the geometry of one image to another by projective methods. The second case study, the violet stains of the Circular Mausoleum, was more complex, as although the surface is practically ﬂat, the position of the camera (distance and angle respect to the stains) was difﬁcult to repeat exactly and some geometric rectiﬁcation was required. This problem was resolved using the “distort” functions of Adobe Photoshop (Mark and Billo, 1999, Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2008) to adjust the geometry using several distinct features of the walls (cracks, grafﬁti…) as spatial references. The radiometry in this case was homogenised by means of the conversion to grayscale (8 bit) images before the elaboration of the synthetic image cubes. Undoubtedly, the third case is the most complex in terms of comparability of the images. Not only did the original images have different pixel depth, the geometry of the cave walls is far from being ﬂat, and it was additionally very difﬁcult to relocate the camera in the exact same positions to repeat the framing. A possible solution for the rectiﬁcation of the geometry could be projection over a good 3D model of the walls and the later creation of ortophotographs, which would serve as the basis for measurements. None of this was possible at the time of the case study, as the available 3D model had considerable differences in resolution from one zone to another. The adopted solution was the adaptation of the geometry of some photographs to certain other ones used as reference photographs (Rogerio-Candelera, 2010). The radiometry of the images was corrected by means of the homogenisation of the histograms as in the ﬁrst case study. The third case studied in this paper, digital image analysis of Altamira Cave, is probably the best example of the impact of the results of these applied research approaches. The research carried out from 2007 to 2009 (Sanchez-Moral, 2014) allowed for the demonstration and quantiﬁcation of the proliferation of microorganisms colonising sensitive areas of Altamira Cave, such as the world-famous Polychrome Hall, and served as a foundation for the proposal of speciﬁc conservation strategies. Together, the studies presented here demonstrate the importance of research techniques based on digital image analysis in the study and conservation of rock art. title: Digital image analysis based study, recording, and protection of painted rock art. Some Iberian experiences
abstract: This work reviews different approaches, with the common point of using digital image analysis techniques imported from the research ﬁeld of environmental remote sensing, used the last years by our research group for obtaining information from prehistoric painted rock panels. The obtained results are relevant for the deﬁnition of the particular environments of different panels (the complex series of elements composing the natural systems in which the rock art constitute one of the parts, and the relations and synergies connecting all them), for the deﬁnition of the rock art itself (in terms of composition, taphonomical history or typology), or simply for improving the vision of faded images, helping in the task of making a secure tracing of the panel. This paper presents several examples in which image uncorrelation by principal components analysis, mixed digital classiﬁcation, and autotracing helps understanding the panels. & 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

Some Iberian experiences Miguel Ángel Rogerio-Candelera Instituto de Recursos Naturales y Agrobiología de Sevilla (IRNAS-CSIC), Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Cientíﬁcas, Avda. Reina Mercedes, 10, E-41012 Seville, Spain a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received 24 September 2014 Received in revised form 3 November 2014 Accepted 6 November 2014 Available online 14 November 2014 Keywords: Digital image analysis Rock art Complex natural systems Non-invasive recording Iberian Peninsula a b s t r a c t This work reviews different approaches, with the common point of using digital image analysis techniques imported from the research ﬁeld of environmental remote sensing, used the last years by our research group for obtaining information from prehistoric painted rock panels. The obtained results are relevant for the deﬁnition of the particular environments of different panels (the complex series of elements composing the natural systems in which the rock art constitute one of the parts, and the relations and synergies connecting all them), for the deﬁnition of the rock art itself (in terms of composition, taphonomical history or typology), or simply for improving the vision of faded images, helping in the task of making a secure tracing of the panel. This paper presents several examples in which image uncorrelation by principal components analysis, mixed digital classiﬁcation, and autotracing helps understanding the panels. & 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Along the last years, the utilisation of digital image analysis techniques has become a standard procedure for the elaboration of tracings of rock paintings. These kind of techniques has been considered by UNESCO as “the best ﬁtted for the reproduction of rock art manifestations, keeping in mind that [⋯] unites the advantages of low cost and low or non-existent threat for the completeness of both the depictions and their supporting rocks” (Collado Giraldo, 2012: 46, my translation). Even, a recent paper declares that “exhaustive, integral and non-invasive documentation of rock art sites is an indispensable requirement both for their study and to monitor degradation and alteration processes, and to guarantee the preservation and authenticity of this heritage” (Domingo et al., 2013: 1880). Most applications of these techniques, nevertheless, are just focused to the obtaining of digital tracings, avoiding the documentation not only of the microtopography of the supporting rock but of the elements of the system in which the rock paintings are included (Rogerio-Candelera, 2009), thus missing an important amount of information crucial for conservation issues, as is the one supported by the interrelations (not only theoretical but physical) of the biotic and abiotic elements which allow establishing the present state of the complex natural systems, and the assessment of the relative importance of the confronted tendencies to homeostasis or entropy, i.e., the existence or not of a steady state of the system. Natural systems including rock art have basically two kinds of location: high and low energy systems. High energy systems (open-air and sheltered locations) are those with a high exchange rate of matter and energy with the environment: high energetic oscillation, and high availability of light and nutrients for macro and microorganisms. Low energy systems (caves), on the contrary, are much more stable in energetic and material terms. The direct consequences are a selection of the local biocenosis and the establishment of different possibilities of deterioration. Thus, a low energy system, is more fragile that a high energy one, as this last one has survived to an aggressive environment maybe for thousands of years. A low impact as the presence of a small group of visitors in front of a rock panel inside a cave, on the contrary, may generate variations in temperature and humidity higher than the offered by the natural conditions of the cave in a complete yearly cycle (Sanchez-Moral et al., 1999, 2000). Another question, and not of lesser importance, is what can we understand under the deﬁnition of “digital image analysis” or “digital image processing”. For older authors, the terms manipulation and subjectivity are commonly included in their deﬁnitions: Thus, for Sabbins (1987: 433) digital image processing means “computer manipulation of the digital-number values of an image”; for Lillesand and Kiefer (2000: 470) “involves the manipulation and interpretation of digital images”; or for Mulders (1987: 151), whose concept of image enhancement relates to “the modiﬁcations of an image to improve its quality as perceived by Contents lists available at ScienceDirect journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/daach Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.daach.2014.11.001 2212-0548/& 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. E-mail address: m.rogerio@csic.es Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 68–78 a viewer. The criterion is subjective and the enhancement is judged by the observer”. Other deﬁnitions (Rogerio-Candelera, 2010a:121, my translation) emphasize the scientiﬁc side: “the set of mathematical operations effectuated with the matrix images obtained by every type of sensor”. This is reﬂected in the common practice of “digital image analysis/processing” applied to rock art, that commonly lie in the ﬁrst kind of deﬁnitions as it is usually based on the use of software developed for image retouching, as Photoshop, PhotoPaint, Paintshop or Gimp, not for image analysis, and consequently with a limited set of analytical possibilities. Nevertheless, there are some digital image analysis applications that fall in the “scientiﬁc” part of the deﬁnition, developed in the last years: most of them using the DStretch plugin to the software ImageJ (Harman, 2008), speciﬁcally designed for the automation of the enhancement of rock paintings by using the Decorrelation Stretch technique (Gillespie et al., 1986), which essentially consists in the calculation of the principal components of the image and the elaboration of colour composite images using the resulting bands. Other researchers have also used principal component analysis (PCA) for the enhancement of rock paintings, directly (Vicent García et al., 1996; Mark and Billo, 2006), or include it in the design of new software tools for these purposes (Hollman and Crause, 2011,; Cerrillo-Cuenca et al., 2014). Most archaeological applications of the technique are nevertheless addressed to satellite remote sensing (Lasaponara et al., 2012; Traviglia and Cottica, 2011, as examples). Basically, PCA consists in the linear transformation of the data codiﬁed in the bands of a multispectral image (i.e. the DNs, “digital numbers” or “pixel values”), in order to elaborate an image referred to a new reference system of normal axes (i.e., uncorrelated). This result is reached by means of the rotation and/or translation of the axes of the original feature space (Lasaponara and Massini, 2012). Other useful approaches for the detection and mapping of the painted surfaces in rock art consist in the application of a mixed supervised and unsupervised classiﬁcation strategy, i.e. the assignation of every pixel of an image to a category based on the spectral behaviour of the pixels, automatically (unsupervised) or by means of speciﬁc training areas (supervised) (Buchner et al., 2000), or even multispectral recording (Robinson and Ware, 2002), a promising tool not still widespread in the rock art research community. The purpose of this paper is to review some recording experiences conducted by our research group along the last decade, understanding them not as ﬁnished results, but as steps in a way under construction. These experiences are addressed to the integral recording of those elements of natural systems harbouring rock art which exhibit a cartographically representable dimension. Some of the reviewed experiences have been published in other places, but some others remained unpublished or published in a very partial way. The ﬁrst group of experiences will only receive a brief reference in the text, as the complete data are publicly available. The second group of experiences will be treated more extensively. Our interest in this paper, with the publication of unpublished and not always successful approaches, is to contribute to the development of the research ﬁeld with the publication of both positive and negative results. 2. A soft review of our published experiences Our experience in rock art recording using digital image analysis begin at the mid-2000s, when trying to develop instrumental techniques allowing the non-invasive characterization of complex natural systems including rock and mural paintings, with a special interest in the biogenic and cultural (the art itself) coverages. The direct cause was the implication of our Research Group in two contracts directed to the conservation of two important subterranean components of Cultural Heritage: the Roman necropolis of Carmona, Southern Spain, and the widely known Altamira cave, in Santillana del Mar, Cantabria, North Spain. Our ﬁrst experiences applied PCA in order to achieve two different goals: (i) to improve the visualization of the coverages of interest, and, (ii) to monitor processes quantifying their results both in absolute and relative terms. At Circular Mausoleum, a Roman subterranean tomb in which a microbial consortium dominated by a Streptomyces species produced a diffusible violet pigment on the walls, the stains were monitored by a modiﬁcation of a technique currently used to quantify burnt surface in brush ﬁres by satellite imagery that implied the elaboration of temporal series of photographs and the application of PCA (RogerioCandelera et al., 2008). An improvement of this technique was used in the Altamira cave in order to detect, differentiate and monitor the development of three types of microbial colonisations threatening the Palaeolithic paintings along a period of three years (Rogerio-Candelera, 2010b). In this case, the information obtained from PCA was complemented with that obtained by means of algebraic operations with images. Under laboratory conditions, research on detecting, measuring and assessing the role of the biological component of these kinds of systems was also conducted in Miller et al. (2010a,b, 2011). On the other hand, the accurate visualization and recording of the cultural coverage of the panels by PCA was explored in laboratory experiences, thereafter tested in real targets, as that of the Roman tomb of Three Gates (Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2011), the Levantine paintings of Muriecho, at Huesca province, Northern Spain (Portillo et al., 2008), or the complex panels of La Coquinera II at Teruel province, Spain (Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2009). During these and the next years, supported by a CSIC project coordinated by J. Vicent (Métodos analíticos para la documentación integral del arte rupestre prehistórico/Analytical methods for the integral recording of prehistoric rock art) and a Consolider-Ingenio programme coordinated by F. Criado (Programa de investigación en Tecnologías para la Conservación y revalorización del Patrimonio/ Research programme on technologies for the conservation and revalorization of Cultural Heritage), we were able to develop the working protocol that is on the basis of our work, and to apply systematically this approach in order to develop detailed integral tracings of the La Hoya Hall, at Altamira Cave, Spain (RogerioCandelera and Élez Villar, 2010), Faia post-Palaeolithic rock paintings (Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2010), and Cachão da Rapa painted panel (Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2013), both in northern Portugal. Along these years, the subjects covered by our research include the mapping and quantiﬁcation of bioﬁlm growth; the differentiation of microbial colonisations using visible and induced ﬂuorescence photographs, quantiﬁcation and monitoring of the process; to improve visualisation and detect non visible motifs; to differentiate pigments in cumulative scenes, to differentiate coverages implied in the conservation of the panels; to elaborate vector tracings; to detect important conservation problems as the endolithic growth in stone substrates; and to monitor the development of bioﬁlms, in general. The list of rock art sites studied, although unpublished (or at least, as we said before, without a complete publication), includes Cueva del Arco and Cueva del Encajero shelters, in Andalusia, and also Cabras Blancas, and Tio Campano shelters in Aragon. Classiﬁcation strategies at Cueva del Arco Cueva del Arco, is one of the rock shelters harbouring parietal art at the Tajo de las Figuras rock art site (Casas Viejas-Benalup, Cadiz, Spain), extensively published in Cabré and Hernández-Pacheco (1914), Breuil and Burkitt (1929), and with actualized tracings and a new study in Mas Cornellà (2001). The shelter is decorated with schematic red paintings depicting some quadrupeds of difﬁcult identiﬁcation and several signs typical of the Spanish schematic art as pectiforms or anchor signs (Acosta Martínez, 1968) as well as anthropomorphs and semi-naturalistic zoomorphs. The starting point of our study was the documentation generated during a research contract with the Andalusian administration for Culture focused on the biodeterioration factors. The original images used for this study were obtained by means of analogical photographic cameras in the mid-1990s. The set of the 35 mm colour slides was digitized with a high resolution scanner (Agfa DuoScan HiD) with a resolution of 2000 dpi. From these digitized RGB images, we tried a mixed approach involving PCA, the application of several supervised and unsupervised digital classiﬁcation algorithms, and the elaboration of false colour images from the classiﬁcation results. The area selected for the image analysis was probably the most deﬁned by the different published studies (i.e. with a high degree of coincidence among the available tracings), the left group of motifs of the right panel of Cueva del Arco (Fig. 1). The application of an unsupervised K-means classiﬁcation algorithm (also known as Lloyd algorithm as in 1957 S.P. Lloyd (1982) was the ﬁrst in deﬁne the standard algorithm) was one of the chosen options. Typically, this kind of classiﬁcation (Hartigan and Wong, 1979) consists in the predeﬁnition of an expected number of classes and the number of iterations. A number of arbitrary centroids are then calculated and two steps follow: (i) assignment step, where each data point is assigned to the nearest centroid, and (ii) update step, adjusting the centroids for matching the sample means of the data points that they are responsible for. Applied to individual motifs, as the pectiform number 61 of Mas Cornellà tracing (Fig. 2), the results obtained were good enough, as they allowed the clear detection of the class “paint” respect to the supporting rock and other different classes present in the images. In this case, the paint class, obtained after 20 iterations and 10 estimated classes, is highlighted in green colour in Fig. 2. Other classes were also well delineated, as the calcium carbonate concretions over the wall (pink) or the ﬂakes (light blue) in the same ﬁgure. Nevertheless, the results obtained using these kind of algorithms are not always so useful, as the number of predeﬁned classes cannot always coincide with the different spectral behaviours of the pixels, showing that in fact the results are dependent of the spectral range covered by the multispectral raw image. This shortcoming is exempliﬁed in Rogerio-Candelera et al. (2011:2576 and Fig. 6). Other supervised classiﬁcation algorithms, based in selected training areas (i.e. areas with clearly recognizable paint) were also used speciﬁcally for the detection of the paint: one based on the Euclidean distance and another one based on the absolute difference of the pixel values in the three RGB bands. The results using these classiﬁers were not especially good, leaving out of the “paint” category pixels of real paint identiﬁed by the naked Fig. 2. Nevertheless, the elaboration of false colour images from PCA and from the classiﬁcation results allowed an acceptable approximation to the reality of the panel (Fig. 3), with a good visualization of both the pectiform and the anchor sign, and improving the vision of elements as the zoomorphic ﬁgures immediately below these depictions. 3.2. Improving the vision of Cueva del Encajero painted motif Cueva del Encajero is a rock shelter placed in Jaen province (Northern Andalusia) discovered in 1984, and published in 1987 (Soria Lerma et al., 1987), which harbour two painted motifs: one schematic anthropomorphic ﬁgure and a Levantine ﬁgure interpreted as an unheaded deer. This ﬁgure was described as intentionally unheaded and assigned to the Levantine artistic tradition on the basis of its realisation technique. In order to enhance the image and conﬁrm the real absence of the head in this ﬁgure, we adopted a double approach: (i) to elaborate false colour images based on PCA, and (ii) try the digital classiﬁcation of the images. PCA (Fig. 4) allowed achieving several goals as: (i) discard the existence of two straight almost horizontal lines deﬁned in the published tracing as the back legs of the zoomorph, (ii) document the presence of two wide vertical lines clearly visible in the second PC band, later assigned to the washing of cyanobacterial colonies by episodes of runoff water, and (iii) detect what we interpret as the remains of the original head of the ﬁgure, erased as an effect of the water ﬂood that also washed the cyanobacterial colonies. The digital classiﬁcation was performed implementing a supervised classiﬁcation algorithm based on the deﬁnition of a training area of 17 paint points and calculating the absolute difference of the digital numbers (DNs). The plot, although far to be perfect, serves to detect the presence of paint points (i.e. points with the same spectral behaviour that the selected as training area) in the zone in which the head of the animal should be placed (Fig. 5). Trying to improve the vision of the possible head of the deer, a false colour image elaborated in the HSI (Hue, Saturation, Intensity) colour space but using as bands those from PCA, allowed to show what seemed to be something similar to a head in the place where a head was expected (Fig. 6). Although this is just a tentative treatment, the results are coherent with the possible presence of paint residues in the area of interest. 3.3. Thresholding the ﬁgures of Abrigo de las Cabras Blancas shelter The Abrigo de las Cabras Blancas shelter was discovered in 1981, concisely published thereafter (Bader et al. 1981), lost, and rediscovered for science ten years after (Collado Villalba et al., 1991–1992). The shelter depicts a Levantine style hunting scene of goats, completely painted in white colour over a carbonaceous ceiling (Fig. 7). The use of white colour is one of the distinctive resources of the Albarracin Levantine rock paintings, very visible when applied over the reddish “ródeno” Buntsandstein sandstone characteristic of the lithology of the area. The composition of the white paint is generally unknown, although some unpublished results point to Barium sulphate (BaSO4) at Cabras Blancas (P. Marzo, personal communication). The available imagery of the rock art harboured by the shelter consisted in a preliminary tracing elaborated by R. Viñas over analogical photographs, and a more precise set of tracings of the decoration of the shelter, without methodological information, published by Collado Villalba et al. (1991–1992). Our documenting strategy was marked by the character of the depictions: painted with white pigment over a black background, allowing an acceptable visualisation of the motifs. Consequently, our strategy consisted in the application of PCA, the inversion of the values of the pixels and the application of image algebra for later segment the images into binary applying an iterative thresholding algorithm (Fig. 8). This algorithm works using the mean of the foreground and the background of the images to establish a new threshold Fig. 4. T ¼ gmax  gmin   ∑ gmid g ¼ gmin pðgÞ ð1Þ where gmax is the highest nonzero grey level, gmin is the lowest one, gmid is the midpoint between the two assumed points of the histogram [gmid¼(gmaxþgmin)/2], and p(g) the probability mass function. The results obtained using this strategy, are the basement for a set of new tracings of the depictions, considerably better ﬁtted to the original than the published ones (Fig. 9) and the advantage of being elaborated using non-invasive methods. The ﬁnal step of the protocol is described in other publications (i.e. Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2010; Rogerio-Candelera and Élez Villar, 2010) and consists in the automatic elaboration of vector tracings by means of the utility for auto-tracing included in the different versions of the software package CorelDraw. The process implies the transformation of amounts of pixels of “0” value (black) of the binary images in polygons, with the advantages of being fully scalable for reproduction purposes and their low weight (something important for storage and data management with GIS-based tools). It is important to remark that the better the segmentation into binary, the easier the separation of the desired coverages. The cleaning of the vector image, nowadays, is only possible manually, following a photo-interpretative approach. This opens a chink to subjectivity in the recording, which can be minimized with a good image analysis strategy including an accurate thresholding algorithm. We must remark the interest of the elaboration of false colour images to detect the pixels which could be misinterpreted, proved useful in works like Sasso et al. (2014). 3.4. The shelter is painted in red over the reddish tone of the sandstone complicating the vision of the motifs. The ﬁrst publications of the shelter (Piñón Varela, 1982, 1983) describe ﬁve ﬁgures (two male deer, two horses and an undetermined zoomorph), although nowadays, the depictions are interpreted as two male deer, a horse, an anthropomorphic ﬁgure, and a red stain, all them conventionally classiﬁed as Levantine in style. The style of the depictions, nevertheless, was redeﬁned by Lopez Payer and Soria Lerma (1991) as “Postpalaeolithic” or “Semi-schematic” arguing the similitudes among the representations of four painted shelters, conventionally classiﬁed as Schematic or Levantine on the basis of their geographic location, and the differences of these shelters with both Schematic and Levantine styles. Also Martínez-Bea (2008) thinks that the art of the shelter could be later, corresponding to a horizon chronologically placed at Copper or Bronze Age and related to other sites in the Albarracín area. In order to improve the vision of the motifs, PCA was applied to several images of the shelter. Usually, the third PC band is the most suitable for reﬂecting the minority elements in the image (i.e., the Fig. 8. Although this was the case, the motifs were not very well deﬁned, and this was reﬂected in the abnormally high values of variance explained by that band (Table 1). For this reason we prepared a 6 bands image cube, homogenising for that purpose the geometry of two images (Fig. 11) and then mixed in a RGBRGB cube. PCA was then applied to the cube, and the amount of variance attributable to the presence of painting in the images was found in the fourth PC band (Fig. 12 and Table 1). It was clear that every combination of bands focused on the enhancement of the pictorial layer of the shelter should use this fourth band product of principal components analysis. The colour combination, useful to detect subtle differences, can be appreciated in Fig. 13, built using the ﬁrst, third and fourth bands. An alternative approach was based on the mix of two enhancement techniques: HSI contrast enhancement and PCA. HSI contrast enhancement was performed following the classical deﬁnition of the method by Gillespie et al. (1986), i.e., stretching the histogram of the bands reﬂecting the saturation (S) and intensity (I) and remapping an RGB image from the bands. This allows the approximate conservation of the colours while improving the deﬁnition of the images. Fig. 14 shows the results obtained with the HSI contrast enhancement of one of the photograph. A false colour image elaborated with PC bands (Fig. 15) was also elaborated. The addition of these two images had as effect the subtraction of the background in the painted area, allowing a more accurate vision of the paint (Fig. 16). 4. Discussion and conclusions The different approaches followed in the four case studies revised have in common the use of principal components analysis, which for us is a basic tool to explore the extent of the different coverages included in digital images. Used alone, or elaborating false colour images, it allows the extraction of useful information from the images by the separation of the minority elements. Thus, the eigenvalues calculated for every image are the module of the eigenvectors deﬁning the components and the sum of the eigenvalues is the sum of the total variance of the dataset. These characteristics of the technique enable it for detecting minority elements included in digital images of rock art, which usually is the art itself. Furthermore, the false colour images composed with all bands also allow the use of accurate classiﬁcation strategies as they Fig. 10. General view of the tafoni of Abrigo del Tío Campano shelter. Maybe, performing a digital classiﬁcation is the strongest method to know the different elements of the system and represent it with the possibility not only of a qualitative knowledge, but also with a quantitative dimension, allowing the numerical assessment of the system behaviour. Consequently with this assertion, the false colour images elaborated weighting the use of one PCA band, or the images product of HIS contrast enhancement, or otherwise the images which have experienced the reduction or alteration of the variance included originally, cannot serve as a reliable raw material for classiﬁcation. Also related to digital classiﬁcation, is the fact that due that it is based on the spectral features of the image, the larger is the information included in the images, the ﬁner will be our classiﬁcation results, i.e., in many cases we will only have really acceptable results if using more than three conventional RGB bands. A good example of this can be the spectral differences between carbon and manganese oxides (see spectral signatures at the spectral library of the US Geological Survey, http://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2003/ofr-03-395/datatable.html, for example), which correspond with their different compositions, but with the same spectral behaviour in the domain of the visible (black colour), which makes them non-differentiable from the information obtained with a conventional RGB image. As a matter of fact, the different case studies exposed exemplify some steps of a recording protocol permanently under construction, in which there is a continuous feedback among the coverages present in the rock art locations and the methods used to record them, most times in an exploratory way. The ﬁnal objective of our approach is the study of rock art, in what we have deﬁned elsewhere as integral or holistic recording (Rogerio-Candelera, 2010a), minimizing the affection that our Fig. 12. We ﬁnd important to remark that not only the methods used for the research, but the very research activity can result a threat for the rock art conservation, particularly in those environments especially sensible, as are the caves. In these environments, the presence of a small group of visitors may generate an increase of temperature and humidity that can be higher than the variation of the values in natural conditions along a yearly cycle (Sanchez-Moral et al., 1999). Even, the human activities in the surroundings of the cave may cause micro-environmental disturbances (Sanchez-Moral et al., 2014). In a small cave like Altamira, the data show that the capacity of the cave has been surpassed just with the presence of the researchers (Cuezva et al., in press). As a ﬁnal remark, this paper has been dedicated to reviewing some of the experiences carried out by our research group along the last ten years focused on creating and adapting techniques and to develop protocols in order to contribute to the standardization of a non-invasive methodology for recording the whole complex natural systems in which rock art manifestations are just one of the structural elements. Although other research approaches, maybe more conventional, are not only possible but common, we bet on a holistic approach, interested not only in the conventional construction of datasets relevant for the study of rock art, but with interest in obtaining all the data relevant to characterise, at least minimally, the dynamics of these systems. As far as I know, this way is the only for an integral recording of rock art, a concept that also includes a social (ethical) perspective, since rock art is part of an endangered heritage, threatened not only for the action of the elements but by many more, including the very activity of the researchers. title: Digital image enhancement for recording rupestrian engravings: applications to an alpine rockshelter
abstract: Image processing software, such as the DStretch plug-in for ImageJ or Photoshop, are currently used to make faint rupestrian pictographs more legible. During the ongoing study of an Alpine rockshelter, these software proved to be equally useful for the visualization of linear engravings and scratchings. This unexpected function of DStretch, created for the study of rupestrian paintings, made it possible to clarify and correct the previous recordings of an incised Iron Age warrior and to facilitate the digital tracing of a modern maritime scene. Even if such convincing results are determined by particular local geological conditions in this case, this function could facilitate the study of engravings in other contexts where the lithology of smooth rock surfaces produces a sharp contrast with incised images. © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Image processing software are now commonly used to help the study of rupestrian paintings. Among them, Photoshop® (Domingo Sanz and Lopez-Montalvo, 2002; McNiven et al., 2004; Mark and Billo, 2006; Brady, 2006, 2007) and DStretch® (Harman, 2005; Mark and Billo, 2006; Le Quellec et al., 2013) are the most widespread. The DStretch® plug-in for ImageJ© was designed by Jon Harman in 2005 in order to facilitate the visualization of faint rupestrian paintings which are difﬁcult to see with the naked eye (Harman, 2005; Mark and Billo, 2006). This program uses the socalled decorrelation stretch algorithm to enhance digital images of pictographs. The contrast in the photographs is intensiﬁed and presented with false and artiﬁcial colors. This software is now currently used throughout the world to study rock paintings (Bahn, 2010), to help to complete previous inventories with images which had previously gone unnoticed and to improve the understanding of superimpositions without any direct contact with the rock surface and the pigments. There is no reason why such computer tools, based on color contrast, could not also contribute to a better record of rupestrian engravings when the contrast with the rock surface is sufﬁciently marked. I thus applied Photoshop® and Dstretch® to Alpine rupestrian images, for which it revealed unexpected and promising possibilities. Frequently used, and even created in the case of DStretch®, for the enhancement of paintings, these software appear to be equally efﬁcient for highlighting linear incisions, in addition to faint pictographs. They could appear also very effective for the production of bidimensional records of linear engravings. The example chosen to illustrate this point is the ongoing study of the Oullas rock shelter (Saint-Paul-sur-Ubaye, Alpes-de-HauteProvence, France).

During the ongoing study of an Alpine rockshelter, these software proved to be equally useful for the visualization of linear engravings and scratchings. This unexpected function of DStretch, created for the study of rupestrian paintings, made it possible to clarify and correct the previous recordings of an incised Iron Age warrior and to facilitate the digital tracing of a modern maritime scene. Even if such convincing results are determined by particular local geological conditions in this case, this function could facilitate the study of engravings in other contexts where the lithology of smooth rock surfaces produces a sharp contrast with incised images. © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Image processing software are now commonly used to help the study of rupestrian paintings. Among them, Photoshop® (Domingo Sanz and Lopez-Montalvo, 2002; McNiven et al., 2004; Mark and Billo, 2006; Brady, 2006, 2007) and DStretch® (Harman, 2005; Mark and Billo, 2006; Le Quellec et al., 2013) are the most widespread. The DStretch® plug-in for ImageJ© was designed by Jon Harman in 2005 in order to facilitate the visualization of faint rupestrian paintings which are difﬁcult to see with the naked eye (Harman, 2005; Mark and Billo, 2006). This program uses the socalled decorrelation stretch algorithm to enhance digital images of pictographs. The contrast in the photographs is intensiﬁed and presented with false and artiﬁcial colors. This software is now currently used throughout the world to study rock paintings (Bahn, 2010), to help to complete previous inventories with images which had previously gone unnoticed and to improve the understanding of superimpositions without any direct contact with the rock surface and the pigments. There is no reason why such computer tools, based on color contrast, could not also contribute to a better record of rupestrian engravings when the contrast with the rock surface is sufﬁciently marked. I thus applied Photoshop® and Dstretch® to Alpine rupestrian images, for which it revealed unexpected and promising possibilities. Frequently used, and even created in the case of DStretch®, for the enhancement of paintings, these software appear to be equally efﬁcient for highlighting linear incisions, in addition to faint pictographs. They could appear also very effective for the production of bidimensional records of linear engravings. The example chosen to illustrate this point is the ongoing study of the Oullas rock shelter (Saint-Paul-sur-Ubaye, Alpes-de-HauteProvence, France). 1. A key site in the alpine environment The site is located 2390 m a.s.l., in the Saint-Paul-sur-Ubaye district (Fig. 1). The southeeast facing rock surface bears an exceptional range of linear incisions, pecked engravings and painted ﬁgurations, from the Neolithic period onwards (Fig. 2). Among the more ancient images, there is one of the eight examples of Alpine Neolithic paintings, composed of ﬁve possible plant patterns. These paintings were then covered with pecked engravings of “Remedellotype” daggers, which provide evidence of the use of this rock surface as an ideological medium between 2900 e 2400 BC and which also supply the only one clear terminus ante quem for such schematic pictographs (De Marinis, 1994). The “Remedello-type” daggers are known in the necropolis in the P^o plain and these images are characteristic of the steles and rocky outcrops of the Central Alps, and of the steles in the Adige Basin and the province of Sion/Aoste. Remains of these daggers appear on a very damaged part of the panel implying that other images, which have now disappeared, also adorned this rock surface. The corpus of images also includes Iron Age incisions, patronymic initials, a painted and decorated signature * 13 rue des sœurs noires, 34000 Montpellier, France. Tel.: þ33 6 85 77 30 91.
