Developments on computer science, digital photography and more speciﬁcally on digital image enhancement have revolutionized the way rock art motifs are recorded and documented today. Conventional software for digital image processing is widely used to produce digital tracings, with reasonable results so far. But this manual process is still time consuming, especially when motifs are either faded, deteriorated or part of complex superimpositions. This paper explores the potential of two advanced digital image enhancement decorrelation techniques, principal components analysis (PCA) and decorrelation stretch (DS) to facilitate and accelerate motif recognition on visible digital images, as a ﬁrst step in the rock art recording process. Statistical analysis revealed that the performance of PCA is slightly better (5%) than DS, when compared to the results of conventional image enhancement solutions. & 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Rock art is considered a fragile cultural heritage dating back at least 40000 years (Pike et al., 2012; Aubert et al., 2014). It is produced on active rock surfaces (affected by running water, surface exfoliation, etc.), which are constantly exposed to different natural and human altering agents gradually threatening and degrading the art. Levantine rock art in particular is located in open-air sites directly exposing the art to harmful environmental inﬂuences. These include constant climate changes (temperature, moisture), exposure to direct sunlight, physicochemical weathering and erosion of the rock surface, biodeterioration, and so forth. Thus, rock art recording is necessary for the long-term preservation of this threatened and unique visual sample of our past. Rock art recording methods are constantly implemented to meet the different requirements that have emerged over more than a century as the discipline progressed. Alongside its role as a tool to either publicize new ﬁndings or study rock art, today rock art recording methods are also used for both monitoring degradation processes and disseminating the art to different audiences through different channels. Traditional direct recording methods may have some sort of impact on both the art and the rock surface, and thus they have been gradually replaced by other non-invasive techniques. A brief overview of some of these methods can be seen at Fritz and Tosello (2007) and Domingo (2014). In the last few decades, computer science has offered professional rock art recorders and archaeologists a wide range of software packages for editing and processing digital photographs to produce digital reproductions or “tracings”, thus replacing traditional manual methods. These computer image techniques aim to: (1) minimize the impact of rock art recording processes on rock art preservation; (2) search for more objective and accurate analysis, both for motif recognition and for the metric reproduction of motifs and the rock surface; and (3) accelerate the rock art recording process. Furthermore, computer enhancement methods allow researchers the opportunity to record, examine and present heavily deteriorated images that would have been missed (undetected) using conventional techniques (David et al., 2001; David, 2004; Brady, 2006, 2007). Digital image and colour space enhancement (Mark and Billo, 2002) fall within the category of conventional computer image enhancement techniques. Today a large number of researchers are using photo editing techniques to produce digital tracings of both rock art paintings and engravings (Vicent et al., 1996; Montero et al., 1998; Cacho and Gálvez, 1999; Villaverde et al., 2000; Clog et al., 2000; Domingo and López-Montalvo, 2002; López-Montalvo and Contents lists available at ScienceDirect journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/daach Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.daach.2015.01.001 2212-0548/& 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. n Corresponding author. Tel.: þ34 934020829. E-mail addresses: ines.domingo@ub.edu (I. Domingo), bertacarrionruiz@gmail.com (B. Carrión), silviablanco87@gmail.com (S. Blanco), jllerma@cgf.upv.es (J.L. Lerma). Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 79–88 Domingo, 2005, 2010; Brady, 2006, 2007; Rüther, 2007; MartínezBea, 2009; Cassen and Robin, 2010; Ortiz et al., 2010; Domingo et al., 2013a). We all work towards a common aim: to provide accurate working documents including interpretative readings of all the motifs, preserving its scale, regardless of their degree of preservation, visibility or spectacularity. Furthermore, graphic recording also seeks to show visually the relationships among motifs (whether they are isolated or included in some sort of scenic or non-scenic compositions, as well as interpretative readings of superimpositions and the possible relations with the rock surface). These visual reproductions usually show the conditions of the art at a speciﬁc point in time, although in the last few years there is an increasing interest in displaying 4D reproductions showing the site over time. Today tracings for each motif are generally produced using visible light images taken in situ, which are then processed with digital image processing software. To record rock paintings, colour selection tools are usually used to select and separate pigments from the rock surface. This technique is quite easy to use but, when motifs are quite faded or complex, it could be time consuming. Moreover, the results depend heavily on our visual perception, on our capacity to identify faded paintings, on our experience in the study of rock art and on our familiarity in the use of digital image processing software. Besides, many digital image processing techniques can be found in literature, such as classiﬁcation algorithms, ﬁltering, data decorrelation, spectral proﬁle analysis and index elaboration. These are non-invasive techniques, more objective, since they are automatized, and they can be used to enhance paintings that cannot be easily distinguished. There are many publications dealing with these techniques. To name some, Lerma (2002) applies supervised classiﬁcation to rock art panels using three visible spectral bands and one near infrared. Rogerio-Candelera et al. (2011) tests the value of PCA. Mark and Billo (2002) apply DS to enhance rock art paintings. Harman (2005)’s modiﬁcation and implementation of decorrelation stretch technique in the Dstretch plugin to the imaging program ImageJ has allowed the widespread use of DS to identify faint rock art painted motifs or even to discriminate superimposed paintings (Gunn et al., 2010). The use of decorrelation image analyses based on advanced computer image enhancement before tracing the art can contribute to automatize the initial part of the process, facilitating motif identiﬁcation and accelerating the process in most cases (Díaz-Andreu et al., 2006; Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2011). This paper focuses on the use of image colour decorrelation techniques and compares the results with those obtained with conventional software tools for digital enhancement, editing and colour selection (speciﬁcally the tools available in Adobe Photoshop). The decorrelation of digital colour image levels is applied following both techniques, PCA and DS. PCA and DS can both be used to transform the image content onto new non-correlated bands based on its variance. False colour spectral bands are created to better visualise rock art motifs. The resulting documents are then used to produce digital drawings or tracings for each of the identiﬁed motifs. To test the strength of both methods two relevant motifs involved in complex superimpositions have been selected. The results achieved with PCA and DS, and the corresponding tracing solutions are compared with conventional digital deliverables produced by rock art specialists. 2. Materials and methods Visible imaging sensors such as digital colour cameras are devices able to acquire the reﬂected radiation coming from a scene (composed of multiples objects) onto solid state sensors that typically incorporate ﬁlters that block the incoming electromagnetic radiation in three spectral bands: Red (R), Green (G) and Blue (B). As the three visible spectral bands (RGB) are most of the times highly correlated, the application of decorrelations algorithms allow users to either reduce or remove any correlation between spectral bands. Therefore, the information content on the visible image is optimised and can be used to highlight differences between spectral bands. The decorrelation analysis on rock art motifs after digital colour image acquisition follows. In particular, PCA and DS are presented, tested and analysed. PCA is a multivariate analysis technique that allows the reassignation of digital number (DN) values from different spectral bands in a new non-correlated reference system. Each new component decreases the variance in each band. The last components can be used to detect invisible features and their plotting. These components contain the non-correlated residual information (Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2011). DS technique is a PCA variant that sometimes improves the multispectral analysis and provides more information. This technique transforms the initial colour values following the Karhumen–Loève theorem. A PCA is performed on the three spectral bands, afterwards, the variance values are normalised and a Gaussian contrast enhancement is undertaken on each component. Finally, the three principal modiﬁed components are combined. The primary purpose of DS is visual enhancement. Decorrelation stretching is a way to enhance colour differences in a multispectral image. The result is an image with higher colour contrast, highlighting complex details. There are many software package solutions in the market to undertake fully automatic spectral and/or multispectral image analysis based on PCA and DS. Just to name a few, DStretch plugin to ImageJ; Hypercube and Multispec, in the category of open source; and ENVI and ERDAS Imagine in the category of commercial software packages for image processing and geospatial analysis. Nevertheless, the ﬁnal deliverables (tracings or drawings) with the painted rock art motifs are not achieved yet without human intervention due to the complexity to determine the true motif. The workﬂow followed in this paper is presented in Fig. 1. The starting point is always the visible (RGB) image acquired on site, normally with a digital camera. 2.1. Case study: Carche rock shelter Carche rock art site, also known as Poveda or Fuente del Candil rock shelter, is located in the eastern part of Spain, and more speciﬁcally in Jalance (Valencia) (Fig. 2). The son of a local historian discovered the site by chance in 1997, when walking through those lands (Poveda, 2001: 30–31). The site is located in the Murell seasonal stream, at an altitude of 446 m over the sea level. The shelter faces southeast and measures 7.29 m long and 4.43 m high (for further details see Domingo et al., 2013b). The site includes painted motifs corresponding to two different post-Palaeolithic rock art traditions: Levantine rock art and Schematic rock art. Both traditions are listed in the Spanish National Heritage list as Bien de Interés Cultural since 1985, and were also included in the UNESCO World Heritage list in 1998. Interestingly Carche rock art site includes a signiﬁcant number of complex superimpositions, which are key to ﬁgure out rock art sequences between different traditions (Levantine and Schematic arts) and within each of them. During the recording process with conventional methods (i.e. modiﬁcation of hue, brightness and contrast, and the subsequent use of colour selection tools to produce digital drawings or tracings for each motif), we experienced some problems that turned this site into the perfect case study to test image enhancement techniques:  First, it was difﬁcult to visualize highly eroded motifs and to deﬁne their limits, especially since most of them are included in complex superimpositions. I. Domingo et al. / Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 79–88 80  Second, the use of conventional digital rock art recording methods to individualize each motif was certainly time consuming, namely with heavily deteriorated and fainted motifs. For this test study we focused on two speciﬁc motifs (Fig. 3):  Motif 8, corresponding to a Levantine naturalistic horse facing right, depicted in red solid inﬁll. It includes anatomical details such as the ears and the mane, which are difﬁcult to identify both in situ and on the visual light photographs, because it is involved in a complex superimposition with motifs 6 (undeﬁned quadruped) and 9 (a male deer). In this case, with the use of DS and PCA we aim for a better deﬁnition of the outline and the details of this ﬁgure.  Motif 11, corresponding to a typical Schematic anthropomorphic motif composed by a vertical thick bar representing the body, and two semi-circles representing the upper and lower extremities. This motif is also involved in a superimposition with motif 10 (a naturalistic Levantine deer depicted in dark red solid inﬁll). Our interest in this case was to better identify the limits of the body of motif 11, and to distinguish which of them was on top of the other. A Fujiﬁlm IS PRO digital camera with a visible ﬁlter was used to take colour images. Two shots were taken, one for each motif. 3. Results In this section the process followed for implementing each of the techniques discussed above is described. The techniques were only applied to a few ﬁgures: motifs 8 and 11. Once the decorrelated images were obtained, a statistical analysis was performed in order to calculate the success rate of the method and which of the two techniques for decorrelation image analysis provides better results. 3.1. Conventional image enhancement techniques The ﬁrst step in the rock art recording process consists of producing individual records (a digital drawing or tracing) for each motif using high-resolution digital photographs. The aim of this document is to isolate the motif depicted from the rock surface, to focus our attention on the details represented. The resulting documents are extremely useful to facilitate motif description and comparison with any others (Domingo et al., 2013a). Conventional tools such as Saturation, Selective Colour, Curves, Hue, Levels, Brightness, Contrast, and Channel Mixer (e.g. in Adobe Photoshop or the GNU Image Manipulation Program GIMP), can be used to enhance faded rock paintings (Brady, 2006, 2007). The enhanced images can be converted to black-and-white to highlight additional features. Similarly, colour selection tools such as Colour Range and Magic Wand tools can be used to make a selection based on tonal or colour values (i.e. select pixels within a similar colour range). To select colours similar or within a certain range of the pigment to be extracted, low tolerance values (15 to 20%) were entered in the Fuzziness value (Colour Range command) or in the Tolerance option (Magic Wand tool). The areas selected are then pasted into a new layer, repeating the process until the entire ﬁgure has been extracted or traced (Domingo and LópezMontalvo, 2002). To achieve better results it is advisable to select and manipulate small portions of a ﬁgure at a time using the Lasso tool, instead of trying to select the entire ﬁgure at once (Fig. 4) (Domingo, 2004, 2007). Fig. 2. Geographic location and overall photograph of Carche rock art site. In red the area where Levantine rock art appears within the Iberian Peninsula. Fig. 1. Digital image analysis workﬂow followed to assess PCA and DS performance. I. Domingo et al. / Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 79–88 81 Using these automatic selection tools, the colour selection process is almost automatized once selection values have been set up, since based on these values the program automatically makes a selection of areas with similar colour to the pixel we have initially selected. However, human intervention is still needed since often the selection will include pixels from shadows, cracks in the wall or the adjoining rock surface with similar chromatic ranges to the motifs. These intrusions must be erased manually by comparing the tracings against the original images. The resulting documents are scaled to their real size and the quality of the tracings is visually checked in situ against the original paintings. Any pigment missing is added in the lab (Domingo and López-Montalvo, 2002; Domingo, 2004, 2007). 3.2. Decorrelation image analysis techniques Applying some of the decorrelation techniques explained in Section 2 to the two visible images before starting the colour selection process, colour differences between the pigments and the rock surface can be emphasized. In the resulting images the different pigments can be more easily identiﬁed, and even some alterations of the rock surface. Therefore, the resultant image is useful for both rock art recording and conservation analysis. Ideally the next step, i.e. the colour selection process to produce tracings from the decorrelated images, should be done with automatic contour extraction tools, in order to reduce human intervention, to increase accuracy and to accelerate the process. However, it was not possible for this site since motifs were part of complex superimpositions, and thus human interpretation was required to decide which pigment belonged to each motif. 3.2.1. Principal component analysis to discriminate motif 8 Motif 8 is difﬁcult to recognize since the pigment is faded and shows several shades of red due to weathering. Furthermore (as noted above), this motif was involved in a complex superimposition. Hence image colour decorrelation techniques are very appropriate to facilitate visualization and digital tracing. After applying PCA three new components are obtained (Fig. 5) from the RGB spectral bands of the visible image. This step is fully automatic. The ﬁrst principal component (PC1) does not show relevant information. The second principal component (PC2) enhances the horse outline. The third principal component (PC3) facilitates the identiﬁcation of the body and the front legs of the horse, as well as the pigments corresponding to other motifs. The composition of false colour images using these new components delivered new colourful images of great interest for motif identiﬁcation. Fig. 6b shows the image divided into different colours (R: PC1, G: PC2, B: PC3). In this image Blue and Green colours deﬁne the horse, and its ears and mane are well deﬁned. Colour pink represents areas where the rock surface has ﬂaked away, and thus where the pigments have vanished. From the composition shown in Fig. 6b we produced a digital tracing. In this case the automatic processing was not possible, because the motif was superimposed. Hence, it was necessary to manually separate the pigments corresponding to the different motifs. Manual selection of colour was made following the process described in Section 3.1. Green and blue colours were selected to deﬁne the motif. The use of the false colour image facilitated the selection of the pigment corresponding to the horse (Fig. 7a), thus obtaining a more reliable result. Finally, as in the conventional techniques, it was necessary to compare the tracings against the original images in order to Fig. 3. Site panel showing the location of the motifs under study. I. Domingo et al. / Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 79–88 82 provide an accurate representation of the motif. In this sense, pixels corresponding to shadows and cracks in the wall were deleted. It would have been ideal to use appropriate lighting during data acquisition to minimize the appearance of shadows. 3.2.2. Decorrelation stretch to discriminate motif 8 This technique highlights very small differences within the image. Similar colours in the original image are highly differentiated in the output image. The result is a colourful image where a Fig. 4. Main steps in the conventional digital recording process: (a) Visible light image. (b) Portion of the motif selected to start the colour selection process. (c) First selection. (d) Final tracing. Fig. 5. Three new principal components obtained after applying PCA: (a) PC1. (b) PC2. (c) PC3. I. Domingo et al. / Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 79–88 83 great number of differences can be appreciated. Decorrelation stretching is a fully automatic tool. After applying DS, a new colourful image was obtained (Fig. 6c), in which motifs are deﬁned by pink and purple colours. Following the same process described above from this new image, pink and purple colours were manually selected to produce digital tracings. The problem with this new image is that the information is very fragmented and the limits of the motifs cannot be easily identiﬁed. Thus, the resulting digital tracing was incomplete, since the head and the neck of the horse are not well deﬁned (Fig. 7b). 3.2.3. Statistical analysis of motif 8 To test the performance of each technique, a maximum-likelihood supervised classiﬁcation was carried out for Fig. 7a and b considering only two classes: pigment and no pigment. After classiﬁcation, a statistical analysis of both ﬁgures is obtained. The most common way to represent the classiﬁcation accuracy of remotely sensed data is in the form of an error matrix (Congalton, 1991). An error matrix is a square array of numbers set out in rows and columns which express either the number of sample units or percentage assigned to a particular category relative to the actual category (truth data). Herein tracing obtained by conventional techniques is considered as ideal tracing (i.e. truth data). The results are presented in error matrix form (Tables 1 and 2) where the overall accuracy, errors of commission and omission, producer’s accuracy and user’s accuracy are presented. The overall classiﬁcation accuracy achieved with both techniques (PCA and DS) is above 99 per cent. Thus, both techniques are considered valid to facilitate the identiﬁcation of the motifs. The overall accuracy obtained is nearly 100 per cent. Almost all image pixels were correctly classiﬁed; only 1% was classiﬁed as no pigment when corresponding to pigment class. DS technique also achieved a very high accuracy (99.6%). Nevertheless, a 10% commission error was made when determining pigment class, i.e. 10% of the pixels classiﬁed as pigment do not belong to the pigment class. If you look at the user’s accuracy, 89.8% of the pixels classiﬁed as pigment, really belong to this class. The user’s accuracy obtained with PCA is 98%. Thus a better classiﬁcation of the pigment class was obtained with the PCA technique. These differences can be visually appreciated in Fig. 7a and b. Fig. 7a shows perfectly the ﬁgure of a horse with its ears and mane but in Fig. 7b, the ears and mane are not represented, since they were not well deﬁned in the Dstretch image (Fig. 6c). On the contrary this area is well deﬁned in Fig. 6b obtained with PCA. Fig. 6. Colour vs. false colour decorrelated images: (a) RGB image. (b) PC1/PC2/PC3. (c) DS. Fig. 7. Final ﬁgure obtained from the false colour composition: (a) PCA. (b) DS. I. Domingo et al. / Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 79–88 84 3.2.4. Principal component analysis to discriminate motif 11 Once again we intend to isolate a complicated ﬁgure involved in a superimposition. After applying the PCA technique, motif 11 is quite distinct in component 3. Using the false colour composition of the obtained components (Fig. 8a) and Gaussian enhancement, motif 11 is discriminated. Afterwards, the motif is isolated using colour selection tools. Given the complexity of this motif, it is impossible to automate the contour. However, the false colour image obtained facilitates the selection of pixels corresponding to the paintings (Fig. 8b), thus obtaining more accurate results. 3.2.5. Decorrelation stretch to discriminate motif 11 DS technique is applied to the visible image (Fig. 9a), obtaining three new bands. Again, the false colour composite image can be used to better discriminate the motif (Fig. 9b). Nevertheless, the pigment corresponding to other motifs is also emphasized and thus it does not facilitate the isolation of those parts of the motif involved in the superimposition. In order to separate the rock surface and the motif, its contour is manually generated (Fig. 10a). Finally this contour is used to select the pigment corresponding to the motif in the original colour image. The idea is to display a more realistic digital tracing showing the original colour of the motif (Fig. 10b). It should be noted that once again the automatic contouring does not work well for this ﬁgure because the image includes other pigments with similar shades corresponding to other motifs that the software cannot automatically discriminate. 3.2.6. Statistical analysis of motif 11 To analyse the accuracy of the applied techniques, the same process explained in Section 3.2.3 is followed. The tracing obtained by conventional techniques is considered as ideal tracing (truth data), and it is compared with the supervised maximum-likelihood classiﬁcations used to obtain motifs presented in Figs. 8 and 10b. After classiﬁcation, a statistical analysis of both ﬁgures is obtained. The overall accuracy obtained with both techniques is over 99% (Tables 3 and 4). Thus, a priori both techniques can be considered as valid to identify and isolate painted motifs. In this case, the Table 2 Error matrix form for accuracy assessment (DS). With PCA technique there is an error of omission of 19% in determining the pigment class. With DS technique a very high accuracy level is also reached (99.7%), with an error of omission of 24.82% for the pigment class. However, the user accuracy is 100% in both techniques. All of the pixels classiﬁed as pigment actually belong to this class. Also a producer’s accuracy of 100% in the no pigment class was obtained following both techniques. Overall, the results achieved with both techniques are good, although PCA technique offers slightly better results. In both cases it is observed that a percentage of pixels (roughly 20%), is not classiﬁed as pigment when they are indeed. These differences can be visually conﬁrmed after comparing the tracings obtained by conventional techniques with those obtained by image decorrelation techniques. 4. Discussion Conventional computer image enhancement have been used over the last three decades to record different sorts of rock art, to improve accuracy, to avoid direct contact with both the art and the rock surface (Domingo and López-Montalvo, 2002) and more interestingly to recover images that are no longer (or hardly) visible to the naked eye (David et al., 2001; David, 2004; Brady, 2006). A step further can be followed with the careful application of advanced image enhancement decorrelation techniques. After the statistical analysis presented herein, it can be conﬁrmed that the two automatic decorrelation techniques (PCA and DS) tested can be employed to enhance painted motifs as a precursor to the elaboration of digital tracings through colour selection tools. The DS technique is useful sometimes, but it is not as good as PCA because there is no way to control image enhancement, which in some cases is very important. This technique applies a default enhancement setting that sometimes is not required by the image. On the contrary, the analysis of the image using PCA techniques allows users to choose the false colour combination and the most appropriate enhancement approach. The use of both techniques conﬁrms that the DS technique differentiates in excess the various image hues. The resulting images are extremely colourful, hindering the interpretation of the different colours. While it is certainly useful to identify nearly invisible motifs or details, it also means that the user can get false impressions of motifs. Therefore, careful inspection of the achieved results is highly recommended. On the contrary, the distinction between pigment and non-pigment areas is clearer in the images obtained through PCA. For example, in Fig. 6b three main classes can be identiﬁed: pigment, non-pigment and areas where the rock surface has ﬂaked off. The colour selection process used to produce digital tracings of rock art paintings is faster and more accurate when using the decorrelated images resulting from these two techniques. If the paintings had ﬂat colours it would be possible to automatically isolate them from the rock surface. However, most of the paintings preserved in open-air sites usually present various colour hues, and thus the automatic extraction is not possible. Furthermore, if the images are part of a superimposition with other paintings, the ﬁgures can only be manually isolated. In the latter case, the execution time is increased. Image analysis techniques based on decorrelation are useful whenever visual correlations exist. Conventional methods are good for analysing ﬁgures with low correlation, but when the discrimination of the rock surface and the paintings is difﬁcult due to poor preservation, deterioration, or when different ﬁgures were produced using similar pigments, the use of conventional techniques can be time consuming. The multiscalar nature of the conventional computer enhancement techniques certainly provides signiﬁcant improvements to record rock art assemblages that would have been missed using conventional recording processes (Brady, 2006). But the results can be improved further if these techniques are combined with fully automatic decorrelation techniques. Fully automatic image analysis decorrelation techniques can be used to help professional rock art recorders and archaeologists to achieve more objective results in less time mainly when single visible imagery was the primary source of information. 5. Conclusions This paper analyses the use of non-invasive digital image analysis techniques to facilitate and accelerate the process of tracing painted rock art. The image analysis techniques used were principal component analysis (PCA) and decorrelation stretch (DS). Both techniques apply a decorrelation to the information contained in the images. The use of these techniques is recommended because it facilitates the identiﬁcation of motifs without impacting directly on them. Furthermore, the areas containing pigment can be more objectively discriminated. As shown in this study, the application of these decorrelation techniques does not cause any loss of information. Furthermore, the results are very satisfactory when statistically compared to those obtained by conventional computer image enhancement techniques. Thus, pre-processing the images and preparing them for manual or automatic selection of colours with either PCA or DS accelerates the recording process. Both PCA and DS improve the ability of a human viewer to interpret the image, especially when motifs are faded, or part of complex superimpositions. Hence, whenever possible, they should become an integral part of the recording process. Statistical analyses revealed that PCA offers better results than DS (around 5%), since DS technique marks excessively the various hues of the image, and details become blurred. However, while archaeologists can use DS by their own thanks to the plugin Dstretch (ImageJ), the use of PCA requires image skills on remotely sense data. The new images obtained by these two decorrelation methods become the basis to produce tracings, allowing users to accelerating the documentation process. To produce the tracings automatic contour is the ideal. However, when dealing with complex superimpositions, only manual selection is possible as shown in this paper. Thus this part of the process is not completely automatic and the Fig. 8. False colour vs. conventional processing: (a) False colour image with PC3 / PC2 / PC1. (b) Manual selection. I. Domingo et al. / Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 79–88 86 experience of a rock art expert is required for image interpretation. If automatic contouring tools are improved in the future, both the execution times and the subjectivity will certainly decrease. In the worst-case scenario the results obtained with these two decorrelation techniques are similar to those obtained by conventional image enhancement techniques. In consequence, it is worth using them at least to accelerate the identiﬁcation and recording process. title: Independent component analysis (ICA): A statistical approach to the analysis of superimposed rock paintings
abstract: Independent Component Analysis (ICA) is a statistical technique for decomposing information from datasets into maximally independent components. ICA allows the researcher to recover two or more independent signals that appear mixed within the same dataset. This paper shows ICA to be an extremely effective method for separating different colours found in rock paintings into discrete images or components. The comparison between the results of ICA and PCA (Principal Component Analysis) shows that ICA accurately separates panels with more than one type of colour, while PCA achieves a lower degree of separation. This study also shows that in scenes with monochrome depictions, ICA tends to be slightly more effective in separating the pigments from the rock. The ICA method has been applied successfully to several rock art panels from Northern Chile, where the use of diverse types of mineral pigments is common. Two analyses conducted at the Pampa El Muerto 11 site in the Northern Chilean highlands reveal how ICA can contribute to a more compelling interpretation of more intricate panels. The comparison between the results of ICA and PCA (Principal Components Analysis) shows that ICA correctly separates panels with more than one type of pigment, while PCA achieves a lower degree of separation. This study also shows that in scenes with monochrome depictions, ICA tends to be slightly more effective in separating the pigments from the rock. ICA algorithm has been successfully in several rock panels from Northern Chile, where the use of diverse types of mineral pigments is usual. Two panels from the Pampa El Muerto site have been analysed with the technique mentioned above, informing that its application can collaborate on a more compelling interpretation of intricate panels.

Independent component analysis (ICA): A statistical approach to the analysis of superimposed rock paintings Enrique Cerrillo-Cuenca a,*, Marcela Sepúlveda b, Zaray Guerrero-Bueno c a Departamento de Prehistoria, Historia Antigua y Arqueología, Universidad Complutense de Madrid, Spain b Escuela de Antropologia, Pontificia Universidad Cat´olica de Chile (Chile), UMR8096 ArchAm (CNRS-Paris 1), France c Independent Researcher, Chile A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Rock art Independent component analysis Statistics Pigments Image processing A B S T R A C T Independent Component Analysis (ICA) is a statistical technique for decomposing information from datasets into maximally independent components. ICA allows the researcher to recover two or more independent signals that appear mixed within the same dataset. This paper shows ICA to be an extremely effective method for separating different colours found in rock paintings into discrete images or components. The comparison between the results of ICA and PCA (Principal Component Analysis) shows that ICA accurately separates panels with more than one type of colour, while PCA achieves a lower degree of separation. This study also shows that in scenes with monochrome depictions, ICA tends to be slightly more effective in separating the pigments from the rock. The ICA method has been applied successfully to several rock art panels from Northern Chile, where the use of diverse types of mineral pigments is common. Two analyses conducted at the Pampa El Muerto 11 site in the Northern Chilean highlands reveal how ICA can contribute to a more compelling interpretation of more intricate panels. The comparison between the results of ICA and PCA (Principal Components Analysis) shows that ICA correctly separates panels with more than one type of pigment, while PCA achieves a lower degree of separation. This study also shows that in scenes with monochrome depictions, ICA tends to be slightly more effective in separating the pigments from the rock. ICA algorithm has been successfully in several rock panels from Northern Chile, where the use of diverse types of mineral pigments is usual. Two panels from the Pampa El Muerto site have been analysed with the technique mentioned above, informing that its application can collaborate on a more compelling interpretation of intricate panels. 1. Introduction Rock art studies are benefiting from an increasing array of methods to digitally enhance paintings, as the vast literature on the subject shows (McNiven et al., 2000; Brady and Gunn 2012; Cerrillo-Cuenca et al., 2014; Domingo et al., 2013; Le Quellec et al., 2015; Quesada Martínez and Harman, 2019; Rogerio-Candelera, 2015). Several authors have proven the advantages of applying such methods in rock art documen­ tation (Ruiz L´opez, 2019), especially in avoiding direct contact with the painted surfaces. Additionally, digital enhancement of depicted motifs has also become an essential part of many archaeological projects (Le Quellec et al., 2015; Rogerio-Candelera, 2015), providing a more precise understanding of rock panels by deciphering their composition, eluci­ dating stylistic aspects, and clarifying overlapping depictions. Some papers have introduced pigment-enhancing methods, many based on statistical analyses of the images in question (Cerrillo-Cuenca and Sepúlveda, 2015; Quesada Martínez and Harman, 2019; Roger­ io-Candelera, 2015). Among these novel approaches, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) (Abdi and Williams, 2010; Jolliffe, 2002) is considered one of the most effective techniques for drawing paintings out from the background, as previously mentioned (Cerrillo-Cuenca et al., 2014; Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2011). The successful application of PCA success in enhancing rock art images is due to its ability to separate pigments from the background into a new set of images. It does this by assuming that in­ formation contained in a dataset (an RGB image) becomes more legible when projected onto a new space as components (convertible to new greyscale images). PCA and comparable procedures can be used as part of more complex procedures such as Decorrelation Stretch (Campbell, 1996; Gillespie et al., 1986)—the method popularized in the DStretch * Corresponding author. New procedures can quickly advance developing fields such as dig­ ital rock art studies, and PCA is a particularly powerful one. It is part of a family of statistical tools designed for breaking down information, including some suited to enhancing images. In this paper, we focus on a similar tool, Independent Component Analysis (ICA) (Comon, 1994; Hyv¨arinen and Oja, 2000; Tharwat, 2018), which emerged from the field of statistical signal processing. The main difference between PCA and ICA is that the former reduces the dimensionality of the data, while the latter aims to separate super­ imposed signals, returning maximally independent components that are also convertible to greyscale images. Hyv¨arinen and Oja (2000) devel­ oped ICA to denoise images, but other investigative fields have employed the method for different purposes, such as medical imaging analysis (Adali et al., 2014). Onta˜n´on et al. (2019) also apply ICA to Palaeolithic rock art recently discovered in Cantabria (Spain) but fail to discuss the results of this technique. Our goal here is to assess the out­ comes of the ICA method and compare them with those obtained from PCA. To achieve this, we have tested the procedure on a set of images from Pampa El Muerto 11 (PM-11), a site in the Andean foothills of Northern Chile that we have reported on previous papers (Cerrillo-­ Cuenca and Sepúlveda, 2015). 2. Methods 2.1. Image processing The RGB images were taken with a conventional Nikon D90 camera in the RAW format during a fieldwork campaign in Northern Chile in 2014. We processed the images through the rawpy library for the Python programming language (version 3), which is, in fact, a binding to the LibRaw library. This step was needed to obtain TIFF images. The colour temperature and the white balance were uncorrected, and the colour- depth kept at 16-bit, as is usual in our work (Cerrillo-Cuenca and Sepúlveda, 2015). For the present study, we produced orthoimages in Photoscan software, following the current state-of-art for photogram­ metric restitution (Fig. 1). The rest of the workflow was developed in Python, using libraries such as the scikit-learn statistical package for Python (Pedregosa et al., 2011). 2.2. Independent Component Analysis (ICA) The ICA algorithm employed in this study comes from the scikit- learn Python library. This module employs a procedure known as Fas­ tICA, which is faster than the original ICA method. A detailed expla­ nation of ICA can be found in the original paper (Hyv¨arinen and Oja 2000) and some more recent ones (Tharwat, 2018); here, we have included only a general description of the method and some of its particularities. Let X be a matrix where two or more independent non-Gaussian signals are mixed. In our case, X is an array of 3 bi-dimensional matrices with an m, n shape, where m and n are the length and width of the image in pixels, respectively. Each of the matrices mentioned above represents one channel of an RGB digital image. Since the non- Gaussian nature of the distributions is mandatory for the application of the FastICA algorithm, we tested the normality of each of the X1-3 matrices through D’Agostino’s K-squared test (D’Agostino, 1971). This normality test is suitable for large-scale data such as this, which includes a high number of pixel values from the bands of the image. D’Agostino’s K-squared test employs kurtosis and skewness to determine if the data distribution falls into a normal (gaussian) distribution. For the same purpose, we performed the Anderson-Darling test to supplement our results. In short, X is the product of two matrices, S and A, where S is the signal and A the linear mixing matrix. The “signals” can be understood as distinct colour shades, among them those that comprise the pigments, so that X = S•A. A is any other factor not related to the paintings, the rock texture for example. Finally, let X be an image formed by an array of three matrices, one for each RGB channel. Before continuing, it was necessary to transform the three matrices into an equivalent number of vectors formed by m x n cases, since the FastICA algorithm can only accept vectors as input. Before processing the vectors, they were centred by subtracting their respective arithmetic means and whitened by projecting the data onto their principal com­ ponents’ direction. After these transformations, the application of the FastICA returned a W matrix that dissociates the signals, such that W • X = S The result is an array S, with an equal number of vectors that of X that hold the FastICA independent components. These resulting vectors were reshaped into matrices with the original dimensions of the image (m x n pixels) and converted to 8-bit greyscale images. Thus, the Figure 1. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) As mentioned above, PCA is one of the most widely applied statistical methods used in the field of Archaeology, particularly for the enhancement of rock art images (Cerrillo-Cuenca et al., 2014; Domingo et al., 2013; Rogerio Candelera et al., 2010). It is also incorporated into algorithms such as Decorrelation Stretch (Quesada Martínez and Har­ man, 2019) and the selective intensification of components (Cerrillo-­ Cuenca and Sepúlveda, 2015). Its application in rock art studies has been discussed in detail else­ where (Cerrillo-Cuenca et al., 2014). Essentially, PCA reprojects the samples or cases contained in a multidimensional dataset into a new space where they gain more prominence. The new variables or compo­ nents are: a) uncorrelated among them, b) retain a linear relationship with the original ones; and c) are formed without losing any information (Jolliffe, 2002). Regarding image processing, PCA maximises the infor­ mation contained in highly correlated image channels into new bands (Gillespie et al., 1986). As in the case of FastICA, the red, green a blue matrices were flat­ tened to three vectors. Following the well-known PCA procedure, after estimating the covariance matrix, the eigenvalues are calculated to project the vectors into new dimensions. These new vectors are the PCA components that need to be reshaped into their original m x n shape to form the greyscale images. In PCA, components are ordered by the amount of explained variance that they retain from the original variables. Therefore, the first component holds a higher percentage of explained variance, while the last usually maintains a very low percentage. Several rock art studies have concluded that the components with less explained variance usu­ ally reveal information about the paintings (Cerrillo-Cuenca et al., 2014; Rogerio-Candelera et al., 2011). Similar behaviour has also been noticed in the processing of multispectral remote sensing images, where the last PCA components usually gather the noise from the image (Levin, 1999). 2.4. Analytical comparison of results The differences between ICA and PCA consist of two ambiguities, as noted by Hyv¨arinen and Oja (2000): in ICA, it is not possible to deter­ mine either the variances of the independent components, or their order, and thus is impossible to assess how much of the variability in the source datasets holds the derived components. In our case, it became impos­ sible to decipher to what extent one resulting image was associated with the original one. To assess the results (Fig. 2), we employed two co­ efficients: MS-SSIM (Multiscalar Structural Similarity Index) and MSE (Mean Squared Error). MS-SSIM is based on the SSIM (Structural Simi­ larity Index). Both SSIM and MSE are common digital image processing techniques that can complement each other (Tan et al., 2013). MSE tends to be less correlated with the visual perception of image quality than SSIM (Zhou Wang and Bovik, 2009). Both procedures have been recurrently used in image processing, as a vast literature attest to (Lin and Jay Kuo, 2011; Mittal et al., 2012; Sheikh et al., 2006; Wang and Li, 2011). SSIM is a common technique used in image processing for comparing two images (Sampat et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2003). SSIM is based upon the assessment of three elements: “the similarity of the local patch luminance (brightness values), the similarity c(x, y) of the local patch contrasts, and the similarity s(x, y) of the local patch structures” (Zhou Wang and Bovik, 2009, p. 105). We employed the algorithm imple­ mented in MATLAB, which returns an SSIM value in the interval [0, 1]. Its interpretation is also straightforward: the higher the SSIM, the more shared information both images retain. Since the image scale can affect the ensuing SSIM index, we used the MS-SSIM, a modification that returns a global value for the image estimated at different scales (Wang et al., 2003). MS-SSIM equation (Wang et al., 2003) is: SSIM(x, y) = [lM(x, y)]αM⋅ ∏M j=i [ cj(x, y) ]βj [ sj(x, y) ]γj where x and y are two discrete non-negative signals (the component and the source image in our case) that are spatially aligned. α, β and γ are parameters to define the relative importance of l (luminance) computed at the M scale, c (contrast) and s (structure). The MS-SSIM index can be mapped in a new image that visually assesses the differences between two images. The mapping of MS-SSIM is especially convenient since the global SSIM renders a unique value for the whole image, which avoids understanding the distribution of local differences in the image. Mapping these local differences in a new image can be useful for appreciating the differences between the original image and the components. Furthermore, we evaluate the relationships between PCA and ICA components through the calculation of the Mean Squared Error (MSE), which is a classic and standard method for assessing image quality (Hall Fig. 2. A visual abstract of the workflow followed in the statistical comparison of images. E. Cerrillo-Cuenca et al. Journal of Archaeological Science 125 (2021) 105269 4 and Dwyer, 1972). MSE equation is: MSE = 1 n ∑ n i=1 ( yi −˜yi )2 where ˜y is a vector of n predictions and y is a vector of observed values of the same length. Despite its intrinsic issues (Zhou Wang and Bovik, 2009), the calculation of MSE is nonetheless straightforward. It consists of summing the squared difference between two images, which returns a value that summarises the likeness between them. The lower the MSE is, the more different the two vectors (images) are. However, as MSE does not supply an absolute value like MS-SSIM does, we have based the interpretation on the comparison between the greyscale reference image and the rest of the components. To do so, we produced a grey image from the orthoimage’s original RGB bands employing a weighted sum of the matrices, using the typical weights in digital photography: G = (r • 0.2989) ​ + (g • 0.587) ​ + (b • 0.114) where G is the resultant grey image, r is the red band, g is the green band, and b the blue one from the same RGB image. 2.5. PM-11 site We selected two panels from the same site to assess the method’s potential. PM-11 is a site located at 3000 m above sea level in the highlands of Northern Chile. The images there recently underwent digital image enhancement (Guerrero-Bueno and Sepúlveda, 2018). The site includes an incredibly complex panel (panel 1) with several over­ lapped paintings attributed to different stylistic groups in the regional archaeological sequence. The attributes analysed at different scales at PM-11 show two contrasting patterns of representation or stylistic groups (SG) (Guerrero-Bueno and Sepúlveda, 2018; Sepúlveda, 2011; Sepúlveda et al., 2013). Two levels of overlap were observed: at the basal level (SG1), yellow and orange motifs were found with red motifs superimposed upon them (SG2). The SG-1 motifs include anthropomorphic depictions represented by small, thin traces placed next to camelids with detailed anatomical features. Both kinds of figures are organised in hunting scenes, with Fig. 5. Panel 1 from PM-11. Details of new detected figures in first and third ICA components. Fig. 6. Comparison between the tracing published in this paper (up) and the published by Guerrero and Sepúlveda (2018). In the upper part, the new figures have been highlighted in red. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.) E. Cerrillo-Cuenca et al. Journal of Archaeological Science 125 (2021) 105269 6 zoomorphic ones exceeding human representations markedly in both number and size. SG-1 corresponds to the oldest phase of representation, the naturalist tradition, based on similarities in theme, scenic compo­ sition, and use of colour with other painted sites in neighbouring regions (Sepúlveda, 2011; Sepúlveda et al., 2013). SG-1 can be attributed to the Late Archaic, based on three 14C dates from PM-11 and nearby rock shelters such as PM-8 and PM-12 (Dudognon and Sepúlveda, 2018; Sepúlveda et al, 2013, 2019). SG-2 displays a preference for anthropomorphic motifs notably larger than those from SG-1, along with the occasional presence of personal ornaments and clothing. Holding long items in their hands, the figures are scenically linked to less detailed representations of camelids in static poses (Guerrero-Bueno and Sepúlveda, 2018; Sepúlveda, 2011; Sepúlveda et al., 2013). The characteristics of the scenes and motifs suggest the association of domestic camelids with pastoralist groups. It is clear, then, that graphic activity in the Pampa El Muerto sector began at the end of the Archaic period (ca. 6000 - 3700 BP) (Guerrer­ o-Bueno and Sepúlveda 2018), in concert with more intensive occupa­ tion of the Precordillera by hunter-gatherer groups. Following this, pastoralist groups painted their own representations directly over the hunting scenes, depicting camelids lined up and featuring prominently human figures, which appear more detailed and lifelike, and taking a leading role over the animals. These representations are associated with the Formative period (ca.3700-1500 BP) (Guerrero-Bueno and Sepúl­ veda, 2018). In PM-11, Panel 1 is the central and most complex panel and is located to the left of the shelter entrance. It has several superimposed layers of anthropomorphic, animalistic, and geometric figures in red (SG-2), orange and yellowish hues (SG-1). Some of the scenes are currently only barely visible, as several areas have been affected by weathering of the pigments. Additionally, the superimposition of motifs hinders the recognition of several figures to the naked eye. On the right, Panel 2 features a scene, painted in red pigment and consisting of an anthropomorphic figure interacting with smaller animals that are distinguishable by their lines of footprints. 3. Results 3.1. PM-11: panel 1 The orthoimage (Fig. 3) has a size of 3058 × 10,093 pixels and resolution of 0.3 mm/pixel (see Appendix 1 for general descriptive statistics). The distribution of the pixel values of the three bands shows negative skewness, a symptom of non-normal distribution (Appendix Fig. 7. Journal of Archaeological Science 125 (2021) 105269 7 1.1.1), which is confirmed by both the D’Agostino and Anderson-Darling tests (appendix 1.2). The correlation matrix revealed a high correlation between all three bands in the 0.98–0.88 interval (Appendix 1, Table 2). The dataset meets the requirements of the FastICA algorithm if we consider the preceding results. The first ICA component (Fig. 4) returned the best results in sepa­ rating the paintings from the rock. This component gathers all the in­ formation about red hues. Orangish shades are present in this component, too, perhaps due to their chromatic affinity with red. Camelids, anthropomorphic figures, and other symbolic motifs are visible in this component. Three camelids have almost vanished on the left side of the image. Finally, we must mention the presence of an anthropomorphic representation in the upper left section of the leading group. To its right, along with three vertical parallel lines, we detected a hardly recognisable anthropomorphic figure with an accompanying zoomorphic one (Fig. 5). The latter figures went unnoticed in the last review published by two of the authors (Guerrero-Bueno and Sepúlveda, 2018) (Fig. 6), which confirms that the method described here makes it possible to recover additional information from the same sources (images). The second ICA component (Fig. 4) reflects the variability in the colour and texture of the rock texture. In this component, the traces of paintings are unclear. The third independent component (Fig. 4) gathers the information about orangish and yellowish pigments but also some of the variability in the natural colours of the rock. The orangish and yellowish paintings consist of naturalistic camelids, already visible to the naked eye, and some small anthropomorphic figures whose foot­ prints are also perceptible. The latter type of representation was not visible to the naked eye. The application of PCA reveals some different results. The first PCA component gathers 95.5% of the explained variance from the original image, and so mixes “signals” from the rock texture and the paintings. The second PCA component reduces its explained variance to 3.78%, and the derived greyscale image combines red, yellowish, and orangish shades in the same resulting image. Lastly, the third component (0.32% of the explained variance) includes some of the rock texture. Red paintings can be perceived, while orangish shades are not present. Yellowish hues appear in a colour different from the red figures, which could be a sign of the separation of the two shades of colour. It is worth noting that some of the red coloured shapes have virtually “vanished” in this third component, such as the anthropomorphic figure standing in the lower right part of the scene. The amount of explained variance for each PCA component is consistent with the values returned by MSE and MS-MSSIM. Therefore, MSE and MS-SSIM decrease as explained vari­ ance does. PM-11: panel 2 The orthoimage of this panel was generated at a resolution of 0.5 mm/pixel (Fig. 8), with a size of 2045 × 3285 pixels. Appendix 2 gathers all descriptive statistics about the information contained in the image channels. The results from D’Agostino and Anderson-Darling tests (p- value lower than 0.05) (Appendix 2.2.) show that the values from the 3 RGB channels did not follow a normal distribution. The correlation be­ tween the bands of the image was estimated, showing highly positive coefficients among all bands, always higher than 0.85 (Appendix 2, section 2.1.2). The three greyscale images obtained showed an effective decorre­ lation of the information (Fig. 9). The first ICA component stands for the features of the rock, where the paintings are not recognisable, except for a zoomorphic figure in the lower left part of the panel. The second ICA component shows a neat separation between the rock and the red paintings. The scene can be perceived, and the traces of the depicted figures are clear enough. The two possible signals in the image, the rock, and the paintings, appear mixed in the third ICA component. Regarding PCA components, the rendered images behave as ex­ pected. The first component condenses a high value of the explained variance (see Appendix 2.3) and yields a graphic output that mixes both the paintings and the rock. The second PCA component shows a similar result, despite a lower explained variance (4.74%). The third component reveals a neat separation between the rock and the paintings with a minimal 0.16% of explained variance. Finally, the results from MSE and MS-SSIM (Appendix 2.4.) show similar behaviour for PCA and for the FastICA method. This outcome is somehow predictable since the signal of the monochrome painting is indivisible in more than one component, and we can expect almost indistinguishable outcomes from PCA and from ICA (Fig. 10). The MSE values are quite similar for the third ICA and first PCA components, which is coherent with the element represented in them. The MS-SSIM value, close to one, also confirms the strong association between the greyscale image and both components. The mapping of MS-SSIM (Fig. 11) reveals how values close to 1 are predominant in PCA 1, while in ICA 3 the paintings seem to have been subtracted from the picture showing lower MS-SSIM values and less coincidence. Regarding the components where the paintings appear cleanly rep­ resented (third PCA and second ICA components), these supply consis­ tent results both in MSE (1996 and 1917, respectively) and MS-SSIM (0.612 and 0.594, respectively). The mapping of MS-SSIM in both im­ ages is also quite similar. Although the first ICA and the second PCA components hold visually akin information, their indices show more variability between them, which is undoubtedly due to the uncertainty in the classification of pixels in the second ICA components that mix information from two different signals. 4. Discussion When comparing the effectiveness of PCA and FastICA, we can contemplate different variables. The main disadvantage of FastICA is that the mathematical reasoning behind the algorithm is less Fig. 11. Mapping of MS-SSIM for the components of the panel 2 from PM-11. E. Cerrillo-Cuenca et al. Journal of Archaeological Science 125 (2021) 105269 9 comprehensible for non-specialists in algebraic operations and image processing than PCA. It is also worth mentioning that, contrary to PCA, the ICA technique does not return numerical outputs in the form of coefficients such as eigenvectors, eigenvalues, or explained variance. These coefficients supply useful information about the resulting images and their relationship with the RGB channels. If we consider the results of MSE and MS-SSIM for PCA components, we can conclude that both coefficients are slightly associated to the explained variance of each component. PCA components with higher ratios of explained variance tend to be statistically less divergent from the source image. Regarding the resulting images, the FastICA results are neater than PCA. In PCA, the information from paintings is usually condensed in the second and third components, while in FastICA it is isolated in non- ordered components. In the case of monochrome compositions, the in­ formation from pigments is only present in a single ICA component, or two components in the case of compositions executed in more than one colour. Therefore, the FastICA technique can “split” the images into new components where the hues of the painting get grouped by their chro­ matic affinity, making it a consistently effective method for analysing panels with superpositioning of pigments of different colours (Fig. 12 and Fig. 13). In the case of panel 1 from PM-11, these hue differences coincide with the graphic styles defined for the rock paintings in this geographic region. Thus, ICA 1 groups the elements belonging to SG2, while ICA 3 groups those stylistically identifiable with SG1 (Fig. 14). This circumstance might not commonly occur in other painted contexts, where the elements belonging to different periods could have more homogeneous colour shades of colour, which might make the separation among them problematic. The application of FastICA also reveals the main difference in the use of decorrelation techniques (Cerrillo-Cuenca and Sepúlveda, 2015) used by software such as DStretch. While decorrelation techniques mix the information of the matrices to gain an enriched visualisation of pig­ ments, ICA separates them sharply. The use of PCA in decorrelation al­ gorithms can explain the differences. The reprojection of components in other colour spaces, as in decor­ relation stretch (Campbell, 1996; Gillespie et al., 1986), also has the potential to yield meaningful results. The independent components can be used for elaborating new RGB images as suggested for PCA (Roger­ io-Candelera et al., 2011; Domingo et al., 2015), or other algorithms like the SISC algorithm family (Cerrillo-Cuenca and Sepúlveda 2015). Fig. 15 shows the application of the SIVC (Selective Intensification of Values from a single Component) algorithm (Cerrillo-Cuenca and Sepúlveda 2015) for the intensification of colours in PM-11’s Panel 1 using inde­ pendent components. In this case, the results obtained by reprojecting the components into an RGB space tend to be neater using ICA compo­ nents than PCA components. This is due to the increased separability between the source and the components obtained through the ICA method. The main disadvantage of this could be that many scientific image processing software packages such as Hypercube or ImageJ do not implement FastICA, which means the method would be unavailable to most rock art specialists. Fortunately, software packages for remote sensing such as Orfeo Toolbox and Monteverdi have incorporated FastICA. Fig. 12. Conclusions The dissemination of digital methods for analysing rock paintings has increased our ability to read complex and/or challenging panels beyond traditional techniques. These advances are undoubtedly enriching our precise understanding of painted scenes. Maintaining a proper balance between the effectiveness of the methods applied and their simplicity is still a much-needed topic of debate, however. Although some archaeologists have stressed that the simplicity of these methods is a crucial factor during the recording of rock panels (e.g., Fig. 13. A detail of monochromatic red figures and the response of methods in panel 2 from PM-11. The human figure can be clearly appreciated in the first ICA component and hardly in the third. The same figure appears in the three PCA components, more clearly in the third. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.) Fig. 14. The two graphic styles in PM-11 separated by its association to different independent components. E. Cerrillo-Cuenca et al. Journal of Archaeological Science 125 (2021) 105269 11 LeQuellec et al., 2015), it must be recognised that more complex methods offer significant results once their behaviour and the logic behind them are understood. The development of digital techniques in the study of archaeological contexts should be grounded in an essential understanding of the nature, limitations, and advantages of each method or process. Although the application of pigment enhancement in rock art will indeed retain a tentative aspect, knowing the fundamentals of these methods can provide the user with specific guidance in analysing such paintings. Application of the FastICA algorithm has demonstrated that it is possible to separate two or more types of pigments from a single panel, which may be a breakthrough for PCA and the techniques that have assimilated this method, such as decorrelation stretch. Although PCA provides excellent results in isolating paintings on rock art panels, Fas­ tICA yields more marked differences when it comes to compositions made with more than one type of pigment. In panels with mono­ chromatic scenes, FastICA offers results effectively equivalent to those of PCA, and thus in these situations, PCA should be the preferred solution, since it returns numerical information about the explained variance for each component. Furthermore, the calculation time is less for PCA than for FastICA because of its lower algorithmic complexity. The method described above shows that research into new methods of digital image analysis still has a long way to go. Different algorithms can enhance the pigments in rock paintings in subtle, distinctive man­ ners, which reinforces the idea that using a single method is not always the best way to analyse a panel. This is particularly true for one of the cases discussed in this article (panel 1), where new representations in both SG-1 and SG-2 were detected only after the application of the FastICA procedure. In this sense, the application of enhancement methods should follow the recommendation from other fields of so- called “Digital Archaeology”: combining results from different tech­ niques is always advisable to maximise the capabilities of image inter­ pretation and thus to improve previous readings. title: Interactive relighting, digital image enhancement and inclusive diagrammatic representations for the analysis of rock art superimposition: The main Pleito cave (CA, USA)
abstract: This paper deals with the documentation, and virtual visual analysis of pictographs using interactive relighting, digital image enhancement techniques and diagrammatic representations. It discusses areas of interest for the analysis of low surface detail, large and geometrically complex superimposed pictographs. The synergy of reﬂectance transformation imaging (RTI) and decorrelation stretch (DS) aimed to improve the study of superimposition via the enhanced visualization of the surface morphology, dominant features, paint characteristics and layering. Additionally, diagrammatic representations of the results of the image-based analysis provided a valuable tool for interpretation and integration of the diverse dataset from the ongoing research in the Pleito Cave in California. This method allows revisiting unresolved hypotheses concerning the site by unpacking chemical and visual data in superimposed sequences. © 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
