In areas where numerous rock art sites exist, much of the rock art is unidentified and therefore remains, unrecorded and unresearched. Manually identifying rock art is time-consuming, tedious, and expensive. Therefore, it is necessary to automate many processes in rock art research, which can be accomplished by Machine Learning. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) can greatly facilitate rock art research in many ways, such as through Object Recognition and Detection, Motif Extraction, Object Reconstruction, Image Knowledge Graphs, and Representations. This article is a reflective work on the future of ML for rock art research. As a proof-of-concept, it presents a machine learning method based on recent advances in deep learning to train a model to identify images with painted rock art (pictograms). The efficacy of the proposed method is shown using data collected from fieldwork in Australia. Furthermore, our proposed method can be used to train models that are specific to the rock art found in different regions. We provide the code and the trained models in the supplementary section. 1. Introduction Rock art identification has so far been a manual task performed by specialists in the field. However, manually identifying rock art in the field is both laborious and expensive. With many advancements being made in archaeology with the help of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) (Davis, 2019; Lambers and Traviglia, 2016; Lambers et al., 2019; Caspari and Crespo, 2019), it is time to explore the applicability of AI and ML methods in rock art research. ML is a sub-field within AI, where the goal is to learn a model from the given example data. Recently, a new field of Deep Learning (DL) has emerged as a sub-field of ML, with a focus on building effective models based on the Artificial Neural Network model. On structured datasets (e.g., image and text), DL methods are more suitable and has been proven to be very useful (Mikolov et al., 2013; Redmon and Farhadi, 2016). There is considerable potential for AI methods (especially DL methods) to be used in rock art research. However, a systematic study identifying the existing application of AI to rock art research and exploring the potential/usefulness of AI methods for rock art research has so far been missing. One of the motivations of this work is to fill this gap. The contributions of this work are threefold: ∗Corresponding author. E-mail address: a.jalandoni@griffith.edu.au (A. Jalandoni). – First, we build an ML model to automatically classify painted rock art (pictogram) images, as a proof-of-concept of the potential of ML for rock art research. Classifying painted rock art images was chosen as a foundation-level task to enable the building of future models that can automate other rock art processes. We chose to start with unedited images to reduce manual tasks and possible bias from tracings. We provide our code written during this research, as well as trained models — which can be useful to other researchers working in this area. – Second, we identify some of the factors that limit the application of AI and ML methods in rock art research. For example, we discuss data sensitivity, data ownership, and data scarcity issues. – Third, we identify various use-cases in which AI and ML methods can facilitate rock art research. We discuss automatic object recognition, automatic motif extraction, object reconstruction, image knowledge graphs, and image representation (terms explained in Table 1). For each of these use-cases, we discuss current challenges in the application of ML methods to rock art data. We argue that this will enable the adoption of AI and ML techniques in rock art research. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jas.2022.105629 Received 22 April 2021; Received in revised form 5 May 2022; Accepted 1 June 2022 Journal of Archaeological Science 144 (2022) 105629 2 A. Jalandoni et al. Fig. 1. Example from Philippines rock art of a similar motif as a petroglyph and pictogram. A triangular-bodied anthropomorph engraved with enhancement and onscreen digital tracing (left two) and painted with onscreen digital tracing (right two, photograph courtesy of Mark Willis and tracing by Aila Shaine Sambo). Table 1 Main Machine Learning terms used in this work. Term Description Image classification Constitutes assigning an image to a certain category from an existing set of predefined categories. For example, an image can be classified as being in a rock art category or in a non-rock art category. Object recognition, is a form of image classification, and the two terms are interchangeably used. One difference between ‘Image Classification’ and ‘Object Recognition’ is that image classification specifically seeks to classify individual pixels within an image, whereas object recognition seeks to classify a group of pixels representing an object. Object detection The task of finding the location of either one or more than one object of interest in an image. Object reconstruction Constitutes completing/reconstructing an object that is either faded or has disappeared from an image for various reasons. Knowledge graphs A data structure that stores some meta-information about an entity (e.g., image, text), which can assist in decision making. Motif extraction Both manual and digital tracings are a form of motif extraction, whereby the rock art is replicated without the background image. The remainder of the article is organized as follows: we consider related work on rock art classification in Section 2; we propose an ML model for rock art detection in Section 3 and present our experimental results in Section 4; we explore various challenges in the application of AI methods in Section 5; we discuss typical AI and ML use-cases in Section 6; and finally, we present our conclusions in Section 7 and discuss our planned future applications. 2. Related work In this section, we discuss previous key works related to rock art image classification and highlight the progress that has been made. The majority of the work for rock art image classification and related computer vision techniques has been conducted on petroglyph sites. The application of machine learning methods on pictogram sites has been relatively less explored. Petroglyphs, or engraved rock art, use a subtractive technique, while pictograms, or painted rock art, involve an additive technique. The two techniques may sometimes share similar motifs; however, traditionally the techniques for studying them have been quite different (Fig. 1). A notable work on petroglyphs is that of Zhu et al. (2010), where shape-based descriptors are used with simple image processing techniques such as the Generalized Hough Transformation. The work is mostly focused on studying the shapes and their variants, instead of better detection or classification of petroglyphs. In addition, a simple shape-based distance measurement technique is proposed in Zhu et al. (2009) for measuring the distance between two petroglyphs. Several studies have been conducted on datasets from Europe and specifically on petroglyph sites in Valcamonica, Italy, which are listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site (Seidl and Breiteneder, 2012; Seidl et al., 2015; Poier et al., 2016; Zeppelzauer et al., 2016). One of these projects explores the segmentation of petroglyphs (Seidl and Breiteneder, 2012). Segmentation is the process whereby an object boundary is extracted (or cut) from the original image. The significance of this work is in the exploration of various features, such as SIFT and DSIFT, and their fusion. In Greece, (Tsigkas et al., 2020) trialed two methods to automatically detect three petroglyph sites and found that deep learning using YOLOv2 and TinyYOLO-v2 outperformed SIFT and Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) if many input instances for training, but the efficiency was reversed if there were only a few annotated images were available. An important conclusion reached is that image context can be more useful than the rock art itself for detecting rock art. In Horn et al. (2021), the authors create a model to locate and classify image objects from petroglyph sites in Sweden. The model was trained using Faster R-CNN, data augmentation, and transfer learning from images generated from 3D scans. The model performed well with some objects, such as boats and circles, but poorly with others, such as animals (the poor performance was attributed to a limited number of training samples). In Seidl et al. (2015), the authors proposed some simple shape descriptors as well as graph-based petroglyph descriptors for the automatic classification of petroglyphs. The main focus of the study is a combination of various descriptors. The nearest-neighbour model was used for classification. In Poier et al. (2016), a high-resolution 3D dataset of natural rock surfaces containing petroglyphs is presented. The authors manually scanned the petroglyphs from various rocks at a sub-millimeter level of accuracy and obtained orthophotos as well as corresponding depth maps. As mentioned earlier, the applications of machine learning on pictograms (painted rock art) are scarce. One notable publication is Purshotam (2015), where in order to process and search over 275,000 images of pictographs from the South African Rock Art Digital Archive, a method was developed to automatically classify and detect the specific objects that appear in each image. The approach used for detection is the same as that of Viola and Jones (2001), the Viola Jones algorithm, which is a typical ML object detection algorithm. In a recent publication, a dataset of 98 traced rock art images was used with AlexNet, VGG, and ResNET trained models to classify Arnhem Land rock art styles in Australia (Kowlessar et al., 2021). Using the learned features, the model provided a stylistic chronology of the rock art. The idea is based on representation learning, where a model is trained using existing object recognition datasets. In this way, a representation is learned in which the style of images can be easily differentiated. Based on their representation, the authors propose a clustering of the rock art images that statistically suggest a chronology. A study conducted on Mongolian rock art involved training a model for automated detection and classification of both engraved and painted rock art (Perenleilkhundev et al., 2019). The technique used, however, is a simple image-processing-based edge detection. The authors evaluated various typical machine learning models and compared their performance. Journal of Archaeological Science 144 (2022) 105629 3 A. Jalandoni et al. Fig. 2. Example of rock art in Anbangbang Gallery (Left) and study area (Right). While the majority of previous ML work in rock art has been done on petroglyphs, it is unknown whether the models can easily be applied from pictograms. Petroglyphs have qualities that are very different to pictograms. Therefore, the applicability of machine learning models and computer vision techniques trained on petroglyphs and then applied to pictograms requires further investigation because, while possible, the quality of the result is yet to be determined. 3. Rock art image (pictogram) classification method In this section, we describe our proposed rock art image classification method. We will start by describing our study area that is, where the data was collected — followed by the deep learning models used. Later, we will discuss the learning strategies used in our study. 3.1. Study area Australia is estimated to have over 100,000 rock art sites and Kakadu National Park alone has over 15,000 sites (May and Taçon, 2014). The Park is a UNESCO World Heritage site in the Northern Territory of Australia and the rock art in the region is predominantly painted. The park includes Burrungkuy (Nourlangie Rock), which has some of the most iconic rock art in Australia (Fig. 2). Despite Kakadu National Park being renowned for its rock art, only a fraction of the rock art sites have been recorded. The area encompassing West Arnhem and Kakadu National Park is a region of significant archaeological activity. The Madjedbebe rock shelter is the oldest known site for the arrival of humans in Australia around 65,000 years ago (Clarkson et al., 2017). Recently, a new rock art style, called Maliwawa figures, was identified in the region (Taçon et al., 2020). Since the 1960s, the rock art sites in Burrungkuy have been visited by David Attenborough (Attenborough, 1963) and the art has been studied to varying degrees by Edwards (Edwards, 1974), Brandl (Brandl, 1968), Jelinek (Jelínek, 1989, 1976), Welch (Welch, 1982, 2015), Lewis (Lewis, 1988), Haskovec and Sullivan (Haskovec and Sullivan, 1989), Chaloupka (Chaloupka, 1979, 1983, 1993), Taçon (Taçon, 1989a,b), and Taçon and Chippindale (Taçon and Chippindale, 2001). The Djok Pathways project brought about a resurgence of rock art research in the area that focused on oral histories (May et al., 2019), and advanced methods, such as 3D recording (Jalandoni and May, 2020) and pigment analysis (May et al., 2020). However, limited time, budget, and numbers of rock art specialists have left the majority of the rock art sites un-researched (Jalandoni and May, 2020). Table 2 Number of images in two categories used in our experiments. Number of images Rock Art 1339 Non-Rock Art 119 Total 1458 3.2. Data The dataset of rock art images consisted of 1339 photographs with easily identified rock art from the sites of Nangulawar, Castle Rock, Anbangbang main gallery and three other smaller sites along the tourist trail in Burrungkuy, Kakadu National Park (Fig. 3). With the exception of Castle Rock, the sites are all publicly accessible. For the dataset of non-rock art images, 119 photographs without rock art were collected from various areas of Kakadu National Park. The photographs were collected for photogrammetry and gigapixel panoramas during the 2018 fieldwork of the Djok Pathways Project. The number of images belonging to each category used in our experiments is provided in Table 2. One characteristic of our dataset is its skewness. We have 1339 rock art images and only 119 non-rock art images. Due to pandemic related restrictions on fieldwork, we were unable to capture further non-rock art images A typical work-around solution would be to collect non-rock art images from the Internet. However, this solution could potentially mislead the classification of non-rock art because the images on the internet may not have the same characteristics of rockshelters from the study area (for example, type, texture, shape, and colour). 3.3. Models As discussed earlier, not many works have studied the application ML methods for classifying painted rock art, and even fewer have studied the specific application of deep learning methods such as VGG, ResNet , and Inception. In this work, we will the explore following variations of deep learning models: – CNN: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) are ideal for image datasets, as they rely on successive convolution and pooling operations. Convolution operation can be considered as multiplying a portion of an input image with another image (also known as the filter), and summing all the values in the resultant image. The successive application of convolution on various areas in an Journal of Archaeological Science 144 (2022) 105629 4 A. Jalandoni et al. Fig. 3. Example of image with rock art and no rock art from the dataset collected at Kakadu National Park, Australia. Fig. 4. A typical Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The network is composed of four kind of layers. Convolution and pooling layers reduce the size of an input image, while increasing its depth. The flatten layer, on the other hand, converts a 3D input into 1D output, which is fed to dense layers of a typical ANN. The size of the flatten layer in our example is 5 × 5 × 16 = 400. input image produces yet another image, which is smaller than the input image. The pooling operation represents a small portion of an image with a maximum pixel value in that portion. Since a small portion (e.g., 3 × 3) will be represented by just one value, it drastically reduces the size of the input image. The convolution and pooling layers are followed by a flatten layer, which converts a three-dimensional image to a one-dimensional vector. This onedimensional vector is fed to a deep ANN, which is trained to produce an output. A typical CNN is shown in Fig. 4, which has two layers of convolutions and pooling, and is followed by a deep ANN model. – VGG: The main motivation for the invention of the VGG model (Simonyan and Zisserman, 2014) was to reduce the hyper-parameters of deep Convolutional Neural Networks, as well as improving their classification performance. One drawback of CNN is that its performance is highly variant depending on the size of the model (i.e., number of layers used as well as their sizes). The VGG architecture was developed to train a general model whose performance is not drastically changed when changing the network size. It led to a simplified model as it had standard blocks of fixed size convolutions and max-pooling layers, which requires little changes with varying datasets. It should be noted that VGG in its original format still has over 138 million parameters to learn. – Inception: (Szegedy et al.), also known as ‘Network in Network’ architecture, is a slightly different architecture from other networks, and is motivated to reduce the size of the model and make it more similar to a biological brain. The introduced ‘inception module’ in an Inception network relies on an intuitive idea of 1 × 1-size convolutional layers, which effectively reduces the number of parameters optimized many times over. – ResNet: The motivation behind the introduction of ResNet (He et al., 2016) architecture was to reduce the effects of the phenomenon of ‘vanishing gradients’, and to be able to train deep neural networks. Vanishing gradients arise in deep architectures where the gradients1tend to zero and hence no learning can occur. ResNet introduced the idea of what is known as ‘skipconnections’ (preventing vanishing of gradients) in the network; as a result, the model can be trained to a much deeper level, resulting in better outcomes. There are several other architectures for deep CNN models as well as various variants of VGG, Inception and ResNet architectures. For this project, we chose the basic architecture as a proof-of-work. 3.4. Learning The learning of these four deep learning models is an end-to-end process. They are trained by optimizing an objective function2such as conditional log-likelihood (also known as cross-entropy or softmax)in an iterative gradient descent optimization process.3 Images are 1 A gradient is a number calculated based on the current output of the model. This number helps the model to update its weight, and hence is crucial to the learning (or training) of the model. 2 An objective function is some function that a model optimizes. Learning a model constitutes of finding some values of parameters for which this function leads to a very small number. 3 If a function that we are optimizing is differentiable, we can compute its gradients. These gradients actually tells us the direction in which to move (i.e., updating parameters positively or negatively) to obtain a small value of the function. The repetition of computing gradients and parameters update is known as iterative gradient descent optimization. Journal of Archaeological Science 144 (2022) 105629 5 A. Jalandoni et al. passed through the network in a feed-forward process. The gradients flow backwards, to update the parameters of the model, in a backpropagation process. We kept the learning process the same across all the four architectures used in this study, — that is, the batchsize, optimization routine, and optimization parameters are exactly the same. Deep learning models have seen some success with transfer learning. This incorporates learning a model on one dataset (in one application), then using this model or re-training it for another dataset (in either the same or a related application). This is particularly useful in cases where we have an application with limited data. We can train a deep learning model on a related application where there is an abundance of data, and later transfer this model to the application where data is scarce. Since we faced data scarcity in this project, we employed transfer learning to investigate whether it helped us in training a better model. Data augmentation is commonly applied in a variety of computer vision learning tasks. The motivation is to control the variance of the models — as various deep learning models are very big with lots of parameters, they can over-fit the training data, resulting in performance degradation. In machine learning problems, over-fitting happens when the model fails to generalize to data that is not part of the training. Controlling for over-fitting (or variance control) is an essential part of any model training. It can be seen that we trained deep models with relatively little data. Therefore, in this project we used data augmentation for variance control. 4. Experiments In this section, we explain experimental details such as input data dimensions, hyper-parameters specification, and network architectures. We also elaborate on the variance reduction strategy used, as well as the transfer learning settings. Finally, we discuss performance evaluation metrics, our results, and the generalization of our models. 4.1. Input-data dimensions Photographs were captured at a high-resolution, with a minimum resolution of 5184 × 3888 × 3 (20 mega-pixels). High-resolution photographs are important for visual characterization because they capture a lot of information. On the other hand, this also increases the number of parameters that a machine learning model needs to learn. For various deep learning models, images are typically resized to a smaller resolution to reduce the number of parameters to be learnt. A typical image size for VGG and ResNet models is 224 × 224 × 3 pixels, while for Inceptionit is 299 × 299 × 3 pixels (Szegedy et al.; Simonyan and Zisserman, 2014; He et al., 2016). In our experiments, we re-sized our images to 224 × 224 × 3 for all the models used. It should be noted that we have not done any cropping of the images. An alternative strategy would have been to resize the image to the largest piece of rock art in all the images. However, our goal is to train a model that (when in use or at deployment) can learn to identify the presence of rock art in many diverse cases, such as varying background and lighting conditions. Training a model on cropped images of rock art only can impede the ability of the model to distinguish between an image of rock-art that has a background. For this reason, we did not employ the strategy of cropping the rock art out of the images and training them against their background. While this strategy can alleviate our class imbalance problem, it requires significant effort for image annotation, — that is, identifying the boundaries of the rock art in an image. 4.2. Model architecture and size We used VGG with 16 layers, ResNet with 50 layers, and Inception with 48 layers, which resulted in 138, 23, and 24 million parameters, respectively. The three models were selected because they are widely used and have been shown to lead to state-of-the-art performance for a wide range of computer vision tasks. The VGG model has different versions, such as VGG16 and VGG19 — the difference is the number of the layers of the model. In this work, we have used VGG16. Similarly, ResNet model has various versions, such as ResNet50 and ResNet100. Again, the difference between the two is the number of the layers of the model. In this work, we have used ResNet50. The CNN model is trained with two configurations: ‘shallow’ and ‘deep’. The shallow model had one block of convolution and max-pooling layer and two dense layers (denoted as ‘CNN (shallow)’), and the deep model was trained with five blocks of convolution and max-pooling layers and five dense layers (denoted as ‘CNN (deep)’). 4.3. Hyper-parameters and evaluation framework settings We used Adam as an optimizer with default parameters in Tensorflow 2.5 framework. A batch size of 32 is used, and we trained our models for 30 epochs. The value of 30 was chosen after initial analysis and observation of convergence pattern. We employed early stopping in case convergence was achieved before 30 epochs. For evaluation, we relied on a random train-test split. That is, all the images were first shuffled, followed by a selection of 50 rock-art images and 50 non-rock images to be used for testing. The remaining 1358 images were used for training. We repeated this process five times and reported the average results. An alternative to this strategy is cross-validation, however, our strategy ensured that the distribution of positive and negative examples remained the same in testing data (i.e., 50/50). The advantage of our strategy was the potential to produce more robust results and an unbiased estimate of the model’s performance. One downside of our evaluation framework was that the size of the test set was quite small (i.e., only 100 data points – around 7% of the data – are used). Another strategy could be to fix the test set (i.e., set aside a dedicated set of images for testing). However, this is less common strategy in the evaluation of machine learning algorithms, and can result in methods over-fitting the test set. We used softmax as the objective function even for our binary classification problem for the sake of generalization to multi-class problems in our future work. Though in practice, softmax is just an over-parameterized form of binary logistic function and gives exactly the same results. The experiments were done on a desktop computer with Intel i7 10700K CPU, 16 GB RAM and with an RTX 3070 GPU card. 4.4. Variance reduction through data augmentation As mentioned earlier, for variance control we used data augmentation, — that is, input images are scaled, rotated and flipped to introduce some variety in the input training data. Specifically, we applied one of the following transformations on an image randomly: – zoom in the range of 0.8 to 1.2, – rotation in the range of 0◦to 30◦, – horizontal flip, – vertical flip. In the results below, we compare the performance of data augmentation with the case of no data augmentation, –that is, the case where the input image is fed directly (without any transformation) to the model. 4.5. Transfer learning settings We used the following transfer learning procedure: – The models were first trained on ImageNet dataset The parameters of the model were saved on the disk. We called these weights ‘pre-trained weights’. Journal of Archaeological Science 144 (2022) 105629 6 A. Jalandoni et al. – We initialized our models with pre-trained weights. – We froze all the weights except the fully connected layer’s weights in the network. Freezing means that these weights are not updated during the training process. – Finally, we re-trained (updated) the parameters of the fully connected layers with our rock art dataset. In the results below, we compare transfer learning with no transfer learning. No transfer learning is the case where we re-trained (updated) the weights of the entire network. 4.5.1. Performance evaluation metrics For evaluating the performance of various architectures, we used two metrics — Accuracy and the F1-Score. Accuracy calculates the percentage of correct prediction for a trained model. When there are class imbalance problems, the Accuracy of a model can give a false impression of model quality. Therefore, in those cases the F1-Score, which removes True-Negatives (TN) from calculation, is generally preferred.4 F1-Score is defined as: 𝙵𝟷−𝚜𝚌𝚘𝚛𝚎= 2 × TP 2 × TP + FP + FN (1) F1-Score can be considered as the (Harmonic) mean of two terms: Precision and Recall, where Precision is the percentage of correct predictions for the positive class, whereas Recall is the percentage of correct predictions for the positive class out of all positive predictions that are made. Other than F1-score, another measure that can be used for evaluating the performance under class imbalance is F-beta score. The F-beta score has a configurable parameter (𝛽) that controls the importance of Precision and Recall. For example, a small value of 𝛽(say 𝛽= 0.5), gives more weight to Precision and less to recall, and vice-versa for a large value of 𝛽(say, 𝛽= 2.0). On the other hand, Accuracy is defined as: 𝙰𝚌𝚌𝚞𝚛𝚊𝚌𝚢= TP + TN TP + TN + FP + FN (2) 4.5.2. The model’s assumption was that training and testing data were sampled from the same distribution (data was independent and identically distributed); therefore, the performance can only be guaranteed on a similar test data, — that is, images that are similar to our dataset. Its generalization beyond this dataset cannot be guaranteed. Machine learning engineers usually evaluate the performance of their models on a dataset that is significantly different from the training dataset. In some cases, deep learning models do have the remarkable ability to perform well on datasets that are different from training. However, this is more of an engineering process and requires some form of systematic experimentation to study variations of the data for which model is robust. If a researcher uses our trained models on rock art that is not in Kakadu National Park, these are our recommendations: – Use our pre-trained models and analyse their performance on new test data (i.e., data from their own site). – Re-train the dense layers of ResNet only (while fixing the remaining layers) with the data from their own site, and observe the performance to see whether it improves. We believe that this form of transfer learning can be useful for researchers who are facing data scarcity (e.g., have very few images of rock-art on their sites). The models and the code are provided as part of this research. 5. Challenges In the following section, we discuss various challenges facing the application of AI and ML methods in rock art research. 5.1. Lack of centralized database As we mentioned earlier, the application of machine learning methods in rock art research requires a lot of data, and this can be accomplished by the development of a globally shared database or an active data-sharing mechanism among rock art researchers. Such data-sharing mechanisms have been common in machine learning, for example the UCI repository on ML (Dua and Graff, 2017) or kaggle datasets. However, the complexities of data sensitivity, ownership, and legislation of rock art make this difficult. Most archives offer a comprehensive list of rock art sites, but focus on their individual projects within their limited network (Haubt, 2016). Many also do not provide full access to their imagery. However, an example of a regional database is the African Rock Art Image Project, which has catalogued approximately 23,000 digital photographs of rock art from across Africa and allows access for research purposes (British Museum, 2021). Another archive with over 200,000 images and permits access for research is the The African Rock Art Digital Archive, managed by the Rock Art Research Institute (Rock Art Research Institute, 2021). In 2010, a UNESCO World Rock Art Archive was proposed but never implemented (Haubt, 2016; Haubt and Taçon, 2016). A proof-of-concept model of a centralized global database to curate rock art was made by Haubt (2016) with the Rock Art Database (www.rockartdatabse.com). After a hiatus due to lack of support, it has recently been redesigned and put back online, although it is still in its nascent stage of development. Without a centralized database, it is difficult to make a determination about whether there is truly a scarcity of data or just a scarcity of data sharing. 5.2. Data sensitivity For First Nations people, rock art is an essential aspect of contemporary Indigenous cultures that connects them directly to ancestors and Ancestral Beings, cultural stories and landscapes (Taçon, 2019). Therefore, rock art is not just data, it is part of Indigenous heritage and contributes to Indigenous well-being (Taçon and Baker, 2019). Some sites contain sensitive material that is only to be viewed by certain people. For example, in Australia men’s sites are to be visited only by men because they pertain to men’s business. Likewise, women’s sites are only to be seen by women. Any database would need to be culturally sensitive and enforce these and other restrictions. Further, First Nations people are the cultural owners of the rock art and would need to be involved in informed discussions about whether they even want the data stored in a centralized database. 5.3. Ownership and legislation In many countries, permission to collect images of rock art is obtained through government agencies. In places where the rock art is on privately owned land, in addition to government permits the landowner’s permission needs to be secured. When the site is owned and managed by First Nations people who have a connection to the rock art, the research needs to be done in partnership. For this project, the ideas were discussed with Jeffrey Lee, the Traditional Owner of Burungkuy, who also approved a final version of this article. We gave the Traditional Owners the final say on how their rock art was used for our research. The gatekeepers of rock art data are the researchers. Most researchers, not just rock art specialists, may be aware of the value of their data but unaware of how to manage their data optimally (Haubt and Jalandoni, 2019). Single purpose acquisition is favoured over sustainable data models involving online repositories and sharing with reasons ranging from regressive work practices to discipline-specific research cultures and economic factors (Haubt and Jalandoni, 2019). Every country has its own challenges for creating a centralized database. Again using Australia as an example, what makes it even more difficult is that the legislation for cultural heritage is not uniform on a national level; it varies from state to state (Winn and Taçon, 2016). A centralized database even just for Australia would have to navigate the cultural heritage legalities of each state’s jurisdiction together with the ownership complexities. Many of these issues will have to be navigated before a global database can be established. There will undoubtedly be difficulties conceptualizing a centralized database that can manage all these requirements. The stakes are high as the research outcomes could have major impacts on living people’s connection to place, land claims, and territory boundaries. It would also require continued input from the cultural owners of the rock art and allow them to limit access to the data as they see fit. Yet these challenges are not a reason to not pursue this line of research. AI use in the field of medicine is full of complexities of data sensitivity, ownership, and legislation (Abdullah et al., 2021). Yet many governments try to centralize some data to some extent with opt-out options (e.g. https://www.myhealthrecord.gov.au). The community that develops AI for medical purposes is working towards a centralized database, though it is not there yet. Similarly, we argue that the community developing AI for rock art research should also work towards a centralized database. 6. Typical ML use-cases In this section, we present typical use-cases in rock art research where AI and ML methods can be utilized to advance and facilitate the research. Journal of Archaeological Science 144 (2022) 105629 9 A. Jalandoni et al. 6.1. Rock art object recognition and detection Object recognition and detection have a long history in Machine Learning and Computer Vision (Forsyth and Ponce, 2012). Object recognition tasks involve classifying an image if it is determined to be of interest, for example, categorizing an image as that of a flower, animal or rock art. Typically, the models have been trained using generic object datasets after extracting some boundary, colour or texture-like features, and any traditional ML models such as NearestNeighbour, Random Forest, or Support Vector machine are used to train a classifier to recognize the extracted features. Typical machine learning modelling incorporates using a portion of the data for training a model, while using the rest for testing the accuracy. The concept of regularization is pivotal to the working of most models, as the trained model should be able to generalize the data that it has not seen before (i.e., any data that is not part of the training). If a model is made too complex, it can start to learn the noise in the data instead of a generalizable pattern. This is known as over-fitting the dataset. It is widely believed that the first generation of Artificial Neural Networks was relatively complex compared with the size of the available datasets, and was not proven to be of much use. In 2013, the world of object recognition had a major breakthough, when the model AlexNet was published (Krizhevsky et al., 2012). A traditional deep artificial neural network based on convolutional layers was trained with some simple tricks to make it run faster with the help of GPU computers. This started a new era of deep learning for object recognition tasks. After AlexNet, several other models with different architecture in terms of size and depth of layers were proposed, such as ResNet, VGG, and Inception, which led to improved performances on image classification tasks (He et al., 2016; Simonyan and Zisserman, 2014; Szegedy et al.). Other than these models, two crucial developments led to major breakthroughs. First, the datasets on which these deep learning models were trained have increased significantly in size due to the rise of big data. Second, the computer hardware on which these models are currently being trained has improved significantly. Typical rock art object recognition is similar to generic object recognition; therefore, the success of ML in generic object recognition can be utilized for rock art object recognition. We propose a simple, yet effective deep learning model for rock art object recognition in Section 3. We also empirically study the effectiveness of the state-ofthe-art models such as ResNet, VGG and Inception architectures in Section 4. Object detection is a different and more difficult task compared with object recognition. In object detection, the goal is to identify the location of the object in the image. This is a challenge as there could be more than one object in the image, or they could be overlapping each other or hidden by an object of non-interest. In rock art this is referred to as superimposition. Historically in ML, a grid-based approach was used, such that an image could be divided into multiple grids and a model of object recognition trained on each grid. Then this advanced to a model that could be applied on each over-lapping cell to learn the detection of an object (Zaidi and Suter, 2008). However, this process was notorious for being computationally intensive and a faster detection algorithm was urgently needed. The advent of deep learning made a huge impact on detection tasks. In 2015, a model named Overfeat was proposed, followed by Yolo, Yolov2 and Yolov3, which became the state-of-the-art object detection algorithms (Sermanet et al., 2014; Redmon et al., 2015; Redmon and Farhadi, 2016, 2018). These methods make use of convolutional processes to automatically detect and recognize the objects in different parts of the image. Yolo and its variants have almost realtime object detection performance. Several other deep learning object detection methods have also been proposed, such as R-CNN (Region Based Convolutional Neural Network) and Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015). Like object recognition algorithms, object detection algorithms can be applied for use in rock art object recognition. A typical output from a hypothetically trained object detection model is depicted in Fig. 7. The model will identify the rock art figures as objects of interest then produce a bounding box around all the figures it has identified in the image. As with object recognition, recent advances in object detection are assisted by the two factors of more data and faster computers. In rock art research, training a decent object detection model will require access to large quantities of quality data and many specialist hours for data annotation, – that is, identifying the boundaries around objects. Rock art recognition and detection are extremely useful tasks and can be utilized both offline and online. In an offline mode, one can apply the model on the images from various rock art sites, which can help in better retrieval and traversing over thousands of images. An online mode can be utilized using drones, where researchers can plan an exploration in search of not only rock art but also specific rock art motifs. It will also help in documenting rock art from places that are difficult to access. As we alluded to earlier, the main problem in the application of object-recognition-and-detection models to rock art images has been the scarcity of data. We discuss other data-related issues in Section 5. Deep learning models for advanced recognition and detection tasks are particularly data hungry — that means millions of images are required for training these models. In rock art research, datasets of such a scale simply do not exist. We recommend that archaeologists adopt an active data sharing and storing strategy, much like the computer vision and machine learning community did in the early days of object recognition research (Dua and Graff, 2017). In the computer vision community, the collection of images was a community-led task, where images were collected and then shared with researchers. In the case of rock art images, we believe there are several challenges to overcome if a similar strategy is to be followed, mainly due to the lack of support for a centralized database (discussed in Section 5). 6.2. Automatic motif extraction Automatic motif extraction, as the name suggests, is the process of extracting the motif from the image with a trained machine learning model. Digital onscreen tracing reduces the time required in the field and can provide valuable spatial data if done in a GIS environment, however, it is still tedious (Jalandoni and Kottermair, 2018; Jalandoni, 2021). Applying ML for automatic motif extraction can replace both manual and onscreen tracing. Fig. 8 shows the typical input and output of desired automatic motif extraction. An AI model can be trained by utilizing deep learning models such as Auto-Encoders (AE) that have a long history in ML. Auto-Encoders (AE) models are variants of typical Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), which try to re-construct their own input (Ripley, 1996). Recently, AE and its variants have been successfully utilized in areas such as watermark removal and increasing image resolution, among others (Ledig et al., 2016; Zhong and Shih, 2019). Notably, variants of the AutoEncoder model known as Variational Auto-encoders (VAE) and Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) have led to a significant breakthrough in several computer vision applications. Rock art motif extraction can also be automated using these variants. GAN or VAE are deep ANN that automatically learn to transform input data. For example, an ANN can learn to transform an input image of a person, into an output image that is of the same person but 10 years older (Yang et al., 2019). We believe a similar strategy can be employed for an input of a rock art image. The model would learn to transform an input of a rock art image to an output image that has no background but instead is only the foreground of the motif from the input image. The main challenge in building the model for automatic motif extraction is the manual labelling process. Models such as AEs and GANs rely on input and output pairs. If we have a large number of good quality pairs, we can build generative models. However, for rock art automatic motif extraction, pairs of input rock art and the associated output motifs could be hard to obtain in the quantities required. Again, we recommend that the rock art research community develop datasharing schemes that can help train deep learning models. One issue in the application of deep learning methods for automatic motif extraction is quite subtle. It is important to note that our trained model can learn to extract a motif that is not the original motif, but a learned representation of the motif. Therefore, it is possible that there is no one-to-one correspondence between extracted motif and original motif. Especially on smaller datasets, this strategy can pose serious issues. An alternative approach for motif extraction is based on pixel classification approaches for semantic segmentation. Semantic segmentation has a long history in computer vision, and consists of categorizing each pixel of an image belonging to a certain category (Zhang et al., 2018; Nontawat et al., 2021). The model is trained on input and output pairs, which constitute an input image and image annotation (specifying which part of an image belongs to which category). 6.3. Rock art image reconstruction Rock art sites are subject to natural and anthropogenic agents of deterioration. Regrettably, many rock art figures have already faded or been destroyed, sometimes with whole panels and even whole sites gone (Taçon et al., 2021). Similar methods discussed in Section 6.2, including Auto-encoders and GANs, can also be used in rock art research for image reconstruction (Liu et al., 2018). While the reconstruction does not repair the physical rock art site, at least the digital image can be restored, which can potentially aid physical restoration. An example of image reconstruction is shown in Fig. 9. Just like automatic motif-recognition, to apply these generative methods we need to pair the inputs and outputs. Our input image will be an original rock art image that has been digitally altered to resemble destroyed or faded rock art, and the output will be the original image. In this way, the model will learn to reconstruct a partially destroyed image. Obtaining the dataset comprising input and output pairs is much easier in this case compared with motif extraction. 6.4. Rock art image knowledge graph Inventories of rock art figures at a site are essential for both research and site management (Jalandoni and May, 2020). Each rock art figure is extensively annotated using empirically determined attributes to create the inventory. Common attributes recorded are a general interpretation of the motif type, the associations with other rock art, whether there is superimposition, the technique, the colour, the style, and many other characteristics including measurements of height and width. The attributes of superimposition and style can allow for a relative age determination for specific figures. Meanwhile, some of these attributes, such as technique and colour, can be used in combination to determine certain artist groups. Colour gives an indication of possible pigments used and opens up questions of material sourcing. In addition, there are several other details that constitute part of the typical inventory process. An example of this inventory process is shown in Table 7. These inventories have traditionally been done in the field at great expense, but accurate inventories can be done from 3D models as well (Jalandoni and May, 2020). However, we can employ a Knowledge Graph (KG) to automate the annotation and accelerate the process of creating inventories, in effect saving countless hours of field and office annotations and economizing research expenses. If we have enough examples of input images and their associated output, we can aim to learn some of these output fields automatically. For example, we can train a model to predict the style, colour, or associated material culture from the figure image and populate the inventory table automatically. However, one of the challenges here is that rock art researchers do not standardize their inventory attributes (Jalandoni and May, 2020). Since their annotations are generated through empirical evidence, they are unavoidably subjective. Rock art research would benefit from a semantic approach using the CIDOC CRM reference model as an ontology to overcome the incompatibility of naming conventions and terminologies, which causes interoperable data (Haubt and Taçon, 2016). 6.5. Rock art image representation Finding similar rock art motifs across sites is necessary for identifying connections across geographical areas, but in current archaeological practice it is still a manual and tedious process. Deep learning methods have led to lots of advancements in the field of Representation, with successful uses in the representation of words, graphs, documents, and images (Goldberg and Levy, 2014; Grover and Leskovec, 2016). The idea is to represent an entity in the form of a vector, such that the semantics of the entity are captured. The entity in rock art would be an image. Some examples of use would be to group representations of similar images of a particular animal or similar images of a particular area or time period. Representation is extremely powerful and can facilitate rock art image searching and querying. Th use of Representation, learned via deep learning models, will help researchers to find similar images in a big collection of rock art images, alleviating the need for manual searching through a database — for example, finding all the images of X-ray barramundi fish across Kakadu National Park in Australia. Furthermore, this will also help rock art researchers identify similarities among rock art images that may not be visible to human eyes. An exciting potential use of Representation can be to assign anonymous work to a known rock artist based on style and other attributes that were previously unidentifiable by researchers. An excellent use-case of this is the recent work in Kowlessar et al. (2021), where styles are distinguished based on the learned representation. The authors demonstrated that their proposed deep learning technique helped support an interpretation of chronology for Arnhem Land rock art styles. Another benefit of image Representation, apart from searching and querying, is that of image compression. A typical image might take say 1024 × 1024 resolution, which is equal to a 1, 048, 576 dimensional vector. With deep-learning based representation techniques, an image can be represented in a vector of size say 512 or 1024. This dense representation is not only small, but also semantically meaningful Therefore, searching and querying can be done based on the content, style, and configuration of the image. 7. Conclusion and future works The purpose of this article has been to demonstrate the potential of machine learning, specifically deep learning, for rock art research by training a model that can be used to identify whether there is painted rock art in a photograph. The immediate benefit for rock art researchers and site managers is that large numbers of images can be loaded into the model to determine which of them contain painted rock art. Non-specialists can photograph suspected rock art, run the model, and confirm whether it is indeed rock art. In places with thousands of potential sites, this could save immeasurable amounts of time and money and lead to the discovery of new sites. In addition, the code from the model can be used to train models from datasets of other regions that have different rock art. Our ML model may even be used to facilitate the training of models from other regions that may have smaller datasets. Refer to the appendix for the code and models built during this research. More significantly, the ML model in this project is a proof-of-concept of the applications of machine learning for painted rock art that will provide the foundation for more complex work to be done in the future. While currently in the early stages, we intend that ML methods will reach a level of sophistication where models can detect rock art from Journal of Archaeological Science 144 (2022) 105629 12 A. Jalandoni et al. Fig. 10. Architecture of our VGG model. Fig. 11. Architecture of our ResNet model. an image as accurately as a rock art specialist in the field. Most rock art researchers use decorrelation stretch to enhance faded painted rock art so the same algorithms can be integrated into the ML process to aid the ML model to identify rock art. Other possibilities include object recognition and detection to automatically find individual figures in an image and classify them; automated motif extractions that will make manual and onscreen tracing of rock art obsolete; image Knowledge Graphs that can automatically annotate inventories; image representations that can lead to style and even potentially artist identification; and re-constructions that can even help virtually reconstruct lost or damaged rock art. Fig. 12. Architecture of our Inception model. Finally, all these applications of ML for rock art research are derived from photographs, which provide data that are already being collected. In fact, the amount being collected has been exponentially increasing since the advent of digital photogrammetry. Where previous researchers would take a dozen photographs of a small rock art site, we now take hundreds to create 3D models. Therefore, rock art researchers globally need to consider the potential of ML applications for their datasets and hopefully work towards one day having a centralized database. title: Pe˜na-Villasenín  ∗ ,  Mariluz  ,  Juan
abstract: The geometric inventory and documentation of rock art present great challenges due to the high number of petroglyphs present in a territory, the distance between them, the state of abandonment of forest areas in many cases, limitations to access, and the geometric characteristics of such art. Structure from motion (SfM) photogrammetry was positioned as an ideal technique for its documentation, but this technique has great variability in its methodology and cost. In this study, an extremely simple and effective method based on SfM photogrammetry with low cost cloud computing software is compared to the use of a terrestrial laser scanner (TLS) and professional SfM workﬂow to generate 3D models for documenting petroglyphs. The comparison is made on the 3D documentation of the Castri˜no de Conxo petroglyph in Santiago de Compostela, Spain, and extrapolated to two practical experiments on other petroglyphs. The meshes are compared by analysing visual, geometrical and operational criteria and how they inﬂuence the radiance scaling shading. The results show that with a SfM photogrammetry methodology, which is extremely simple and accessible to everyone, it is possible to obtain better results in geometric and visual aspects than those obtained with TLS, and they are valid for a detailed analysis of this type of rock art in a massively social approach of documentation that is not possible through other approaches. © 2018 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.

All rights reserved. 1. Introduction and research aims New methods of geometric data acquisition are revolutionizing the ways of documenting archaeological heritage [1–5]. To accurately represent the information of complex sites or objects with irregularities or curvatures, it is necessary to construct highdensity 3D point clouds. Currently, structure from motion (SfM) photogrammetry [6], the terrestrial laser scanner (TLS) [7] and the combination of both [8–13] have been established as the most widely used systems in the acquisition of high-precision and highresolution textured point clouds. These techniques, which enable the acquisition of 3D models of high-quality metric and photorealistic results, represent a powerful tool for the identiﬁcation, monitoring, conservation and restoration of heritage elements [14–16]. The Galician archaeological ensemble of rock art is the most signiﬁcant artistic expression of the communities that inhabited these ∗Corresponding author. E-mail address: simon.pena@rai.usc.es (S. Pe˜na-Villasenín). lands in the Bronze Age. Catalogued within the Atlantic Art family, these petroglyphs have a strong personality that makes them recognizable among many others. Considering both the number and the quality of the designs, Galician rock art is one of the most important cultural manifestations of this type in Europe [17–20]. The documentation of the phenomenon of the Galician petroglyphs is challenging, and their geometric characteristics with shallow engravings tend to be located on the semi-horizontal faces of stones that are not particularly high, which makes them challenging to discern under natural light. Traditionally, techniques such as frottage or plastic tracing have been used [21–23]. These techniques are slow and tedious, have a high degree of subjectivity and can potentially damage the rock art. This challenging record has resulted in partial documentations and interpretations of the archaeological ensemble, as discussed in [24], where it is shown that the Galician territory has a number of catalogued petroglyphs higher than 3000 and that year after year, this catalogue increases. However, in most cases, the only existing documentation is a textual description and its geographical location, which leads to a partial interpretation of the phenomenon. The geometric documentation of the entire heritage is unfeasible for technical and economic reasons, and at the https://doi.org/10.1016/j.culher.2018.10.009 1296-2074/© 2018 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved. S. Pe˜na-Villasenín et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 37 (2019) 238–246 239 same time, this documentation is a very necessary task due to the high risk of deterioration of this heritage, which is totally exposed to external agents. Social collaboration is the only way to geometrically document this cultural phenomenon in a massive way. For this reason, it is of vital importance that social agents know and manage tools and techniques whose use for this task is agile, simple, economical, and effective. Photogrammetry has proven to be more adaptable to the particularities of different scenes, as in [25]. In addition, the main advantages of photogrammetry are presented in the time and cost of execution; thus, it is the most appropriate technique for this massive documentation. However, these variables worsen if professional software and ground control points (GCPs) are used in the process. On the other hand, articles such as [26] describe extremely simple methodologies that also prove to be accurate for the study of rock art. Therefore, photogrammetry can be executed with different data collection methodologies, among which there are remarkable differences in performance and cost that are interesting to investigate on the same object of study. Regarding the TLS, the operability, for example, in conditions of low illumination, is superior to that of SfM photogrammetry. However, despite the beneﬁts of the TLS in documenting archaeological heritage, the required ﬁeldwork time, the cost of the surveying equipment, the challenges of transportation and the complexity of the related data management often lead to severe issues of practicality at some archaeological sites [14]. In addition, the combination of separate scans introduces inconsistencies in the orientation of individual surveys, and most TLSs are not well speciﬁed regarding accuracy [27]. Previous studies have compared TLS and SfM photogrammetry, normally in optimal conditions for recording cultural heritage, with similar results found for scanners and SfM [28]. Other comparisons as [29,30] have shown that the optimal choice of technique depends on the object to be modelled and the conditions of the work, requiring a performance evaluation of both approaches in each particular case, as in the modelling of petroglyphs, where determining factors exist in the application of both techniques, such as the geometry of the rock, the vegetation of the environment and the challenges associated with access. There are other comparisons between TLS and photogrammetry, as in the studies [31–35], but the photogrammetry methodology used in them is too complex for non-expert proﬁles because it requires GCP (which involves the use of topographic devices and desktop software) or the use of other devices such as poles, drones or expensive cameras. For this reason, this case study compares the results of a complex documentation using TLS with the application of an extremely simple methodology of SfM photogrammetry. In addition, other photogrammetric workﬂows with professional software and use of GCP are checked to evaluate the difference in time consumption between these photogrammetric methodologies. Different variables such as cost, ﬁeldwork, processing performance, geometric and visual quality and the potential of the detection engravings of TLS and simple photogrammetry are analysed. In addition, with the aim of extrapolating the results and evaluating the real efﬁciency and simplicity of the technique, two practical experiments in which this simple methodology is used for other examples of rock art are conducted. 2. Methods This section describes the object of study, the methodology used in the TLS and SfM photogrammetry processing, the comparison method and the two experiments on other examples of rock art. 2.1. Object of study: Castri˜no de Conxo The Castri˜no de Conxo petroglyph is located in Santiago de Compostela, Galicia in the northwest of Spain. It is one of the most important panels of arms [36] within the group of Atlantic rock art. In addition to the general particularities of this type of heritage, several conditions complicate the complete documentation in this case; these include the smoothness of the “U”-shaped engravings that are 2–3 cm wide by 0.5–1 cm deep, the large dimensions and slope of the rock (approximately 8.20 × 2.80 m), the existence of a metal wall on the southern edge and the existence of important vegetation in the environment that considerably limits the workspace, a situation that occurs in the majority of petroglyphs of the North Atlantic, introducing difﬁculty in the application of both techniques and motivating the choice of this representative site. The location of this element is shown in Fig. 1 (Point 1). 2.2. Terrestrial laser scanner Since the development of the ﬁrst TLS in 1999, this technology has seen a phase of continued product development, growth and expansion into multiple survey areas. In this tracking-based modelling method [37,38], active sensors are used that directly capture the 3D geometric information of the object through the use of laser or structured light, as in [39]. These devices recover 3D information by applying different measurement principles such as triangulation, ﬂight time or modular amplitude. They are robust and expensive systems that present complex handling issues, assuming, at times, serious problems of operability for documentation of rock art or of being too expensive for all catalogued elements [40]. 2.2.1. TLS data acquisition The rock was scanned using a Focus3D X 130 scanner by FARO (Florida, USA). This TLS is a 360◦scanner with an accuracy of 2 mm and a range of 0.6 m to 130 m. The scans were performed approximately 1.5 to 2 m from the rock in ﬁve positions. These positions are chosen by adapting to the limitations and geometrical conditions of the rock so that the hidden areas would be minimized. Four reference spheres were used for the relative alignment of the scans. The positions of the scans and spheres are shown in Fig. 2A. 2.2.2. TLS data processing The processing and alignment of each scan was performed with the FARO SCENE software, which processes the data obtained directly from the TLS. The software automatically detects the reference spheres and aligns and uniﬁes the set of scans. Once the deﬁnitive point cloud is obtained, it is reﬁned and exported in the PTX format. Subsequently, the Geomagic Studio software imports the point cloud ﬁle to generate the triangular mesh. The small holes of the mesh in areas where the protuberances of the rock do not allow for a correct scan are ﬁlled to obtain a homogeneous grid. To work efﬁciently, the model is then simpliﬁed. 2.3. SfM photogrammetry Photogrammetry is a technique with a long trajectory that, in parallel with the expansion of the laser scanner, has an important evolution resulting in a growing popularity in sectors such as archaeology [41], particularly for the documentation of rock art [42–44]. The development of image-based 3D data-extraction algorithms [45,46], and the calibration methods for non-metric cameras [47,48] have contributed to the further expansion of SfM. This technology uses an automated process to obtain point clouds, triangular meshes and full 3D textured models from photos [6,49]. The development of certain algorithms such as scale-invariant feature transform (SIFT) algorithms [50,51], the bundler adjust240 S. Photo shot arrangement. ment [52–55] and clustering views for multi-view stereo (CMVS) and Patch-based Multi-view Stereo (PMVS) algorithms [56,57] are notable achievements in the evolution of this technique. Desktop software such as Eos Photomodeler [58], Agisoft PhotoScan [59] or Pix4D Mapper [60] are the photogrammetric software tools most commonly employed by professionals. Desktop open software, such as VisualSfM [61], is also available. However, over the past decade, the use of cloud computing resources has increased, and many image-processing services are performed on an external server. Examples of such services are ARC3D [62], the now-defunct 123DCatch [63,64] and Autodesk Memento [65] [which is the beta version of Autodesk Remake [66], now Autodesk Recap Photo (2017)]. Even modern smartphones can be used to take photos and generate and visualize 3D models in a single device using applications such as SCANN3D or TRNIO. 2.4. Photographic shooting The photographic shooting was performed in the simplest way possible using only a conventional camera, speciﬁcally an Olympus Pen Ep-1 with a ﬁxed focal length of 14 mm, which is equivalent to 35 mm in the full frame format. A total of 99 photos (64 from the outside to the inside and 35 longitudinally to the rock, as shown in Fig. 2B) were taken with ISO 200, a 1/160 shutter speed and a 6.3 diaphragm aperture. No tripod was used because it signiﬁcantly delays the shooting and is a limiting factor in the choice of arrangement of photos. All types of automatic features were deactivated except the autofocus. For stabilization, as the only method used, the camera was attached to the neck with a strap at the time of capture. The only requirement in the shooting was to cover the entire surface of the rock at a distance of approximately 1.5 m from the focused point with photographs that had a high overlap between them. S. Pe˜na-Villasenín et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 37 (2019) 238–246 241 Fig. 3. Mesh comparison. A. TLS mesh. B. Autodesk Remake mesh. With the aim of evaluating the ﬁeldwork time of this task, GCPs, which improve the photogrammetric adjustment in the processing with professional software (Photoscan and Pix4D), were measured; therefore, prior to the shooting, 10 targets were placed in the scene. The coordinates of these were measured with the reﬂectorless total station TOPCON GPT 7005 with an angular accuracy of 5¨and a nonprism (NP) measurement distance accuracy of 5 mm + 2 ppm. 2.4.1. Photogrammetric processing With the same collection of images, three independent photogrammetric processes are performed to evaluate the processing time of professional photogrammetry with the use of GCP and desktop software, such as Photoscan or Pix4/D, and a simple photogrammetry without GCPs and with the low-cost cloud computing software Remake. In both desktop programmes, the workﬂow is similar. The photographs are imported, and a ﬁrst orientation of the set of images is made. Next, ground control points (GCPs) are introduced to georeference and provide geometric accuracy to the scene. Then, the images are readjusted, and ﬁnally the deﬁnitive point cloud and triangular mesh are processed. In Autodesk Remake, the workﬂow is extremely simple. The processing is performed automatically by the server. The processing is performed in the cloud, and one can close the program once the photo uploading is complete. When the processing is ﬁnished, an e-mail message notiﬁcation is sent, and it is possible to download and visualize the ﬁnal mesh in the application. In this step, the mesh can be scaled and oriented simply based on a reference measurement and an orientation restriction by means of a plumb and a compass. 2.5. Comparison criteria The TLS and different photogrammetric workﬂows were compared. To do so, the costs, work time and cloud and mesh statistical results were analysed. Then, a comparison was made between the mesh obtained through TLS and that obtained using the simple photogrammetry. Three criteria were analysed: the mesh geometric accuracy, the texture quality and the potential for detection and analysis of engravings. For geometric accuracy, the photogrammetric and laser scanner models were imported into Geomagic Studio in different reference systems. This software aligns the meshes using an automated process showing maximum errors, means, residual median squares (RMSs) and deviation values of the alignment. Furthermore, the presence of holes or inconsistencies in the models was analysed. For the visual criteria, the quality of the texture and colour of the meshes generated in the previous stages was analysed using a subjective visual comparison. To evaluate the potential of detection and analysis of engravings tasks, it is relevant that SfM photogrammetry or TLS textured 3D models are very complete geometric documentation approaches; however, post processing is necessary to observe the engravings. During recent years, the application of various algorithms such as radiance scaling [67] or decorrelation stretching such as DStretch [68,69] has been used to enhance the engravings and paints, with strong results. As shown in [70], the radiance scaling plugin helps enhance minor variations in depth across a model, in many cases identifying details that are not visible to the naked eye. This plugin adjusts the reﬂected light intensities in a manner dependent on both the surface curvature and material characteristics. As a result, diffuse shading or highlighted variations become correlated to surface feature variations, thus enhancing surface concavities and convexities [70]. The radiance scaling plugin is used in this case to enhance the engravings present on the rock. The results are compared with older documentations evaluated with other methods, such as frottage or digital photography with artiﬁcial light. 2.6. Rock art recording by simple SfM photogrammetry The expansion of the experiment was conducted along two lines of work. First, in experiment 1, the documentation of different petroglyphs not yet geometrically documented was performed in a geographic area with a radius of 6 km. Its localizations are shown in Fig. 1 (Point 2). The documentation methodology was the simple photographic shooting with a conventional camera. This task was performed by a technician with 5 years of experience in terrestrial photogrammetry, with the objective of evaluating the number of elements that could possibly be documented in a working day. Second, in experiment 2, the documentation of some petroglyphs, some of them not yet geometrically documented, was performed by rock art amateurs using conventional cameras, without previous knowledge in photogrammetry other than a training session of 1 hour. Its localizations are shown in Fig. 1 (Points 3 to 8). Table 1 Costs, time of work and processing results comparison.
