Results and discussion As a result of the TLS ﬁeldwork, ﬁve point cloud ﬁles were obtained, which were aligned and exported as a single cloud of 9.5 million points. From this cloud, a simpliﬁed textured mesh of 8 million triangles was obtained (Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B shows the mesh obtained with the photogrammetric program. The level of detail is high, and the engravings of the scene are faithfully documented. The results obtained by three software implementations are visually approximate. 3.1. Costs, ﬁeldwork and processing Table 1 shows the costs and processing results of the TLS technique and the analysed photogrammetric workﬂows. The costs and ﬁeldwork times are considerably lower for the SfM than for the TLS approach. With the simple methodology, without GCP, the ﬁeld time is notably lower than the professional photogrammetry workﬂow with GCPs. Remake costs are for annual licences, but this software offers the possibility of acquiring a monthly license, which could be a suitable option for many professionals and non-experts. The cost of the camera used in this work is approximately $300. In TLS, the georeferencing time is the sum of the time of the tasks of placing spheres and the alignment of the scanning processes, while in photogrammetric processing, the time is the sum of the tasks of placing and taking targets as GCPs in the ﬁeld and marking them in the software. Georeferencing time in the simple SfM workﬂow is the time of scaling with a reference distance and oriented by a plumb and a compass. Fig. 4. Four examples of Castri˜no de Conxo documentation. A. Digital photography by the A Rula Collective. B. Rock rubbing by A. de la Pe˜na-Santos (1979). C. TLS model. D. SfM photogrammetry model. S. Pe˜na-Villasenín et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 37 (2019) 238–246 243 Fig. 5. Experiment 1, ﬁnal results and shooting arrangement. 3.2. Geometric accuracy A signiﬁcant difference in the photogrammetric software workﬂow is that both PIX4D and PhotoScan allow the introduction of GCPs into the photos involved in the photogrammetric adjustment. However, Remake allows us only to introduce points on the model that readjust their position in space but do not inﬂuence the camera’s calibration. Thus, since the GCPs in Remake are not used in the adjustment, their accuracy results are less certain. The marking of the points in all the photos on the desktop software is expected to be more precise since this approach inﬂuences the adjustment, but the procedure is more tedious than using those marked on the mesh, as is required in Remake. We seek an agile method for archaeologists, and we ﬁnd that the use of Remake and the scaling with a reference measurement of a plumb and a compass satisﬁes this requirement. At this stage, the model obtained with the Remake software is selected for the comparison with the TLS. This software was chosen 244 S. Pe˜na-Villasenín et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 37 (2019) 238–246 for its lower costs, its simplicity of use and its cloud computing nature that allows for the use of a low-performance PC, making it a particularly notable software for a group of amateur archaeologists. In addition, if the accuracy of the Remake mesh is sufﬁcient for the documentation of rock art, then its results can be extrapolated to desktop software, which is expected to be more accurate. The analysis shows that in almost 90% of the surface of the outcrop, the differences between the meshes do not exceed 2 mm. Table 2 shows the statistical results of the alignment of the TLS and photogrammetry mesh. The RMS shows that despite a high maximum error of 11 cm in the marginal zones, the results of both meshes are similar. The maximum error and the mean are independently calculated based on the positive and negative values. Even when scanning from ﬁve positions, the ﬁnal mesh generated from the TLS has holes due to the shape of the stone causing hidden areas (Fig. 3A). For detecting engravings on vertical zones, the TLS captures the measurements perpendicular to the surface and obtains excellent results. However, this strong performance is not obtained with Galician rock art designs that are typically located on low-height horizontal outcrops. Thus, data collection is challenging for the TLS since oblique scans on the surface of the rock become necessary. 3.3. Visual quality As shown in Fig. 3, the texture and colour of the SfM photogrammetry model (Fig. 3B) has better contrast than that obtained by TLS (Fig. 3A). We expected this result since the photographs of the photogrammetric process are taken at a more perpendicular orientation and closer to the outcrop than those taken by the TLS, which is more limited in its positioning since it exclusively uses the 360◦ photographs with 100 MPx resolution in total, that it captures from the 5 scanning points. This point is important since a satisfactory texture of the stone can be useful for conservation studies. 3.4. Potential in detection and analysis No signiﬁcant differences were identiﬁed between the results obtained with the Remake mesh (Fig. 4D) and the TLS mesh (Fig. 4C), but photogrammetry offers less noisy results after the application of the radiance scaling shader’s ﬁlters. This ﬁnding is attributed to the possible errors in the combination of scans. Fig. 4A is a digital photograph obtained using artiﬁcial light, which also provides a satisfactory result. Fig. 4B shows a documentation using a rock rubbing technique that does not document all the engravings. 3.5. Experiments results In the ﬁrst experiment, in a single ﬁeld day, it was possible to document a total of 20 items of different typology. The results of the documentation of 4 of the 20 elements together with the photographic arrangement used in each of them are shown in Fig. 5. The methodology has been truly efﬁcient owing to the indications of local collaborators for the location of the petroglyphs and the agility of the technique, making it possible to execute a massive documentation of an area with a great afﬂuence of rock art. In the second experiment, non-experts in photogrammetry performed the geometric documentation of petroglyphs not documented to date or whose documentation was incomplete. The experiment shows that the simple methodology is accessible for non-photogrammetry experts. 4. Conclusions The main conclusion of this study is that with an extremely simple photogrammetry technique that is accessible to everyone and that has great performance in terms of execution and cost, results were obtained that improve on those obtained with TLS for the documentation of a petroglyph with complex geometry. The comparison shows that photogrammetry is an improved alternative since it allows precise results to be obtained with models without holes, with better textures and with upgraded detection of engravings relative to those obtained with the TLS approach. The work time with SfM photogrammetry is much lower than that with the TLS, and several teams can work simultaneously at low-costs. The geometric accuracy comparison shows that the differences between both meshes in the study area are less than 2 mm. Thus, the simple SfM photogrammetry can be considered geometrically close to TLS. The experiments conducted in this study show the performance and accessibility of the technique. It is possible that through simple photogrammetry, the documentation of a large number of elements in a single ﬁeld day can be accomplished, as shown in the ﬁrst experiment. In addition, this technique is accessible to everyone with a minimum of instruction, as shown in the second experiment. Only a conventional camera is necessary to perform a documentation with sufﬁcient quality for a geometric analysis of the rock art, and owing to altruistic social collaboration, it is possible that future massive documentation campaigns of a number of rocks can be conducted that would otherwise not be documentable for technical and economic reasons and that would require professional expertise. In addition, in this study, an analysis of the engravings with radiance ﬁlters in the TLS and photogrammetry model was performed. The results show that this technique represents an alternative that is potentially superior to that of traditional methodologies in both cases. Photogrammetry shows slightly better results than the TLS approach after the application of this treatment. Other enhancements such as the DStretch used in [69], which is used more often in paintings but is sometimes effective for petroglyphs, would be convenient to perform an in-depth analysis of the operation of these post processing methods and to evaluate their potential in the context of other examples of rock art. title: Revisiting palaeolithic rock art. Reading the graphic overlapping in terms of reuse behaviours in the Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave (Cantabria, Spain)
abstract: The study of figures overlapping in Palaeolithic rock art has been approached by the scientific discipline fundamentally from two perspectives. The first has been interpreted as the result of temporal relationships and the second as a question of the spatial composition of the panels or compositional units of prolonged artistic production. This work complements these assessments with a re-reading of the overlaps in terms of graphic appropriation behaviours produced throughout the Upper Palaeolithic. To this end, we propose a methodology that combines the study of Palaeolithic panels with new photographic technologies, their reading in decorative phases through the application of the Harris Matrix, the analysis of networks and the systematisation of the observed behaviours. As a result, the different reappropriation behaviours of the Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave are identified and characterised, demonstrating a link between the ‘different arts’ of the Upper Palaeolithic.

full_text: Revisiting palaeolithic rock art. Reading the graphic overlapping in terms of reuse behaviours in the Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave (Cantabria, Spain) Lucía M. Díaz-Gonz´alez a,b,*, Diego Garate c a Museo Nacional y Centro de Investigaci´on de Altamira, Ministerio de Cultura. Avda. Marcelino Sanz de Sautuola, s/n, 39330, Santillana del Mar, Spain b Escuela de Doctorado (EDUC), Edificio Interfacultativo, Universidad de Cantabria, Avda. de los Castros s/n, 39005 Santander, Spain c Instituto Internacional de Investigaciones Prehist´oricas de Cantabria (IIIPC), Universidad de Cantabria, Gobierno de Cantabria, Avda. de los Castros 52, 39005 Santander, Spain A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Palaeolithic Rock Art Reuse Reappropriation Human Behaviour Network Analysis El Castillo cave Overlapping A B S T R A C T The study of figures overlapping in Palaeolithic rock art has been approached by the scientific discipline fundamentally from two perspectives. The first has been interpreted as the result of temporal relationships and the second as a question of the spatial composition of the panels or compositional units of prolonged artistic production. This work complements these assessments with a re-reading of the overlaps in terms of graphic appropriation behaviours produced throughout the Upper Palaeolithic. To this end, we propose a methodology that combines the study of Palaeolithic panels with new photographic technologies, their reading in decorative phases through the application of the Harris Matrix, the analysis of networks and the systematisation of the observed behaviours. As a result, the different reappropriation behaviours of the Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave are identified and characterised, demonstrating a link between the ‘different arts’ of the Upper Palaeolithic. 1. Introduction Palaeolithic rock art is the most enduring artistic manifestation in the history of humankind. It is perpetuated over at least 30 millennia, constituting a temporal continuum that characterises our earliest cul­ tures. However, since the beginning of its study, the scientific discipline has agreed that it is not so continuous in terms of its cultural dimension, identifying different characteristics, styles, techniques or themes throughout all the Upper Palaeolithic cultures (Alcalde del Río et al., 1911; Cartailhac and Breuil, 1906; Piette, 1907). This unitary conception of Palaeolithic art has been overcome by the increasing knowledge of the variability of contexts and geographies of this cultural manifestation, leading to the assumption of the complexity of its interpretation and the possibility of the multifunctional nature of art for these societies (Garate, 2007; Alcolea Gonz´alez and de Balbín Behrmann, 2006; Santos, 2017). Many studies assume this complexity for a record of such a long chronology and focus on the search for quantifiable and systematisable premises to address aesthetic, technical and temporal questions. These works represent a break from formalist and linear stylistic analysis and assume the coexistence of several artistic cultures during the Upper Palaeolithic. (Conkey, 2001, 1987; Criado- Boado, 1999; d’Errico and Vanhaeren, 2023; Fritz and Tosello, 2007; Hernando ´Alvarez, 2010; Hodder, 2009; Moro Abadía and Gonz´alez Morales, 2013; Soffer and Conkey, 1997). This approach, together with the rise of new technologies applied to the study of Palaeolithic art and the implementation of dating tech­ niques (Garate, 2007; Garate and Unzueta, 2017; Lorblanchet, 1995; Rivero et al., 2019b; Valladas et al., 2013; Aujoulat, 1987; GRAPP, 1993) highlight the necessity to revisit caves with research trajectories of more than a century, as exemplified by the recent studies in the Cantabrian caves of Santimami˜ne (Gonz´alez Sainz, 2010) and La Pe˜na de Candamo (Corch´on Rodríguez et al., 2017). Moreover, the techniques of analysis, data collection and reading of the graphisms allow us to make more precise approximations of the realities of the past (Quesada, 2008; Rivero et al., 2019b; Bayarri et al., 2019; Ord´as Pastrana et al., 2019). Moreover, these advances have led to a shift in the research trajectory of * Corresponding author at: Museo Nacional y Centro de Investigaci´on de Altamira, Ministerio de Cultura. Avda. Marcelino Sanz de Sautuola, s/n, 39330, Santillana del Mar, Spain. E-mail addresses: lucia.diaz@cultura.gob.es (L.M. Díaz-Gonz´alez), diego.garate@unican.es (D. Garate). Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jasrep https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jasrep.2025.105048 Received 9 December 2024; Received in revised form 8 February 2025; Accepted 10 February 2025 Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 62 (2025) 105048 Available online 22 February 2025 2352-409X/© 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies. Palaeolithic rock art after its first century of development as a scientific discipline. Some studies address the recurrence and appropriation of under­ ground symbolic spaces from their interpretation as Palaeolithic sanctu­ aries (Breuil, 1952; Freeman, 2005; Laming-Emperaire, 1962; Leroi- Gourhan, 1964, 1965, 1983) or as aggregation sites (Conkey, 1992, 1987, 1980), whose ethnographic dimension has also been applied to the Palaeolithic (Lorblanchet, 1995, 1994, 1980). In terms of overlaps, studies have also moved from interpretations of temporal cumulative generation (Fortea, 2007; Gonz´alez Sainz, 2004; Moure Romanillo, 1994) to the introduction of the spatial dimension, as well as the consideration of relevant thematic associations and regional particu­ larisms (Hernando ´Alvarez, 2010; Jord´a Cerd´a, 1988; Leroi-Gourhan, 1983; Sauvet, 2019; Sauvet and Wlodarczyk, 2000). There are also proposals for a re-reading of the traditional interpretations of overlaps, understood as a compositional form of Palaeolithic art (Gonz´alez Sainz and Ruiz Redondo, 2010; Leroi-Gourhan, 1983; de la Rasilla Vives et al., 2004), and others that propose their interpretation as evidence for the identification of recurrence and reuse behaviours of these subterranean spaces (Díaz-Gonz´alez, 2020; Garate et al., 2021). Focusing on the studies that have addressed questions about the patterns of graphic appropriation of decorated caves during the Palae­ olithic, there are still few studies that do so at the micro level, that is, through the reading of parietal art as a key to identify recurrence and reuse behaviours of these subterranean spaces (Díaz-Gonz´alez, 2020; Díaz-Gonz´alez and Garate Maidagan, 2024, “in press”). Therefore, the reuse of Upper Palaeolithic graphic spaces from an artistic point of view is a question in an initial state of study. So far, the proposals set out in this sense have been scarce and inconclusive. Therefore, it is necessary to incorporate new methodologies for data collection and analysis that allow us to consider the reuse of these spaces in terms of material evi­ dence of cultural behaviour. To address this question, it is thus necessary to study caves with a long chronology of frequentation, both in terms of rock art and occupation sites (Díaz-Gonz´alez, 2020). Specifically, those with long-standing artistic compositional units, the complex panels known as palimpsests (Fortea, 2007; Garate et al., 2021; Gonz´alez Sainz, 2004; Gonz´alez Sainz and Ruiz Redondo, 2010; Moure Romanillo, 1994). For this purpose, the graphic stations with evidence of frequent use in terms of compositional recurrence throughout all the stages of the Upper Palaeolithic (Conkey, 1980; Lasheras Curruchaga et al., 2005) will be potential subjects of study. In this sense, the number of cavities with these characteristics present in the French-Cantabrian region is particularly relevant, especially in the central and western part of this region, as it has a higher rate of reuse of cavities among the different Palaeolithic chronocultures (Garate et al., 2021; Gonz´alez Sainz, 2004; Gonz´alez Sainz and Ruiz Redondo, 2010). Examples of this on the Cantabrian coast are the caves of Altamira, El Castillo and Pasiega and the Asturian caves of La Pe˜na de Candamo, Tito Bustillo and Llonín, among others. This proposal aims to generate an empirical and contrastable meth­ odological framework, as well as to review Palaeolithic art in search of evidence that shows social, cultural and/or personal behaviours of Palaeolithic individuals. For this purpose, it is applied to one of the most studied palimpsests since its discovery, the Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave (Cantabria, Spain). 2. Materials: The cave of El Castillo The cave of El Castillo (Fig. 1) is located in the mountain of the same name in Puente Viesgo (Cantabria, Northern Iberia), where there is an important group of caves with Palaeolithic parietal art declared World Heritage by UNESCO in 2008 (El Castillo, La Pasiega, Chimeneas and Las Monedas). It was discovered in 1903 by H. Alcalde del Rio who, together with H. Breuil and H. Obermaier, began the archaeological works in the cave, documenting a powerful stratigraphy of almost 18 occupation levels (Alcalde del Rio et al., 1911; Bernaldo de Quir´os and Maíllo- Fern´andez, 2009; Cabrera Vald´es, 1984; Cabrera Vald´es et al., 2006, 2003; Cabrera Vald´es et al., 2005; Casta˜nos Ugarte, 2018; Gonz´alez- Molina et al., 2024), which today is assumed to date at least from the beginning of the Middle Palaeolithic to the Middle Ages (Martín-Perea et al., 2023). The study of its rock art also began in the early part of the 20th century, and continuing to the present day (Alcalde del Río, 1906; Alcalde del Río et al., 1911; Cabrera Vald´es and Bernaldo de Quir´os, 1994; Collado Giraldo and García Arranz, 2018; d’Errico et al., 2016; Gonz´alez Echegaray, 1972; Gonz´alez Morales and Moure Romanillo, 1984; Groenen, 2008, 2007, 2006a, 2006b; Groenen et al., 2012; Groenen, 2006). The turn of the century and the application of dating techniques have improved our knowledge of their long artistic frequentation (Cabrera Vald´es and Bernaldo de Quir´os, 1994; García- Diez et al., 2015; Moure Romanillo et al., 1996; Pike et al., 2012; Val­ ladas et al, 2013, Valladas et al., 1992), demonstrating the reuse of some of its panels during several Upper Palaeolithic chronocultural periods (Garate, 2006a; Groenen, 2008, 2007, 2006a, 2006b; Moure Romanillo and Gonz´alez Sainz, 2000; Ripoll L´opez et al., 2014; Ripoll et al., 2015; Ruiz Redondo, 2010). Fig. 1. Location of El Castillo cave (Cantabria, Spain) and other sites mentioned in the text. L.M. Díaz-Gonz´alez and D. Garate Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 62 (2025) 105048 2 The compositional unit under study, the Frieze of the Hands, is located between the Descending Gallery and the Gallery of the Hands, and it has been one of the most traditionally studied spaces (Alcalde del Río et al, 1911; Bayarri Cay´on, 2020; Collado Giraldo and García Arranz, 2018; Garate, 2006a; García Guinea and Gonz´alez Echegaray, 1996; Gonz´alez Echegaray, 1972; Gonz´alez Echegaray and Moure Romanillo, 1970; Groenen, 2008, 2007, 2006a, 2006b; Ripoll L´opez et al., 2014; Ripoll Perell´o, 1956; Ripoll et al., 2015; Ruiz Redondo, 2010). The term Frieze of the Hands is used here, as this is the historical name given by Breuil (Alcalde del Río et al., 1911) to this panel. In general, there is a consensus in favour of interpreting five decorative phases (four of Palaeolithic chronology and a final one later). An interesting aspect of the study of the rock art in the cave is also the approach in terms of its conservation and the alterations it has under­ gone. In this sense, it should be noted that the Frieze of the Hands has been altered by contemporary graffiti and its subsequent cleaning, which may have partially affected some of the figures (Bayarri et al., 2019). The choice of the El Castillo cave and, specifically, the Frieze of the Hands, for the study of graphic recurrences, responds to two funda­ mental questions. On the one hand, it meets the necessary conditions to consider it a place with a long chronology of reuse at a graphic level and, on the other, it is a cave with a long history of research with basic premises for the present study that are widely accepted by the scientific community. 3. Methods: Microspatial analysis of the Frieze of the Hands 3.1. Data capture To achieve these objectives, a fieldwork plan was developed between 2021 and 2022, consisting of the in-situ revision of all the graphic units of the Frieze of the Hands in the El Castillo cave; their documentation using conventional photography and macro photography for digital processing (DStretch software); their documentation using photogram­ metry and microphotogrammetry (Agisoft Metashape software); and the preparation of digital tracings (Adobe Photoshop software). The pho­ tographs were taken with a Nikon D5100 camera with Nikon DX18-55 mm and AF MICRO NIKKOR 60 mm lenses and a Dino-Lite AM4113/ AD4113 portable microscope. 3.2. Parietal stratigraphy The arrangement of the graphic units within the decorative phase to which they belong, as well as the proposed temporal execution of the different decorative phases of the compositional unit have been graph­ ically captured through the application of the stratigraphic methodology of the Harris Matrix (Fritz and Tosello, 2007; Harris, 1991; Hernando ´Alvarez, 2010; Loubser, 1997). The visualisation through this Matrix (Harris, 1991) allows the identification of certain graphic units that play a key role in terms of overlapping, as they group in their figure strati­ graphic links in relation to other figures from their own phases and/or from other decorative phases. These key graphic units are the core ele­ ments of this methodological proposal for the identification and analysis of overlaps in Palaeolithic art (Díaz-Gonz´alez, 2020; Gonz´alez Sainz and Ruiz Redondo, 2010; Leroi-Gourhan, 1983). 3.3. Network analysis Once the stratigraphy of the compositional unit has been defined using the Harris Matrix methodology (Harris, 1991), a Network Analysis has been carried out to study the behaviour of the overlaps understood as nodes in the communication network between the different compo­ sitional phases of the panel. In other disciplines, including other branches of archaeology, this type of analysis is carried out to describe the patterns of relationships that exist between elements that form a network, as well as to identify elements that play key roles in the network (Curtin, 2017; Kuby et al., 2009). In this case, network analysis has been carried out using Gephi software (Bastian et al., 2009) through the elaboration of an adjacency matrix, i.e. the stratigraphic relationships observed between the 135 graphic units that are in a situation of overlap with other graphics from different decorative phases. Once the matrix is established, the weight of the network nodes was weighted using the statistical representation Radial Axis Layout from the Circular Layout plugin in Gephi (Groeninger, 2023), to establish which are the key figures among these nodes and thus proceed to their analysis. This statistical analysis groups the nodes of the network on axes radiating outwards from a central circle, the centroid being the nodes of the network with the highest weight. Different clusters of the network were also detected using the Modularity algorithm (Blondel et al., 2008) and represented by different colours. 3.4. Systematisation and analysis of reuse behaviours Placing the focus on these key graphic units at a stratigraphic level, different types of reuse behaviour of the pre-existing ones were sys­ tematised. Based on the typification of these overlaps, we proceeded to the conceptualisation and systematisation of intervention features on the pre-existing graphics (Díaz-Gonz´alez and Garate Maidagan, 2024). 4. Results: The graphic complexity of the Frieze of the Hands The Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave (Fig. 2) presents four clearly differentiated decorative phases of Palaeolithic chronology, in line with previous proposals (Alcalde del Río et al., 1911; Garate, 2006a; Ruiz Redondo, 2010), although some of them could indeed be further divided into sub-phases (Díaz-Gonz´alez, 2020). 324 graphic units (UG) have been identified in this frieze whose attribution to each decorative phase has been made based on four criteria: chronological attribution when there is an U/Th and/or radiocarbon date of a figure on the panel, the stratigraphic position, the execution technique and the colour’s recipe used in the case of painted figures. Nevertheless, these graphic units are distributed similarly among the four phases, belonging to clearly differentiated chronocul­ tural horizons (Table 1). A first decorative phase is identified, phase 1, the earliest, which corresponds to the motifs of hands in negative (n = 45) and discs (n = 57), as well as two stains and a line that seems to complete the negative silhouette of a hand. All of them in paint projected against the wall by dissolving the red and/or violet pigment, with the exception of the aforementioned line. This horizon was interpreted chronologically close to the Aurignacian and early Gravettian (Garate, 2006a; Ruiz Redondo, 2010) and later corroborated by U/Th dating (Pike et al., 2012). The second decorative phase (phase 2) corresponds to the horizon of yellow paintings, where the representation of zoomorphs (17 bovids, 2 horses and a doe) predominates, in relation to signs (2 quadrangular) (Fig. 8), stains or other graphic evidence (dots, lines, rows and clouds of dots, etc.) also present in this phase. A bison (Fig. 3) is anatomically complete in this phase, painted in yellow and at least partially reworked in red, the back half of which is affected by a modern cleaning of the wall. The technique used in this phase is drawing with paint using different dyes, mainly yellow. A Gravettian chronology is proposed due to its stratigraphic relationships and the stylistic and iconographic par­ allels of both its representations and those found underneath and superimposed on them (Garate et al., 2021). The third decorative phase proposed includes digital strokes and dots and abstract signs, with zoomorphs being anecdotally represented in this phase by the presence of a horse’s prothorax, a possible auroch and an indeterminate animal’s head. These motifs were executed using similar techniques of applying paint directly to the wall. Their technical execution, together with the stratigraphic situation of both the strokes L.M. Díaz-Gonz´alez and D. Garate Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 62 (2025) 105048 3 and digital dots and the abstract signs, place them above the phase of yellow animals and below the phase of engravings. We propose that phase 3 would fall within a Gravetto-Solutrean horizon based on its themes (mostly paired digitations, series of punctuations and quadran­ gular and triangular signs), as well as in relation to the morphologies and stylistic conventions of the zoomorphs (similar to the horizons of dotted red figures present, for example, in the neighbouring La Pasiega cave) (Garate, 2006b). The stratigraphic relationships of its graphic units with respect to the chronology of phase 4 also support this chro­ nological proposal. Concerning the last decorative phase assignable to the Palaeolithic in this panel, there is a greater presence of other graphic evidence (mainly lines) accompanied by a good number of zoomorphs and some signs. It is made up entirely of engraved graphics with incised lines, mostly Fig. 2. Díaz-Gonz´alez and D. Garate Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 62 (2025) 105048 4 multiple, with the presence of striations and interior scratches on the figures. Among the zoomorphs, deer are the most common (accounting for 74 % of this category in this phase), with an average size among the complete zoomorphs of 48.50 cm in length and 31.58 cm in height. This phase, both for its subject matter and execution techniques, is assigned to a Lower Magdalenian chronological horizon due to the parallels with the portable art (de Heras Martín, 2018; Rivero et al., 2019a). In relation to the technical analysis of this compositional unit as a whole, we observe a primacy of painting over engraving, the latter only present in phase 4, as well as a striking absence of the use of black colour, abundant in the Palaeolithic rock art record and present in other compositional units in the El Castillo cave. Based on the graphisms studied, the Frieze of the Hands would be composed of red, violet, yel­ low and brownish dyes, as well as their mixtures. Regarding the icon­ ographic review, the weight of bovids’ representation stands out in phase 2, which changes to cervids in phase 4, while the number of representations of other taxa, such as horses, is low, although they are represented in all the phases with zoomorphs. The spatial analysis of recurrence shows that there are particularly concentrated decorative episodes, such as phase 4, which is distributed in only three sectors of the panel, as opposed to other more expansive moments, such as the first phase, which is practically distributed throughout the entire space of the panel. Moreover, there is also an area of the panel with a very low level of graphic occupation, coinciding with an area of high visibility from the entrance to the room where it is located (Fig. 2). The systematisation of this compositional unit in the Harris Matrix (Fig. 4), after reading the 159 documented overlapping relationships, also shows the existence of certain key figures in terms of overlapping (Figs. 6 and 7). The results of this panel allow us to affirm that there is a certain tendency towards overlapping, which is also present from the first decorative phase (Fig. 5A). The percentages of accumulation of overlaps and the systematic appearance of key figures in the Harris Matrix stand out in both phase 2 and phase 4 (both being the least Fig. 3. Yellow bison (CAS.PMI.132) with red repaint from Phase 2 of the Frieze of the Hands from El Castillo cave. Fig. 4. Areas of the Harris Matrix (Stratify software) of the Frieze of the Hands from El Castillo cave where some key figures from the panel are located. Source: Modified from Díaz-Gonz´alez (2020). L.M. Díaz-Gonz´alez and D. Garate Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 62 (2025) 105048 5 numerous in terms of the number of graphic units), an issue that co­ incides with the fact that both phases are those in which the iconography with the highest rate of overlapping (zoomorphs) are mostly distributed (Fig. 5B). The complete analysis of the stratigraphic relationships of the graphic units of the panel (Figs. 4 and 5) has revealed that 49 % of them are in a relationship of overlapping. In other words, practically half of the figures are in direct contact with other figures in the panel. Besides, those that are not, they are in a relationship of close juxtaposition (ac­ cording to the terminology of Leroi-Gourhan, 1983) with the others. Network analysis focused on 135 graphic units that are in an over­ lapping relationship with graphic units from other decorative phases. (Fig. 6). Among them, the key figures with greater weight in the network correspond mainly to zoomorphic representations and primarily asso­ ciated with the second decorative phase of the panel. Two bison (CAS. PM.I.133 and CAS.PM.I.83) are the central figures that articulate this composition in terms of stratigraphic relationships. Five dominant clusters were identified in this analysis, grouping half of the 135 graphic units. Two of them, in grey and maroon, have an identical weight, with a representativeness of 12.59 % each in terms of importance in this overlapping network. The grey cluster has only one key figure, CAS.PM. I.83 (bison, phase 2, Fig. 7). The maroon cluster is composed of four dominant figures, CAS.PM.I.14 (zoomorph, phase 3), CAS.PM.I.20 (hind, phase 4), CAS.PM.I.16 (hand, phase 1) and CAS.PM.I.29 (bison, phase 2). The next most important cluster (with a representativeness of 11.85 %), coloured in orange, is constituted by a single key figure, CAS. PM.I.133 (bison, phase 2). The fourth cluster (with a representativeness of 7.41 %), coloured in black, consists of a single key figure, CAS.PM. I.102 (bison, phase 2). And the fifth cluster (with a representativeness of 6.67 %), mustard-coloured, is formed by a single key figure, CAS.PM. I.221 (sign, phase 1). The study of the types of overlapping detected in the Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave (Díaz-Gonz´alez and Garate Maidagan, 2024) allows us to affirm that in this panel most of the superimposed compositional recurrence behaviour is what we call Overlapping without consideration (Fig. 9B), defined as the ‘existence of a direct overlapping between two graphisms without any intentionality of resignification of the pre-existing figure’ (Díaz-Gonz´alez and Garate Maidagan, 2024). This Overlapping without consideration appears both between figures belonging to the same decorative phase and between figures from different phases (Figs. 8 and 9B). In this panel, two other recurrent behaviours are located in smaller proportions. Overlapping with assimilation (Fig. 9C) is also identified as the ‘reuse of graphic units by modifying or completing pre-existing graphisms, in order to integrate them and keep them in force in subse­ quent compositions’ (Díaz-Gonz´alez and Garate Maidagan, 2024). The other behaviour is the Overlapping with integration (Fig. 9A), or what would imply the ‘reuse of previous graphic units through the composi­ tional articulation of the new figures. The significance of the previous graphisms is maintained in the new compositions without requiring any modification’ (Díaz-Gonz´alez and Garate Maidagan, 2024). Overlapping with assimilation has only been documented in zoomorphic figures at different times, and its modifications belong to a decorative phase that occurred after the one when it was created (Figs. 8 and 9C). The Over­ lapping with integration appears in a slightly larger proportion, is found both in figures belonging to the same decorative phase and different phases, as well as in zoomorphic signs and figures (Figs. 8 and 9A). 5. Discussion The Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave, due to its char­ acteristics of location, accessibility, visibility and compositional recur­ rence, would fall within the concept of Palaeolithic sanctuaries (Freeman, 2005; Leroi-Gourhan, 1965) as it is located in an area separate from the Fig. 5. A) Stratigraphic relationships of overlapping by decorative phases and B) by iconography detected in the Frieze of the Hands from El Castillo cave. Fig. 6. Network analysis of the Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave. The size of the nodes is defined by their degree of relation with other nodes. The colours represent the different identified clusters. L.M. Díaz-Gonz´alez and D. Garate Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 62 (2025) 105048 6 activities of daily life. This term has a particular significance in this cave, since according to Leroi-Gourhan (1965) these sanctuaries present a single main panel of compositional recurrence or palimpsest. Although there is another panel in this cave with similar cumulative characteris­ tics in terms of decorative phases, known as the Panel of the Polychromes, it cannot be considered to be of equal symbolic impor­ tance as it has a much smaller number of graphic units. Therefore, this Frieze of the Hands could be considered to be the main panel in the cave of El Castillo. In this way, the cave is also in line with later proposals regarding the variability and complexity of the iconographic structuring Fig. 7. Example of a study of the bison CAS.PM.I.83, a key figure in terms of overlapping according to the network analysis (Fig. 6), from the Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave using macro photography and digital image processing applied to the pictorial technique. In this case, 13 areas with overlaps are identified (containing 19 overlapping points photographed with a macro lens and a larger area in which macro-photogrammetry is carried out), to be studied in detail with DStretch (filters used: lab, crgb, lbk, yye, ywe and lds) and to proceed to the reading of their stratigraphic relationships. L.M. Díaz-Gonz´alez and D. Garate Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 62 (2025) 105048 7 of subterranean symbolic spaces (Gonz´alez Sainz, 2005; Jord´a Cerd´a, 1988; Sauvet, 2019). Furthermore, these same characteristics of the Frieze of the Hands also make it a good example of the aggregation sites (Conkey, 1992, 1980; Lee, 1979, 1972, 1976) concept, in line with their public nature (Moure Romanillo, 1994; Rouzaud, 1993) and, therefore, as a material manifestation of social acts of Palaeolithic human groups (Sauvet, 2019). For this reason, the Frieze of the Hands from the El Castillo cave is an ideal place for the study and understanding of the compositional recurrence behaviours (Díaz-Gonz´alez and Garate Maidagan, 2024) of Upper Palaeolithic human groups, as it presents decorative phases with a broad chronological sequence. This question is the starting point for suggesting that the recurrent behaviours observed, both in terms of representativeness and temporality, could be more social than individ­ ual acts (Sauvet, 2019). The temporal organisation of this broad sequence in relation to the concept of decorative phase proposed in this work is in line with what has been proposed in previous studies (Alcalde del Río et al., 1911; Garate, 2006a; Ruiz Redondo, 2010). Although here we have identified a further phase that complements the phases of the previous proposals for chronologies earlier than the Magdalenian (Ruiz Redondo, 2010). These differences in the definition of the pre-Magdalenian phases may be due to a lesser general knowledge in the discipline of these older periods, which limits the ability to discern the graphic activity of each pre-Magdalenian cultural period. This question has been overcome through the parallelism of certain figures in this frieze with others belonging to other sites with well- documented pre-Magdalenian decorative phases. One of the cases has been the location of red horse protome, both in the Frieze of the Hands and in the cave of La Pe˜na de Candamo (northern Iberia). In the latter, according to its stratigraphic relationships, the protome belongs to a Solutrean decorative phase of the Muro de los Grabados (Corch´on Rodríguez et al., 2017). This horse protome from El Castillo is also technically and stylistically closely related to the red-figure horizon well documented in many sites of the Cantabrian coast, such as Covalanas and El Pendo, among others (Garate 2006b). Likewise, in the Iberian pre-Pyrenean region we can also find the cave of La Fuente del Trucho, another important and well-documented analogue of these pre- Magdalenian decorative phases. As in the Frieze of the Hands, an ancient compositional sequence (from the Aurignacian, Gravettian and Solutrean) has been documented in this cave, consisting of negative hands at the base of the stratigraphy, painted zoomorphic figures and a series of dots and digitations on both (Utrilla et al., 2012). In all the phases, including the earliest (Phase 1), different in­ teractions of overlapping and juxtaposition between figures have been identified, as well as between figures from two or more decorative phases, whether simultaneous in time or not. The fact that practically half of its graphic units are in direct contact with other graphics on the panel allows us to affirm that the people who added figures to the composition had an explicit intention to interact with the existing graphisms. That is, it denotes that compositional recurrence, overlaps and close juxtapositions are intentional behaviours in this panel throughout the Upper Palaeolithic (Figs. 5 and 8). This fact refutes the arguments that so far have argued that the overlaps were merely accumulations resulting from the compositional recurrence of the panel, taking place mostly in the last decorative phases Fig. 8. Garate Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 62 (2025) 105048 9 of the Upper Palaeolithic, the only time for which the intentionality of the overlapping had been proposed (Gonz´alez Sainz and Ruiz Redondo, 2010; Leroi-Gourhan, 1983). The first studies that mention the reuse of decorated panels in caves such as Altamira and El Castillo (Alcalde del Río et al., 1911) attribute the construction of these palimpsests first to the oldest phases of the Upper Palaeolithic (for example, the Gravet­ tian), then to the intermediate phases (Solutrean and Early Magdale­ nian) and finally to the Middle Magdalenian (Moure Romanillo, 1994). These studies, which attribute the overlaps to the cumulative fact of palimpsest formation, without understanding a cultural intentionality in them beyond the reuse of a decorative surface, has been reflected in studies of cavities throughout the Cantabrian region for half a century (Fortea, 2007; Garate et al., 2021; Gonz´alez Sainz, 2004), and this panel seems to differ from this pattern. Furthermore, from the first decorative phase of the Frieze of the Hands, in very early chronological horizons, we can also observe the compositional intention to occupy the entire available space, that is, to appropriate the entire canvas from the beginning of the composition. This panel is also evidence of the sys­ tematic recurrence of these palimpsests throughout all the phases of the Upper Palaeolithic, as shown by the multitude of overlaps observed on it. Another argument in favour of the interpretation of overlaps as an intentional behaviour is the fact that the graphic units that are not in a situation of overlapping, do so in a relationship of close juxtaposition, as opposed to broad juxtaposition (Leroi-Gourhan, 1983). This evidence leads us to propose close juxtaposition as further evidence of the compositional intentionality of the graphic recurrence, since the new figures are made in the same place as the figures from previous periods (Díaz-Gonz´alez and Garate Maidagan, 2024), demonstrating an inten­ tional use of the same cave space and thus reinforce the importance of the use of this area in different chronological times. This hypothesis is also supported by the spatial analysis of the occupation of the available area of the panel throughout the composi­ tional recurrences of the different decorative phases. The trend observed in terms of overlapping in this panel does not respond to the spatial needs of the composition, as there are sectors of the panel without an abundance of graphic evidence, one of them being an area of high vis­ ibility. This means that the non-intervention in certain sectors available with high visibility in later phases may indicate an intentional selective behaviour to reseal specific areas decorated with specific themes or at specific times (Fig. 2). In addition, the study of the overlapping relationships through the Harris Matrix of the panel (Fig. 4) demonstrates the existence of some key figures that seem to act as a link between the different decorative phases of the compositional unit. The network analysis of the strati­ graphic relationships observed in the compositional unit has also allowed us to assess the weight of these nodes and identify key figures in terms of communication between cultural codes from different chro­ nological stages (Fig. 6). Several figures function as communication nodes both intra-phases, i.e., within the same decorative phase, and inter-phase, as they share significant amounts of super- and infra- position with other figures (Figs. 6 and 7). These key figures, espe­ cially in the relationships between the different phases, are highly rep­ resented in phase 2 of this panel, i.e., the intentional overlapping is observed from a Gravettian chronocultural horizon. In this panel these key figures are mostly zoomorphs, and the first cluster of key figures only has the type Overlapping without consideration represented. The iconographic trend observed in the key figures in this panel, allows us to affirm that a high percentage of them are zoomorphic figures. It is also relevant that four of the five majority clusters present a single key figure, of which 75 % correspond to the representation of a bison and belong to the phase attributed to the Gravettian. In order to consider these trends statistically representative, it is necessary to reinforce them with a study on a macro-spatial scale. The same situation applies to whether the articulation of compositions of broad chronological sequence can take place around key figures from other cultural horizons. Furthermore, such a macro application will allow us to advance the hypothesis that this is a continuous behaviour of these societies on both the spatial and temporal scales. Focusing on the compositional recurrence behaviours in terms of overlapping, the type defined as Overlapping without consideration is overrepresented. Furthermore, this type of behaviour is present in all the decorative phases of the panel (Fig. 8). Both issues suggest that this is a recurrent behaviour among all the human groups that decorated this panel throughout the Upper Palaeolithic. The hypothesis of a trans­ cultural behaviour of prolonged temporality could be proposed. Nevertheless, further studies on a macro spatial scale, including other graphical stations from other geographic areas, are required to verify it. Another observation that can be made in this panel is that phases 2 and 4, which according to our proposal correspond to the Gravettian and Early Magdalenian horizons, are the ones with the highest rate of overlapping (Figs. 4, 5 and 6). This suggests that, in palimpsests with a long chronology of graphic intervention, the Gravettian and Magdale­ nian phases are very likely to be superimposed on previous phases and that they contain the key figures that articulate the panel in terms of diachrony. Although the macro-spatial study will allow us to contrast pre­ liminary interpretations, the identification of compositional recurrence behaviours in minority percentages in the Frieze of the Hands enables us to raise several hypotheses about reuse behaviour. In the case of the Overlapping with assimilation, its appearance only in zoomorphic figures in differentiated decorative phases may be evidence of a momentary need to update the iconographies in order to keep them valid at later times. In other words, transforming an iconographic code to adapt it to the current message at the time of its re-signification. The low per­ centage in which this type of overlapping appears may be linked to the proposal that it is a question of individual behaviour (Sauvet, 2019), although it is true that a larger corpus of data on a macro scale is needed to confirm this statement. A good example that illustrates the behaviour of Overlapping with integration is the yellow quadrangular sign, made in a Gravettian chro­ nocultural horizon that is later circumscribed by a thin striated engraving to delimit its upper part, during the Lower Magdalenian (Fig. 9A). This situation, which occurs more often in this panel and curiously in figures from both cultural horizons, allows us to consider the possibility of finding evidence of a behaviour of reappropriation of a pre-existing iconography long after (Díaz-Gonz´alez, 2020). It could also confirm the hypothesis of the compositional intentionality of the over­ lapping behaviour in the Palaeolithic, as we propose that it could be evidence of an interest in giving meaning to a Gravettian figure at the Magdalenian time. This behaviour of integrating the past into the pre­ sent is also documented at Australian Aboriginal rock art aggregation sites (Bowdler, 1988; Lorblanchet, 1995, 1994, 1980). In these sanctu­ ary sites, the aboriginal groups joined to retouch the already existing figures within the context of a series of rites of nature regeneration. Therefore, this Overlapping with integration would be in line with the interpretation proposed by M. Lorblanchet (Lorblanchet, 1994, 1980) for the reading of Palaeolithic palimpsests. Furthermore, the macro-scale study of the Overlapping with integra­ tion will be of particular interest in order to determine whether there are specific types of zoomorphic signs and figures in which this behaviour appears. If so, we could be looking at the presence of specific cultural codes shared cross-culturally over several millennia and, perhaps, in a large part of the European geography in which these artistic manifes­ tations are developed. 6. Conclusions The application of the proposed methodology proves to be an important tool for the analysis of diachronic panels of Palaeolithic rock art and the approach to the study of the symbolic behaviours of these societies. This work is the first to establish different types of overlaps L.M. Díaz-Gonz´alez and D. Garate Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 62 (2025) 105048 10 systematically and through quantitative data, as well as to apply network analysis to the statistical study of overlaps in Palaeolithic rock art. Therefore, this approach has proved to be ideal to contrast or corroborate hypotheses that are currently under discussion and need to be processed with abundant data to support them. However, it is necessary to extend the study on a macro scale to other graphic stations of compositional recurrence, such as those mentioned in the Cantabrian region, to advance our understanding of this human behaviour (Díaz- Gonz´alez, 2020). Our study provides new data for understanding certain social and cultural behaviours in terms of compositional recurrence codes in graphic material during the Upper Palaeolithic. This recently opened research line on rock art will undoubtedly allow, in its application on a large scale, to reveal behaviours shared by the human groups of the different technocomplexes of the Upper Palaeolithic, just as other more consolidated lines of research do through the study of the material culture of these societies. title: RTI and the study of engraved rock art: A re-examination of the Iberian south-western stelae of Sete ﬁ lla and Almadén de la Plata 2 (Seville,
abstract: RTI is a powerful technique for recording, interpreting, and disseminating rock art. RTI enhances the perception of the micro-topography of the rock surface and it is particularly helpful for the study of engraved art. Subtle details, such as the traces left by different engraving techniques, the outlines of motifs or superimpositions are more clearly revealed through RTI's interactive re-light and enhancement tools. This paper describes the application of RTI for the re-examination of two Iberian south-western stelae, Seteﬁlla and Almadén de la Plata 2, whose preserved decoration is engraved. Previous studies focused on the iconographic analysis of motifs and employed methods of examination and recording that posed limitations. Based on the more robust data provided by RTI and supported by RTI's tools for surface interpretation, we provide a new analysis of the decorated surfaces of both stelae, including insights into their manufacturing techniques and later modiﬁcation. & 2015 Published by Elsevier Ltd.

full_text: RTI and the study of engraved rock art: A re-examination of the Iberian south-western stelae of Seteﬁlla and Almadén de la Plata 2 (Seville, Spain) Marta Díaz-Guardamino a,n, Leonardo García Sanjuán b, David Wheatley a, Víctor Rodríguez Zamora b a University of Southampton, Department of Archaeology, Avenue Campus, Southampton SO17 1BF, United Kingdom b University of Seville, Department of Prehistory and Archaeology, María de Padilla s/n, 41004 Seville, Spain a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received 11 March 2015 Received in revised form 6 July 2015 Accepted 9 July 2015 Available online 17 July 2015 Keywords: Reﬂectance Transformation Imaging (RTI) Rock Art Engraving Techniques Life Histories Late Bronze Age South-western stelae Iberian Peninsula a b s t r a c t RTI is a powerful technique for recording, interpreting, and disseminating rock art. RTI enhances the perception of the micro-topography of the rock surface and it is particularly helpful for the study of engraved art. Subtle details, such as the traces left by different engraving techniques, the outlines of motifs or superimpositions are more clearly revealed through RTI's interactive re-light and enhancement tools. This paper describes the application of RTI for the re-examination of two Iberian south-western stelae, Seteﬁlla and Almadén de la Plata 2, whose preserved decoration is engraved. Previous studies focused on the iconographic analysis of motifs and employed methods of examination and recording that posed limitations. Based on the more robust data provided by RTI and supported by RTI's tools for surface interpretation, we provide a new analysis of the decorated surfaces of both stelae, including insights into their manufacturing techniques and later modiﬁcation. & 2015 Published by Elsevier Ltd. 1. Reﬂectance Transformation Imaging (RTI) and the study of engraved rock art Reﬂectance Transformation Imaging (RTI) is a computational photographic method that uses the reﬂectance properties of any surface to enhance the perception of its shape (Earl et al., 2010; Mudge et al., 2006; Mudge et al., 2012). RTI requires the capture of around 60–70 images of a still object from a ﬁxed camera position with changing and knowable light directions spaced evenly through a hemisphere. RTI software uses these datasets to calculate the reﬂectance function of the object's surface per-pixel and to extract 2.5D information about its shape (surface normals perpixel). The resulting RTI ﬁles produce isometric (2.5D) representations of the object's surface through interactive re-illumination and the application of various transformations that enhance perception of its texture and shape. There are two main methods of capturing the images required to produce RTI ﬁles. One is a semi-automated method that uses a physical lighting dome of ﬁxed size which is populated with a number of lights whose positions are known. A more ﬂexible system is the so-called Highlight-RTI (HRTI) method (Cultural Heritage Imaging, 2013b), which uses a glossy sphere incorporated into the scene to record the highlight of the source for every image. This information is then used to calculate the source light directions during RTI processing. HRTI is ideal for capturing large objects that do not ﬁt within a physical dome or for any setting where a physical dome is not a viable option, such as rock art sites. RTI processing is quite easy and accessible. The software required, the RTIBuilder1, is open source, and the steps are described in detail in a comprehensive guide published by Cultural Heritage Imaging (CHI) (2011). Once produced, the RTI ﬁle can be explored with the free RTIViewer2 (Cultural Heritage Imaging, 2013a), also available from the CHI website. RTIViewer enables enhanced perception and interpretation of the object's surface through interactive rendering the surface with dynamic re-lighting and permitting the application of various enhancement transformations, called ‘rendering modes’, to shape and/or colour information per Contents lists available at ScienceDirect journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/daach Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.daach.2015.07.002 2212-0548/& 2015 Published by Elsevier Ltd. n Corresponding author. E-mail address: M.M.Diaz-Guardamino-Uribe@soton.ac.uk (M. Díaz-Guardamino). 1 Available at: http://culturalheritageimaging.org/What_We_Offer/Downloads/ Process/index.html (last accessed 15/12/2014). 2 Available at: http://culturalheritageimaging.org/What_We_Offer/Downloads/ View/index.html (last accessed 15/12/2014). Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 41–54 pixel. Speciﬁc views can be ‘snapshotted’ and saved as separate JPEG ﬁles or saved as ‘bookmarks’ associated with the RTI ﬁle (bookmarks record the parameters that deﬁne a particular view, allowing its recreation and annotation by other viewers), fostering the engagement of multiple researchers in the interpretation of the view or detail in question. RTI has already been successfully applied for the representation, conservation and analysis of a wide range of cultural heritage artefacts (e.g. Earl et al., 2011,, 2010; Kotoula and Kyranoudi, 2013; Piquette, 2011) and its application to rock art is gaining momentum (e.g. Díaz-Guardamino and Wheatley, 2013; Mudge et al., 2006; Mudge et al., 2012; Pitts et al., 2014a; Pitts et al., 2014b). RTI is an extremely powerful technique for recording, interpreting, and disseminating rock art: RTI enhances the perception of the micro-topography of the rock surface and is, therefore, particularly helpful for the study of engraved art. Subtle details, such as natural ﬁssures and cracks in the rock surface, traces left by different engraving techniques or outlines of engraved motifs are more clearly revealed using RTI's interactive re-lighting and enhancement tools and because the use of raking lighting, for example, is familiar to many rock art specialists, RTI provides a relatively ‘natural’ digital methodology that draws on and enhances the existing interpretative skills of the specialist community. Overall, RTI's interactive rendering tools facilitate the interpretation of the shape of these features and the rock canvas, their interrelationship and structure, allowing archaeologists to explore them in terms of cultural production. This paper describes the application of RTI for the re-examination of two Iberian south-western stelae, Seteﬁlla and Almadén de la Plata 2 (Seville, Spain), whose preserved decoration is engraved. Previous research on Iberian south-western stelae has typically been focused on the formal analysis of their iconographies: little or no attention has been devoted to the techniques employed in their making, or to the modiﬁcations they underwent throughout later stages of their long-term ‘lives’. As argued below, these aspects could greatly advance our understanding of Iberian south-western stelae in social, cultural and historical terms. Additionally, research on south-western stelae has usually employed methods of examination and recording that posed limitations for their interpretation, i.e. scale drawings and tracings made from photographs, aided by limited sources of raking light. Based on the more robust data provided by RTI and supported by the use of RTI tools for surface interpretation, here we provide a new analysis of the decorated surfaces of both stelae, including some insights into the techniques involved in their making and later modiﬁcations, as well as new interpretative tracings. 2. Iberian south-western stelae: the many dimensions of a late prehistoric ‘tradition’ The stelae examined in this paper, those of Seteﬁlla and Almadén de la Plata 2, belong to a well-known stelae tradition that developed in the west and the south-west of the Iberian Peninsula, along the western and south-western periphery of the Iberian Central Plateau and the middle-low Guadalquivir valley, throughout the Late Bronze Age and the beginning of the Early Iron Age (c. 1400/1200-750 BC) (Díaz-Guardamino, 2012) (Fig. 1). These are variously known as ‘Extremaduran’, ‘Tartesian’,’Warrior’ or ‘South-western’ stelae (for recent syntheses see: Celestino Pérez, 2001; Díaz-Guardamino, 2010; Harrison, 2004). More than 130 stelae have been published until now while at least a further dozen have been reportedly found in recent years and await scientiﬁc examination. Traditionally, research on south-western stelae has been focused on the formal analysis, classiﬁcation and seriation of their iconography and on the cultural afﬁliation of the objects represented on them (Almagro-Gorbea, 1977; Celestino Pérez, 2001; Gomes and Monteiro, 1977; Pingel, 1974). Relevant issues, such as their landscape dimension, archaeological context, the biographies of these monuments or the techniques involved in their making have only started to be explored more recently (Díaz-Guardamino, 2006, Díaz-Guardamino, 2008, 2010, 2012; Díaz-Guardamino and Wheatley, 2013; Enríquez Navascués and Fernández Algaba, 2010; Galán Domingo, 1993; García Sanjuán, 2011; García Sanjuán et al., 2006). South-western stelae are large (c. 50–160 cm long), often dressed, stone blocks that bear engraved decoration on one of their sides, usually in their upper and middle regions. The lower Fig. 1. Distribution of south-western stelae in southern Iberia with indication of the sites where the stelae of Almadén de la Plata and Seteﬁlla were found. M. Díaz-Guardamino et al. / Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 41–54 42 part is usually left plain and/or unworked, probably because the stela was meant to be imbedded in the ground. The preserved decoration consists of engraved motifs depicting, among other things, weapons (spear, sword, and shield and sometimes helmet), human ﬁgures, two-horsed chariots, objects relating to personal grooming (such as combs and mirrors), brooches and small quadrupeds, possibly dogs. Although these stelae could have been painted, no analysis has been undertaken to prove this possibility until very recently3. These decorated monoliths are generally found as un-stratiﬁed remains in the open landscape and, as a result, have mainly been treated as decontextualised remains. Almost no systematic archaeological examination of their ﬁnd-spots has been undertaken until very recently4 (García Sanjuán et al., 2006) and only a very few stelae have been discovered within, or in association with, conventional (stratiﬁed) archaeological contexts. Most of these contexts have not been recorded archaeologically and our knowledge of them is mostly based on the oral accounts of the discoverers (Díaz-Guardamino, 2012, 2014). In terms of their iconography, south-western stelae have conventionally been classiﬁed into three main groups which are deﬁned by the presence/absence of particular motifs (Almagro-Gorbea, 1977: 168–174; Díaz-Guardamino, 2012; Gomes and Monteiro, 1977: 185–188; Pingel, 1974). Subtype IIA (also called B or ‘Basic’) are restricted to representation of three elements – the spear, shield and sword – arranged on the stone surface as if the stone itself were the representation of a body. Subtype IIB (or BþO: basic with additional objects) may include the same basic panoply but with some additional objects such as combs, brooches or mirrors. Finally, subtype IIC (or A: with anthropomorphic ﬁgure) includes the basic panoply, additional objects, and also a schematic human ﬁgure which tends to occupy a prominent place in the iconographic display. Some stelae may include two or more human ﬁgures, occasionally in compositions that seem to have a narrative arrangement, perhaps constituting a fourth subtype IID. The ascription of south-western stelae to a ‘tradition’ relies on the resemblance they share, which mainly emerges from the combined representation of swords, shields, spears and/or human ﬁgures that are depicted in similar styles. Within this tradition, the broad ‘types’ or groups traditionally distinguished by researchers – described above – show supra-regional distributions. In this context, the understanding of the iconographic variability of south-western stelae in cultural and social terms becomes challenging, because their variability in terms of presence/absence of motifs shows no obvious geographical pattern. In fact, a recent study of motifs represented on south-western stelae by means of Correspondence Analysis suggests that the objects that are being represented are the objects – or the knowledge of them – that are circulating across western Iberia throughout the Late Bronze Age and the beginning of the Iron Age, and that their inclusion in the iconographic vocabulary of Late Bronze Age stelae is closely related to the changing patterns of longdistance social interaction documented in the peninsula during those periods (Díaz-Guardamino et al., 2015). Our current research is focusing on the analysis of other layers of variability that may advance our understanding of this tradition in social and historical terms. We are starting to investigate the positioning and topology of motifs which may exhibit some spatial patterning. We are also examining the techniques employed in the manufacture of stelae in order to clarify their variability at higher resolutions, to assess different technological styles, the skill of the engraver, and how these varied in time and space. Currently, we suggest that south-western stelae appear to exhibit different degrees of standardisation at different levels (i.e. presence/absence of motifs, their topology on the stone canvas and the technique employed in their manufacture), and each of these needs to be examined separately to achieve a cogent understanding of this complex tradition as a social product that emerged through time. The commemorative role of these monuments has also been examined during the last decade (Díaz-Guardamino, 2006, 2008, 2010, 2011, 2014; García Sanjuán et al., 2007; García Sanjuán and Wheatley, 2010). These monuments were clearly conceived to endure and must have been visible in the landscape. Some of them were actively involved in social life long after their conception and manufacture: they could be reused in many different ways, even modiﬁed through later interventions that sought to transform the original engravings (by erasing or re-engraving old motifs or engraving new motifs), or even destroyed (e.g. fragmentation of the stones) (García Sanjuán and Díaz-Guardamino, 2015: 189–196). Documenting these later interventions is therefore also key to assessing the enduring social impact of these monuments through time. 3. The stelae of Seteﬁlla and Almadén de la Plata 2 (Seville, Spain): brief history of research The stelae of Seteﬁlla and Almadén de la Plata 2 have been studied previously, although their histories of research are rather different. The stela of Seteﬁlla (Fig. 2:A) was discovered in 1927, during the excavations carried out by G.E. Bonsor and R. Thouvenot at the necropolis of Seteﬁlla (Lora del Río, Seville, Spain) (Bonsor and Thouvenot, 1928: 35–37). Overall, Bonsor and Thouvenot explored ten tumuli, some of them covering large monumental burial chambers, and ten isolated ﬂat graves, all dated to the Iron Age, as well as six Roman graves. When found, the stela was laid – presumably in secondary position – covering a ﬂat grave with its decorated surface facing lateralwards (Fig. 2:B). The grave, dug into the soil and bedrock, was about 65 cm deep, and contained the remains of an urn, charred bones and a poorly preserved skeleton orientated north-south. Perpendicular to this grave was a second one containing another skeleton and pottery sherds dated to the Early Iron Age (Bonsor and Thouvenot, 1928: 32). These authors published a preliminary sketch of the decoration of the stela (Fig. 3:A) which included a human ﬁgure, a shield and a possible helmet, and mentioned the width of the engravings, 7 mm (Bonsor and Thouvenot, 1928: 32, Fig. 25). Bonsor and Thouvenot did not provide information on the techniques they employed to examine and represent the engravings of this stela. Given the techniques available at the time, we may assume that they deployed raking light and scale drawing. The same may be suggested for the more detailed drawing (Fig. 3:B) published by M. Almagro Basch some decades later (Almagro Basch, 1974), which only differed from that published by Bonsor and Thouvenot in some minor details (Almagro Basch, 1974: 11–15, Fig. 5). Almagro Basch offered a formal analysis and description of the engraved motifs found on the stela, but did not consider other aspects such as the techniques employed in their making. During the 1970s, Aubet (1975, 1978) undertook new archaeological excavations at the necropolis of Seteﬁlla. Her intervention focused on two of the already-known tumuli containing burial chambers – dated to the Early Iron Age – and two tumuli discovered beneath them in the course of these excavations covering a series of plain stelae and several pits containing urn-cremations. Currently, these underlying tumuli and cremations are dated to the end of the Late Bronze Age and the beginnings of the Iron Age 3 Dr. Miguel Ángel Rogerio Candelera, from the Instituto de Recursos Naturales y Agrobiología of Seville (IRNAS-CSIC), Spain, has applied digital image analysis techniques to the study of the south-western stela of Mirasiviene (Seville). The results will be soon published as part of the publication of the broader research led by the ﬁrst three authors (MDG, LGS and DW) to study the stela of Mirasiviene and its archaeological context. 4 See note 3. So far, the evidence suggests that the site was most intensively used for mortuary practices during the end of the Late Bronze Age and the Early Iron Age but, as further data reveals, the site seems to have been persistently used for mortuary purposes well before (Late and Middle Bronze Age) (Aubet, 1997 , 2009: 84) and after this period (Late Iron Age, Roman period) (Aubet, 1978; Bonsor and Thouvenot, 1928). The description of the context in which the stela was found is quite concise but strongly suggests that the grave it covered dates to the Early Iron Age. The manufacture of the stela can only be loosely dated by considering the motifs depicted on it, which would suggest a date around the 11th–10th centuries BC as a terminus post quem, which would mean a timeframe of two-three centuries if we take into account the timespan attributed to this stela-making tradition (Díaz-Guardamino, 2012: 397). The most recent examination and recording of the stela of Seteﬁlla has been published by Celestino Pérez (2001: 417–418). Again, the techniques employed to examine and record the engravings are not made explicit but, taking into account the methods used at the time, these may have included photography with raking light and tracing from photographs. The main differences between his interpretation and previous ones are in the representation of the helmet, the handles of the sword and the shield, and in the feet (Fig. 3:C). With respect to the engraving techniques employed, this author mentions traces of surface preparation and the shallowness of the engravings, interpreted as the product of erosion of the rock (Celestino Pérez, 2001: 417–418). The stela of Almadén de la Plata 2 was discovered in 2005 by one of us (LGS) when inspecting the ﬁnd-spot of another stela (Almadén de la Plata 1) at Dehesa del Viar (Almadén de la Plata, Seville, Spain) (García Sanjuán et al., 2006). The study of the stela of Almadén de la Plata 2 included petrological characterisation of the rock (identiﬁed as a tuff known from the local area), description of the iconography, and some consideration on the techniques involved in its manufacture. The study of its engravings relied on a series of high-quality studio photographs taken under oblique light which were also used to produce a scale drawing of the stela (Figs. 4 and 5). The iconography comprises two anthropomorphic ﬁgures of equal size, each with their heads adorned: one apparently with a headdress or diadem and the second with horns (perhaps indicating a helmet with horns), the latter accompanied by a shield and a sword. The stelae of Almadén de la Plata 1 and 2 were found in association with a clearance cairn formed by farmers as they remove large stones from land to be cultivated. An intensive surface survey of the site revealed an unusual high density of white quartz and quartzite pebbles (Fig. 5) which is potentially meaningful since white quartz pebbles have been found associated with megalithic mounds in the area, and these are also sometimes covered by clearance cairns (García Sanjuán et al., 2006: 140–143 ). 4. The application of RTI and digital tracing We selected the stelae of Almadén de la Plata 2 and Seteﬁlla to apply RTI because they were particularly relevant to our ongoing ﬁeldwork research and both were available for study. By applying RTI we aimed to:  assess previous interpretations and evaluate the techniques previously employed in the examination and representation of these stelae;  document and investigate the techniques employed in the manufacture of these stelae (e.g. describe and compare them, assess the skill of the engraver, evaluate the existence of possible ‘technological styles’);  examine traces that could have been added later in the lives of these stelae, evaluating their possible intentionality or unintentionality;  evaluate the contribution of these results to the advancement of our understanding of this stela-making tradition in social, cultural and historical terms. Both stelae are currently on display in the Museum of Archaeology of Seville, and can be found in the Bronze Age room (Number IV) located on the ground ﬂoor of the museum. This setting was ideal for carrying out HRTI capture since we could control the ambient light. Nonetheless, due to space restrictions, and since the stelae could not be moved5, we found some limitations in sampling the whole hemisphere intended to cover each of the stelae, since the adequate distance between the light source and the stela should have been at least 2 times the diagonal measurement of the stela. We tried to compensate this by taking more shots with light coming from additional angles in the sections of the hemisphere within which we could reach the necessary distance. The resulting RTI ﬁles provide best results for the central and middle-upper part of the stela of Seteﬁlla, and for the lower, central and middle-upper part of the stela of Almadén de la Plata 2. In applying the HRTI capture method we used the following equipment:  Nikon D300 camera with sensor resolution 2848  4288 (12.3 megapixels) and Nikon AF-S 18–70 mm f/3.5–4.5G IF-ED DX lens;  Tripod;  Light source: Nikon SB900 Speedlight ﬂash connected to the camera through radio remote triggers;  A black snooker ball (a black sphere with a highly polished surface);  String to maintain a constant distance between the light source and the stela. We undertook two capture sequences with different camera positions of the stela of Almadén de la Plata 2 and one of the stela of Seteﬁlla. We then processed the image data with the RTI Builder v.2.0.2 (Cultural Heritage Imaging, 2011), employing the Polynomial Texture Map (PTM) ﬁtting algorithm (Malzbender et al., 2001: 519– 528). During the exploration of the resulting RTI ﬁles we took between 20 and 30 snapshots of each of them produced through varying rendering modes provided by the RTIViewer (notably varying values for Diffuse Gain and Specular Enhancement). These snapshots, which offered the best views of the details that were relevant to our research questions, were then imported as JPEG ﬁles into Corel Draw X7 as superimposed layers and ﬁnally the raster information available on each layer was used to generate synthesis drawings of our interpretations. This process was also aided by the simultaneous real-time visualization of the RTI ﬁles. 5. Results 5.1. The stela of Seteﬁlla Although this has not been determined by means of petrologic/ lithological analyses, the rock of the stela of Seteﬁlla has been characterized as limestone (Almagro Basch, 1974; Aubet, 1997: 169). This type of rock, in particular Tortonian limestone, is naturally found in the surroundings of the necropolis (Aubet et al., 1987). Eventually, the determination of the type of stone the stela was made of will be carried out in order to fully comprehend its process of manufacture – including its quarrying – and reassess its subsequent alteration through natural and cultural agents. 5.1.1. Surface preparation The surface of the decorated face of the stela of Seteﬁlla shows a broad variety of alterations (see below) that complicate the interpretation of some aspects and/or sections of the stela. Despite this, the visualisation of the surface with the RTI viewer, speciﬁcally through the application of the ﬁlter ‘Diffuse Gain’, reveals a levelled and smoothed surface preserved in the upper and lower thirds of the face of the stela, where the surface shows a lighter brown, almost yellowish, colouration (Fig. 6:B and C, 10:1). This was produced during the initial stage of the manufacture of the stela, after the block had been shaped, and almost certainly extended over the whole surface before later alterations (see below). This levelling may have been done through pecking, although straightforward marks of this are not detected at present, whereas the smoothing of the surface seems to have been done through abrasion (Fig. 7:B and C). 5.1.2. Motifs and their engraving RTI visualisation has allowed us to assess existing interpretations of already known motifs, detect previously unidentiﬁed features, and gather information about the techniques employed in their manufacture. a. Helmet: the lines previously interpreted as ‘helmet’ (after Bonsor & Thouvenot and Almagro) or as ‘helmet’ and part of a possible ‘spear shaft’ (Celestino) (Fig. 3: A–C), seem to be part of the representation of the same object, effectively, a conic helmet (Fig. 3:D, 6, 7, 10:2).
