Shield: As identiﬁed by other authors, the shield is represented through four concentric circles and a handle. The interpretation of the latter has differed greatly from one author to the other (Fig. 3:A–C; 10:2). Through RTI visualisation we could see that some of the elements interpreted as part of the handle were linear marks produced on the rock at a possibly later stage and which, in fact, extend beyond the area of the handle across the external circles of the shield (Fig. 9:B and C). The grooves of the concentric circles are broader (c. 30 mm) and deeper than the ones composing the human ﬁgure, and their delineation is more irregular than previously thought (Fig. 3:D). d. Other motifs: in addition to the motifs previously known, RTI has revealed two other features. One is a small, well-deﬁned circular cup mark situated below the right arm of the human ﬁgure and above the sword (Fig. 7:B and C, 10:2). The second is an irregularly shaped lozenge whose grooves have variable depth and are narrower than the ones forming the human ﬁgure (Fig. 7:B, 10:2). This shape could be interpreted as the representation of a spear point, although the lack of clear marks interpretable as a shaft invites us to be cautious about this possibility because spear points, when represented on Late Bronze Age stelae, are usually shown as complete spears. 5.1.3. The most noticeable alterations include: a. Irregular surface with patterned negative marks of removal of the rock: found in the central part of the obverse face, where the surface shows a darker grey colouring (Fig. 6:B and C, 8, 10:3). This altered surface was most probably produced at some later stage, after the motifs had been engraved, since the engraving of the right foot of the human ﬁgure was eliminated by some of the removals (Fig. 7:B). It must be noted that part of the white colouring seen in this area, as well as over the human ﬁgure, was produced during the 20th century when the outline of motifs was marked with white chalk. The darker grey colouring of the rock can also be seen in the upper part of the shield, where it shows clear marks of ﬂaking that were probably produced through these removals (Fig. 8:B). The fact that the engraved grooves representing the shield in this sector were not completely eliminated can be explained by the fact that the grooves of the shield have greater depth than those representing the human ﬁgure, so that the removals did not reach enough depth to completely eliminate them. b. Randomly distributed ﬂake marks: these are mainly found in the upper end of the obverse (Fig. 10:3). Importantly, they affect the representation of the head of the human body and the helmet. On the right side of the upper part, ﬂaking has resulted in the elimination of a portion of the representation of the helmet, as a large ﬂake mark indicates (Fig. 7:B and C). Other marks can be seen nearby the handle of the shield. 5.2. The stela of Almadén de la Plata 2 Petrographic analysis has shown that the rock used to make this stela is a tuff, a volcanic rock which is naturally found in the immediate area (García Sanjuán et al., 2006: 142). Of the rocks available in the local area, tuff is one of the softest and, therefore, easiest to work with the technology available during the Late Bronze Age, a factor which may have been involved in its selection. 5.2.1. RTI has enabled us to examine these marks in detail and assess their extension (Fig. 14:1). Additionally, there are a series of deeper marks, generally rounded, distributed across the lower half and the centre of the stela surface (Fig. 14:1). Motifs and their engraving With RTI we have been able to examine the motifs that had already been detected previously in greater detail, assessing the techniques used to make them, and reconsidering potential new features that were already known, but whose interpretation had remained unclear, as an integral part of the iconography (Fig. 14:2). a. Human ﬁgure with ‘diadem’: we have detected the same outline that was identiﬁed in the previous study. Regarding the techniques involved in its manufacture, we have been able to determine that the wider grooves composing the motifs of the stelae, which have a V section, were not only engraved with incision, as we initially suggested, but most probably begun through pecking, then abraded, and then ﬁnished off with incision (Fig. 12:B and 14:2). In the case of this human ﬁgure the wider grooves are used to represent the neck, torso, arms and legs, whereas the lines depicting the ﬁngers of the right hand6 seem to have been mainly drawn through incision. b. RTI has also revealed a shallow pecked line in its inner circle that may be interpreted as handle (Figs. 14:2 and 19). This line is partially affected by later alterations (see below). e. Spear?: RTI has also revealed two features beneath the shield. One shows a lanceolate shape and could have been produced through slight pecking of all its surface. The second is an incised line that departs from the lower end of the previous element (Figs. 14:2 and 19). These marks could be potentially interpreted as the representation of a spear but the lanceolate shape is quite faint and diffuse, so this possibility remains doubtful. f. Weights?: The three small aligned round marks that are situated between the arm and the torso of the human ﬁgure with horns might be interpreted as weights, but since they are similar to other marks that may be associated to the phase of surface preparation (Figs. 13, 14:1–2,14:4 and 17), this interpretation remains uncertain. 5.2.3. Later alterations There are a series of ﬂaking marks randomly distributed across the surface of the stela that alter existing motifs (Fig. 14:3). As previously noted, one of these marks is situated on the shield, affecting the outline of its handle (Fig. 19). The largest and most relevant mark of ﬂaking is in the centre of the stela, occupying the area where the hands of both human ﬁgures met (Fig. 17:B and C). It is plausible that this mark could have resulted from the intentional erasure of the hands at a later stage and, although it cannot be proved, this is a possibility that should be borne in mind in case comparable cases are documented in the future. 6. Conclusions The interpretation of engraved rock art poses speciﬁc challenges to researchers. RTI offers a comprehensive and affordable solution that produces a robust record of engraved rock art, the RTI Fig. 17. Detail of the stela of Almadén de la Plata 2: Flake marks located on the area where the hands of both human ﬁgures once met. A, ‘Default’ mode (no transformation), B, ‘Specular Enhancement’ and C, ‘Diffusse Gain’ rendering modes, with the same light positions. Fig. 18. Line drawing of the stela of Ategua (Córdoba, Spain). Drawn after Celestino, 2001. M. Díaz-Guardamino et al. / Digital Applications in Archaeology and Cultural Heritage 2 (2015) 41–54 52 ﬁle, while it enhances the perception of the shape of the stone surface through the tools provided by the RTIViewer, facilitating its interpretation signiﬁcantly. The level of detail offered by RTI depends to a great extent on the quality of the lighting during the capture session and on the sharpness and resolution of the photographs. If the capture fulﬁls the necessary prerequisites, and photographs are well focused and captured at high resolution, RTI can provide enough detail to answer questions that go beyond the identiﬁcation and delineation of engraved motifs. Detailed scrutiny of marks can provide key information to make inferences about the techniques that were employed to work the stone and their sequence. Additionally, RTI ﬁles can now be annotated and easily disseminated, facilitating the sharing of data and collective interpretation of the subject at hand within a community of researchers as well as its dissemination to the general public. The application of RTI to the Iberian south-western stelae of Seteﬁlla and Almadén de la Plata 2 is a good example of the potential offered by this technique since in both cases it has enabled us to reassess known motifs, detect new ones, identify a broad variety of additional marks, trace their outlines, identify and characterize techniques of manufacture and (in some instances) identify their sequence. In the case of Seteﬁlla, we were able to identify two new possible motifs (a spear point and a cup mark), draw a reliable outline of the delineation of all engraved motifs to resolve discrepancies between previous interpretations, and characterize the techniques employed in the making of the stela, which may be considered simple in technological terms. Additionally, we have detected removal marks invading a large area of the centre that affect the engravings. The orientation of these extraction marks is indicative of the direction from which they were performed: the left of the stela from the viewer’s perspective. Since the stela was lying down precisely on its right side, the extractions need to have been done before it was reused to cover the Early Iron Age burial grave on which it was found, implying that before its reuse there had been a deliberate attempt to erase part of its engravings. In the case of Almadén de la Plata 2 we were able to detect surface preparation marks, delineate known motifs, detect possible new motifs (bracelet and shield handle), and assess their manufacturing techniques, evidencing the combination of these, as well as the integration into the iconography of previous marks which were possibly left through the preparation of the surface. Finally, we were able to trace a series of ﬂake marks affecting various motifs. The former were necessarily produced at a later stage than the latter. The ﬂaking marks on the centre of the stela, which obliterated the area where the hands of both human ﬁgures met, are of particular interest since they could have been produced intentionally to erase such motifs. To conclude, we can highlight two main contributions of RTI to our own research. Firstly, RTI has enabled us to gather relevant information on later interventions that altered the original iconography of both stelae, illustrating later stages of their life-histories and, secondly, we have been able to gather relevant data on the techniques involved in their manufacture. In fact, one of the most relevant ﬁndings of this research emerges from comparing the two stelae in technological terms. While sharing a common iconographic language and formal conventions that are widespread across various regions of south-western Iberia, these stelae, found only 42 km from each other, are manufactured with entirely different sets of techniques which required differing levels of knowledge and skill. This may be suggesting that there was no shared consensus on how to manufacture a stela, that these stelae were probably manufactured by locals, probably non-specialists, who had different levels of knowledge and skill, which perhaps may be related to differences in local rock art traditions. title: Testing the value of low-cost Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry for metric and visual analysis of rock art
abstract: The discussion of digital photogrammetry and/or 3D laser scanning for recording the geometry of rock art sites is not new, but the production of high-quality 3D models requires great expenditure and specialized personnel that are not accessible to everyone or applicable to all sites. While the use of low-cost cameras and photogrammetry has been suggested as an alternative for fast visualization of rock art, do the results have enough quality for the metric and visual analysis of the art? This paper explores the potential of low-cost Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry performed by non-professional surveyors, with no camera calibration and no tripod to speed the process, as an alternative cost-eﬀective tool for quantitative and qualitative analysis of rock art. The innovative aspect of this paper is that we have veriﬁed the metric accuracy and graphic quality of low-cost 3D models and shown that they can be used as an eﬀective low-cost tool for detailed analysis and tracing of rock art.

The innovative aspect of this paper is that we have veriﬁed the metric accuracy and graphic quality of low-cost 3D models and shown that they can be used as an eﬀective low-cost tool for detailed analysis and tracing of rock art. 1. Introduction 1.1. Research objective This paper explores the potential of low-cost Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry performed by non-professional surveyors, as an aﬀordable, accessible, easy and fast to use tool for data acquisition and 3D reconstruction of the geometry of rock art sites. While the potential of inexpensive digital cameras and photogrammetry to record rock art has been previously discussed (Chandler et al., 2007; Chandler and Fryer, 2005), the innovative aspect of this paper is to verify the accuracy of the resulting products. Are low-cost solutions just an alternative for fast visualization of rock art (Domingo et al., 2013)? Or are the resulting 3D models accurate enough to be used for metric analysis of the art? This paper will test the metric accuracy of the low-cost 3D digital models, comparing physical measurements taken in situ with digital measurements. Furthermore, we will also assess the quality of the resulting lower-resolution digital model, to be used for digital rock art recording (digital tracings production) and as a tool to monitor physical degradation of rock art sites over time. 1.2. Background information of the research area The site, known as WR100 located in the Wellington Range of northwest Arnhem Land, Northern Territory, Australia was selected as the master model to test the metric accuracy and graphic quality of the 3D photogrammetric acquisition (Fig. 1). The site was found by a geologist conducting a geological survey in the 1970s. A photo was taken but the site was not recorded. It was relocated in 2009 and recorded by a team that included two of us (PT and ID) at that time. The main panel features an exceptionally large male ‘Dynamic Figure’. Dynamic Figures are believed to be late Pleistocene/early Holocene in age (May et al., 2017). Unusually, it is surrounded by many small female Dynamic ﬁgures with dilly bags, digging sticks and other items of material culture. It is a unique composition unlike any other Dynamic Figure panel found in Arnhem Land or nearby Kakadu National Park (e.g. see Chaloupka, 1984, Chippindale et al., 2000). Thus we felt it was interesting to oﬀer other researchers the chance to explore for themselves the dimensions of the motifs by oﬀering an accurate 3D model. 2. Material and methods To test the metric accuracy of the 3D model produced using inexpensive equipment we compared measurements of several motifs taken in situ (physical measurements), with the same measurements taken on the digital model (digital measurements). All rights reserved. T models is a key question. But how can reliability be measured? In rock art research quantitative analysis of sizes and proportions are usually conducted measuring the motifs in situ. But are human measurements taken in situ reliable? To answer these questions we will test the accuracy of the physical measurements by comparing measurements taken by diﬀerent people using both similar and diﬀerent equipment. The idea is to determine the degree of accuracy we consider reliable when measuring motifs, and thus to assess if the obtained digital model is reliable enough for archaeological analysis. The 3D models were generated using Agisoft PhotoScan, the software used in many archaeological projects (De Reu et al., 2013; Koutsoudis et al., 2013; Willis and Jalandoni, 2011). To test the graphic quality of the resulting 3D model as the basis to analyse details and to produce digital tracings, we will digitally record the same motif from a high-resolution photograph taken with a Canon D2X, and from an orthomosaic from the medium-quality model and the optimized model. The results will be compared in terms of the level of detail visible in each sample. The digital tracings have been produced using digital image enhancement (Dstretch) and colour selection tools available in the photo editing software Photoshop, following a methodology already discussed elsewhere (Domingo et al., 2013; Domingo Sanz, 2014). 2.1. Equipment A prosumer dSLR, Nikon D7000 (< $1000), was used with an AF-S DX Micro NIKKOR 40 mm f/2.8G (< $350) ﬁxed lens. The key features of the D7000 for this research are 16.2 MP (megapixel) resolution and clean images at high ISO ranges. Contrary to what is expected, higher MP ratings expose more focus problems and are more likely to necessitate camera calibration (Mansurov, 2014). Rockwell (2010) compared the ISO results of Nikon D7000, D300, D3 (D700), and Canon 5D Mark II and, back in 2010, found the Nikon D7000 to be the cleanest in the world at ISO 25,600. The D7000 images are very clean at ISO 100 through 800 and start to get noisy with higher ISOs (Imaging Resources, 2016). Even if the camera was more than half a decade old, it was capable of capturing high-quality images to produce SfM-generated models. Experiments conducted by Shortis et al. (2006) on the stability of lenses conﬁrmed that ﬁxed focal length lenses are more stable and geometrically accurate than zoom lenses. Additionally, zoom lenses may accidentally change focal lengths between shots, which makes it more diﬃcult to match up features in photogrammetry software. Therefore, ﬁxed lenses are preferred for photogrammetric modelling, particularly Structure-from-Motion photogrammetry which relies heavily on the geometry of the photograph to compute camera locations. Prime lenses (ﬁxed focal length) between 20 and 80 mm are preferred, but the “nifty-ﬁfty” (50 mm focal length, 75 mm ﬁlm equivalent) is recommended by the photogrammetry software we used (Agisoft, 2013). The AF-S DX Micro NIKKOR 40 mm f/2.8G was used because the micro (Nikon's equivalent of macro) capability allows images to be captured up to 0.163 m away from the object, a feature which is occasionally useful in rock art recording. As a general rule, the lens with the least distortion is best. While professional surveyors recommend camera and lens calibration before going into the ﬁeld, this was not done. Most archaeologists do not calibrate their cameras and lenses, and our intent is to test 3D models that could be made with equipment available to the majority of archaeologists without being intimidated by the requirements of specialists. Agisoft Lens can calculate the lens distortion and export the results to Agisoft Photoscan, where in addition there are camera optimization parameters that help calibrate the 3D model (Appendix A). In order to create a workﬂow that other archaeologists can easily follow, we did not use Agisoft Lens, but we did perform camera optimization for the high-quality model. Fig. 1. Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 17 (2018) 605–616 606 The model was scaled with printed out Agisoft PhotoScan coded markers, which are automatically detectable by the software, attached to aluminum rulers on the 0 cm and 30 cm tick lines. Markers were placed on or near the panel and included in the photographs. Therefore, the distance between those markers was known to be 30 cm in the 3D model (Fig. 2). While not all the markers were automatically detectable, likely due to slight movements from the wind, they were still useful for manual placement. The samples were measured by zooming into the area and creating markers at the exact points to be measured. Provided the model is scaled, the program provides the distance between the markers and the margin of error. Flash was not used in any of the photographs because it changes the exposure of every photograph, which makes it more diﬃcult for the software to process the model. Photographs were taken with at least 60% overlap from at least two perspectives, which is a basic photogrammetry requirement (Fig. 3). Images were saved in both raw (NEF) and ﬁne formats (JPG). The Agisoft PhotoScan software was conﬁgured to use maximum CPU and GPU cores for processing. 2.2. Creating medium-quality 3D model and making digital measurements In 17 min, 83 photographs were taken of the painted rock scene. The camera settings were ﬁxed at F/5.6 and ISO800 because there was medium-low natural light. Shutter speed varied between 1/100 and 1/ 40 in the extremes, but the majority of the photographs were taken at 1/60. No tripod was used to expedite recording in the ﬁeld. The ﬁne format created by the camera were 7–8 MB JPG ﬁles of the images and those were processed the same day on the ﬁeld laptop with no optimization. The ﬁeld laptop was a 2011 Toshiba Satellite P750 with Intel® Core™i7-26300QM CPU 2.00 GHz, 8 GB RAM, and NVIDIA® GeForce GT 540M. It is recommended that low or medium quality 3D models are created in the ﬁeld to identify problems in the dataset while still capable of returning to the site to retake. This is especially important in remote areas, such as the area under study, where returning to the site can be expensive both ﬁnancially and time wise. The camera-generated JPG images were loaded into Agisoft PhotoScan for photogrammetric processing and produced a scaled 3D model. According to the Agisoft Photoscan logs, the complete mediumquality 3D model was processed in 144 min (Appendix B). 2.3. Creating a high-quality 3D model for digital tracings The medium-quality model produced for quick measurements was not of suﬃcient quality to yield good digital tracings (Fig. 4). While it provides a good overview of the ﬁgures, the image does not remain completely sharp when zooming in to examine details, which is crucial when recording rock art. Therefore, another set of photographs was taken and run through a quality-focused process. In 40 min, 304 photographs of the entire panel were taken with the same settings as the previous set and also with neither ﬂash nor tripod. The images were saved in both raw and ﬁne format. The photos were converted from lossless raw format NEF to lossless TIFF with Adobe Bridge and Photoshop. The converted photographs had an average ﬁle size of 47.2 MB. The 304 photographs were weeded for quality and superﬂuousness, and only 67 were used to construct the model. The model was processed on a 2010 Alienware M17x with an Intel® Core™ i7-2720QM CPU 2.20 GHz, 16 GB RAM, and NVIDIA GeForce GTX 460M. Agisoft PhotoScan logged the total processing time for the highquality 3D model at around 4.5 h including optimizations (Appendix B). In the high-quality 3D-model of the whole site, some of the faded motifs were blurred when focused and enlarged. It may be possible to ﬁx this by masking, but in favor of the uncomplicated solution, we decided to focus on one faded motif (Fig. 4). In order to evaluate the eﬀectiveness of digital tracing on low-cost models a high-quality model of that ﬁgure was produced from 6 TIFF images (Figs. 3, 5). HighFig. 2. The idea was to test the quality of 3D models for tracing the art against previous methods based on high quality 2D photographs. 2.4. Creating an orthomosaic from a 3D model Screenshots of a 3D model as the bases for digitally tracing the motif or for 2D publications may be substandard because the quality of a screenshot depends on the resolution of the display device. Therefore, we recommend creating an orthomosaic from a 3D model for digital tracings of rock art. Orthomosaic generation is the process of Fig. 3. Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 17 (2018) 605–616 608 orthorectifying images by removing perspective distortion and relief eﬀects using a 3D model and mosaicking them into a single image. The result is a scaled, planimetrically correct image. Orthomosaic generation diﬀers from photo stitching because of the number of keypoints. Photo stitching works for perfectly ﬂat surfaces, which is rarely the case in rock art. If there are contours, misalignment is common and measurements will likely be inaccurate. Most SfM photogrammetry programs provide the option of creating an orthomosaic of the 3D model. The model should be scaled, and providing local coordinates is recommended so that the image is oriented correctly. From Agisoft PhotoScan, the orthomosaic can be exported as a TIFF, JPEG, or PNG ﬁle. TIFF is ideal for editing and preserving quality. The resulting TIFF ﬁle can be opened in Adobe Photoshop, GIMP, or any image editing software for tracing the rock art. 2.5. Taking physical measurements Physical measurements were taken on site with plastic and metal rulers, a metal tape measurer, and a laser distance measurer. All rulers and tape measurers were compared and found to be equivalent. All the lengths were estimated orthogonal measurements, since this method has prevailed in prehistoric rock art studies. The sample set consisted of six distinct features of the rock art (e.g. left corner of right ear measured to lowest point of right foot of motif) (see Fig. 1). The samples were selected for their varying directions and lengths. Physical measurements are estimated to the nearest millimeter, while the Digital measurements are in micrometers. The ﬁrst set of measurements we took with rulers served as a baseline or “true” value, called Physical1, from which all other sets of measurements were compared. For Physical2, an external researcher was instructed to measure the same samples with the same tools used for Physical1 (Fig. 6). The set of Physical3 measurements was taken by a diﬀerent external researcher using her personal tape measure with a hooked steel tab. Lastly, a laser distance measurer was used and the set is referred to as Laser. 3. Theory Photogrammetry or the use of overlapping photographs for the three dimensional reconstruction of real objects or scenarios has been around for a century. However, the recent proliferation of digital cameras and free or reasonably priced processing software is contributing to the widespread use in archaeology (e.g. De Reu et al., 2013; Koutsoudis et al., 2013), as well as rock art research (e.g. Willis and Jalandoni, 2011). The beneﬁts of 3D modelling applied to rock art recording have been long discussed (Brady et al., 2017; Díaz-Andreu et al., 2006; Domingo et al., 2013; Lerma et al., 2010; Plisson and Zotkina, 2015; Robin, 2015). Both digital photogrammetry and 3D laser scanners deliver high-quality digital duplications of sites, including accurate information on dimensions and shape. Moreover they are noncontact, and thus non-invasive, techniques providing high-resolution models of undeniable value for analysing motifs, compositions and the rock surface and to accurately reproduce the volumetric nature of rock art. The application of both techniques to rock art research result from interdisciplinary research, which is so highly valued these days as a way to obtain feedback from expertise in diﬀerent disciplines, to promote scientiﬁc creativity and innovation, and to demonstrate that projects adapt to international standards. To us, interdisciplinary collaborations and expertise should certainly be a priority, since it improves the results, widens knowledge and promotes the development of new methodological approaches to solve research questions. However, the production of high-quality 3D models requires great expenditure and specialized personnel (Domingo et al., 2013). The limited amount of funding usually available to archaeology and rock art research, the fragility of rock art (constantly exposed to all sorts of natural and human impacts threatening the long-term preservation of this heritage) as well as the remote and/or inaccessible location of some sites, forces archaeologists to look for alternatives ways to be more cost-eﬀective, in order to fully record as many sites as possible without compromising preservation and accuracy. Archaeologists studying rock art worldwide face hundreds (e.g. European Palaeolithic rock art) or even thousands of sites (e.g. Aboriginal rock art in Arnhem Land or Postpalaeolithic rock art in Mediterranean Spain), in many cases listed in the National Fig. 5. In such contexts, the prohibitive costs of professional 3D recording fully justify any attempt to ﬁnd low-cost alternatives at the expense of reducing expertise and getting less but still acceptable accuracy to record as many sites as possible. 4. Results 4.1. Assessing physical vs digital measurements The sets of Physical1 and Physical2 demonstrate that even using the exact same measuring equipment, there are minute diﬀerences in the results of the two individuals (Table 1). When comparing the observed values of Physical2 with the “true” values of Physical1, the average percent error is 1.46%. The average error of the Digital observed value compared with Physical1 is only 0.95%. Both Physical2 and Digital measurements have very low and acceptable percentage errors. Even without camera calibration, the Digital measurements are just as, if not more, accurate than two individuals taking physical measurements on site. The laser distance measurer and Physical3 inaccuracies are due to the diﬃculty in obtaining orthogonal measurements. For the laser distance measurer, a ﬂat surface was required to bounce the laser oﬀat the second point. It was almost impossible for one person to take the measurement, while holding both the laser distance measurer and the ﬂat surface at perfect 90° angles, on an uneven rock surface. Physical3 was also erroneous due to the tape measure with a hooked steel tab imposing a slope and forcing the researcher to estimate (Fig. 7). By charting the mean, standard deviation, and measurements for each sample, the inaccuracy of Physical3 and Laser are emphasized (Fig. 8). Physical3 was out of range deviation in four out six samples and Laser was out of range three out of six times. Physical1 and Digital were outside the standard deviation for Sample B. 4.2. Digital tracing The close up photo and the digital recordings produced from an orthomosaic of the medium-quality 3D model, an orthomosaic of the optimized 3D model and a high-resolution photograph capture with a Canon D2X using a tripod demonstrates that while both 3D models are of use to obtain accurate geometric data, only the orthomosaic of the optimized 3D model oﬀers enough quality to produce detailed digital tracings similar to those traditionally obtained from a high resolution 2D photograph. This is especially so if the motifs or details are small (less than 5 cm). Fig. 9 shows how the head of the motif selected for this test is blurred in the orthomosaic of the medium-quality 3D model, while it is sharper in the orthomosaic of the optimized model. We can conclude then that digital optimized models produced with inexpensive equipment, with no tripod and no camera calibration can certainly be used for both metric analysis and digitally tracing rock art, while the medium-quality 3D model is certainly useful for metric analysis and a general understanding of the art, but no so much for digitally tracing the art. 5. Discussion The purpose of this paper was to estimate the value of low-cost Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry performed by non-professional surveyors, for metric analysis and digital tracing of rock art motifs. The tests conducted with no previous camera calibration and without using a tripod to speed the process demonstrates that both human and low-cost digital methods of measuring the art have low margins of error. From our perspective this low margin of error is acceptable in rock art quantitative research, since it equals or even improves the results of direct physical methods used to date worldwide. Thus, since both methods have similar levels of accuracy, producing these sorts of low-cost 3D models will certainly contribute to reducing the time and equipment needed in the ﬁeld to measure each motif. Furthermore, it allows measuring inaccessible motifs (Fig. 10), while minimizing or even eliminating any chance of unintentionally damaging the artwork when trying to take measurements, since physical measurements will no longer be necessary. Finally, and more importantly, the use of 3D models facilitates repeatability without having to visit the site. Similarly, while medium-quality 3D models do not seem to provide the amount of detail needed to produce digital tracings of the art (especially when referring to small ﬁgures or details), the results from optimized 3D models of speciﬁc motifs do provide enough quality to be used for digital rock art recording. These ﬁndings are extremely important since the production of low-cost 3D models will allow the number of sites geometrically recorded in each ﬁeld season to multiply by speeding the process and reducing costs. Thus the 3D models produced by low-cost Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry are not only of use for public dissemination but also an eﬃcient solution for quantitative and qualitative analysis of rock art. Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports 17 (2018) 605–616 610 While the whole process of creating a 3D model might be overwhelming, there are copious amounts of information available online from forums and manuals to step-by-step videos and blogs. A background in photogrammetry, photography, and programming is an advantage, but it certainly is not a prerequisite. With the usability of Agisoft PhotoScan and overlapping photographs, it is entirely possible to create a basic 3D model on the ﬁrst try. 6. Conclusions In conclusion, this research has demonstrated that professional surveyors and expensive professional photographic equipment are not always needed to produce 3D-models accurate enough for measuring and tracing rock art. While there may be speciﬁc contexts that require surveyor expertise, as well as more expensive photographic equipment, for the majority of rock art sites SfM 3D-modelling has proven to be a cost-eﬀective alternative. title: Borg  a ,  Michelle  Dunn  a , ∗ ,  Andrew  Ang  a ,  Carl  Villis  b
abstract: The conservation of artwork is an important aspect of preserving human culture and artistic expression. Both knowledge and technology can be essential in artwork conservation. Without such knowledge, the lifespan of numerous works of art by artists spanning over centuries might have been compromised and consequently not exist today. Artwork naturally deteriorates over time or can be damaged unknowingly through artiﬁcial processes. Art conservators strive to improve the condition of artwork through non-destructive preservation and restoration procedures. These can be time consuming and require signiﬁcant patience when diagnosing artwork and applying the suitable process. For many years this work has been assisted by various technologies such as photography, x-ray imaging, and speckle pattern interferometry. More recently there has been an introduction of newer technologies allowing conservators to gather even more details about the artwork. Improvements to classic analytical tools such as spectroscopy and infrared imaging have been applied to artwork analysis. Moreover, the use of computing power for photogrammetry and artiﬁcial intelligence for pattern recognition have seen use in studying artwork digitally. This article provides a review of the state-of-the-art technologies used in art conservation with some investigation into their established counterparts. Some of the technologies covered here include x-ray ﬂuorescence spectroscopy, holographic interferometry, Raman spectroscopy, thermal-quasi reﬂectography, and terahertz imaging. These techniques allow the detailed analysis of paintings, murals, statues, and other artefacts providing details about the surface topology, stylometry, pigment and media constituents, and restoration history. The integration of all these factors can provide art conservators and historians with important information on various artwork and ultimately assist in conservation for many years to come. © 2020 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.

All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Artwork conservation with information from technological techniques has been practiced and studied extensively in recent times. Non-contact and non-destructive techniques available for diagnosing and analysing paintings is appreciated by art conservators [1]. Non-contact instruments provide an easily accessible and safe means to observe various components of a painting [2]. Understanding the structure and composition of a painting provides conservators the opportunity to apply their qualiﬁed knowledge and deduce the most appropriate method to restore and preserve the artwork [3]. Optical techniques such as spectroscopy and microscopy for diagnosing paintings provide spectral infor∗Corresponding author. E-mail addresses: braeden.borg.96@gmail.com (B. Borg), jdunn@swin.edu.au (M. Dunn), aang@swin.edu.au (A. Ang), Carl.Villis@ngv.vic.gov.au (C. Villis). mation on the artwork, revealing distinctive features not visible to the naked eye. Many technologies available enable in situ imaging of the artwork which causes no permanent, physical damage. The literature reveals that non-contact diagnostic tools have prominently been successful in characterising many different physical features of paintings. These features provide intelligence of any hidden defects or deﬁciencies in the artefact that can lead to potential damage. Interpreting this data can illustrate the history of an oeuvre (painting) and allow historians and art conservators to apply the necessary practices to maintain its condition. This literature review is a study of the various state-of-the-art technologies established that can analyse and characterise paintings of different centuries. The studies aim to ﬁnd powerful, non-destructive methods of conserving artwork. Experiments have been conducted through research over the past half-century to utilise various technologies that analyse artworks chemically, topographically, and forensically. Results from these studies can provide art historians https://doi.org/10.1016/j.culher.2020.02.010 1296-2074/© 2020 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved. 240 B. The application of these techniques is also provided. and conservators with vital information that ensures longevity to the life of the artwork. There are several different types of technologies and techniques available for the application of proﬁcient artwork conservation. The large number of techniques with their advantages and disadvantages can be challenging to realise from the literature. In order to provide clariﬁcation for artwork conservators and researchers, a comprehensive overlook of all the analytical techniques discussed throughout this review have been compiled in Table 1 for reference. Details of each analytical technique listed in Table 1 can be found in the section mentioned in the second column. In addition, a map illustrating the relationship of these techniques with each other has been provided in Fig. 1. Previous reviews in B. Borg et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 44 (2020) 239–259 241 the literature have been discussed in Section 2. Section 3 brieﬂy discusses the nature of electromagnetic radiation and its uses in artwork conservation. Section 4 explores the prevalent use of x-ray imaging to analyse underlying structures within historic paintings. Section 5 evaluates some possible uses of computer vision and machine learning algorithms in identifying artist painting styles and characterising canvas bolts. This is followed by Section 6 which examines the various uses of laser-based holography to analyse the surface topology or metrology of paintings. Section 7 investigates the application of infrared radiation in the analysis of artwork, including spectroscopy, reﬂectography and tomography. Finally, Section 8 explores the application of terahertz radiation in imaging possible defective regions within the stratigraphy of paintings. These sections aim to explore appropriate techniques employed in the laboratory for precise artwork analysis and diagnostics. 1.1. Research aim The aim of this article is to provide a comprehensive review on the current technologies used in artwork conservation. State-ofthe-art technologies that are included in this review include x-ray ﬂuorescence spectroscopy, holographic interferometry, Raman spectroscopy, thermal-quasi reﬂectography, and terahertz imaging. The application of typical techniques including photography, x-ray imaging, and speckle pattern interferometry have also been included. Paintings, murals, statues, and other artefacts can be analysed with these techniques to provide information about surface topology, stylometry, pigment and media constituents, and restoration history. As such, these technologies can provide historians and art conservators with important information and assist their conservation efforts into the future. 2. Previous reviews Previous papers in the literature have reviewed the application of technology as a means of aiding artwork conservation efforts. The number of different technologies and techniques available for artwork conservation is huge. As such, it is impossible to cover them all in one review. Other review papers have been presented to illustrate where certain techniques are discussed in detail for the application of analysing various works of art. A review on engineering techniques that can be applied to artworks for identifying deformation and strain has been completed by Dulieu-Barton et al. [4]. The techniques investigated include physical strain-gauge sensors for measuring planar deformation, and optical techniques such as photogrammetry and holography that can measure physical displacements over time [4]. Another review completed by Sotiropoulou et al. [5] looks into monitoring techniques that can provide intrinsic information into material degradation of mural paintings. As such, the paper considers state-of-the-art spectroscopy tools that can provide insight into degradation processes occurring at the chemical level. The authors investigated the aging behaviours of ancient binding media and pigments in murals using colour readings, infrared spectroscopy, and chromatography-mass spectrometry [5]. Future work from these analyses aimed to contribute to the knowledge on the reactivity of metallic pigments with ancient binding media, and the deterioration of pigments in polysaccharide and proteinaceous binders [5]. A review completed by Fischer and Kakoulli [6] investigated the application of multispectral and hyperspectral imaging techniques. The technology used in multispectral and hyperspectral imaging has been used previously in other ﬁelds. However, it was discussed that such techniques and associated technologies could be applied to artwork conservation for the analysis of paintings and written artefacts [6]. As a non-destructive technique, imaging with a speciﬁed and controlled domain of radiation can provide useful data for artwork analysis. Moreover, the data is obtained spatially allowing pigments, biodeterioration, restoration treatments, and ﬁnally binding media to be mapped on the artwork [6]. Another review which was conducted by Gavrilov et al. [7] investigated different imaging techniques used in the analysis of paintings. Much of the techniques discussed are based upon thermographic imaging for analysing the condition of paintings. The technique was demonstrated to be proﬁcient in analysing concealed defects in pictorial paintings. Moreover, thermographic techniques were argued to be non-destructive to the artwork when conducting analysis for conservation, and safer than x-ray imaging to the people involved [7]. In the article, the application of infrared imaging, x-ray imaging, and thermography techniques were discussed. In addition to a breakdown of these techniques, the types of defects that each method can identify in speciﬁed works of art were described [7]. Many of these techniques will continue to see application in artwork conservation due to their proﬁciencies. However, it was argued that some techniques are safer than others to both preserving the artwork and health of those people involved. A review by Alfeld and Broekaert [8], investigated technologies that can be used for subsurface characterisation. The techniques discussed in the paper include typical penetrative imaging techniques such as infrared reﬂectography, x-ray radiography, x-ray ﬂuorescence, and terahertz imaging [8]. Fundamentally, the application of these techniques for easel paintings was presented. It was shown through the review what type of information could be expected with each technique. Contrasts in greyscale images of the paintings were used to illustrate how the distribution of materials or stratigraphy of painting subsurface layers could be identiﬁed [8]. Alfeld and Broekaert pointed out that none of these optical techniques are obsolete [8]. However, each technique has inherent limitations to their application. That is, some techniques are technically infeasible for certain works of art. Other factors such as a shortage of expertise and money in adopting some of these techniques can also limit their suitability for practical applications [8]. From these previous reviews, it is clear that numerous techniques and technologies exist which can provide highly beneﬁcial information into various works of art. It is the aim of these studies to compile information for both the technical and practical implementation of these tools to aid artwork conservation. This review aims to not only identify techniques for speciﬁc types of artworks or applications, but to present all types with their advantages and disadvantages. Some older techniques may be better-suited to art conservators who are able to adopt the technology and get the most out of it. From spectroscopy to photogrammetry, this paper presents techniques that have been used as a means to aid artwork conservation. 3. Electromagnetic radiation Electromagnetic radiation (light) is essentially the carrier of visual information that exists in different forms (wavelengths) across a distinct spectrum. The human eye can only perceive wavelengths of light that exist in the visible range of approximately 400 nm–700 nm making up all the known colours. However, other wavelengths beyond the visibility of humans can provide interesting and useful information on different aspects of artwork. X-rays are the most energetic class of light considered in this review, existing in the spectrum range of 0.01 nm (100 m) to 10 nm. X-ray radiation is proﬁcient in penetrative imaging and spectroscopy at the atomic level. 242 B. Borg et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 44 (2020) 239–259 Ultra-violet radiation is more energetic than visible light and exists in the spectrum range at 10 nm–400 nm. Ultra-violet radiation can cause chemical reactions upon exposure leading to a glow or ﬂuorescence. Spectroscopy and structural imaging can be achieved by taking advantage of ultra-violet ﬂuorescence. Transcending from the visible spectrum is infrared radiation which occupies the range from 700 nm to 1 mm. Infrared radiation is split into three sub-spectrum domains for low, mid and far-infrared. Low-infrared naturally interacts with matter at the molecular level and is proﬁcient for spectroscopy and reﬂective or transmission imaging. Mid-infrared on the other hand is used for thermal imaging of materials. Finally, terahertz radiation populates a small domain in the spectrum at 100 m to 1 mm (overlapping infrared). Terahertz radiation, like x-rays, is capable of penetrative imaging lacking the energy (and hazard) of x-rays. Speciﬁc equipment is required to detect these different wavelengths and construct photographs or spectra (histogram of detected wavelengths of light across a desired domain). Fig. 1 illustrates the techniques covered in this review associated with each of these wavelengths of light. It is important to understand what these different wavelengths of light can tell us about different types of artwork when it comes to comprehensive practise in art conservation. As an example, Fig. 2 illustrates an interesting ﬁnding in Patch of Grass by van Gogh, painted in 1887, where the visage of a lady was detected using x-ray and infrared reﬂective imaging. As demonstrated by these ﬁndings, different wavelengths of light can penetrate paint layers and highlight structures and materials not visible to the human eye. This makes other wavelengths of light unique for different analytical applications especially for artwork conservation. 4. X-ray radiation 4.1. X-ray ﬂuorescence and X-Ray diffraction imaging X-ray radiation is a powerful tool in chemical analysis due to its unique interaction with different atomic bodies [10]. It can provide signiﬁcant imaging power due to its penetrative abilities. As a result, x-ray imaging and spectroscopic analysis has been applied to many areas of scientiﬁc analysis [11]. X-ray techniques vary in the technology that has been used for aiding artwork interpretation and analysis. X-ray imaging techniques used in medical applications such as radiography have been used in the past to image underpaintings [12]. Radiography has also been classically used in learning more about the history of a painting by revealing its underpaintings. However, advancements in the technology, despite radiography still being a useful technical in understanding more about an artwork, has improved its ability to aid art conservation [13]. By being able to identify different materials, painting technique, and preservation state, current x-ray radiography reveals important information for conservators [9,13–15]. X-ray ﬂuorescence (XRF) spectroscopy is a technique that uses x-ray radiation to excite materials at the fundamental level to determine their chemical properties [16–18]. XRF occurs when atomic bodies are excited under controlled conditions leading to the instantaneous emittance of x-ray radiation. The x-rays produced can be analysed so that the intrinsic properties of the material can be characterised and identiﬁed. As a result, this technique can be used to identify chemical compositions in a painting. X-ray spectrometers are instruments developed to measure the wavelength of x-rays produced through XRF to classify materials in a specimen. These devices generate x-rays by accelerating electrons from an electric ﬁeld emitter. These x-rays excite the material, and incur ﬂuorescence in the material allowing emitted x-rays to be analysed with a photosensitive detector [16]. X-rays reﬂected from a specimen are detected by measuring the quantity of energy released from the x-ray, subsequently converting it into an electric signal. The signal can then be interpreted by suitable apparatus for spectral analysis. A photodetector is used to measure this reaction, and interprets the information to construct a spectrum of the material [16]. X-ray ﬂuorescence and other x-ray techniques have been used together in the analysis and identiﬁcation of constituents present in some artwork [17–20]. These artwork fundamentally include paintings, but other artefacts have been analysed. X-ray techniques have been used to identify minerals present in the paint pigments of paintings as well as crystalline structures that have formed within ceramic glazes [21]. Eveno et al. [21] applied both XRF and x-ray diffraction (XRD) to examine the material composition of paintings to assist in the conservation science. XRF was used to determine pigment minerals in paint and as a result colours in the painting. Further examination was achieved with XRD ﬁlling in information missing from XRF scanning alone. Pigments that featured high concentrations of lead, such as lapis lazuli, were easily identiﬁable from the XRF-XRD technique [21]. XRD was also performed on artwork surfaces that use a ceramic glaze to map the crystalline structure of the protective coating [21,22]. Separate peaks in XRD spectra revealed different crystalline phases in the ceramic glaze. However, the efﬁcacy of the technique could be controlled by the incident angle of the x-rays and x-ray intensity (penetrability) [21]. This technique aimed to identify heavy pigments in paintings and crystals in ceramic glazed artefacts [21]. X-ray ﬂuorescence in spectroscopic analysis (Energy Dispersive Spectrometry) operates according to the phenomenon where atomic structures irradiated by incident x-rays change the energy states of electrons [21]. The unique energy level of the emitted photon is dependent upon the atomic structure of the element probed. Hence, the material can be identiﬁed. Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX) analysis can be very useful, but also has limitations. It is impossible for EDX electron scanning microscopy to successfully detect lighter elements from helium to neon. In addition, the atmosphere between the specimen and equipment sensors affects the instrument sensitivity, increasing the difﬁculty of detecting low-energy elements [21]. For assistance in detecting the lighter elements in the painting and to analyse ceramic glazing, XRD techniques were applied. XRD records unique diffraction patterns that are generated by the crystalline structures in polycrystals or powders. This is achieved by irradiating the specimen with monochromatic x-rays and recording how the beam scatters from within the surface material. X-rays that enter the material are scattered in succession by parallel atomic planes of the crystalline structure [21]. Since these layers are displaced from each other at the same distance, x-rays may interfere upon scattering as a result of their reﬂection angle. If the light waves are synchronous, then they will interfere constructively meaning their amplitudes will add together. This phenomenon is described by Bragg’s Law [21,23]. The resulting interferences can be detected as reﬂection spots on the observed optical fringe pattern. Since these reﬂection spots are characteristic of the crystalline structure, the material can be identiﬁed [21]. Eveno et al. [21] evaluated the use of XRF and XRD imaging techniques on six paintings of the San Zeno Altarpiece by Andrea Mantegna (1456–1459). XRF spectroscopy can detect many leadbased compounds in the pigments present in the paintings. The lead-based compounds include yellow massicot/litharge, lead-tin yellow, red/orange minium, and ﬁnally black/brown plattnerite and scrutinyite [21]. The identiﬁcation of these lead compounds provides an understanding on the degradation, mixture of paint types (contemporary or original), and help verify the original artist’s palette. However, not all of these compounds are detectable by XRF alone as it does not detect light elements. Further examination of B. Analytical Chemistry, 2008. 80(16): p. 6436-6442. Copyright 2008 American Chemical Society. the painting using XRD is then necessary to identify the lighter lead compounds [22,24]. XRD has also been useful in imaging the expensive lapis lazuli. The use of lapis lazuli in ancient artworks is typically restricted due to its preciousness [25]. As such, lapis lazuli is often replaced or mixed with azurite. Azurite is much less expensive than lapis lazuli but similar in colour making it an ideal substitute. Since both of these pigments are relatively light in atomic mass, they can be easily distinguished with XRD imaging [21]. From this technique, lapis lazuli and dynamic combinations of azurite have been discovered in the San Zeno Altarpiece [21]. The use of this mineral is important for conservators and historians as it provides insight into the historic practices of ancient painting using different blue pigments. Eveno et al. also discussed the application of XRD imaging, independent from the XRF, to analyse ceramic glazed paintings from Seville (Spain) [21]. The aim of using XRD was to qualitatively identify structure in the glaze appearing as polycrystalline structures or powders. Organic varnishes can also be determined. However, their transparency to x-rays must be accounted for to identify the material correctly [21]. Pagès-Camagna et al. [26] investigated the use of an XRFXRD technique to analyse ancient Egyptian wall paintings. Images collected from these techniques provided information on the elemental and structural characteristics of the Egyptian murals. A portable XRF-XRD system was designed in a laboratory to image the murals [26]. To detect XRD measurements across the surface of the artwork, an imaging plate was used to collect the diffraction beam during scanning. The amalgamated techniques were able to image the artwork simultaneously making the process more efﬁcient. Analysis was completed on the Egyptian paintings highlighting the presence of multiple pigment types and arsenic compounds. The presence of arsenic sulphide and arsenic trioxide indicated that the paint had an accelerated degradation and discolouration sequence due to exposure to light [26]. Other iron elements were found in the Egyptian paintings with XRF imaging. Spectra with iron-rich compounds were associated with yellow from goethite and jarosite compounds. Moreover, red colours were associated with spectral signatures of hematite compounds [26]. Characterising pigments with two different techniques can provide important, coherent data. The presence of particular elemental compounds and their state of degradation were demonstrated to be determined using an XRF-XRD technique [26]. The application of XRF and XRD imaging in artwork conservation has been demonstrated through the identiﬁcation of pigments and crystals in paintings [21,22,24,26]. Together, these imaging techniques can detect a wide range of mineral types that are commonly 244 B. Borg et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 44 (2020) 239–259 used in historic paintings. Ceramic glazes and organic varnishes can be detected with XRD due to their crystalline structures inducing reﬂection spots in the resultant diffraction patterns. Moreover, pigment minerals or compounds present in a painting can be determined through XRF-XRD imaging [24]. XRF is able to detect heavy materials such as lead-based compounds in the paint whilst XRD images lighter elements more effectively [21]. 4.2. Synchrotron-based X-Ray ﬂuorescence microscopy A recent outcome from innovation in x-ray imaging is micro xray ﬂuorescence spectroscopy (micro-XRFS). XRF spectroscopy has been used extensively over the past century expanding upon the understanding of particle properties. Micro-XRFS is a similar technique to XRF but uses a highly coherent excitation beam to image the specimen typically achieved with a synchrotron. This provides a spatial resolution at the micrometre level for more precise imaging [18]. The compromise is the expense and difﬁculty of setting up a system to apply powerful excitation sources inducing XRF at this spatial level. With the use of a synchrotron, micro-XRFS has been achieved demonstrating high imaging penetrability and accuracy [16]. Synchrotron radiation is generated at extremely high frequency pulses. Electrons are generated and accelerated at this frequency to interact with the specimen at the atomic level. These speeds allow the x-rays to probe the specimen with high spatial precision, revealing a signiﬁcant amount of detail within its structure [27]. The resulting ﬂuorescence of the specimen is then measured with spectrometers so that the chemical composition can be identiﬁed in a similar manner to XRF. Paintings can be probed in particular areas so that information regarding its chemical composition can be identiﬁed with great accuracy [18,28,29]. A study was conducted by Dik et al. [9] to realise the practice of painters abolishing their old work to start a new composition. Using synchrotron based XRF a few paintings by Vincent van Gogh were studied. This technique allowed the underlying pigment elements within van Gogh’s artwork to be mapped. The analysis of the work Patch of Grass is known for hiding the face of a woman from a previously abandoned painting. Previously, X-Ray Reﬂectography (XRR) and Infrared Reﬂectography (IRR) have been used to reveal the silhouette of the woman shown in Fig. 2. However, the level of detail acquired is low making a decent reconstruction of the face difﬁcult [9]. As such, the additional applications of XRF to obtain intrinsic details about the pigments used in the underlying painting were used to improve the reconstruction quality of the underlying painting. Imaging with this technique provided greater contrast in different layers of the painting allowing the concealed face to be highlighted. In a speciﬁed region of the painting, the distribution of mercury and antimony elements present in the paint were mapped with micro-XRFS. These pigments depict red and light-red tones underneath the surface layers. Through extrapolation of these colours, the facial skin tones of the lady could be determined [9]. The ﬁnal representation of the hidden lady’s facial features was highly successful. Synchrotron-generated XRF was an effective technique in analysing concealed paint layers to determine the pigments used [9]. The colours identiﬁed aided in the reconstruction of the underlying painting. A study conducted by Cotte et al. [28] used synchrotron-based X-Ray Microscopy (XRM) to identify abnormal pigments present in paintings completed by Grünewald. His famous Isenheim Altarpiece, painted between 1512 and 1516, was analysed with XRM [28,30]. Due to the colour complexity of the piece, microanalysis was conducted to analyse the pigments present with high accuracy. Synchrotron x-ray microprobe techniques were employed in the analysis to make micrometre-scale analysis of chemical compounds and present in the painting. The study was focused on the precise analysis of the painting to reveal the presence of antimony, lead and sulphur [28]. The results veriﬁed the identiﬁcation of antimony, sulphur and a heavy presence of stibnite. Stibnite is a natural mineral that is generally involved with antimony sulphide [31]. The analysis also indicated the prevalence of stibnite from the energy level of the antinomy species, and disproved any actuality of a more oxidised compound such as Naples yellow [28]. High resolution images illustrated that micrometre-sized grains of antimony, sulphur and stibnite were in lead-white matrices. Another study was completed by Cotte et al. [32] into the use of synchrotron-based micro-XRFS to analyse the blackening of paintings. Pompeian cinnabar paintings were analysed due to their deep-red colour darkening. Various paintings were selected for analysis that exhibited different levels of degradation on the surface paint layers [32]. To determine the processes behind the blackening of the paint, an elemental analysis of the regions that showed darkening were probed. From the information collected from a particular cinnabar sample as seen in Fig. 3, associations between black deposits and other compounds were correlated with various blackening processes [32]. Sulphur was found to be concentrated in black and brown regions with the depleted presence of red mercury. The distribution of chlorine and sodium were correlated to the regions of red and black colours. From this, it was determined that chlorine and sulphur were involved in the chemical degradation of multiple cinnabar painting samples [32]. Additional analysis was completed using x-ray absorption spectroscopy (XRAS) in the regions that conﬁrmed degradation using micro-XRFS. From micro-XRAS, it was determined that two independent degradation processes were prevalent in the paintings. The ﬁrst process was a chemical reaction between chlorine and cinnabar, leading to the development of grey chlorine-mercury compounds in the paint [32]. The second process was attributed to a process involved with calcite causing black coatings to form on the painting surface. With its ability to inspect elementary compounds and identify them accurately, x-ray techniques have demonstrated use in analysing 5. Visible light 5.1. Statistical machine learning Artiﬁcial intelligence (AI) and machine learning algorithms have the potential to assist experts in the ﬁeld of art conservation [33–35]. An AI system can be trained to recognise recurring patterns or features present in a collection of images. This process of machine learning enables features that are typically inconspicuous to the naked eye to be identiﬁed and even described by a computer. Image processing techniques have been shown to characterise the stylometry of a painter’s work by analysing ﬁne details in the structure of a painting [33]. This application can provide an impartial method of determining the authenticity or attribution of a painting [35]. A study into the stylometry of a classical painter was completed by Jafarpour et al. [33]. Using the dual-complex wavelet transform [36], Hidden Markov Tree (HMT) chains [37], and a number of computational classiﬁers, paintings from Vincent van Gogh were analysed. Digital images of paintings by van Gogh were examined to characterise the unique painting style of the artist. Using image processing and machine learning algorithms, artistic features evident within the images could be statistically characterised [33]. By modelling the style of the painter using wavelet transforms and HMTs, a computer can then be trained to statistically recognise a painter’s style [33]. This can help to describe the date of completion for an painting through the distinguishable stylometric features of the artist trained for the AI system [33]. B.
