Determining the visual stylometry of a work of art has been completed with several different image processing techniques. One such study [35] completed included the holistic analysis of the colours present in a digital image of a painting to typify the style regarding an artist and artistic era. Other efforts have included the analysis of pixels and their arrangement to characterise brushstrokes imprinted in a digital image of a painting [35]. These techniques all aim to analyse the information presented in a digital image of a painting and determine stylometric features that are extracted through statistical properties. Moreover, it is described that the use of statistical analysis in digital images of works of art is synonymous with human perception [35]. That is, the human eye perceives statistical structures within the real world. As a result, it is promising to train machine learning algorithms to detect important features in artwork similar to human perception. These features can improve the understanding of the work of art for experts and help characterise its artistic style [35]. 5.2. Machine learning as a supplementary tool Machine learning as a post-analysis tool has been used in conjunction with other imaging techniques [38]. Components of a painting not visible to the naked eye, such as the unique texture of the canvas embroidery, can be digitally analysed using machine learning algorithms [38]. The computer will learn to recognise these patterns across a variety of different artworks. Other techniques in characterising thread patterns and matching weaves have been studied [39]. Warp-thread patterns are most discernible in canvas sheets travelling vertically through the canvas. These patterns are a major characteristic used to match different paintings together from the bolt of canvas [39]. In addition, the subtending angle of weft-threads from the sides of the canvas assists in determining the total thread count. A map of the weft angles can provide evidence of strong and weak cusping occurring in the canvas. Together, these features of a canvas can be used to further investigate the originality of a painting at its matching bolt of canvas [38,39]. Applying a machine learning analysis to radiograms of canvas paintings was completed by van der Maaten et al. [38]. An analysis of paintings was completed from Nicolas Poussin (Triumph of Bacchus, Triumph of Pan and Triumph of Silenus) painted between 1635 and 1636, and Vincent van Gogh (Two White Butterﬂies, Bedroom in Arles, Mother Roulin with Her Baby and Shepherdess) painted between 1888 and 1889 [38]. XRR imaging was used on all paintings to visualise the canvas ﬁbre texture imprinted on the base lead-paint layers. These radiographs were then processed with a computer to characterise the weave patterns [38]. A machine learning algorithm was used to detect thread-speciﬁc signals from the radiographs so that they could be correctly identiﬁed. The thread features would then need to be isolated from the rest of the painting for analysis. To detect thread-crossings in the radiographs, an algorithm that was able to differentiate the contrasts between warp and weft patterns was created [38]. Histogram normalisation was then applied to the results to improve contrast and subsequently visibility of the thread-crossings [38]. This technique deﬁned a trained logistic regression model, which ensured that thread-crossings were detected with certainty. Corresponding maps of the interweft and inter-warp orientation distances were acquired. These distances characterised the threading density of the canvas. The warp-threads are important for conducting a match to another canvas as discussed previously [39]. By sliding one canvas signal over another, the thread-distance signals could be compared and matched. A statistical analysis of the average distance for the thread signals was used to validate a canvas match [38]. This technique was successful in showing that a match existed for all the artworks evaluated by Poussin and van Gogh. Each one, 246 B. Borg et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 44 (2020) 239–259 when reconstructed as a mosaic image of all of the canvas sheets, clearly illustrated that each painting canvas originated from the same canvas bolt [38]. The thread patterns of the canvas weaves were recognisable to a computer with the use of machine learning tools. The histories of the canvases to their respective artists were conﬁrmed with this supplementary information. 5.3. Photogrammetry Photogrammetry is the process of using non-contact images or scans of an object to obtain a three-dimensional (3-D) model from surface measurements [40–43]. The exact processes involved in photogrammetry can change depending upon the application. However, the general concept is the same. Photogrammetry can be used as an analytical tool for objects or environments allowing surface properties to be measured. With a 3-D mode, it can also be used as a way to display an object or environment digitally for educations purposes [40]. Photogrammetry has the capability of being a powerful tool, allowing for the generation of a complete, faithful copy of a real-world object. As a result, a digital model can be used for analysis by expert art historians without the requirement of removing the artwork from its exhibit, or travelling to see it on display [42]. Photogrammetry has also been used to image geographical structures and archaeological sites from the air [44]. For the purposes of creating 3-D models, photogrammetry is the precise measurement of features between different digital photographs of an environment or object [40]. Measurements made of a real-world object or scene can be used to construct a representative model in virtual space. Thus, spatial information about the scene or features that deﬁne the object is required for a faithful reconstruction [40]. There are a number of different methods available to construct a spatial coordinate system. An easy method is to use known positions of the camera for each photograph and successively triangulate the position of the object [40]. A computer is used to look at an array of images that have captured the scene and attempts to connect characteristic features on the object between images. The difference in position of these features between images is described as a transformation [40]. The more photographs that are captured about the object or scene, the greater the chances are of detecting these transformations improving accuracy. Once the characteristic points are measured, they are deﬁned spatially within the coordinate system. Numerous procedures are then completed by software to construct a surface using these points eventually resulting in a complete model [40]. Alternative photogrammetric methods use laser scanning techniques to measure surface topology in real-time [44]. Laser scanning makes frequent interferometric measurements of a surface as the object is scanned. As such, the scanner calculates the relative distance to the reﬂected surface from the optical measurements [44]. With knowledge on the position of the scanner (azimuth and inclination of the device), measurements can be made from the object in a relative XYZ coordinate system [44]. For both photogrammetry and laser scanning, a point cloud is constructed from the measured points from the object in the virtual environment. Initially, a sparse point cloud is generated in the virtual space. Then a dense point cloud can be constructed using interpolation from the sparse point cloud [44]. The accuracy and density of the point cloud is important as it links colours from the original image textures to the new 3-D model [42]. A mesh can subsequently be formed from this dense point cloud to deﬁne the surface of the 3-D object [44]. Photogrammetry employed by El-Hakim et al. [42] was successful in modelling the surface geometry of Aboriginal rock art. Realistic 3-D models were obtained with a technique combining digital photogrammetry and laser scanning interferometry. These models were accessible for virtual visualisation on a computer. As such, art conservators and historians can analyse the mural art without the need to travel to the actual site [42]. Moreover, the model obtained is a form of documentation for the artwork, and will be of great value if the real mural degrades uncontrollably over time [42]. The accuracy and resolution capabilities of photogrammetry are limited to the imaging technology used. On the other hand, the ability to model artwork, whether that may be a sculpture, painting or mural, can become a beneﬁcial tool for virtual analysis [42]. 6. Coherent radiation 6.1. Holographic interferometry Holography has been established in many scientiﬁc and engineering ﬁelds for the purposes of accurate, non-contact analysis [45–48]. Since the invention of the laser, holography has been implemented into a large ﬁeld of research aimed at improving the proﬁciency of current analytical technologies [46]. Holography is the reconstruction of electromagnetic waves of an object from its unfocused diffracted pattern [49]. The resulting reconstruction is called a hologram. The reconstruction of waves for a hologram relies on the interference of light scattering from the object of focus and a reference beam. The interference pattern is indicative of the proﬁle and shape of the object scanned [46,50]. Holographic interferometry has been used in analysing artworks to detect physical damage such as cracks and fractures [50–52]. Over the past few decades, different types of holographic interferometry techniques have been produced for many areas of industry. These include Real-Time Holographic Interferometry (RT-HI) and Double-Exposure Holographic Interferometry (DE-HI) [50,52]. Each has beneﬁts for acquisition speed, fringe pattern contrasting and longevity of the reconstructed hologram [49,53]. For DE-HI, two holograms are taken of the specimen using the same reference beam. The ﬁrst image is taken when the specimen is in an arbitrary position and the second when the specimen has slight change of position or form relative to its initial state [49]. Interferometry of these two holograms detects patterns in contour shapes. The two holograms, when reconstructed together, will illustrate new contour lines due to an interference of the original fringe pattern. Bright centres appear where the fringes constructively interfere. As a result, the surface metrology of the painting can be described by this difference in contour geometry [49]. This technique is accurate in diagnosing surface defects in paintings [51]. Microstructures, such as fractures and cracks on the surface, are easily detectable for analysis. However, due to its high cost and complexity, other techniques have been researched leading to a range of alternative methods being introduced [54]. This expanse of knowledge in the ﬁeld of spectral analysis covers a greater range of applications for artwork conservation. A study into the use of holographic interferometry for diagnosing paintings was completed by Amadesi et al. [51]. The investigation examined the structural conditions of paintings using holographic interferometry for wood-based panel paintings. These detached regions within the painting were found to be easily recognisable using DE-HI imaging. Despite the imaging technique being applied to wooden panel paintings, it was expected that results of equal use could be achieved with canvas-based paintings [51]. Both panels and canvas ﬁbres deform under varying thermal cycles. As a result, the experiment could be similarly simulated to diagnose both painting types [55]. The resulting interference patterns imaged from a wooden panel sample correlate to the deformation processes that took place [51]. The distortions in the wood represented the natural grain patterns present in the timber structure. The outline of knots and striations in the wooden grains were shown in the DE-HI images. Further B. Borg et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 44 (2020) 239–259 247 imaging was subsequently completed on a primed wooden panel. The sample was artiﬁcially prepared with plastic sheets inserted into the primed substrate layer acting as detached regions between the primer and wooden panel [51]. Thermal drifting was applied to the specimen to instigate thermal deformation. The specimen was initially heated to a temperature of 40 ◦C and allowed to cool, consequently distorting the wooden panel. Despite thermal expansion occurring, it was noted that no permanent damage was inﬂicted. DE-HI images obtained showed distinct, localised regions where the embedded plastic sheets were placed. Their shapes were clearly evident in the fringe patterns obtained [51]. Holographic interferometry was applied to an Italian panel painting from the ﬁfteenth century by Amadesi et al. [51]. Preliminary double-exposure holograms were obtained of the panel painting using ambient drift. The minute changes in the ambient temperature induced small changes in the painting micro-structure that could be identiﬁed using double-exposure holography. The resulting images showed slight evidence of detachment present within the painting [51]. Despite the induced deformation being only temporary, the wellbeing of the artwork could be endangered with frequent use of this technique. Nevertheless, the images achieved after thermal drifting clearly illustrated internal deformations due to detachments between the wooden panel and the primer layers [51]. A comparative study was completed by Ibarra-Castanedo et al. [50] in the use of holographic interferometry and thermography to analyse defects present in panel paintings. In this study, both DEHI and RT-HI were compared in conjunction with thermographic techniques to detect subsurface defects [50]. Two different thermographic techniques were looked at to determine their efﬁcacy for relatively safe panel inspection. These included pulsed (with different exposures and frequencies) and differential absolute contrast thermography [50]. The panel painting investigated for the experiment was manufactured by [56] for the purposes of the experiment. The panel painting had four fabricated defects implemented into the painting for detection [50]. Holograms were obtained from the specimen after stressing the painting thermally with an infrared lamp for approximately one minute. The panel would react to the induced temperature exacerbating the defects in the wooden substrate [50]. Contortions in the fringe pattern indicated regions where a defect was present (Fig. 4). Holographic techniques have developed considerably since their successful application in the late twentieth century [52]. The capabilities of holography have improved in artwork conservation for improvements in available technology. This has enabled additional holographic techniques to come to light as a reliable tool for conservators. As discussed previously, HI has been applied successfully as a non-destructive tool for identifying subsurface defects in panel paintings. Ambrosini and Paoletti [52] reviewed the applications and development of holographic techniques. HI is described a seeing an important development in holography. Such techniques are described with examples on their application with real panel paintings [52]. Additional techniques which had seen development with more powerful computers include electronic speckle pattern interferometry (ESPI) and speckle decorrelation [48,52]. The phenomena of speckle-based holographic methods are derived from the interaction of coherent light on optically rough surfaces [57–61]. Coherent radiation that scatters from the surface may have its phase, amplitude, and intensity randomly altered. However, the resulting alterations made to the light can be attributed to the micrometre-scale features of the surface. As such, they are intrinsic of the microstructure and topology of the surface. The pattern that is obtained will include an array of varying bright and dark spots, also known as speckles [58]. ESPI images the speckle pattern that results from a coherent light source illuminating a diffuse surface. The interference of the light reﬂecting from the surface is determined by the surface microstructure. As such, small changes in surface structure due to strains and defective regions can be identiﬁed in the resulting speckle fringe patterns [52]. Similar to typical holographic techniques, a reference wavefront is used to produce phase-related (interferometric) speckle patterns [58]. However, unlike typical HI, ESPI operates by capturing interferometric patterns using a video image [58]. ESPI has been used in identifying detachments in any relatively ﬂat work of art. Unlike the other holographic techniques, ESPI does not retain any 3-D information. It also provides lower resolution fringe patterns due to the numerous micro-defects on the surface of a painting making anomalies difﬁcult to locate [52]. A study by Tornari et al. [61] investigated the application of an ESPI system developed in-house. The digital speckle pattern interferometer consisted of a high frequency pulsed micro-laser and holography measurement system. Coherent light produced by the pulsed laser was split into an object and reference beam. The object beam was used to illuminate the surface of the artwork to produce speckle pattern scatter [60]. Simultaneously, the reference beam was collimated, expanded, and then connected to a charged-couple device (CCD) camera. This camera was also used to record the image of the specimen illuminated by the object beam. The combination of the two light beams allows a hologram to be produced. Subsequently, the phase difference in two holographs taken at each laser pulse produces an interferogram [61]. As the name suggests, the interferogram illustrates the constructive and destructive interference of the two holograms as a fringe pattern [61]. The technique was applied for its sensitivity in being able to non-destructively detect micro-deformation on the surface of an artwork [61]. Small changes in the microstructure of the specimen can be induced by static, dynamic, or thermal loads [61]. To test the in-house system, several different artefacts were imaged demonstrating how the surface topology and micro-structures can be identiﬁed. Some of the artefacts included a bronze Faistos disc, alabaster bust of Nephertite, and a restored wall fresco [61]. From the ESPI images of the artefacts, the physical topology can be corroborated in the fringe patterns. Likewise, an internal defect in the mortar of the fresco was identiﬁed from the fringe patterns [61]. Applying this diagnostic tool on paintings can provide important information on the structural condition of an artwork. It may then notify art conservators that repair or substrate replacement is necessary to preserve the work of art. Additionally, this technique can be considered for canvas paintings, expanding the range of holographic interferometry analysis applications [51]. Nevertheless, the use of thermal drifting on paintings for thermal analysis should be considered prior to testing for fragile works of art. Some defects were much more difﬁcult to detect than others and was dependent upon the severity of the defects in the wooden panel. Major defects detected in the wooden panel were shown to be located and analysed using holographic interferometric techniques [52]. The severity of these defects in the panel could be identiﬁed so that future precautions in the conservation of the painting could be acknowledged. 6.2. Conoscopic holography Holography is a technique that uses wave interference between a reference beam and light scattering off of a surface of an object to reconstruct a hologram [46,49]. The interference of the light waves is recorded through exposure on a photographic plate. Conoscopic holography using incoherent visible light (wavelengths that are not necessarily in phase with one another) has the advantage of generating light interferences that have identical geometric paths. The technique can be used for metric visualisation or geometric mapping at the micrometre scale. Technology from this technique has the advantage of imaging complex shapes and topologies that photogrammetry would struggle with. Conoscopic holography can 248 B. Conoscopic holography was used by Pezzati et al. [62] to conduct micro-proﬁlometry on a stone statue. The purpose of the study was to demonstrate the use of conoscopic holography to reveal the ﬁnest details on the surface of an object. Due to the design of the prototype equipment, the surface proﬁlometry of statues and panel paintings could be imaged. Moreover, the technique is impartial to colour changes, and reﬂective or diffuse surfaces [62]. The equipment was able to distinguish very ﬁne surface detail, such as topological features and surface texture [62]. The statue of David by Michelangelo was imaged with the conoscopic micro-proﬁlometer. The areas selected for imaging were ones that might have been damaged or compromised during previous restorations [62]. From this information, the surface roughness parameters and visual information obtained from the conoscopic micro-proﬁlometer could be used to describe surface degradation of the statue [62]. Conoscopic holography was also used by Fontana et al. [47] for an investigation into the surface proﬁles of historic paintings. Numerous 3-D imaging techniques have been used to analyse works of art, such as statues or artefacts, which exhibit complex geometries. However, for the complex micro-geometries of painting surfaces, few imaging techniques have been deemed capable of imaging with adequate resolution for analysis [47]. As a result, conoscopic holography was employed for surface characterisation of paintings. The technique was practised on various historic paintings to quantify morphological details on the artwork surfaces [47]. Surface deformation, artistic engravings, incidental inscriptions, and craquelure on the oeuvre were identiﬁed using conoscopic techniques [47]. The surface proﬁle can be mapped from each reading, providing data on the depth of the surface measurement across a spatial region. Due to the high resolution capabilities of the technique, micro-fractures can be distinctly detected on the surface [47]. In addition, the technology is not sensitive to contrast variations. That is, conoscopic imaging of vast chromatic variations in the painting does not impede the accuracy and detail of the images [47]. An analysis on multiple historic paintings was conducted by Fontana et al. [47] using a conoscopic micro-proﬁlometer. The main painting studied was the Madonna dei Fusi, painted by Leonardo da Vinci circa 1501. The painting suffered major alterations in its modern lifetime due to restoration interventions [47]. The painting was moved from a wooden panel base onto canvas, and back again to a wooden panel. The protective varnish on the painting surface was also replaced with a modern (synthetic) varnish. Furthermore, incorrect overpainting of the paint was visible on the painting as a result of past restorations. Holographic imaging of the painting was scanned [47]. 3-D images of the painting obtained illustrated textures in the surface of the painting pertaining to the imprint of the canvas substrate. Inhomogeneities were also prominent in the surface topography where thick spots were highlighted. These were the result of poorly applied overpaint [47]. A photographic image of the artwork was superimposed onto the topological data scanned. Major regions of restoration between the two images that correspond to one another were revealed. The visual data highlighted damaged regions, providing a comprehensive illustration of the damaged surface. The texture of the canvas imprinted into the surface paint layer showed evidence of micrometre size cracks [47]. In conclusion, this technique was successful in imaging the surface topography of a painting, distinguishing surface decrepitude due to restoration interventions [47]. 6.3. Fibre-optic interferometric proﬁlometry Interferometry has been a principal technique in interpreting the information obtained through various holography methods [47,50,51,63,64]. Imaging the shape and surface texture of materials using proﬁlometry can be enhanced with ﬁbre-optics for better transportability [63]. The technology used for generating 3-D models to conduct surface or topological analysis has improved over time. Fringe projection techniques, similar to what is used in hologB. Borg et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 44 (2020) 239–259 249 raphy, have been used extensively in many different ﬁelds [63]. A comprehensive review of fringe techniques and applications, including modern technology with ﬁbre optics, can be gathered in the paper by Gorthi and Rastogi [63]. For artwork conservation, using interferometry to make precise measurements of surface depths on paintings has been successful [47]. Recent technology has since improved upon the technique using optical ﬁbres to generate highly controlled electromagnetic waves [65–67]. This makes the equipment advantageous in its range of imaging applications that differ greatly by size and physical complexity. Moreover, optical ﬁbres have the advantage of being physically compact and portable [66]. This makes proﬁlometry possible on larger and more complex objects with greater spatial resolution. However, this technique only performs well on relatively ﬂat surfaces. As a result, equipment with optical ﬁbres can be attractive for conservation analysis on paintings, statues and other artefacts [64,65]. A special interferometry technique using ﬁbre-optic capabilities was realised for the use of artwork inspection by Spagnolo et al. [67]. Peaks and troughs on the object surface shape the pattern of the fringe projection, acting like a contour map [64,67]. The reﬂected projection can then be captured using a CCD camera, so that a computer can be used to interpret the spectral data. However, minor adjustments are required for both imaging modes as perturbations in the reconstruction can occur. These can include the introduction of speckle noise or issues with the contrast in contours [67]. Nonetheless, this technique is advantageous in its operation simplicity, non-contact approach and minor requirement for mechanical support [67]. Another technique was proposed by Spagnolo and Ambrosini afterwards utilising a diffractive optical element (DOE) spectrometer for 3-D surface proﬁling [68]. The physical set-up for the system was described as being inexpensive, simple, and small which is suitable for analysing artefacts with complex shapes [68]. However, the mathematical derivation of the surface height from a fringe projection using the DOE spectrometer and spherical wave illumination requires phase unwrapping. The system was used to produce 3-D models of a terracotta lamp and marble statue of Saint Matthew by Michelangelo, created between 1504 and 1506 [68]. The resulting models enabled the surface topology to be digitally stored for analysis. Fibre-optic interferometric proﬁlometry was again applied by Spagnolo et al. [65] in analysing the deterioration effects on cultural heritage artefacts. The method involved using interferometry with optical ﬁbres to image the topography of visually damaged regions on ancient stone artefacts. Two historic stone artefacts were analysed by using this proﬁlometry technique. Erosion was visually depicted upon an ancient Roman-age head and an ancient marble Etruscan votive lamp. Fibre-optic interferometric proﬁlometry was applied to the ancient stone head, revealing missing stone material in one of the cheeks [65]. The impinging fringe pattern on the stone clearly highlighted the nature of the topography. A contour image of the affected region highlighted past deterioration of the stone where the usual rounded geography curved in centrally. This contour map thus allowed for the outlier shapes in the surface proﬁle to be removed from the image [65]. A digital reconstruction of this damaged region through alteration of the contour map was created. The 3-D model exhibited the surface proﬁle of the statue head with the deteriorated stone regions replenished. This technique could be applied to an array of other types of artwork where eroded or corroded regions can successfully be mapped or even reconstructed digitally [65]. Interpretation of the spectral information provided a numerical mapping of the surface topology. Given that high enough resolution is achievable from the contour pattern, the sufﬁcient volume of material absent could be determined [65]. There are, on the other hand, a few restrictions to this technique. Most notably, the artefact being analysed must be relatively ﬂat such that the infringement pattern is consistent with the mapped surface. Otherwise too much information will be either lost or distorted for a proper reconstruction. As such, only small regions of the artwork can be imaged at one time [65]. Finally, only regions that indicate damage, or are known to be damaged, can be proactively analysed with this technique [65]. Another study was completed by Inci and Bulut [69] in using a ﬁbre-optic augmented proﬁlometer. A four-core optical ﬁbre was used to image the surface proﬁle of rigid objects. Several different objects were scanned with the four-core optical ﬁbre proﬁlometer. However, the most important object scanned was a sculptured head artefact [69]. A small area on the side of the head was scanned with the optical proﬁlometer. The surface topology of the sculpture could be interpreted in a 3-D model reconstructed in software. The reconstructed surface of the region scanned can be seen in Fig. 5. The surface roughness could be determined from the maximal depths recorded on the surface at the micrometre scale. However, no analysis of the surface topology or roughness was completed. It was stated that the resultant resolution of the surface detail could be improved with a more sophisticated phase unwrapping algorithm [69]. That is, the methods to extract the object proﬁle form the deformed fringe pattern. This allows the proﬁle to be analysed by conservators to identify potentially damaged regions. 7. Infrared radiation 7.1. Raman spectroscopy Quantum theories have successfully been implemented and used in modern imaging technology [70–72]. These technologies have been effective in assisting analytical studies over a wide range of applications [73,74]. Raman spectroscopy is a relatively recent achievement for the application of high accuracy, non-contact chemical analysis. Unlike x-ray spectroscopy techniques, Raman relies on very small, exposed samples for analysis. Portable technology is available, but like XRF, it is limited to analysing micro-regions on the surface of artwork making it difﬁcult to do holistic characterisation. Raman spectroscopy measures the low vibrational and rotational frequencies of molecular systems [70]. Due to its ﬁdelity, Raman spectroscopy is predominately applied in chemical and biological studies looking at molecular systems. Many different chemical compounds associated with pigments and other constituents can be identiﬁed with Raman excitation. As such, Raman spectroscopy has been used successfully in analysing painting constituents for conservation purposes [74]. Raman spectroscopy is proﬁcient in its ability to accurately identify chemical structures within various artworks [75–77]. Due to its accuracy in depicting the colour of pigments in paintings, Raman spectroscopy has been used on previously analysed historic paintings for a reassessment of the pigments present [74]. As the ﬁdelity of instruments increases and the cost declines, Raman spectroscopy is likely to see an increase of implementation in galleries and museums for artwork analysis. However, this technique is limited to its applicability in surface analysis for materials that can be excited with the Raman Effect. Raman spectroscopy has been used predominately to investigate the molecular structure within heritage paintings. In particular, Raman spectroscopy has been used on heritage paintings to examine surface pigments and microbial degradation at the micrometre level [78,79]. An examination of discriminating different textile ﬁbres using micro-Raman Spectroscopy (micro-RS) in historical artworks was completed by Kavkler et al. [80]. The study involved analysing different cellulose ﬁbres of painting canvases and linings [80]. Both modern and ancient samples of ﬂax, hemp, cotton and jute ﬁbres from Slovenia were used in the study. Vol. SPIE5855: p. 643-646. Transform Raman Spectroscopy (FTRS) was employed to distinguish different ﬁbre types from their respective Raman spectra. The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) [81] allows incoming spectral data to be rapidly interpreted by a computer. Data obtained from FTRS is useful for extensive analysis of the spectral ﬁgures. It also allows for comprehensive graphical representation of the spectral data. Spectral data received from early images of the paintings provided some unexpected conclusions. Degradation discovered on various ﬁbres caused superﬂuous luminescence in the Raman spectra, making it very difﬁcult to analyse the material correctly [80]. Prolonged exposure times and ‘signal quenching’ with visible excitation lasers were used to reduce any further discrepancies. With a higher exposure time, the spectral data obtained managed to correctly yield the chemical characteristics of the material probed [80]. Diagnosing the condition of a painting can be achieved by analysing the Raman spectra obtained. Information from these spectra may provide additional information on the material analysed. That is, the condition of the material can be determined through Raman analysis of a small region [80]. The presence of particular bands, ﬂuctuations in data and broadening of points are key features of damaged material [80]. Kavkler et al. crossreferenced the micro-RS results with literature data. Reference material such as the ELCHER database for chemical spectra can be used for heritage specimens [82]. The results, in many cases, exempliﬁed human treatment of the paintings using the spectra of these particular ﬁbres [80]. As a result, past amendments to the painting could be identiﬁed from precise regions on the surface of the artwork. The regions known to be handled through this technique might show evidence of dereliction [80]. Micro-RS was successful in determining the type and condition of various cellulose ﬁbres. The data acquired allowed comparisons of literature to categorise the material [80]. Raman spectroscopy is also a prominent tool in the analysis of pigments for artwork conservation [75,76,83]. Raman spectroscopy has been used to analyse the pigments present in ancient manuscripts from Iceland, China, and Persia. Chemical characteristics of the pigments can be used to help differentiate manuscripts between artists. In addition, it can describe the types of pigments used on parchments for a period of time or across a geographic region [83,84]. Raman spectroscopy has been used to identify the degradation of pigments due to exposure to light. One common outcome of this process leads to the formation of pararealgar (yellow photodegradation compound) in the chemical structure of the artwork surface. Raman spectroscopy has been used to depict pararealgar in ancient Egyptian artefacts, panel paintings, mediaeval manuscripts and Nepalese murals [83]. With its proﬁciency in characterising chemical structures, Raman spectroscopy is beneﬁcial for pigment and binding media analysis. The accuracy and reliability of Raman make it a powerful tool to ensure that paintings are conserved with appropriate colours and paint or media types. 7.2. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy Infrared spectroscopy (IRS) [85] is a well-established analytical technique in the ﬁelds of chemistry and biochemistry [86–88]. IRS is the measurement of vibrational energies of a molecular system when excited by infrared radiation. Despite the similarities between Raman spectroscopy and IRS, the two techniques generally offer different information that is thus complementary to each other. The two techniques observe different changes to a molecular system when excited by a particular wavelength of light. This makes some information unique to either Raman or IR spectroscopy. In most cases, this means that both techniques are required to obtain the full picture when analysing a painting. Both methods have been used together in the past to obtain a wider domain of information on a specimen [87]. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IRS) has been widely used to measure all classes of biochemical compounds [89–91]. FT-IRS is a method for determining the nature of chemical compounds from various types of artwork [91]. FT-IRS is used for its proﬁciency in high ﬁdelity micro-analysis. Different sampling methods have been established for FT-IRS (transmission, attenuated total reﬂectance and specular reﬂectance) making it a reliable tool for different applications [92]. Appropriate artwork for this technical method includes ancient wall paintings and canvases. These samples can be analysed using FT-IRS to characterise the molecular structure in a select region. Moreover, this allows the constituents to be categorised as organic or inorganic [90]. Spectral databases for comparing experimental results with peer-reviewed FTIR spectra are important for identifying the material analysed B. Borg et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 44 (2020) 239–259 251 [93]. Typically, qualitative comparisons are made with spectral curves in literature libraries. A study was completed by Carbó et al. [91] outlining the procedures to carefully collect micro-samples for FT-IRS analysis. The samples collected for analysis were extracted from sixteenth and seventeenth century works of mural and canvas art. The results were successful in scanning multiple paint micro-samples from frescoes by A. Palomino from Santos Juanes Church, Spain [91]. An FT-IRS analysis of these samples permitted the characterisation of organic pigment compounds present in the paint. In the deeper layer of the paint samples, absorption bands of natural minerals such as calcite and quartz were found. Further work with FT-IRS was successfully practised on canvases from La Capilla del Ecce-Homo [91]. There were a couple of difﬁculties surrounding the proﬁciency of the project [91]. Firstly, an analytical chemist is required to select the correct solvents and cleaning agents for collecting samples, as discussed by Degrigny et al. [94]. Secondly, as advised by Masschelein-Kleiner et al. [95], pure samples must be obtained to ensure the spectral analysis provides information of the correct chemical composition. These processes and factors are important to consider when conducting spectroscopic analysis on artwork. FT-IRS is an effective method in analysing micro-samples from paintings to determine the physical and chemical nature of the constituents present. However, this technique requires the removal of material from the artwork for analysis [91]. Non-destructive techniques are always desired in this ﬁeld of practise. However, the removal of material is sometimes necessary for the conservation of paintings. As such, FT-IRS and even Raman spectroscopy can be applied during the conservation process as a means of scientiﬁc documentation. 7.3. Infrared reﬂectography Infrared radiation is widely used in optical procedures to identify samples in virtually any state. IRS measures the energy difference of a molecular structure when pummelled with infrared photons [90–92,96]. An infrared spectrum can be obtained by measuring the amount of energy absorbed by the specimen after the impinging infrared radiation interacts with it. Other techniques exist which also use infrared radiation to conduct imaging and spectroscopy such as infrared luminescence. Infrared-based luminescence imaging has been used in the identiﬁcation of pigments in manuscripts and Egyptian artefacts [97,98]. This demonstrates that infrared radiation is important to several spectroscopic tools which analyse and characterise molecular structures [92]. Nevertheless, infrared radiation is also proﬁcient in imaging the internal structure of painting specimens using the technique of reﬂectography [9]. Reﬂectography can be achieved with different wavelengths of infrared radiation (near to mid-infrared) [99–101]. Infrared generally has optimum transmissibility through paint layers. Therefore, it is a viable means to penetrate and image the internal layers of a painting with most paint types. This technique is similar to using x-ray as a technique to image underpaintings [102,103]. Infrared radiation naturally is limited in its penetrability of certain elements in pigments such as iron-gall inks and sepia [102]. Nevertheless, various details visible on the surface of thirteenth century paintings, as an example, can be highlighted at increasing wavelengths of infrared [99]. As such, varying the wavelength of the infrared light for reﬂective imaging can be used to ﬁnd different pigments [99,104]. Multispectral imaging has the added beneﬁt of capturing inconspicuous defects or overpaint for a wide range of infrared wavelengths [99]. Some pigments become more transparent to infrared at increasing wavelengths outside of the visible range. This can be useful in identify the types of pigments present in the painting surface or underpainting. Literature exists which can be used to help identify different pigments with multispectral techniques, ranging below nominal infrared to include visible and ultraviolet radiation [105]. Some of these spectral regions are not limited to reﬂectography for pigment identiﬁcation. Some blue pigments, such as cobalt blue and Prussian blue, absorb infrared whereas some others, such as phthalo blue, reﬂects infrared [105]. Additionally, binding media and other constituents present in the painting may impact the transmissivity of the infrared light [100]. This phenomenon is similar to the limitations of x-rays when imaging lead-based constituents. As such, infrared reﬂectography (IRR) can be used to look for underdrawings or composition planning, identify pigment or media types, and highlight other anomalies [99]. A study was completed by de Boer [106] in using vidicon technology to image IRR real-time on medieval paintings. With a mathematical analysis of the transmissivity of paint layer thicknesses (regarding the optimum wavelength for probing), de Boer used IRR to image underdrawings in historic artwork [106]. Despite the technology used by de Boer being considered obsolete, the theoretical approach and overall success of applying IRR is important to recognise. IRR fundamentally has not changed since it was ﬁrst realised by de Boer [107]. Technological advancements in digital systems and high ﬁdelity imaging devices (CCD cameras) can replace vintage methods of real-time imaging [107]. IRR has been proven successful in imaging underdrawings and abandoned compositions of paintings [100,107]. However, the study of certain paintings can be hampered by the presence of certain paint compositions. Pigments involving malachite and azurite, for example, cannot generally be penetrated by infrared radiation. This is due to the physical composition of the paint compound, causing most photons to scatter or be absorbed [106]. As a result, collecting image detail through paint layers can be challenging. To conduct precise imaging using infrared radiation, an understanding of the paint layer thicknesses and composition is important for penetration [106]. Furthermore, the binding media and protective varnish layers of historical paintings impede light transmission [106]. There are many variables involved when working with paintings due to their structural complexity. However, the effect of small details such as varnishes and binding media need to be tested to determine their overall effect on examining the desired paint layers. IRR can provide non-contact thermal imaging of a painting allowing the detection of damaged regions [106]. These regions will be highlighted by their thermal characteristics allowing a temperature analysis of the painting. The ﬁnal reﬂectograms obtained from the vidicon equipment on The Rest on the Flight into Egypt by Gerald David, painted in 1510, have been shown in Fig. 6. Underdrawing details not usually noticeable on the paint layers were exposed from this technique [106]. The infrared wavelength calculated for the study completed by de Boer worked well in penetrating the primary strata [106]. However, the calculations were not entirely accurate as the formula utilised does not consider mineral concentration in the paint layers, or the homogeneity of the pigments. Cross-sectional analysis of the painting initially could assist in the determination of the optimum wavelength used for infrared reﬂectance [106]. The resulting images demonstrated the use of IR in analysing the underlying strata and damaged regions in medieval paintings. 7.4. Optical coherence tomography Electromagnetic radiation that exhibits high penetrative capabilities is desired for numerous imaging techniques [108–110]. Classically, the ability of light to penetrate is used in many medical practices for in vivo imaging. Internal images are important to diagnose illnesses or disease within a person [111]. Reprinted by permission of the publisher (Taylor & Francis Ltd, http://www.tandfonline.com). ilar fashion, this technique can be applied to paintings to obtain internal images of a work of art. As discussed previously, infrared radiation exhibits the potential to image thermal features of paint layers underneath the surface [100,102,106]. However, it can be used to image the cross-section of composite paint layers in a painting [112]. Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) is a technique used to image the internal structure of a specimen by using crosssectional mapping [110,113]. By applying low-energy visible light or infrared radiation, qualitative images of the internal structure of an object can be derived. The technique is limited in application due to the natural scattering and absorption of radiation in various media present in the specimen. As a result, alternative approaches or solutions have been established which reconstruct missing information from OCT scans [110]. Infrared radiation is typically used in artwork conservation due to its ability to penetrate the paint layers of an oeuvre [112,114]. Partial amounts of reﬂected radiation are received at the interface of each consecutive paint layer. The depth of these individual layers can be interpreted by the reﬂected light received at the source. From this information, cross-sectional images of paint strata can be constructed [112]. These images illustrate the stratigraphy of the painting, highlighting the existence of paint or preparatory layers not associated with the surface painting [114]. OCT can be achieved with measurements in the time and spectral domain [115]. Time-Domain Optical Coherence Tomography (TD-OCT) reads interferometric measurements as a function of distance and time. These readings can be obtained from the phase calculations or interference pattern from a typical Michelson Interferometer [115]. On the other hand, Fourier-Domain Optical Coherence Tomography (FD-OCT) operates by measuring the spectral response of the reﬂected radiation intensity as a function of frequency rather than time. This enables the device to obtain coherent depth data synchronously during operation [115]. Data acquisition is only limited to the speed of the CCD camera used to record the backscattered light. Furthermore, FD-OCT operates with greater sensitivity, providing high resolution images of the painting analysed [115]. FD-OCT can provide cross-sectional images within a precise region of a painting. Gathering accurate information about the thickness of media layers at the micrometre scale can indicate layers associated with paint or varnish. High resolution images using FD-OCT can show the difference in media layer interfaces to help distinguish media types [112]. One major problem that arises when using FD-OCT is the potential variance in penetrability of the constituents present in the painting. Different pigment types and elemental compositions can completely negate imaging past the principal paint layers. That is, only the upper layers are imaged successfully. Nevertheless, since glazes and varnishes have high transmissivity to infrared radiation, they can easily be detected using OCT imaging techniques [112]. A historic painting was analysed by Targowski et al. [112] using FD-OCT. The painting is known as Leonard of Port Maurizio, painted ostensibly by St. Leonard in the early eighteenth century. The painting by St. Leonard was analysed using x-ray imaging, exposing a hidden inscription in the painting strata [112]. The inscription was incomprehensible using x-ray imaging alone. It was determined that the paint layer used for the inscription was thick enough to be detected using infrared radiation [112]. FD-OCT scanning of B. Borg et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 44 (2020) 239–259 253 this region yielded cross-sectional images of the painting stratigraphy. Layers of varnish and various paint layers were perceptible. A particular area showed that a typically opaque paint layer was completely covered by another layer of paint. Further analysis showed at least three layers of varnish was applied between the two paint layers. This characterised a new painting being applied to the surface of the original painting [112]. For historic paintings, this was indicative of renovation techniques that were commonly practised by artists. On the other hand, the new paint layers could be evidence that the name and date of the original painter were intended to be removed by a forger [112]. A diagnosis of the painting using FD-OCT highlighted the original date inscribed within the deep layers of the painting structure [112]. The inscription was found to be concealed underneath several paint and varnish layers deep within the painting. Targowski et al. [112] contended that the original date was obscured during the last renovation of the painting. Whether the removal of the inscription was done on purpose making the original artist unidentiﬁable was uncertain [112]. Another study into the use of OCT for imaging the stratigraphy of paintings was completed by Liang et al. [113]. For this study, two en-face scanning TD-OCT systems were used to image the stratigraphy of paintings in high resolution and dynamic range. Both two-dimensional (B-scans) and 3-D (C-scans) were achieved with museum paintings [113]. The system produced for the study operates somewhat differently to a conventional TD-OCT system. The en-face method allows the painting to be imaged rapidly perpendicular to the surface plane. This provides valuable feedback to the user of the instruments as the painting can be navigated with ease [113]. Simultaneous measurements are collected using the typical time-domain system to image the painting strata. Depth measurements of the painting stratigraphy were completed at a constant value across the entire area of the artwork. The cross-sectional scans using this technique have been provided in Fig. 7. As a result, the two sets of data can be combined after multiple depth scans to provide detailed C-scan images [113]. FD-OCT and TD-OCT imaging are techniques that can uncover the underlying layers within the surface layers of a painting. A stratigraphic representation of the painting can provide valid information not achievable with other, in situ scanning methods such as x-ray imaging [112]. An analysis of cross-sectional images can describe details on renovations and alterations made to the painting in the past [113]. The stratigraphy of a painting can elucidate on the history of the oeuvre and artist [112]. Understanding the history of a work of art is important as experts can learn more about the historic practices undertaken by artists. It might also highlight the possibility that a forgery has taken place on a historic painting, changing the understanding experts have on the work of art. 7.5. Thermal quasi-reﬂectography Thermal Quasi-Reﬂectography (TQR) is a novel technique that allows for the non-destructive analysis of painting constituents [1,2,116–118]. The technique conducts spectral analysis in the thermal infrared radiation domain. In essence, the tool applies thermal reﬂection imaging to detect structure and possible pentimento (changes made to the painting by the original artist) in the painting [1]. TQR can be used to compliment the practice of using infrared radiation to analyse a painting. That is, spectroscopic analysis can provide intrinsic information on the material composition in a specimen at the molecular scale. What is more, TQR can be used to highlight structural features in the artwork hidden within the paint layers [1,117]. Typically, thermology is used to measure the surface temperature of a specimen by looking at infrared radiation emitted from the object. On the other hand, TQR observes infrared radiation that is reﬂected off of the surface of a painting to obtain a thermal mapping [2]. This technique is like IRR but uses a different range of infrared radiation which thermally excites particular materials within a painting. Reﬂected infrared radiation will change its thermal characteristics based upon the type of material it has interacted with. Therefore, the thermal properties of the composition provide insight into the physical structure of the object examined and what types of materials are present [1,116]. As such, structural defects within the paint strata can be highlighted [1]. A study into TQR and what it offers to art conservation applications was completed by Daffara et al. [1]. Murals of a fresco painted by the Zavattari family in the Chapel of Theodelinda were investigated. TQR imaging of a small section of wall provided signiﬁcant details regarding the microstructure of the painting. The artistic monument was painted using an array of different organic paints with the extensive use of gold, silver and other paste work adornments [1]. Metallic materials were used to accentuate certain features within the painting images. TQR images taken of the mural shown in Fig. 8 clearly contrasted different metallic lavishes on the artwork. The depiction of metallic adornments emphasised their use in this mural and accompanying fresco technique. TQR imaging successfully identiﬁed changes made to the fresco where real gold and silver decorations had been overpainted [1]. The thermal properties of the metallic adornments contrasted well to the paint constituents present. The application of TQR for this mural was most effective in rendering armour detail, rather than imaging with colour and near-infrared photography [1]. An additional study was completed by Daffara et al. [1] on another artwork called The Resurrection by Piero della Francesca, painted in 1465. TQR imaging of the painting using MWIR differentiated various materials and regions from the painting surface. The tool was able to highlight very small areas where replacement plaster was applied during earlier periods of restoration [1]. In addition, it showed clear contrasts in the painting colour where different paint compounds (of the same colour) have been integrated. Further details are visible in the scans showing inhomogeneities in a shield and sword. Knowledge from a restorer of the artwork explained that the region analysed with TQR was painted using two different fresco techniques [1]. TQR was shown to be a proﬁcient optical and non-destructive tool, which can analyse various thermal features of mural paintings. It was able to differentiate selected pigment types, previously restored regions, and metallic adornments present on surface of an Italian mural [1]. The application of TQR has continued to be improved upon and juxtaposed with typical IRR. In a paper by Daffara et al. [118], the use and efﬁcacy of TQR was demonstrated on various works of art. It has been shown in previous studies that TQR is proﬁcient in highlighting potentially damaged regions in mural artworks. Defects such as metallic adornments and imperfect overpaints using different pigments could easily be identiﬁed. However, a new and developed approach improved TQR by implementing a dual-mode imaging system [118]. Two radiometric images are required which present TQR reﬂectograms and thermal emission images. By combining these two sets of data, a more comprehensive representation of the surface and subsurface details of a mural was achieved. This is a result of combining two sets of complementary data together to form a new image [118]. The dual-mode TQR imaging system in the MWIR (3–5 m) was applied to a Venetia fresco painted in the sixteenth century. A comparison of IRR (from 1.2 to 2.5 m) and TQR was presented for this mural. It demonstrated the relative proﬁciency of TQR to identify and map pigments in the painting not visible from normal IRR [118]. TQR was also shown to be capable of detecting surface degradation on a fresco by the abundance of salt crystallisation. It was reported that sulphate and nitrate salts were not detected with standard UV imaging [118]. Adapting new technologies and techniques can bring to light effective tools for conservators. Terahertz radiation 8.1. Terahertz imaging Alternative techniques are utilised in scientiﬁc research to improve upon the proﬁciency of current methods. In a number of studies outlined previously, topological analysis using interferometry or reﬂectography can yield information valuable for analysing the surface of artwork [1,50,106]. Modern imaging methods that improve upon these techniques have arisen in other ﬁelds [119,120]. Terahertz imaging is a modern, analytical technique that has been applied to the conservation of artwork [120–123]. Terahertz radiation is attractive for artwork analysis, as it can penetrate opaque materials and reach a valuable depth in contrast to other imaging wavelengths. This makes terahertz radiation ideal for internal and 3-D imaging [124]. Moreover, terahertz radiation is non-destructive to the material it is irradiating. As such, it is safe for imaging and for conducting spectroscopy of artwork in comparison to x-rays which pose more danger [123,124]. Terahertz imaging can be used to obtain structural details within a painting [125]. Since paintings require a consistent evaluation of their structural integrity, a simple and economic set-up would be appreciated [120]. Terahertz imaging can provide a robust tool in diagnosing the structural conditions of various painting types [120,122]. Terahertz radiation, particularly deﬁned within the range of 0.1–10 THz (transition domain from infrared radiation to microwaves), has effective inspective properties for the application of paintings [124]. As a result, terahertz radiation will allow for decent penetration of the painting without oversaturating the acquired image. Moreover, a satisfying contrast across the image can be achieved [120]. The surface topography and subordinate strata are highlighted in the images. Important features can be detected in these images revealing structural defects [120]. A study was completed by Groves et al. [120] on using terahertz imaging to analyse damaged wooden panel paintings. Terahertz imaging was used as a secondary tool to obtain distinct features within the structure of the painting for diagnosis of the painting [120]. Features within the painting were highlighted individually by analysing the amplitude of the terahertz pulses, and then the time-delay of the same pulses across the surface area of the painting. Damaged regions may correlate to these distinct features illustrated in the terahertz images [120]. For the investigation, an ad hoc wooden panel painting was created. The artiﬁcial specimen was designed precisely so that it matched the typical stratigraphic structure of paintings from that era. By replicating the traditional wooden panel style, an effective analytical technique could be tested. Structural defects that were detected on the sample paintings may reﬂect defects existent on authentic works of art [120]. Research into the use of terahertz radiation for imaging the stratigraphy of wooden panel paintings was completed by Picollo et al. [126]. Due to the safe application of terahertz imaging for artwork, a portable terahertz time-domain spectroscopy (THz-TDS) system was developed to image wooden panel paintings. THz-TDS is an analytical technique that can provide a wide range of information regarding the structure and material composition of a painting [126]. This technique was used to determine the condition of decorative gilding, surface degradation, various internal material layers, and ﬁnally some constituents present in wooden panel paintings. The ﬁrst wooden panel painting analysed was the Badia polyptych by Giotto, painted between 1301 and 1302. Due to the size of the polyptych, numerous sections were selected for analysis with the THz-TDS system. Terahertz images were obtained from the artwork providing information on the reﬂectivity of the surface [126]. Terahertz signals that reﬂect from the surface are reconstructed and interpreted as greyscale regarding their intensity. As such, high intensity signals were correlated with golden decorations on the polyptych. Other materials or features of degradation could then be determined from the contrasts in the greyscale [126]. A greyscale terahertz image of the Virgin from the polyptych indicated regions of surface deterioration as shown in Fig. 9. Analysis of the regions surrounding the infant’s head shows the presence of a gold leaf in the painting covered by some paint layers. The presence of metallic materials was indicated by to the high intensity reﬂected terahertz signals from the image. As a result, golden gilding can be detected despite being covered by paint layers. Moreover, similar greyscales can be ascribed to the present of lead white paint pigments, since lead-white reﬂects most terahertz radiation [126]. Further analysis on the polyptych was completed in describing the stratigraphy of the artwork. Due to the different refractive indices of the materials in each layer of the painting, reﬂective terahertz radiation can image the entire cross-section structure making it an effective tool [126]. Another investigation into the use of terahertz radiation for the safe imaging of murals was completed by Jackson et al. [125]. Timedomain terahertz spectroscopy was used to image the underlying paint layers of mural paintings. Due to the low energy and power of terahertz radiation, this technique is non-destructive in analysing the constituents in a painting. In addition, terahertz exhibits great penetrability for most materials in paintings, as such provides values for detecting materials underneath the surface layers [125]. For this study, multiple mural samples were produced for different pulsed-terahertz imaging techniques. The sample frescoes were composed of dry pigment paints on a thick plaster material slab. The image of a butterﬂy was covered by a coating of surface paint. This image illustrating the ﬁgure of the underlying drawing of a butterﬂy is displayed in Fig. 10. The underlying butterﬂy visage was revealed from the terahertz spectral information [125]. Terahertz imaging techniques were successful in highlighting underlying defects and pigments in different works of art. Alternative imaging techniques can be used in the beginning to image the entirety of the oeuvre locating regions of interest [120]. This could prove useful in revealing cracks and fractures on the surface of a painting, or other damage to the subsurface layers [125]. Terahertz imaging can then be applied to the specimen, imaging smaller regions where deﬁciencies have formerly been detected. The detection of potentially defective regions by means of a multi-sensing approach is valuable for diagnosing artworks [120,125]. 8.2. Terahertz time-domain reﬂectography XRF and IRR imaging has been used in revealing whole paint compositions underneath the primary paint layers [9]. However, this method suffers when imaging anything underneath some metallic pigments (such as lead-white paint) due to the density of the material [122]. As a result, many paintings cannot be imaged thoroughly or effectively as lead-white paint is ubiquitous in historic artworks. This led to the investigation for alternative imaging techniques that could penetrate most paint types present in archetypal historic paintings. As discussed previously, terahertz radiation provides modern imaging technologies the ability to conduct precise and thorough imaging on the stratigraphy of paintings [120]. As such, it showcases the ability to contrast well with materials deep within the strata of different painting types [122,125]. The application of terahertz radiation for time-domain imaging of hidden defects in wooden panel paintings was completed by Skryl et al. [127]. Like previous uses of terahertz radiation for timedomain spectroscopic imaging, time-domain pulsed imaging can be used identify defects within the subsurface layers of a painting. For this study, terahertz radiation was used to accomplish transmission imaging, and two types of reﬂectography imaging techniques. THz-TDR was used to accurately image the different layers within the surface of a painting [127]. From this information, tomographic images can be constructed allowing a cross-section of the painting to be studied. The different types of material and their thicknesses can be resolved with THz-TDR techniques. Moreover, hidden defects and paintings can be detected with tomographic methods with terahertz radiation [127]. THz-TDR was used to detect hidden defects in a Russian wooden panel painting. Imaging was conducted on a region that visually exhibited degradation and cracking of the surface media layers. It was determined that formerly, a knot was removed from the wooden substrate and was ﬁlled with a foundational layer of media. Furthermore, the painting at the surface of this region was also replaced at the time of ﬁlling in the knot hole to restore the painting back to its original visage [127]. A study conducted by Adam et al. [122] showed how terahertz radiation could be used to image underpainting structures in canvas paintings. Terahertz radiation is desirable for penetrative imaging as it is non-ionising and poses no risk of radiation damage to the specimen. Juxtaposed with x-ray radiation, it has a higher potential to image the stratigraphy of a painting with the lowest risk of damage [122]. Terahertz Time-Domain Reﬂectography (THz-TDR) is a technique that uses terahertz radiation to image the different layers of material from within a painting. An ad hoc painting was produced for the purpose of applying THz-TDR. The painting consisted of three strips of umber with varying thicknesses placed on a canvas substrate. Brush strokes were modiﬁed for each new layer of umber adding differing surface textures and thicknesses [122]. To replicate the typical practice of historic painters, a layer of leadwhite paint was applied over the surface of the umber to act as a preparatory layer. THz-TDR was then applied to image the artiﬁcial prominences (varying thickness and brush-stroke textures) in the sample’s stratigraphy [122]. Interpretation of this information indicated the thickness of the paint material for each layer. The existence or non-existence of an umber layer can be characterised by analysing the data obtained from THz-TDR. By measuring the time difference between major peaks in the reﬂected electric ﬁeld, the thickness of the umber could be determined [122]. Moreover, if the thickness of both materials is considered to be a constant hypothetical value, then the refractive indices of the umber and lead-white paint can be determined [122]. The study showed that terahertz radiation was effective at measuring through the dense primal layers of an experimental painting. It illustrated the potential to be an effective tool that can detect underlying layers within a historic painting. The methodology discussed, on the other hand, had the difﬁculty of deﬁning any layer with a thickness of less than 50 m. Higher signal processing methods would be required to separate the details of closely-spaced paint layers [122]. The painting stratigraphy can still be measured and correspondingly quantiﬁed using time-variant spectral analysis. B. Borg et al. / Journal of Cultural Heritage 44 (2020) 239–259 257 9. Conclusion Technologies available for conservation science are instrumental in understanding art that deﬁnes our culture and artistic expression. Numerous types of analytical techniques have been presented in this review paper. Technologies have been shown to be suitable for examining canvas and panel paintings, murals and various types of artefacts [42,80,112,120]. When applied for analytical purposes, they can assist in the evaluation of a work of art’s physical condition. All forms of artwork will deteriorate over time due to natural phenomena [84,128]. However, unnatural exposure to the elements or incorrect handling of art can lead to the exacerbation of the artwork’s degradation [100]. Optical techniques have been shown to identify and characterise these factors [102,116,129]. Moreover, a formal diagnosis of an artwork using optical techniques can correlate the nature of the material present to the state of decay [77]. This information can be highly valuable to art conservators in taking the right steps to preserve the condition of an artwork. In most cases, early detection of fault or damage in an artwork is important to ensure its longevity with correct, expert treatment. Technology has assisted both scientists and historians alike to work together in improving analytical methods in preserving artwork. Precise, non-destructive methods are fundamental to reliably diagnose artwork [74,120,124]. Electromagnetic radiation and its wavelengths are rudimentary for this kind of analysis. The properties of each wavelength explored throughout this review paper have demonstrated their individual capabilities and strengths. Information on the age, chemical composition, stratigraphy, and surface condition of a painting can be acquired [9,75,122,123,130]. In addition, the stylometry of an artist, defects in a painting substrate, and evidence of a painting forgery can be characterised [33,38,131]. Finally, many of the state-of-the-art techniques discussed in this review paper can assist art conservators and historians to accurately and safely analyse works of art from historic and contemporary times. title: The colours of rock art. Analysis of colour recording and communication systems in rock art research
abstract: Colour recording is a crucial aspect of rock art documentation. In this paper, we evaluate different systems to record the colour of rock art and propose a system to describe and communicate it in a reproducible and non-subjective way. Recording and communication of colour in rock art studies, and in general in archaeology, has been treated as dissociated dimensions. Colour rendition charts incorporated into photographic scenes have been used for recording. This process tries to preserve pictures with colours as close as possible to reality. In addition, the use of subjective terms for description and communication of colour has been replaced by the use of standard colour charts (Pantone, Munsell) that are still based on the naked eye observation of the researcher. During recent years, other systems, such as spectroradiometers, colourimeters or mobile platform apps for recording of colour, have been seen as alternatives. Some of these procedures lack internal corroboration or are useful only for recording or only for communication but not for both purposes. That limitation is a contradiction to the basis of colourimetry, the science of colour. The system proposed in this paper contends with the recording and communication of colour in rock art in a comprehensive, reliable and reproducible way, from digital photographic recording to the ﬁnal communication of colour in publications. © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
