filename: 04-New Dimensions in Conservation Imaging_ Combining Photogrammetry and Photometric Stereo for 3D.pdf
title: New Dimensions in Conservation Imaging: Combining Photogrammetry and Photometric Stereo for 3D Documentation of Heritage
abstract: NEW DIMENSIONS IN CONSERVATION IMAGING: COMBINING PHOTOGRAMMETRY AND PHOTOMETRIC STEREO FOR D DOCUMENTATION OF HERITAGE ARTEFACTS DANIEL BORNSTEIN AND THOMAS J. KEEP  The Grimwade Centre for Cultural Materials Conservation, University of Melbourne, Acton, Australia Classics and Archaeology, School of Historical and Philosophical Studies, University of Melbourne, Melbourne, Australia With the increasing ubiquity of digital media engagement it is worth considering how heritage artefact digitisation can evolve and adapt. Twenty-ﬁrst century computing developments bring low-cost three-dimensional (D) imaging into reach, creating opportunities for cultural materials conservators to implement efﬁcient and comprehensive new avenues in non-contact documentation and engagement. This research presents a workﬂow for combining efﬁcient photometric stereo capture workﬂows with structure-from-motion photogrammetry for comprehensive modelling of high- and low-frequency artefact geometry and surface texture. Presenting a range of potential applications in conservation, the excellent topographic mapping of planar surfaces brings granular accuracy and unparalleled detail to condition documentation and digital reproduction of ﬂat objects with ﬁnely textured surfaces, such as paintings or printing plates. KEYWORDS: D heritage documentation, Cultural materials conservation imaging, Photogrammetry, Photometric stereo, Reﬂectance transformation imaging, Online collections, Digital heritage, Digitisation INTRODUCTION OVERVIEW Global digitisation of art and cultural heritage has made heritage collections more accessible than ever before. Unprecedented possibilities in documentation and imaging support the display, management, organisation, and conservation of signiﬁcant objects and works. Digital outreach has become an expectation of modern collecting institutions, deepening possibilities for public access, research, and engagement. Policies and standards around digitisation and asset management typically relate to photography and -dimensional (D) digital assets, but increasing interest and awareness of D digital models, coupled with developments in computing power and efﬁciency, opens up new possibilities for institutions to document and display more sophisticated and holistic digital representations of cultural materials. Photogrammetry is an accessible D imaging technology which has been successfully used to digitise the geometry of museum artefacts, in some cases to sub-micron accuracy. While it handles high-frequency geometry well, in the authors’ experience capture and resolving power is typically not sufﬁcient to represent the low-frequency ﬁne surface topography which is integral to ‘two-and-a-half-dimensional’ (.D) objects, such as paintings, cuneiform tablets, or relief carvings, where surface texture is integral to signiﬁcance. Although capture of low frequency details is possible with photogrammetry alone, ﬁne surface resolution typically requires expensive specialist equipment such as macro probe lenses which restrict the ﬁeld of view and are most appropriate for small objects such as coins rather than small details on larger objects; Yanagi & Chikatsu ; Percoco, Lavecchia & Salmerón ). Within the conservation industry reﬂectance transformation imaging (RTI) has © The Author(s). DOI: ./.. AICCM Bulletin , Vol. Nos. –, – * Corresponding author. tkeep@unimelb.edu.au / thomas.j.keep@gmail.com, tom.keep@unimelb.edu.au provided a means of documenting such features, however in support for the required software is waning and nearing deprecation and it may be time to explore options with broader support in other industries. Photometric stereo is a technique which may provide a viable alternative to RTI, producing analogous but more cross-compatible results through a simpler process. Often used in videogame development and animation, photometric stereo is better supported by broader communities of users and commercial software producers. Unlike RTI, the results can be exported as standard non-proprietary image formats used in D rendering, making it possible to combine imaging techniques for more comprehensive modelling. This article presents a method for combining the high frequency data of photogrammetry with the low frequency data of photometric stereo recording, resulting in more detailed, reﬁned, and complete documentation. Assets produced through this combined technique facilitate better conservation and public access outcomes through better representation of the visual and spatial qualities of .D artefacts in digital models. PHOTOGRAMMETRY Photogrammetry as a broad technique identiﬁes, tracks, and triangulates common points on the surface of a real-world object within a photograph set, using collinearity equations to calculate and model changes (Historic England , p. ). Structure-from-motion photogrammetry (hereafter, ‘photogrammetry’) was developed subsequently with advancements in digital computing, and uses the photogrammetric equation to generate D digital models from many D photographs taken from different perspectives of an artefact. The relative position of identiﬁed points in three-dimensional space are determined using perspective parallax to create a ‘pointcloud’ (Figure a), which is then connected to create a geometric wire mesh (Figure b). The mesh is ﬁlled to create solid geometry (Figure c), to which a texture is applied (Figure d), creating a digital reconstruction of the artefact (Ackermann ). While many mature technologies have seen use in the heritage industry for D imaging artefacts (including CT scanning, D laser scanning, and structured light scanning) the accessibility of photogrammetry, which requires little in the way of specialist equipment to produce a high quality digital representation, has led to its widespread adoption in the industry (Pavlidis et al. ; Howland, Kuester & Levy , p. ). At a minimum, passable results can be achieved using any digital camera and open-source image-processing software, allowing for most heritage practitioners to establish workﬂows without additional capital outlay. It has found use in heritage applications such as: digitisation of museum objects and collections (Barsanti & Guidi ); compositional analysis of FIGURE . The stages of photogrammetric modelling: a. point cloud, b. wire mesh, c. solid geometry, d. textured model. Artwork: Ramsay, Hugh (–), James S. MacDonald, , .... Gift of Mrs J.O. Wicking, , reproduced with permission from the University of Melbourne Art Collection. NEW DIMENSIONS IN CONSERVATION IMAGING  AICCM Bulletin , Vol. Nos. –, – museum artefacts (Emmitt, Mackrell & Armstrong ); documentation of heritage sites and rock art (Fraile, García & Klink ; Jalandoni & May ); archaeological scoping and planning (Estes, Jensen & Tinney ) and; creating D assets for use in videogames and virtual displays (Cantatore, Lasorella & Fatiguso ; Carvajal, Morita & Bilmes ). Photogrammetry has been broadly used and recommended for documentation of heritage collections and can play an important role in developing digitally informed conservation strategies (Vogiatzis & Hernández ; Stojićević ). While photogrammetry can produce results which match or exceed the quality of specialist equipment developed for other technologies, it is limited in its ability to record ﬁne surface details, to accurately capture and represent non-Lambertian, or heavily textured, surfaces, and translucent surfaces (Colby ; Mathys et al. ). Combining photogrammetry with complementary image processing and D modelling techniques presents an opportunity to overcome these shortfalls and create high quality, ﬁnely detailed, and accurate D digital models, utilising equipment which is readily available in most conservation or digitisation laboratories (Karami, Menna & Remondino ). PHOTOMETRIC STEREO Photometric stereo is a photoclinometric (‘shape-from-shading’) imaging technique used to record and reconstruct the shape and surface topography of an object. This is accomplished through analysing changes in shadows as a light source is rotated at oblique angles to its surface (Karami, Menna & Remondino , p. ). Largely underutilised in practical settings due to early limitations, recent advances to the underlying algorithms have turned photometric stereo into a viable and convenient means of D documentation (Dessì et al. , p. ). Capture is simple, fast and can be achieved with basic equipment, making it more budget, resource, and time effective when compared with RTI. The process requires only a minimum of three images under varied lighting conditions, however error is minimised with or more images (Brenner, Zambanini & Sablatnig , p. ; Miyazaki & Uegomori ). Due to the ease of use and low capture time of photometric stereo and the technique’s ability to produce highly detailed and lifelike digital renders of real world surfaces it has found use within a broad range of industries, including the development of digital assets for use in videogames and digital animation ﬁlms (Hilliges et al. ; Bieryt ). This contrasts with RTI which is principally used within heritage imaging applications (Mytum & Peterson ; Coules, Orrock & Seow ), restricting the availability of learning resources. Photometric stereo is capable of recording high frequency surface topography, capturing ﬁner detail than conventional photogrammetric modelling with considerably less input data (Karami, Menna & Remondino , p. ). This high degree of spatial resolution allows photometric stereo to capture ﬁne planar surface details of real-world objects typically too minute for conventional photogrammetry, such as the faces of historical coins (Brenner, Zambanini & Sablatnig ; Hess, MacDonald & Valach ), or relatively smooth, featureless surfaces which cannot be modelled photogrammetrically (Smith & Fang ). It can also be used to document material characteristics of an object under disparate ambient conditions—for example, researchers have used photometric stereo to accurately record and subsequently simulate the surface gloss and reﬂectivity characteristics of oil media in ﬁne-art paintings under changing lighting conditions (Hasegawa et al. ). The level of detail which can be captured is limited only by the resolution of the camera used, since every mappable pixel is used for reconstruction (Smith & Fang ). Photometric stereo is typically used to generate three types of data: surface normal maps; height or depth maps; and albedo maps (Figure ). Each of these has its own value in digitally representing surface topography, surface gloss and reﬂectivity and characteristics of artefacts. NORMAL MAPS Photometric stereo was ﬁrst proposed by Robert Woodham () as a means of determining surface orientation at each image point, referred to as the ‘surface normal’, from which geometry can be emulated (Karami, Menna & Remondino , p. ). Rather than producing true geometry, surface normal maps describe the behaviour of light at each point of a texture, informing D rendering engines of which direction light should move as it encounters each pixel of an image texture. When these textures are projected onto D digital objects, they can simulate the appearance of ﬁne detail surface irregularities, thereby displaying a highly realistic appearance of geometry and lighting interaction beyond what is possible with conventional photogrammetric modelling (Blinn ). Normal maps have seen broad use in the gaming industry to add realistic textures to low polygon-count assets, signiﬁcantly increasing computing efﬁciency and reducing storage or bandwidth limitations. Although not true D, this simulated surface is referred to as a .D reconstruction and can be used to add complexity to objects which may be challenging to image in D (Vogiatzis & Hernández , p. ). ALBEDO MAPS Albedo textures produced by photometric stereo represent the colour information of the recorded object. Colour data is also generated from photogrammetric modelling, but as a diffuse map generated as a texture image, rather than an albedo map. Diffuse maps represent colour data as it appeared under the static lighting conditions at the time of capture, with  DANIEL BORNSTEIN AND THOMAS J. KEEP AICCM Bulletin , Vol. Nos. –, – any shadows and highlights integrated into the texture. Albedo maps, alternatively, represent colour data composited from many images of an object under different illumination, removing the inﬂuence of shading (Dessì et al. ). This allows the colour of the object to be digitally represented or simulated more accurately under a variety of arbitrary lighting conditions. HEIGHT MAPS In addition to albedo and normal data, photometric stereo is also capable of estimating the relative surface height of an imaged item, generating a greyscale height map where each grey pixel’s luminance value represents the relative elevation of a planar surface relief. These maps are used to digitally represent variations in physical displacement of geometry and can supplement normal maps by providing lower frequency data which indicates how the surface of a digital object should be displaced, rather than the direction light will reﬂect as represented by the normal map. When normal, albedo, and height maps are combined, they produce a highly detailed digital representation of a surface. REFLECTANCE TRANSFORMATION IMAGING Photometric stereo capture is somewhat analogous to RTI, a technique which has seen broad adoption in heritage imaging. Some applications include: the documentation of cuneiform tablets by Earl et al. (); paintings Hughes-Hallet, Young and Messier (); ancient footprints by Silver (); badly damaged coins by Ciortan et al. () and Mudge et al.(); and rock art by Díaz-Guardamino and Wheatley (). Similar to photometric stereo, during RTI capture the camera and the object remain in a static position while images are taken underapproximately  oblique illumination angles, followed by analysis of the shadow locations to map topographical surface texture. RTI evolved in the early twenty-ﬁrst century out of a modelling system called polynomial texture mapping, or PTM, and (as with photometric stereo) it has the capacity to construct normal maps which take into account Lambertian (i.e., smooth) surfaces and diffuse non-Lambertian (ie., intricate, rough) surfaces (Ciortan et al. , p. ). Highlight Capture RTI, or the ‘black ball’ method, similar to the photometric stereo workﬂow described above, has been used in heritage settings since its development by Cultural Heritage Imaging (CHI) and HP Labs in . Capture time for most objects is roughly –min, and generally requires two or more people to accurately position the light source and expose each image. Beyond acquisition, RTI has a few key differences from photometric stereo. Theoretically, the additional illumination elevations in RTI should provide richer low-frequency surface reﬂectivity than photometric stereo and better resistance to reconstruction errors, or ‘low-frequency warps’ (Mudge et al. ). In the FIGURE . Photometric stereo output ﬁles: a. albedo map, b. height map, and c.-d. two normal maps produced. Artwork: Ramsay, Hugh (–), James S. MacDonald, , .... Gift of Mrs J.O. Wicking, , reproduced with permission from the University of Melbourne Art Collection. NEW DIMENSIONS IN CONSERVATION IMAGING  AICCM Bulletin , Vol. Nos. –, – early days of photometric stereo, when capture and rendering was more complex, the occlusion of D geometry was said to have made RTI “more affordable in terms of costs and processing times” (Mudge et al. , in Díaz-Guardamino & Wheatley , p. ). In however, RTI appears to have had limited uptake outside of cultural heritage applications (Coules, Orrock & Seow ) and processing and viewing of results at present is dependant on legacy software, CHI’s RTI Builder, which was last updated in early . Although RTI Builder provides a userfriendly interface, the requirement for the software to be installed at root level on the computer’s boot drive restricts usage on many institutional workstations. CHI’s software must also be used for viewing RTI’s normal maps, which are produced in proprietary ﬁle formats (.ptm, .rti and .mview). By contrast, normal maps generated with photometric stereo are exported as standard non-proprietary image ﬁles, broadly supported in the industry. In March , CHI announced that they are collaborating with Visual Computing Lab to release RelightLab, a new open source RTI software to replace RTIBuilder and Viewer (Schroer ). However this software is still in ‘active development’ (CHI ), with documentation and full user guides yet to be released. By merit of its ongoing support, adoption and standardisation in other industries, and also of its markedly simpler capture methodology (-times fewer images shot from just a single elevation), photometric stereo should be considered as a viable alternative RTI. Anecdotally and qualitatively, the authors have not encountered low-frequency warp errors with photometric stereo, ﬁnd its acquisition and post-processing workﬂows considerably simpler, and believe the technology has reached a point of practical equivalence with RTI for most applications in heritage imaging. Additionally, normal maps generated with photometric stereo can be combined with other imaging methods, as demonstrated in the below workﬂow . COMBINED WORKFLOW METHODOLOGY BACKGROUND This section presents a methodology for combining photogrammetry and photometric stereo in the imaging of heritage objects, using Hugh Ramsay’s oil-on-canvas portrait of James S. McDonald (Figure ) as a case study. The workﬂow presented was developed while imaging a selection of cultural objects from the University of Melbourne Miegunyah Collection, including paintings by Hugh Ramsay, Henry Gritten (Figure ), and George Lambert; a wood carving by Robert Prenzel; and a series of carved woodcut printing blocks by Helen Ogilvie. Digitisation using photogrammetry and photometric stereo was undertaken as part of the authors’ research project, A New Digital Dimension: Pursuing a Holistic Digitisation Approach to the Miegunyah Collection, supported by the University of Melbourne’s Miegunyah Student Project Awards and the Grimwade Centre for Cultural Materials Conservation (Keep & Bornstein ). Results from this project were presented at the Percy Grainger Museum in December , and where permissions have been granted assets have been made available via the University of Melbourne’s Pedestal D model hosting platform (The University of Melbourne ; see Data Availability). IMAGE CAPTURE Two sets of images were captured for each object digitised: one set for photogrammetric modelling to capture high frequency data for the creation of an overall geographic mesh, with an additional set for photometric stereo imaging to create normal and height maps to provide low-frequency data to improve the rendering of ﬁne surface details (Figure ). PHOTOGRAMMETRY IMAGES Images for photogrammetry were captured using a Sony aR iii digital mirrorless camera. While in principle FIGURE . Image capture setup: a. photogrammetry setup where ‘shadowless’ lighting is created using on-camera ring-ﬂash, off-camera strobes and reﬂectors—note that the artwork is positioned on a turntable; b. full photometric stereo setup showing capture angles demarcated on backdrop, camera on boom arm and off-camera strobe set on a tripod for height angle repeatability; c. photometric stereo capture  DANIEL BORNSTEIN AND THOMAS J. KEEP AICCM Bulletin , Vol. Nos. –, – any camera can be used for photogrammetry, high resolution sensors and lenses will yield better results, reducing noise and digital artefacts when compared with less accurate capture devices such as mobile phone cameras (Fawzy ). The camera was mounted on a tripod to prevent camera shake and manual exposure settings were used for more granular control and consistency. The camera’s base ISO () was used to minimise digital noise and expand dynamic range, and a narrow aperture (between f/and f/) was used to broaden the depth-of-ﬁeld. To optimise perspective and ﬁeld-of-view a mm prime lens was used for small objects, and a mm prime lens for larger. Photos were taken in Sony’s raw ﬁle format (.arw) to allow for maximum capture of detail, dynamic range, colour information and to avoid lossy compression. Objects were shot in a lightbox with soft, even white lighting, in order to mitigate lighting inconsistencies and reduce the impact of specular reﬂections (direct reﬂections of the light source) on point detection. To ensure at least % overlap between images, objects were placed on an automated turntable and photographed at ° intervals to produce six arrays of images each, with additional sets added for close details. This approximates the -axis photography workﬂow suggested by Tang, Cerasoni and Hallett () whereby an object is rotated onto a different face between image sets. An X-Rite ColorChecker Mini or Passport card was photographed alongside each object for colour proﬁling, with small adjustments made in Adobe Lightroom where necessary. A mm scale bar was photographed alongside each object for scaling the D models. PHOTOMETRIC STEREO IMAGES Images for photometric stereo imaging were captured using a Sony aR iii digital mirrorless camera ﬁxed to a C-stand and positioned directly above the target object with the lens parallel to the object. Manual settings were used on the camera, with a short shutter speed and low ISO to prevent ambient light from being captured during the process. Photos were taken in Sony’s native raw ﬁle format (.awr). A monopod-mounted portable strobe ﬂash was used to light the target object at an elevation of approximately °, with photographs captured from the same position as the ﬂash was rotated around the target at ° intervals. Similar to the Highlight RTI methodology endorsed by CHI, a string line was used to mark the correct distance and intervals, and a reﬂective sphere was included in all exposures to enable the software to calibrate the exact position of illumination by analysing specular highlights (direct reﬂections of the light source) on the ball. IMAGE PROCESSING PHOTOGRAMMETRIC MODEL PROCESSING Photogrammetric modelling was undertaken in Agisoft Metashape v... Images were masked to exclude background areas of the image, such as the lightbox and turntable, then processed on ‘high’ settings. A Metashape standard workﬂow was used to produce the ﬁnal mesh, with minimal necessary cleaning to remove low conﬁdence points. An k texture ﬁle (× pixels) was generated to provide colour data. The models were exported as .obj D model ﬁles with .jpg texture images. While this workﬂow was developed for Agisoft Metashape, in theory any photogrammetry software can be used for this step, and the reader is encouraged to explore open-source options. PHOTOMETRIC STEREO PROCESSING Images were processed using Details Capture v... All images were loaded as .tiff ﬁles, converted from the camera’s raw .awr format. Albedo, normal, and height maps were generated automatically by the software. A range of paid and open-source software options are available for photometric stereo processing, but Details Capture was used for its simplicity, speed and satisfactory results. CHI’s RelightLab may provide a good open-source option when full software and documentation is released. COMBINATION WITHIN BLENDER INITIAL APPROACH With the models exported from Agisoft Metashape and texture ﬁles exported from Details Capture, the next step was to combine these ﬁles together in a physically based rendering (PBR) workﬂow to create a more realistic digital representation of the physical artefact. PBR texturing uses image textures, such as those generated through photometric stereo, to procedurally modify the surface appearance in response to a simulated light source. Figure compares a standard photogrammetric model generated with Agisoft Metashape and the same model combined with photometric stereo derived texture maps, as seen under a simulated raking light source within a PBR rendering engine (Blender’s ’Cycles’ engine). This combination was initially achieved by deleting and replacing the surface of the canvas with a basic plane within the D modelling software package Blender v... This replacement surface was textured within Blender using the texture maps produced in Details Capture. For this object results were satisfactory, however issues were anticipated for models without clearly delineated and regular edges. Where rectangular planes do not neatly ﬁt within the model created in Agisoft Metashape assets showed poor integration and geometry clipping in the render, detracting from the verisimilitude of the model. UPDATED WORKFLOW An alternative approach was subsequently found to produce a cleaner effect without the need to combine objects. By default, the texture ﬁles exported with photogrammetry models are an image ﬁle (.png or NEW DIMENSIONS IN CONSERVATION IMAGING  AICCM Bulletin , Vol. Nos. –, – .jpg) made up of a visually unintelligible arrangement of pixels, which are ‘baked’ (or mapped) to UV coordinates on the D mesh (u and v coordinates are used for the D plane, since x, y and z are used in the D). By reprojecting the UV map coordinates of the digital model, new texture ﬁles can be generated with elements which are visually intelligible to humans, as in Figure . These textures can be manually manipulated using image editing software such as Adobe Photoshop, transplanting the normal maps generated using photometric stereo. In this workﬂow: • the models produced in Agisoft Metashape are imported into Blender • within Blender, the models are remeshed into a more organised and uniform arrangement of quadrilateral polygons (Figure ) • UV coordinates are reprojected onto the remeshed model, allowing textures to be mapped according to a predictable and editable arrangement • the reprojected model is imported back into Agisoft Metashape and retextured with both a diffuse map and a normal map derived from the original model, while retaining the imported UVs • a normal map is generated in Agisoft Metashape for the remeshed model, using the original model as the source for the normal map data. This step generates a normal map organised intelligibly according to the same projected Uvs, seen in Figure d • the newly generated intelligible normal map can now be directly overlaid and aligned with the normal map produced via photometric stereo in Adobe Photoshop, creating a new merged normal map With the overall UV arrangement of the normal map unchanged, the new merged normal can still be mapped onto the remeshed D model, thereby combining the low frequency surface data from photometric stereo with the high frequency shape data of photogrammetric modelling. This achieves considerably greater detail than is possible through photogrammetry alone, as shown in Figure . RESULTS AND DISCUSSION COMPARISON OF INDEPENDENT AND COMBINED RESULTS The adapted workﬂow using UV reprojection and composited normal maps proved to be highly effective, efﬁcient, and inexpensive. Requiring no specialist equipment, and with minimal investment in hardware and software, high-quality digital surrogates were produced for artworks from the University of Melbourne’s collections. The digital surrogates reproduce not only the low frequency data representing the overall shape of the pieces, but also the high frequency data representing the ﬁne surface details of the most integral portion of the work, the painted canvas (Figure ). The combined model presents a much more holistic, complex, and lifelike representation of the work. The two models were visually compared within Blender’s Cycles engine under a simulated raking light source to determine the degree to which the addition of the photometric stereo data inﬂuenced the interaction of the light source with ﬁne details on the surface of the canvas. In the original photogrammetry model, canvas ﬁbres and impasto details are too minute to be captured in the geometry of the mesh but are visually represented within the diffuse texture. The diffuse texture being static, these ﬁbres do not interact in any way with simulated light sources, creating ﬂat and lifeless renders in a virtual environment. With the combined normal map, each individual ﬁbre and brushstroke is captured in the texture and topographical data, with a distinctive and dynamic ability to interact with a simulated light source, casting small shadows and diffusing the light as it moves around the surface. LIMITATIONS AND PROBLEMS The combined photogrammetry and photometric stereo workﬂow remains reasonably simple while it can rely on automated UV reprojection functions, and as such is well suited to modelling objects with large and distinctive ﬂat planar surfaces, such as wood panels, printing plates or a canvas within a frame. While the process may conceivably work for objects with greater geometric complexity, the workﬂow would require manual manipulation of UV maps within D modelling software, a practice which is highly technical to the point of impracticality (see Karami, Menna & Remondino ). Practitioners are beginning to explore the potential for automated photometric stereo and photogrammetry combination workﬂows on complex geometry (socalled ’photometric extended photgorammetry’) but this technique requires sophisticated custom-made hardware and at present no workﬂow guide is available. Deep frame mouldings around the canvas cast shadows at raking light angles, resulting in some issues in the normal maps. These issues were relatively minor, resulting in approximately mm of unusable data near the edge of the frame rebate, which could be cropped from the normal map. However, this leaves a small strip around the edge of the combined model which does not beneﬁt from the photometric stereo normal data, perhaps noticeable by viewers FIGURE . While these shadows could be ameliorated by positioning the ﬂash at a higher level, photometric stereo has been demonstrated to have lowest error around a ° degree elevation, with increased error above this angle (Brenner, Zambanini & Sablatnig ). There is potential for misalignment to create misrepresentations on the combined model’s rendered surface. If photometric stereo and photogrammetry image capture was undertaken concurrently, radial targets could be included in both image sets to provide clear points of alignment, provided that these targets are not at all moved during photography. SCALABILITY AND AUTOMATION This process presents a fast and cost-effective solution for comprehensive physical documentation of artefacts where three-dimensionality and ﬁne surface detail is integral to signiﬁcance. While capture is marginally slower than simple photography, it would not be especially onerous to implement photometric stereo and photogrammetry capture workﬂows into standard digitisation or even conservation treatment documentation procedures. A practitioner with limited training can undertake capture for most objects using photogrammetry in –min, and for photometric stereo in –min. Approximately– min of hands-on manual post-processing should be sufﬁcient for most objects, if this step is not outsourced, and for those not willing to commit to commercial software solutions open-source options are available. The introduction of a specialised space with illumination angles marked out, or even an automated illumination and shutter-release system, could conceivably reduce capture time from minutes to seconds. With a relatively minor investment in training, cameras, lighting equipment and a high-powered PC, this process would allow for a small team of technicians to produce several combined models daily. Partial capture automation, as was used in this research, is easily achieved for only a few hundred dollars using readily available programmable turntables designed for commercial product-photography. This workﬂow can be tweaked and improved with programmable scripts or electronic exposure triggers. With a higher capital outlay, capture efﬁciency can be further improved with a fully automated turntable setup for photogrammetry and an automated dome lighting setup for photometric stereo, plausibly achieving full object capture in mere seconds. As an example of mass-automation of photogrammetry within the cultural sector, Fraunhofer IGD’s CultLabD uses a conveyor belt to transport an object through two stations made up of calibrated cameras on arced rails and an articulating arm, completing model capture in minutes with up to ,-megapixel images (Santos et al. ). Automated RTI domes, featuring calibrated hard-mounted lights within a rigid semi-spherical structure, have been used to reduce capture of + exposures at multiple elevations from –min down to just –min (Schroer et al. , p. ; Florindi et al. , p. ). For photometric stereo, which requires far fewer images, subminute capture time is realistic. Commercial options exist for domes, but these can be rivalled in speed and capture detail by inexpensive home-builds (Florindi et al. , p. ). Some domes implement multi-spectral imaging, making them effective and efﬁcient systems for a range of heritage documentation applications (Watteeuw et al. ). Such setups are ideal for reasonably homogenous collections, since capture scalability generally suffers with the speciﬁcity of the setup—for example, calibrated system FIGURE . NEW DIMENSIONS IN CONSERVATION IMAGING  AICCM Bulletin , Vol. Nos. –, – architecture such as a rigid dome structure limits the size of objects which can be imaged (Giachetti et al. , p. ). While post-capture workﬂow bottlenecks remain a barrier to large-scale digitisation, post-processing and hardware resourcing can be economised online through cloud-based photogrammetric processing servers, such as the one natively provided with an Agisoft Metashape Professional Edition licence. Free and open-source options exist for all the software packages used, for example, GIMP in place of Adobe Photoshop, and AliceVision Meshroom in place of Agisoft Metashape, and online resources are widely available to help users learn the software. CHI’s transition to RelightLab for RTI may provide a good opensource interface for photometric stereo processing, once documentation and stable releases are available. The launch of the Creative Commons Public Domain Dedication (CC) for D digital assets (Sketchfab ) allows institutions to explore more vivid and accessible options for digital outreach with online asset libraries such as Sketchfab and Pedestal D. These platforms also provide an opportunity to crowdsource metadata generation, which has been used effectively in the heritage sector to improve classiﬁcation, transcription, correction, revision, and documentation of collection metadata, or to ‘nichesource’ data from expert knowledge-pools which it would otherwise be unviable for a curatorial team to pursue (Navarrete , p. ). The raw data and output being standard image and D formats, ﬁle storage should be manageable with existing digital asset management workﬂows. POSSIBLE APPLICATIONS VIRTUAL REPRODUCTION Online D model galleries such as Pedestal D or SketchFab allow for open web hosting of these models, with the capacity to embed the D model viewer within any webpage and viewed from any browser that supports an HTML iframe, as is used for YouTube or Vimeo videos. Beyond this basic accessibility, the models can also be imported into D virtual environments and facilitate the creation of true virtual galleries viewable within a virtual reality headset (Forte, Pescarin & Pujol-Tost ; Economou & Pujol-Tost ; Carvajal, Morita & Bilmes ). Surrogacy in heritage and the arts is no new concept, with a longstanding tradition of plaster casting sculpture or, since the invention of the camera, photographing paintings. While theorists have considered the implications of this process on the original work, the creation of a reproduction or surrogate is an essential process in the democratisation of art (Benjamin ). With the new capacities for high-deﬁnition recording that emerged in the digital age, the creation of high-quality digital surrogates of heritage objects or works of art facilitates a more accessible, informative, and lifelike experience for viewers who are unable to access these works in person. ‘Virtual museums’ have been disseminating art online since the s for greater public access, but this has typically meant relatively ﬂat, low quality images which fail to convey the evocative surface minutiae and spatial elements which give a work depth and visual complexity (Carvajal, Morita & Bilmes , p. ). To see a photograph of a van Gogh, no matter the resolution or accuracy of the colours, is a far stretch from viewing it within the gallery space, where the thick and rich impasto catches the light, casts subtle shadows, and produces glimmering specular highlights (Wei Luo et al. ). While photographic reproduction has contributed signiﬁcantly to the international fame and popular recognition of van Gogh, for example, a different appreciation of his work is reserved for those with the privilege of viewing one in person. High quality D digitisation presents an opportunity to narrow this gap, and with progressive improvements to web-hosted D model viewing, online exhibitions using D models have become an easily accessible resource for modern curators (Champion & Rahaman ). The value of digital surrogacy has been well noted, particularly for works which are not publicly available or displayed. Discussing the digitisation of photographs, for instance, Paul Conway has noted that "if information from analogue sources is not readily available in digital form, it simply does not exist from the perspective of the vast majority of potential users" (Conway , p. ). Public cultural institutions have a responsibility to make their collections accessible to the public, while providing a representation as close as possible to viewing in person. Digital surrogacy is a promising avenue to facilitate this. In combining photometric stereo and photogrammetry, custodians can enrich the online experience of heritage objects through fuller digital surrogacy—by producing digital assets which can better stand in for the physical artefact when access is restricted by distance, wealth, circumstance, or even destruction of the original (Arnold & Kaminski ). PHYSICAL REPRODUCTION The D mesh itself has potential applications for computer aided design and manufactured (CAD/ CAM) physical replicas which can be used to facilitate public outreach (Wilson et al. ), object-based learning in school and university settings (Pollalis et al. ), and supplement existing physical collections by contextualising existing works with D printed replicas of similar objects (Cooper ). Converting a digital D model format ﬁle (such as .obj) into a D print ﬁle is easily achieved with freely available, user-friendly D printer slicer software packages. Objects which have previously separated from physical audience interaction by the protective barrier of the vitrine could be digitised, converted to a D print ﬁle, and printed as a replica within the space of a single day.  DANIEL BORNSTEIN AND THOMAS J. KEEP AICCM Bulletin , Vol. Nos. –, – The simplicity of photometric stereo brings into the conservator’s reach richer avenues of reproduction for ‘ﬂat’ artefacts, typically reproduced in two dimensions. The high-frequency surface data derived from photometric stereo is sufﬁciently detailed to mill texturally accurate substrate facsimiles. Such substrates can be overlaid with digital inkjet prints to create facsimiles of paintings accurate down to the impasto, or even multispectrally colour-matched to the original artefact using multi-layer pigment printing (Zalewski ; Shi et al. ). Mechanical printing plates such as woodblocks or copper etchings could be D printed or milled to micron-level accuracy, opening up the possibility to run a proof or edition from facsimile plates without stressing or wearing the original artefact. Where it is not necessary to run a full edition, or else the rights to do so are complicated by copyright or other reasons, a hybrid reproduction workﬂow can be employed. As an example, the University of Melbourne Miegunyah Collection holds a series of woodcut printing blocks by artist Helen Ogilvie, however the collection does not hold any prints taken from these plates. The woodcuts, commissioned as Christmas cards by Mab and Russell Grimwade depict their Toorak estate Miegunyah and surrounds. During the New Digital Dimension project (Keep and Bornstein ), the authors were able to generate simulated prints from these plates. This was done by using the ‘curves’ tool in Adobe Photoshop to isolate a speciﬁc band of grey tones within the depth map image, essentially isolating a single cross-sectional elevation of the print-surface of the blocks. Tones outside this range were then clipped, and the canvas ﬂipped horizontally, leaving a perfect black and white reproduction of the print surface which could be viewed digitally or printed onto a ﬁne printmaking paper (assuming custodians had the legal right to do so). ADVANCED CONDITION DOCUMENTATION The planar surface detail in photometric stereo scans and the geometric information captured with photogrammetry can be used to document dimensional and topographical changes to an object with greater accuracy and comprehensiveness than any condition report or overlay. As an example, consider bark paintings in a travelling show. Bark paintings are irregularly shaped objects, especially sensitive to environmental ﬂuctuation, often containing friable or unbound pigment-media. Consider how much simpler it would be to track distortion or media loss in bark paintings using overlaid digital models or surface texture maps than it would be to physically describe or even photograph such changes in a travelling show. If models were captured before and after transit or display then the context under which any change occurs could be determined unequivocally. With simple contrast adjustments, depth maps can be quickly adapted into impasto or craquelure maps of a painted surface. Figure shows one such mapping of Henry Gritten’s Melbourne from Botanical Gardens, , generated by the authors during their New Digital Dimension research in . Considering that depth maps are generated using only – images, captured in minutes or less, these would be ideal for pre- and post- treatment or loan documentation records. Damage is captured and can be tracked over time, even before it is detected by conservators. As simulated illumination is used for viewing, the same model would sufﬁce to show an object under normal illumination or raking light, capturing detail or damage which a photographer might miss or fail to emphasise at the time of capture in a regular photograph. Workﬂows have been developed for undertaking RTI using photomicrographs, giving it potential as a tool for accurately documenting change which would be imperceptible to the naked eye (Cosentino ; Hughes-Hallett, Young & Messier ). As an example, it could be used to monitor a paint or glass surface for micro-ﬁssures which may occur during low-temperature pest treatment, or due to the stress of transit or display conditions. CONCLUSIONS In , digitisation standards for art and heritage collections generally represent artefacts in just two dimensions, even where the aspects which contribute to their cultural meaning may be three-dimensional. As D surrogation begins to see adoption in virtual collections it is important to consider how digitised assets can best represent the signiﬁcance of the objects they represent. Combining photogrammetry with photometric stereo presents a low-cost and time effective methodology for generating highly detailed digital surrogates for a range of object-types commonly found in heritage art and collections. While currently post-processing complexity and software requirements remain a barrier to large-scale digitisation projects, capture time for individual objects is fast and achievable for a practitioner with limited training and minimal capital outlay. The workﬂow is also scalable depending on an institution’s documentation needs and commitment to the technique. This methodology captures richer data relating to visual elements and surface behaviour of artefacts, useful for documentation, conservation, and public outreach. title: DIGITAL IMAGE ANALYSIS OF THE VISIBLE REGION THROUGH SIMULATION OF ROCK ART PAINTINGS
abstract: Non-destructive rock art recording techniques are getting special attention in the last years, opening new research lines in order to improve the level of documentation and understanding of our rich legacy. This paper applies the principal component analysis (PCA) technique in images that include wavelengths between 400-700 nm (visible range). Our approach is focused on determining the difference provided by the image processing of the visible region through four spectral images versus an image that encompasses the entire visible spectrum. The images were taken by means of optical filters that take specific wavelengths and exclude parts of the spectrum. Simulation of rock art is prepared in laboratory. For this purpose, three different pigments were made simulating the material composition of rock art paintings. The advantages of studying the visible spectrum in separate images are analysed. In addition, PCA is applied to each of the images to reduce redundant data. Finally, PCA is applied to the image that contains the entire visible spectrum and is compared with previous results. Through the results of the four visible spectral images one can begin to draw conclusions about constituent painting materials without using decorrelation techniques. Key words: digital archaeology, cultural heritage, documentation, rock art, decorrelation techniques, digital analysis. Resumen: Las técnicas documentación no destructivas de arte rupestre están recibiendo especial atención en los últimos años, abriendo nuevas líneas de investigación para la mejora del nivel de documentación y comprensión de nuestro patrimonio. Este artículo aplica la técnica de análisis de componentes principales (ACP) en imágenes de longitudes de onda entre 400-700 nm (rango visible). Nuestro enfoque se centra en determinar las diferencias proporcionadas por el procesamiento de imágenes de la región del visible a través de cuatro imágenes espectrales y una imagen que abarca el espectro visible completo. Las imágenes se tomaron con filtros ópticos que recogen longitudes de onda determinadas y excluyen parte del espectro.Se preparó una simulación de pigmentos de arte ruprestre en laboratorio. Para ello se hicieron tres tipos de pigmentos diferentes simulando la composición del material de las pinturas rupestres. Se analizaron las ventajas de estudiar el espectro visible en imágenes separadas. Además, se aplicó ACP a cada una de las imágenes para reducir la información redundante. Finalmente, se realizó ACP a la imagen que abarca todo el espectro visible y se comparó con los resultados anteriores. A través de los resultados de las cuatro imágenes espectrales del visible se pueden sacar conclusiones sobre los materiales que constituyen la pintura sin aplicar técnicas de decorrelación. Palabras clave: arqueología digital, patrimonio cultural, documentación, arte rupestre, técnicas de decorrelación, análisis digital

Palabras clave: arqueología digital, patrimonio cultural, documentación, arte rupestre, técnicas de decorrelación, análisis digital 1. Introduction Rock art is constantly exposed to natural weathering, threatening and degrading the art. There are many natural and human agents that affect the integrity of cultural sites. Independently of the level of the attacks, the different agents are steadily altering the main features conditions of the archaeological sites. Levantine rock art is located in areas exposed to direct light. Therefore, rock art recording is necessary for the longterm preservation of the fragile archaeological heritage. 169 Carrión-Ruiz, Blanco-Pons, Lerma, 2016. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons 4.0 International License (CC BY-NC-ND 4.0) EDITORIAL UNIVERSITAT POLITÈCNICA DE VALÈNCIA Combining controlled photographic techniques and digital image processing techniques allow users to get ordered matrices of quantitative data, that collect the variability of certain physical properties (e.g. digital number, distribution and variability of pigments), and this variability is accessible to quantitative analysis methods (Alcalde et al. 1998). Nowadays the analysis of rock art with digital images is considered an indirect technique essential (Brady and Gunn 2012; Cerrillo-Cuenca and Sepúlveda 2015; Clogg et al. 2000; Mark and Billo 2006). Current digital cameras are easy to use and offer high quality imagery. These devices are able to acquire the objects’ reflected radiation in three visible spectral bands (Red (R), Green (G) and Blue (B)) highly correlated. On the other hand, current digital image processing techniques are based on remote sensing techniques. (Alcalde et al. 1998) reports controlled transposition of concepts, methods and techniques from the field of remote sensing to the rock art context. These techniques are non-invasive, and they can be used to enhance paintings that cannot be easily distinguished (Mark and Billo 2006; Rogerio-Candelera 2015). This is one of the central research lines today. These concepts provide the scientific exploitation and promote the dissemination of rock art knowledge. One of the most popular techniques used in the remote sensing field is principal components analysis (PCA). PCA is a very useful technique to achieve the decorrelation of digital image levels. Due to a high correlation between visible bands, decorrelations algorithms are used to reduce the redundant data. Thus differences between spectral bands are highlighted. Many references can be found in which digital image processing was undertaking with these decorrelation techniques. (Rogerio-Candelera et al. 2011) examined through a laboratory test the meaning of each of the principal components obtained in PCA. (Mark and Billo 2002) applied the decorrelation strech (DS) technique to enhance rock art paintings. Furthermore, (Le Quellec et al. 2015) reported the advantage of recording deteriorated figures that would have been missed. Using decorrelation image techniques can automate part of the image analysis. Therefore motif identification is improved and the process is accelerated in most cases (Díaz-Andreu et al. 2006; Domingo et al. 2015; Gunn et al. 2010; Rogerio-Candelera et al. 2011). This paper applies the PCA technique in images that include wavelengths between 400-700nm (visible range). Our approach is focussed on determining the difference provided by the image processing of the visible region through four images that divide the visible spectrum with a multiband image versus an image that encompasses the entire visible spectrum. The advantages of studying the visible spectrum in separate images are analysed. In addition, PCA is applied to the multiband image. Finally, PCA is performed to the image that contains the entire visible spectrum and is compared with the previous results. 2. Nature of light Humans perceive objects’ colour by the reflection of light. That light travels in the form of electromagnetic waves (EM) and the wavelength () is the most important characteristic of EM radiation. Humans and visible imaging sensors such as digital cameras only can capture a portion of visible EM radiation. This range contains wavelengths between 380 nm and 750 nm (Fig. 1). Figure 1: The Electromagnetic spectrum. The imaging sensors receive the reflected radiation off from a scene. Nevertheless, these devices usually incorporate filters that divide the incoming EM radiation in three visible spectral bands (RGB). As (Verhoeven 2008) describes, digital cameras use a Bayer pattern with photodiodes in groups of four. In each group there are two green filters (summing up 50% of the incoming light), one red filter (summing up 25% of the incoming light) and one blue filter (summing up 25% of the incoming light). As (Domingo et al., 2015) reported, the RGB bands are most of the times highly correlated, yielding little differences between spectral bands. This aligns with the application of decorrelations algorithms. 3. Principal component analysis (pca) PCA is a multivariate analysis technique that allows the reassignation of digital number (DN) values in a new non-correlated reference system without losing information. As Cerrillo-Cuenca and Sepúlveda (2015) noted, PCA is efficient in the treatment of multiband images (RGB colour images and multispectral images). The result of this technique is a group of non-correlated images that are the principal components. The number of principal components depends on the number of input image bands. In these components the original image information is divided. (Rogerio-Candelera et al. 2011) reported that the second component (PC2) and the third component (PC3) can be useful to create a new colour image. Occasionally, the last component PC3 only has residual information and in some cases it allows users to detect invisible features (Cerrillo-Cuenca and Sepúlveda 2015; RogerioCandelera et al. 2011). 4. Laboratory simulation and test For the simulation of rock art in laboratory we made three different pigments and simulated rock art paintings in laboratory. Manganese and charcoal were used to reproduce mineral and organic black pigments, respectively, and iron oxide was used to emulate either red or reddish colours. Some photographs were taken afterwards the pigments were painted on a stone. 170 DIGITAL IMAGE ANALYSIS OF THE VISIBLE REGION THROUGH SIMULATION OF ROCK ART PAINTINGS This work is licensed under a Creative Commons 4.0 International License (CC BY-NC-ND 4.0) EDITORIAL UNIVERSITAT POLITÈCNICA DE VALÈNCIA Our approach is based on the possibility to analyse motifs in rock art by means of visible images. For the testing, we analyse four images that divide the visible spectrum, each of the images contains a portion (wavelength range) of the visible region. It allows us to treat each visible spectral wavelength range separately. On the other hand we analyse the image that covers the entire visible range. 4.1. Pigments simulation For the simulation of rock art paintings in laboratory, the sketches were painted based on the procedures developed by several authors (López-Montalvo et al. 2014; Vicent et al. 1996). In that way three different pigments were made:  To reproduce mineral black pigments found in black features in Levantine rock art, manganese was used.  To emulate organic black pigments, we used charcoal of burned pine branches.  To emulate red or reddish colours we used iron oxide, one of the most used materials for elaborating red figures. The technique consists in a mix of the pigment with water and animal fat. These mixes are applied on sandstone of approximately 4 x 4 x 0.5 cm and over it simple motifs were painted with a feather (Fig. 2). Figure 2: Simulation of rock art paintings. 4.2. Recording All of the images were taken with a Fujifilm Finepix IS Pro camera with a camera lens of 60 mm focal length, f/8, ISO 100 and variable exposure time depending on the wavelength. Optical filters were required to take specific wavelengths and exclude parts of the spectrum. The filters selected were bandpass filters spanning the visible electromagnetic spectrum. These types of filters reflect unwanted wavelengths. Trying to recreate the illumination conditions of the rock art locations, the paintings were photographed with sunlight We used five different filters of Midwest Optical Systems Inc (Midopt, 2016), Fig. 3:  BP-550 (Near-IR/UV Block-Visible Bandpass Filter): Passes all visible light (useful range 410-690 nm).  BP-470 (Blue Band pass Filter): This filter has a spectral bandwidth in the blue range (useful range 425-495 nm).  BP-525 (Light Green Bandpass Filter): This filter passes the green range (useful range 500-555 nm).  BP590 (Orange Bandpass Filter): This filter has a spectral bandwidth in the orange range (useful range 560-600 nm).  BP635 (Light Red Bandpass Filter): This filter has a spectral bandwidth in the red range (useful range 615-645 nm). Figure 3: Five bandpass filters used for the testing. The number of MidOpt filter is pointed out in each image. Conventional digital cameras convert the reflected radiation off from the objects in three bands: Red (R), Green (G) and Blue (B). The results after interchanging the filters were five images of three-bands each one (Fig. 4), stored in RAW format, which creates files with higher colour depth and allow users to correct the exposure (Verhoeven and Schmitt 2010). 4.3. Digital image analysis Images taken are analysed individually first and then jointly: 1) BP470, (blue image); 2) BP525 (green image); 3) BP590 (orange image); 4) BP635 (red image); and 5) BP550 (entire visible spectrum image). Figure 5 shows the workflow of the digital image analysis. 4.3.1. Greyscale Visualization According to (Brady and Gunn, 2012) monochrome images are easiest to deal with as only one colour needs 171 Carrión-Ruiz, Blanco-Pons, Lerma, 2016. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons 4.0 International License (CC BY-NC-ND 4.0) EDITORIAL UNIVERSITAT POLITÈCNICA DE VALÈNCIA to be adjusted. Therefore, each band is converted to greyscale getting monochrome images. In these images the division of pigments can be seen much more clearly (Fig. 6). Figure 4: Output images after blocking the light with the five bandpass filters. The number of the filter used is pointed out in each image. Figure 5: Digital image analysis workflow. The three types of pigments are observed in the greyscale images. In the orange image the differentiation between iron oxide and manganese pigment is seen. Furthermore, the iron oxide pigment and the charcoal pigment with the same colour are seen (Fig. 6c). That is, there are differences between red and black pigments, but there are not differences between black pigments. In both the blue and the green images there are not differences between pigments (Figs. 6a and 6b). Finally the red image shows a differentiation of the three pigments (Fig. 6d). Hence, in this case differences between these simulated pigments are seen as recorded wavelength increases. Figure 6: Detail of paintings in greyscale after bandass filter photography: a) blue, b) green, c) orange, d) red. 4.3.2. Multiband image PCA To reduce redundant data, PCA is applied to a multiband image composed by blue, green, orange and red images. For this multiband image, four components are obtained. Analysing the components obtained, it is observed that the lighting conditions are an important factor. Lighting should be as homogeneous as possible, avoiding areas of sun-shade. In this case the light is not homogeneous and this affects the principal component analysis. We are going to analyse the second, third and fourth principal component (PC) due to fact that reflect hardly visible information. The first component PC1 shows all the paintings in detail although without differentiation between pigments (Fig. 6). In the PC2 the iron oxide pigment is enhanced in black; black manganese pigments are enhanced in light grey and charcoal is almost imperceptible (Fig. 7). Figure 6: Principal component 1 (PC1) from the multiband image. The third component PC3 (Fig. 8) reveals a differentiation between red pigment and black pigments. However if PC2, and PC3 are compared it is observed that the information provided is similar. Finally in PC4 all pigments are displayed in the same colour, charcoal pigment is included (Fig. 9) although this component contains much noise and makes difficult the visualization. 4.3.3. BP-550 PCA In this image, three components are obtained. After the analysis of principal components, PC2 shows that the 172 DIGITAL IMAGE ANALYSIS OF THE VISIBLE REGION THROUGH SIMULATION OF ROCK ART PAINTINGS This work is licensed under a Creative Commons 4.0 International License (CC BY-NC-ND 4.0) EDITORIAL UNIVERSITAT POLITÈCNICA DE VALÈNCIA paintings are divided in two tonalities, pigment composed by iron oxide is displayed in light grey/white colour and pigments composed by charcoal and manganese are visualised in dark grey (Fig. 10). Therefore manganese and charcoal pigments are not divided. In PC3 charcoal disappears remaining manganese very attenuated and iron oxide in black (Fig. 11). Figure 7: PC2 from the multiband image. Figure 8: PC3 from the multiband image. Figure 9: PC4 from the multiband image. Figure 10: PC2 of the BP-550 image. Figure 11: PC3 of the BP-550 image. 4.4. Discussion of results In BP-550 image, PC2 shows an iron oxide well separated from the rest of the pigments. Besides, the distinction between the pigments and the stone basis is clear. PC3 shows that the charcoal disappears, remaining manganese, what means that these two pigments are also differentiated so that they are different pigments. Hence photographing visible spectrum without splitting into bands is useful, especially when the aim is to separate either the pigment from the stone basis or separate different types of pigment. Nevertheless, it is necessary an image processing with decorrelation techniques to obtain good results. On the other hand, with images of partial parts of the visible spectrum, one can begin to draw conclusions without applying decorrelation techniques as long as the theory that supports these images is known. The analysis of greyscale images shows that in the blue image, there are not differences between any pigments, pigments reflect the same amount of light therefore the pigments can not be differentiated. This indicates that the pigments in the blue image have a different behaviour than the rest of the images. It is necessary to ask whether this behaviour is due to its chemical composition. So, if we know the spectral behaviour of the pigments, an image that partly differentiates the visible spectrum can be very useful. 173 Carrión-Ruiz, Blanco-Pons, Lerma, 2016. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons 4.0 International License (CC BY-NC-ND 4.0) EDITORIAL UNIVERSITAT POLITÈCNICA DE VALÈNCIA The red image shows a differentiation of the three pigments. In order to differentiate the pigments used, the red filter is essential. Thus, the differences between these three pigments are seen as far as the recorded wavelength increases. Therefore, in this case the division of the visible range helps users to understand the behaviour of the absorption/reflection of light in the constituent pigment materials as increases/decreases the wavelength. Analysing the PCs of the multiband image, we can observe that charcoal disappears or attenuates in some of the principal components. PCA interprets it as a different data type. It is necessary to ask whether this is due to its chemical composition or to the pigment density on the stone. It is no possible at the moment to give the right answer. The information about the spectral response of the pigments in the visible spectrum is needed to dismiss any of the proposed options. By comparing the results of the analysis between the greyscale images and the PCs of the multiband image, it is observed that PCA complements the analysis of the greyscale images, as it allows a higher enhancement of the pigments. Even it helps to separate painted pigments from the stone basis. In this case both PCA (BP550 and multiband image) show detailed information about the different pigments. 5. Conclusion As we have presented in this paper, digital image processing gives users the opportunity to record and analyse simulated/experimental rock art paintings in detail. The test discussed highlights the value of relatively inexpensive digital technology in recording rock art. The utilization of digital images in short wavelength ranges and the interpretation of these images related with the reflectance of the materials, allows users to establish new methodologies that might help to discover parameters such as the chemical composition of the materials. The use of many multispectral bands (eventually hyperspectral images) can be very useful to discriminate constituent materials. The more spectral bands we consider, the more information about the pigments behaviour to light will be drawn. Nowadays, there exist a wide variety of filters available, even outside the visible region for the ultraviolet and the near infrared regions. These regions offer valuable information in the field of rock art documentation. After the simulation and experimental analysis, more research will be devoted to contrast whether or not the results presented herein can be extrapolated to real Levantine rock art motifs composed by similar pigments and stones. Future research is devoted to analyse the behaviour of the pigments less concentrated on overlaying areas under controled lighting conditions.
